title: Tiling the Plane
teacher_goals: Compare (orally) areas of the shapes that make up a geometric pattern.
Comprehend that the word 'area' (orally and in writing) refers to how much of the plane a shape covers.
purpose: In the first lesson of the course, students compare the amounts of a plane covered by different shapes and recall what they know about area. The investigations allow students to experience two important ideas that will be made explicit in the next lesson: (1) If two figures can be placed one on top of the other so that they match up exactly, then they have the same area, and (2) The area of a region does not change when the region is decomposed and rearranged.
student_facing_goal: Let's look at tiling patterns and think about area.

task_events.answer: 1 blue rhombus covers the same area as 2 green triangles
task_events.excerpt: One blue rhombus covers the same amount of the plane as do 2 green triangles, so 32 blue rhombuses cover the same amount of the plane as do 64 green triangles.
Area of A is 15 square units. Area of B is 15 square units. Area of C is 12 square units. The area of the entire region is 15 + 15 + 12, or 42 square units.

boundary_note: This lesson establishes area as 'how much of the plane a shape covers' through visual comparison of geometric patterns, not yet through formula or grid-square counting. Students commonly misjudge area by perimeter, size, or visual appearance rather than actual coverage; the lesson breaks this by showing that different shapes (triangles, rhombuses, trapezoids) can cover equal amounts of plane. The main arithmetic reasoning involves discovering shape equivalences (e.g., 1 rhombus = 2 triangles) by working with concrete tiling patterns.

title: Finding Area by Decomposing and Rearranging
student_facing_goal: Let's create shapes and find their areas.
teacher_goals: Calculate the area of a region by decomposing it and rearranging the pieces, and explain (orally and in writing) the solution method.
Recognize and explain (orally) that if two figures can be placed one on top of the other so that they match up exactly, they must have the same area.
Show that area is additive by composing polygons with a given area.
purpose: In this lesson, students learn to reason flexibly about two-dimensional figures to find their areas. Students begin by revisiting and refining earlier definitions of "area" that can be used for the rest of the unit. Using tangram pieces, students explore three strategies: composing a figure from smaller pieces with known areas, decomposing a figure and adding the areas of its parts, and decomposing and rearranging into a familiar shape. By the end of the lesson, two key principles are explicit: Figures that match exactly have equal areas, and area is additive (the area of a figure is the sum of the areas of all non-overlapping pieces that compose it).

task_events.excerpt: Notice that you can put together 2 small triangles to make a square. What is the area of the square composed of 2 small triangles?
The area of the small triangle is _____ square units. I know this because...
The area of the medium triangle is _____ square units. I know this because...
The area of the large triangle is _____ square units. I know this because...
The square in the middle has an area of 1 square unit. What is the area of the entire rectangle in square units? Explain your reasoning.
The area of a rectangular playground is 78 square meters. If the length of the playground is 13 meters, what is its width?

boundary_note: Area is fundamentally a measure of how many unit squares cover a region without gaps or overlaps—not a count of pieces or a rote formula. Students often confuse the number of pieces in a composite figure with the number of square units, especially when dealing with non-whole-number areas (e.g., a triangle with area 1/2 square unit). The lesson's core work is to establish that decomposing and rearranging preserve area, and that different shapes can be proven equal in area by exact superposition, building an additive reasoning model that precedes and underlies formula-based approaches.

title: Reasoning to Find Area
student_facing_goal: Let's decompose and rearrange shapes to find their areas.
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally) different strategies for calculating the area of a polygon.
Find the area of a polygon by decomposing, rearranging, subtracting, or enclosing shapes, and explain (orally and in writing) the solution method.
Include appropriate units (in spoken and written language) when stating the area of a polygon.
purpose: This lesson prompts students to find areas of figures—first on a grid and then without a grid. Students continue to use strategies from earlier explorations to find area: decomposing figures into known shapes, decomposing and rearranging, considering a figure as a familiar shape with missing pieces to subtract, and enclosing a figure to subtract extra pieces. For now, rectangles are the only shapes whose areas students know how to calculate, but the strategies will become more powerful as students' repertoires grow.

task_events.position: Activity 3.2 / On the Grid, Figure A
task_events.excerpt: Decompose the figure into rectangles. One way is shown here. (2 · 6) + (4 · 3) = 24

task_events.position: Activity 3.2 / On the Grid, Figure B
task_events.excerpt: Subtract the area of the inner square from the larger square. (6 · 6) – (3 · 3) = 27

task_events.position: Activity 3.2 / On the Grid, Figure C
task_events.excerpt: Decompose into right triangles and rearrange into rectangles. (2 · 4) + (4 · 2) = 16

task_events.position: Activity 3.2 / On the Grid, Figure D
task_events.excerpt: Decompose the shaded square into four right triangles and 2-by-2 square. Rearrange triangles into two 2-by-4 rectangles (16 sq units) plus small square (4 sq units) = 20 sq units.

task_events.position: Activity 3.3 / Off the Grid, Figure E
task_events.excerpt: Decompose the two triangles and rearrange them to form a rectangle with side lengths of 6 centimeters and 2 centimeters.

task_events.position: Activity 3.3 / Off the Grid, Figure F
task_events.excerpt: Decompose the triangles and rearrange them to form two rectangles with side lengths of 4 centimeters and 3 centimeters.
4-by-12 rectangle has area 48 square inches. Two triangles compose into 4-by-6 rectangle (24 sq in). 48 + 24 = 72

boundary_note: This lesson hinges on area invariance under decomposition and rearrangement: students must see that an irregular polygon can be split, rearranged, enclosed, or compared to a larger shape and maintain the same total area. The critical threshold occurs in Activity 3.3 (off-grid figures) and especially with the enclosing-and-subtracting strategy when the inner shape's dimensions are unknown—students cannot numerically compute what they cannot measure, which forces a pivot from grid-counting to reasoning about congruent geometric pieces.

title: Parallelograms
student_facing_goal: Let's investigate the characteristics and area of parallelograms.
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally) different strategies for determining the area of a parallelogram.
Describe (orally and in writing) observations about the opposite side and opposite angles of parallelograms.
Explain (orally and in writing) how to find the area of a parallelogram by rearranging its parts or by enclosing it in a rectangle.
purpose: In this lesson, students recall the defining attributes of parallelograms and use reasoning strategies to find areas. One key approach is decomposing a parallelogram and rearranging pieces into a rectangle; another is enclosing the parallelogram in a rectangle and subtracting extra regions. Through working with various parallelograms, students see that shape influences strategy choice. By repeated reasoning, students discover that parallelograms have related rectangles useful for finding area, preparing them to express area as a formula.

task_events.excerpt: Decompose the parallelogram to create a right triangle and move it to form a rectangle that is 6 units by 6 units. (6 · 6 = 36)
Enclose the parallelogram within a rectangle and subtract the combined area of the extra triangles.
Decompose and rearrange the pieces to form a rectangle and multiply the base and side lengths of the rectangle to find the area. 5 · 3 = 15
Enclose the parallelogram with a rectangle and subtract the area of the extra pieces. (6 · 9) − (6 · 3) = 12
Decompose the parallelogram and rearrange into a rectangle. Multiply the base and height lengths of the rectangle. 6 · 4 = 24
The area of the parallelogram is the same as the area of the rectangle, which is 45 square units.
Find the area of the parallelogram with base 10 cm and height 3 cm.

boundary_note: This lesson's power lies in helping students see that a parallelogram's area always equals the area of a related rectangle—whether found by decomposing and rearranging pieces or by enclosing and subtracting. The most common student confusion: thinking the slant of the sides changes the area, or failing to recognize that orientation doesn't change what a parallelogram is. Students also struggle to choose efficiently between decomposition and enclosure strategies depending on the parallelogram's shape (narrow and stretched favors enclosing; more compact favors rearranging).

title: Bases and Heights of Parallelograms
student_facing_goal: Let's investigate the area of parallelograms some more.
teacher_goals: Comprehend that the terms "base" and "height" refer to one side of a parallelogram and the perpendicular distance between that side and the opposite side.
Generalize (orally) a process for finding the area of a parallelogram, using the length of a base and the corresponding height.
Identify a base and the corresponding height for a parallelogram, and understand that there are two different base-height pairs for any parallelogram.
purpose: In this lesson, students learn about bases and heights of a parallelogram and generalize the process for finding the area of a parallelogram.

task_events.answer: Parallelogram A: base 6 (or 4) units, height 4 (or 6) units, area 24 sq units; B: base 5 units, height 3 units, area 15 sq units; C: base 2 units, height 3 units, area 6 sq units; D: base 4 units, height 2 units, area 8 sq units
task_events.excerpt: For each parallelogram: Identify a base and a corresponding height, and record their lengths in the table. Find the area of the parallelogram and record it in the last column of the table.
In the last row of the table, write an expression for the area of any parallelogram, using b and h.

task_events.answer: Parallelogram S: base 7, height 6, area 42 sq units; Parallelogram T: base 3, height 6, area 18 sq units
task_events.excerpt: What are the values of b and h for each parallelogram? Use the values of b and h to find the area of each parallelogram.

task_events.answer: Parallelogram A: 8 sq units; B: 10 sq units; C: 8 sq units
task_events.excerpt: Find the area of each parallelogram.

task_events.answer: A: 36 sq cm (base 9 cm, height 4 cm); B: 20 sq cm (base 5 cm, height 4 cm); C: b·h (algebraic)
task_events.excerpt: Find the area of each parallelogram. A [9 cm base, 4 cm height]; B [5 cm base, 4 cm height]; C [b base, h height]

boundary_note: The critical breakthrough is that any side of a parallelogram can serve as a base, and height must be perpendicular to that base—not vertical or along an edge. Students routinely confuse side length with height, think height must lie inside the shape, or assume only a horizontal side can be a base. The key insight, developed through decomposition activities, is that cutting a parallelogram and rearranging it into a rectangle preserves area, yielding the formula b × h.

title: Area of Parallelograms
teacher_goals: Apply the formula for the area of a parallelogram to find the area, the length of the base, or the height, and explain (orally and in writing) the solution method.
Choose which measurements to use for calculating the area of a parallelogram when more than one base or height measurement is given, and explain (orally and in writing) the choice.
purpose: This lesson allows students to practice using the formula for the area of parallelograms, and to choose the measurements to use as a base and its corresponding height. The parallelograms are shown both on and off a grid. As students work to find the areas, they begin to see that some measurements are more helpful than others. For example, if a parallelogram on a grid has a vertical or horizontal side, and one of those sides is used as a base, then both the base and the height can be relatively easily determined. Students also see that parallelograms with the same base and the same height have the same area because the products of those two numbers are equal, even if the parallelograms look very different.
student_facing_goal: Let's practice finding the area of parallelograms.

task_events.excerpt: A: base 10 cm, height 6 cm (marked with dashed perpendicular line)
B: height 8 cm (marked), side 10 cm; base-height pair is 15 × 8
C: measurements 8 cm, 7 cm (perpendicular), 9 cm; area = 9 × 7
D: on grid with 1 cm scale; base 7 × height 5 from counting grid squares
In Parallelogram B, what is the corresponding height for the base that is 10 cm long?

task_events.answer: Both have area 24 square units; first is 6×4, second is 3×8
task_events.excerpt: Here are two different parallelograms with the same area. Explain why their areas are equal.

task_events.answer: Two parallelograms with area 20 sq units; valid pairs: 5×4, 10×2, 4×5, or 2×10
task_events.excerpt: Two different parallelograms P and Q both have an area of 20 square units. Draw two parallelograms that could be P and Q.
A base is 9 cm and its corresponding height is 6 cm. 9 × 6 = 54
Was there a length measurement you did not use to find the area? If so, explain why it was not used.

boundary_note: This lesson is fundamentally about measurement selection: height must be perpendicular distance, never the slant side. Students chronically confuse the side length with the height. The arithmetic (base × height) is elementary, but the geometric reasoning—that two differently-shaped parallelograms with the same base-height products have equal area, and that choosing a horizontal or vertical base on a grid makes perpendicular height visible—is the substantive work.

title: From Parallelograms to Triangles
teacher_goals: Describe (orally and in writing) ways in which two identical triangles can be composed, i.e., into a parallelogram or into a rectangle.

Show how any parallelogram can be decomposed into two identical triangles by drawing a diagonal, and generalize (in writing) that this property applies to all parallelograms, but not all quadrilaterals.
purpose: This lesson prepares students to apply what they know about the area of parallelograms to reason about the area of triangles. Highlighting the relationship between triangles and parallelograms is key. The activities use decomposition (breaking a quadrilateral into triangles) and composition (joining two triangles into a quadrilateral) to help students see that a parallelogram can always be decomposed into two identical triangles, and any two identical triangles can always be composed into a parallelogram.
student_facing_goal: I can explain the special relationship between a pair of identical triangles and a parallelogram.

task_events.excerpt: The base of the parallelogram on the left is 2.4 centimeters; its corresponding height is 1 centimeter. Find its area in square centimeters.
The height of the parallelogram on the right is 2 centimeters. How long is the base of that parallelogram? Explain your reasoning.

boundary_note: This lesson establishes that a triangle occupies exactly half the area of a parallelogram sharing the same base and height. The critical misconception to watch for: students often overgeneralize and think any quadrilateral can be split into two identical triangles by drawing a diagonal. The lesson explicitly shows this is false—only parallelograms work. When checking if triangles match, students may incorrectly conclude congruence by rotation alone; they must physically verify the match-up, potentially rotating or even reflecting the cutout before overlaying.

title: Area of Triangles
teacher_goals: Draw a diagram to show that the area of a triangle is half the area of an associated parallelogram.
Explain (orally and in writing) strategies for using the base and height of an associated parallelogram to determine the area of a triangle.
purpose: In this lesson, students reason about areas of triangles by applying strategies such as decomposing and rearranging, or enclosing and subtracting. They observe that the area of a triangle is half of the area of a parallelogram that shares the same base and has the same height. Students arrive at this observation by recalling that two copies of a triangle can be composed into a parallelogram, or by reasoning about rectangles with the same height as the triangle.

task_events.excerpt: The area of each parallelogram is 24 square units. Sample reasoning: The base and height measurements for the parallelograms are 4 units and 6 units, or 6 units and 4 units.
A: 8 square units. Sample reasoning: 8 · 2 = 16, so the area of the rectangle is 16 square units. The area of the triangle is half of that of the rectangle, so it is 8 square units.

C: 10 square units. Sample reasoning: If we make a copy of the triangle, rotate it, and join them along the longest side we would get a parallelogram. The base length is 5 units and the height is 4 units, so the area of the parallelogram is 20 square units. The area of the triangle is half of that area, so it is 10 square units.

D: 12 square units. Sample reasoning: Decompose the triangle into a trapezoid and a small triangle by drawing a vertical line 3 units from the left side. Rotate the small triangle to line up with the bottom side of the trapezoid to create a parallelogram. To get the area of that parallelogram: 4 · 3 = 12.
Elena, Lin, and Noah all found the area of Triangle Q to be 14 square units but reasoned about it differently. Elena used rectangles, Lin used a parallelogram, and Noah decomposed the triangle.

boundary_note: This lesson bridges parallelogram area reasoning to triangle area by establishing the half-relationship: a triangle always has half the area of a related parallelogram. The critical misconception is that students rely on counting grid squares rather than composing/decomposing to build the reasoning. A secondary challenge is identifying perpendicular base-height pairs when a triangle's orientation is irregular; students may pick a non-perpendicular side as a base and struggle to locate its height. The lesson works because it leverages what students already know about parallelograms (base times height) rather than introducing a new formula in isolation.

title: Formula for the Area of a Triangle
teacher_goals: Compare, contrast, and critique (orally) different strategies for determining the area of a triangle.
Generalize a process for finding the area of a triangle, and justify (orally and in writing) why this can be abstracted as ½ · b · h.
Recognize that any side of a triangle can be considered its base, choose a side to use as the base when calculating the area of a triangle, and identify the corresponding height.
purpose: In this lesson, students begin to reason about areas of triangles more methodically: by generalizing their observations up to this point and expressing the area of a triangle in terms of its base and height. Students first learn about bases and heights in a triangle by studying examples and counterexamples. They see that any side of a triangle can be its base, as is the case for parallelograms. They also learn that the height that corresponds to a chosen base is the length of a perpendicular segment that connects the base to the opposite vertex. Next, they identify base-height measurements of triangles and use them to determine area. Then students look for a pattern in their reasoning to help them write a formula for finding the area of any triangle (MP8). They also have a chance to build an informal argument about why the formula works for any triangle (MP3).
student_facing_goal: Let's write and use a formula to find the area of a triangle.

task_events.answer: b · h ÷ 2 or ½ · b · h or A = ½ · b · h
task_events.position: Activity 9.2, Student Task Statement (final row of table)
task_events.excerpt: In the last row, write an expression for the area of any triangle, using b and h.

task_events.position: Activity 9.3, Student Task Statement and Response (Triangle A)
task_events.excerpt: Triangle A: 15 square cm, b = 5, h = 6, A = 5 · 6 ÷ 2 = 15

task_events.position: Activity 9.3, Student Task Statement and Response (Triangle C)
task_events.excerpt: Triangle C: 10.5 square cm, b = 7, h = 3, A = 7 · 3 ÷ 2 = 10.5

task_events.position: Activity 9.4, Student Task Statement and Response (Triangle A)
task_events.excerpt: Triangle A: 9 sq in. Sample reasoning: b = 3, h = 6, area: 9 sq in, ½ · 3 · 6 = 9

boundary_note: Students must understand that the height must be perpendicular to the chosen base (and any side can be a base). The most frequent errors are using non-perpendicular measurements when multiple lengths are labeled, and omitting the division by two from the formula. Connecting to parallelograms (a triangle covers half the area) helps address both misconceptions.

title: Bases and Heights of Triangles
teacher_goals: Draw and label the height that corresponds to a given base of a triangle, making sure it is perpendicular to the base and the correct length.
Evaluate (orally) the usefulness of different base-height pairs for finding the area of a given triangle.
purpose: In this lesson, students further their ability to identify and work with bases and heights in a triangle by drawing a triangle with a given area (reversing the reasoning process they have done so far) and learning to draw (not just to recognize) a segment to show the corresponding height for any given base. The lesson also includes an optional activity in which students learn to choose appropriate base-height pairs to enable area calculations. Because there are three possible pairs of bases and heights in any triangle, some care is needed in identifying the right combination of measurements.
student_facing_goal: Let's use different base-height pairs to find the area of a triangle.

task_events.answer: Multiple valid triangles with area 12 square units (e.g., base=8 height=3; base=6 height=4; or parallelogram base=12 height=2)
task_events.excerpt: This right triangle has a base of 8 units and a height of 3 units. The area is half of 3 · 8 or half of 24, which is 12.

task_events.answer: Perpendicular height segments drawn from opposite vertex to each chosen base
task_events.excerpt: Draw a line segment to show the height for the chosen base in each triangle.

task_events.answer: Triangle A: 22.5 sq units; B: 44 sq units; C: 36 sq units; D: 33 sq units
task_events.excerpt: Triangle A: b = 9 and h = 5, 9 · 5 ÷ 2 = 22.5, area: 22.5 square units

task_events.answer: 4 square units for all four triangles (all with base 2 and height 4)
task_events.excerpt: All of the triangles have the same area: 4 square units. They all have a base of 2 units and a height of 4 units.

boundary_note: Any side of a triangle can serve as a base, and for each base there is exactly one perpendicular height forming the base-height pair. The lesson breaks a core student misconception: that bases must be horizontal and heights must lie inside the triangle. For obtuse triangles, the perpendicular height falls outside the triangle, requiring students to extend the base line and draw the height segment outside the figure's boundary. Students often misuse tools (like index cards) as mere straightedges without ensuring perpendicularity, or confuse side length with height measurement.

title: Polygons
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally) different strategies for finding the area of a polygon.
Describe (orally and in writing) the defining characteristics of polygons.
Find the area of a polygon, by decomposing it into rectangles and triangles, and present the solution method (using words and other representations).
purpose: In this lesson, students explore the defining characteristics of polygons. Then they find the areas of polygons by decomposing the regions into triangles or parallelograms.

task_events.answer: 12, 14, 15, 18, 18, or 28 square units (depending on which quadrilateral A-F selected)
task_events.excerpt: Find the area of two quadrilaterals of your choice. Show your reasoning.
Find the area of the shaded region in square units. Show your reasoning.

student_facing_goal: Let's investigate polygons and their areas.
boundary_note: This lesson introduces formal polygon vocabulary (vertices, edges, sides) and the decomposition strategy for finding areas. Students often initially conflate the polygon outline with its interior region, focusing on perimeter rather than area. The work stays on unit grids with whole dimensions, so arithmetic is straightforward once the decomposition strategy is grasped; no new calculation demands arise that learner automata cannot handle.

title: What Is Surface Area?
teacher_goals: Calculate the surface area of a rectangular prism and explain (orally and in writing) the solution method.
Comprehend that the term 'surface area' (in written and spoken language) refers to how many square units it takes to cover all the faces of a three-dimensional object.
purpose:
Student-facing goal: Let's cover the surfaces of some three-dimensional objects.

Lesson Purpose: This lesson introduces students to the concept of surface area. They use what they learned about the area of rectangles to find the surface areas of prisms with rectangular faces. Students begin by exploring surface area in concrete terms, by thinking about the number of square sticky notes it would take to cover a filing cabinet. First, they make an estimate, and then they think about what information is needed to calculate the actual number of sticky notes. Because no techniques are given, students need to make sense of the problem and persevere in solving it (MP1). Students learn that "surface area" (in square units) is the number of unit squares it takes to cover all the faces of a three-dimensional figure without gaps or overlaps. Later in the lesson, students determine the surface area of rectangular prisms that are built from snap cubes.

task_events.excerpt: How many sticky notes would it take to cover the cabinet, excluding the bottom?

task_events.position: Activity 12.2 Covering the Cabinet (Part 2) — Student Task Statement
task_events.excerpt: How could you find the actual number of sticky notes it will take to cover the cabinet, excluding the bottom?

task_events.position: Activity 12.3 Prisms Built from Cubes — Question 1
task_events.excerpt: Show that it has a surface area of 32 square units.

task_events.position: Activity 12.3 Prisms Built from Cubes — Question 2
task_events.excerpt: What is its surface area? Be prepared to explain or show your reasoning.

task_events.position: Activity 12.4 A Snap Cube Prism — Student Task Statement
task_events.excerpt: A rectangular prism is 3 units high, 2 units wide, and 5 units long. What is its surface area in square units?

boundary_note: This lesson introduces surface area as the total area of all faces of a 3D shape. Students frequently miscount visible faces or omit hidden/opposite faces—especially when working from isometric sketches—and may conflate surface area with volume; repeatedly emphasize that all six faces of a rectangular prism must be counted, including those not drawn.

title: Polyhedra
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally and in writing) features of prisms and pyramids.
Comprehend and use the words 'face', 'edge', 'vertex', and 'base' to describe polyhedra (in spoken and written language).
Understand that the word 'net' refers to a two-dimensional figure that can be assembled into a polyhedron, and create a net for a given polyhedron.
purpose: In this lesson, students learn about polyhedra and their nets. They study prisms and pyramids as types of polyhedra with certain defining features. Students begin by identifying the defining characteristics of polyhedra and reviewing terminology (faces, edges, vertices). Next, they explore the defining characteristics of prisms and pyramids, considering the polygons that make up their faces and how to arrange them into nets that can be assembled into the given polyhedron. As students analyze and distinguish these figures, they practice looking for and making use of structure. An optional activity allows students to assemble a net into a polyhedron and identify the number of vertices, edges, and faces.
student_facing_goal: Let's investigate polyhedra.

task_events.answer: Nets 1 and 2 can be folded into Pyramid P; Net 3 cannot
task_events.excerpt: Which of these nets can be folded into Pyramid P? Select all that apply.

task_events.answer: Sample: Prism A—2 triangles and 3 rectangles; Pyramid R—1 pentagon and 5 triangles
task_events.excerpt: Decide which polygons are needed to compose your assigned polyhedron. List the polygons and how many of each are needed.

task_events.answer: Sample: A—6 vertices, 9 edges, 5 faces; B—8 vertices, 12 edges, 6 faces
task_events.excerpt: How many vertices, edges, and faces are in our polyhedron?
Is this polyhedron a prism, a pyramid, or neither? How many faces, edges, and vertices does it have?

boundary_note: This lesson builds the vocabulary and structural vocabulary (faces, edges, vertices) needed to reason about polyhedra, and establishes nets as the 2D representation of 3D solids. Students often struggle with counting vertices and edges accurately when assembling or analyzing polyhedra; the lesson recommends labeling parts and organizing counts by location (top, bottom, sides). The key misunderstanding students bring is that all faces of a prism must be the same shape—prisms can have rectangular faces that are not squares, as long as they have two identical parallel bases.

title: Nets and Surface Area
student_facing_goal: Let's use nets to find the surface area of polyhedra.
teacher_goals: Match polyhedra with their nets and justify (orally) that they match.
Use a net with gridlines to calculate the surface area of a prism or pyramid and explain (in writing) the solution method.
Visualize and identify the polyhedron that can be assembled from a given net.
purpose: This lesson extends students' understanding of polyhedra, their nets, and their surface area. Students begin by matching polyhedra and their nets. Then they work in groups to assemble two prisms and a pyramid from given nets and use nets to find the surface area of each polyhedron. To support students in reasoning about area, the nets are presented with a grid. As students calculate surface area and consider how to account for all the faces of a polyhedron, they have opportunities to look for and make use of structure, in both the arrangement and measurements of the polygons that compose the polyhedron (MP7).

task_events.excerpt: The surface area is 82 square units. 2(6 · 1) + 2(5 · 1) + 2(6 · 5) = 82
The surface area is 48 square units. (4 · 4) + 4(1/2 · 4 · 4) = 48
The surface area is 52 square units. 2(3 · 4) + 2(2 · 4) + 2(2 · 3) = 52
Area of three rectangles: 20, 15, 25 square units. Two triangles: 2(1/2 · 4 · 3) = 12. Total: 20 + 15 + 25 + 12 = 72

boundary_note: Students must mentally rotate a flat net into a 3D polyhedron and systematically calculate surface area. The central challenge: ensuring all faces are accounted for without double-counting or missing any. Visual confusion between prisms and pyramids occurs when students don't name the base shape; stress naming polyhedra by their base type. The lesson's strength is showing that nets make all faces visible at once, making systematic calculation possible if students organize their work carefully.

title: More Nets, More Surface Area
student_facing_goal: Let's draw nets and find the surface area of polyhedra.
teacher_goals: Draw and assemble a net for the prism or pyramid shown in a given drawing.
Interpret (using words and other representations) two-dimensional representations of prisms and pyramids.
Use a net without gridlines to calculate the surface area of a prism or pyramid and explain (in writing) the solution method.
purpose: This lesson further develops students' ability to visualize the relationship between nets and polyhedra and their capacity to reason about surface area. Previously, students identified polygons that make up the faces of a polyhedron and arranged them into a net. They also assembled given nets into polyhedra. In this lesson, students reason about the nets of polyhedra with less scaffolding. They practice mentally unfolding three-dimensional shapes, drawing two-dimensional nets, and using nets to calculate surface area. As students coordinate edge lengths and arrangements of polygons in drawings of three-dimensional figures to those in the corresponding nets, they practice reasoning concretely and abstractly.

task_events.excerpt: (6 · 6) + 4 · (1/2 · 5 · 6) = 96. The surface area of this square pyramid is 96 square units.

task_events.answer: Box A uses least cardboard: 42 square centimeters; Box C holds most cubes: 27 cubic centimeters
task_events.excerpt: Box A uses the least cardboard. The surface area of A is 42 square centimeters: 4(2 · 3) + 2(3 · 3) = 42.
168 square units. Two triangular faces with area 24 sq units each. 2 · 24 + 50 + 40 + 30 = 168
56 square units. Base area is 16 sq units. Each triangular face has base 4 and height 5, area 10 sq units. Total: 16 + 10 + 10 + 10 + 10 = 56.

boundary_note: Students learn to visualize how a 2D net folds into a 3D shape and calculate surface area by summing all face areas. The critical misconceptions: faces arranged incorrectly on the net (overlapping or misoriented), forgetting to count all faces, and miscalculating triangle areas (applying base×height instead of ½·base×height). Success requires careful tracking of edge lengths between the 3D drawing and its net.

title: Distinguishing Between Surface Area and Volume
teacher_goals: Comprehend that surface area and volume are two different attributes of three-dimensional objects and are measured in different units
Describe (orally and in writing) shapes built out of cubes, including observations about their surface area and volume
Determine the surface area and volume of shapes made out of cubes
purpose: This optional lesson reminds students of the distinctions between measures of one-, two-, and three-dimensional attributes. It reinforces that length is one-dimensional, surface area is two-dimensional, and volume is three-dimensional.
student_facing_goal: Let's contrast surface area and volume.

task_events.excerpt: Build two different figures using 8 cubes each. They both have a volume of 8 cubic units.

task_events.answer: 24 and 28 square units for two different eight-cube figures
task_events.excerpt: The first has a surface area of 24 square units. The second has a surface area of 28 square units.

task_events.answer: All three prisms: 46 sq cm surface area; volumes 11, 14, 15 cubic cm
task_events.excerpt: Prism A: 4(11·1)+2(1·1)=46 sq cm, 11 cu cm; Prism B: 46 sq cm, 14 cu cm; Prism C: 46 sq cm, 15 cu cm

boundary_note: Students discover that surface area and volume are independent measurements—same volume does not guarantee same surface area, and vice versa. Critical error: miscounting exposed faces when composite figures are built from unit cubes; interior faces where units touch vanish from the surface area calculation. Students often assume one measurement constrains the other, leading to faulty predictions.

title: Squares and Cubes
teacher_goals: Generalize a process for finding the volume of a cube, and justify (orally) why this can be abstracted as s³.
Include appropriate units (orally and in writing) when reporting lengths, areas, and volumes, e.g., cm, cm², cm³.
Interpret and write expressions with exponents ² and ³ to represent the area of a square or the volume of a cube.
purpose: In this lesson, students learn about perfect squares and perfect cubes. They see that these names come from the areas of squares and the volumes of cubes with whole-number side lengths. Students are introduced to exponents in this context and learn to use the exponent ² to express the area of a square and ³ to express the volume of a cube. They learn that the words squared and cubed describe expressions with these exponents and see the geometric motivation for this terminology. The term "exponent" is deliberately not defined more generally at this time.
student_facing_goal: Let's investigate perfect squares and perfect cubes.

task_events.excerpt: A square has side length 7 in. What is its area?
The area of a square is 64 sq cm. What is its side length?
What is the volume of the built cube? [constructed from 27 snap cubes]
What is the volume of the built cube? [constructed from 64 snap cubes]
A cube has a side length of 4 cm. What is its volume?
A cube has a side length of 10 inches. What is its volume?
A cube has a side length of s units. What is its volume?
A square has side length 10 cm. Use an exponent to express its area.
A cube has edge length 5 in. Use an exponent to express its volume.

task_events.answer: s³ units³ or s³ cubic units
task_events.excerpt: A cube has edge length s units. Use an exponent to write an expression for its volume.
A cube has an edge length of 21 cm. Use an exponent to express its volume.

boundary_note: This lesson introduces exponent notation (² and ³) through geometric meaning—squaring and cubing—grounding symbolism in area and volume computation. Students often confuse the exponent (2 or 3) with the side length itself and may multiply by 2 or 3 instead of squaring/cubing; the hands-on building with snap cubes and explicit connection to repeated multiplication help prevent this. Recognizing perfect squares and cubes requires familiarity with multiplication facts; this is the main prerequisite barrier.

title: Surface Area of a Cube
teacher_goals: Generalize a process for finding the surface area of a cube, and justify (orally) why this can be abstracted as 6 · s².
Interpret (orally) expressions that include repeated addition, multiplication, repeated multiplication, or exponents.
Write expressions, with or without exponents, to represent the surface area of a given cube.
student_facing_goal: Let's write a formula to find the surface area of a cube.
purpose: In this lesson, students practice using the exponents "2" and "3" to express products that describe surface areas and volumes of cubes. They also use exponents to write square units and cubic units. Students begin by writing numerical expressions to represent the surface areas and volumes of cubes with whole-number side lengths. Then, they generalize their observations to write variable expressions for the surface area and volume of any cube. As they write numerical expressions, students practice looking for and making use of structure (MP7). They also practice looking for and expressing regularity in repeated reasoning (MP8) to write formulas for the surface area and the volume of a cube.

task_events.excerpt: What is the surface area of this cube? (edge length 5 units)
What is the volume of this cube? (edge length 5 units)
Write an expression for the surface area, in square units. (cube with edge 17)
Write an expression for the volume, in cubic units. (cube with edge 17)
Write an expression for the surface area. (cube with edge length s)
Write an expression for the volume. (cube with edge length s)
A cube has a volume of 7³ cubic centimeters. What is its surface area?

boundary_note: This lesson introduces cube surface area and volume formulas using exponents (6·s² and s³). Students often conflate the two quantities or misinterpret exponent notation—for example, thinking (17+17)² or writing 6·(17·17)² instead of 6·17². The real work is writing and justifying concise expressions without requiring extensive computation; the structure itself conveys the mathematical meaning.

title: All about Tents
student_facing_goal: Let's find out how much material is needed to build some tents.
teacher_goals: Apply understanding of surface area to estimate the amount of material in a tent, and explain (orally and in writing) the estimation strategy.
Compare and contrast (orally) different tent designs.
Interpret information (presented in writing and through other representations) about tents and sleeping bags.
purpose: In this culminating lesson, students apply what they learned in this unit to solve problems about surface area in context. Students begin by looking at different examples of tents, analyzing two simple tents of different sizes, and reasoning about the amount of material needed to construct these tents. Then, they can create their own tent design and estimate how much material is needed for the tent. They need to ensure that their design meets specified parameters and their estimate is backed by sound reasoning and calculations.

task_events.excerpt: The surface area of the smaller tent is 91.5 square feet. Left and right panels: 2 · (3.75) · (6.5) = 48.75; Front and back panels: 2 · ½ · (4.5) · 3 = 13.5; Floor panel: (4.5) · (6.5) = 29.25; Total: 48.75 + 13.5 + 29.25 = 91.5
The surface area of the larger tent is 128 square feet. It has 3 rectangular faces = 104 square feet. Each triangular face: 2 · ½ · 6 · 4 = 24 square feet. 104 + 24 = 128
Estimate of material needed: About 424 square feet. Floor: 96; Side panels: 80; Roof panels: About 104; Front and back: 144. Total: 96 + 80 + 104 + 144 = 424

boundary_note: This lesson bridges pure polygon-area work to the synthesis challenge of three-dimensional surface area: students must identify which faces of a tent actually need material, make assumptions about floor coverage and which ends are included, and correctly decompose composite shapes (especially triangular roof sections requiring slant-height reasoning). The core misconception is incomplete face accounting—students often miss triangular end faces or forget to include the floor. The lesson makes this a feature, not a bug: design decisions about what material to include are explicitly part of the mathematical modeling task, and students must justify their assumptions in writing.

title: Introducing Ratios and Ratio Language
student_facing_goal: Let's describe two quantities at the same time.
teacher_goals: I can write or say a sentence that describes a ratio.
I know how to say words and numbers in the correct order to accurately describe the ratio.
purpose: In this lesson, students use collections of objects to make sense of and use ratio language. They see that there are several different ways to describe a situation using ratio language. For example, if we have 12 squares and 6 circles, we can say the ratio of squares to circles is 12 : 6 and the ratio of circles to squares is 6 to 12. We may also see a structure that prompts us to regroup them and say that there are 6 squares for every 2 circles, or 3 squares for every one circle. Expressing associations of quantities in a context—as students will be doing in this lesson—requires students to use ratio language with care. Making groups of physical objects that correspond with "for every" language is a concrete way for students to make sense of the problem. It is important that in this first lesson students work with objects that can be physically rearranged. This experience can help students make sense of the increasingly abstract representations that they will encounter as the unit progresses.
boundary_note: This lesson focuses on ratio notation, language, and representation. All student tasks involve identifying, writing, or describing ratios from given collections using different symbolic and verbal forms (colon notation such as "6:3", "to" language such as "6 to 3", and "for every" language such as "2 squares for every 1 circle"). The lesson does not ask students to compute with or operate on numbers via arithmetic. No tasks ask for addition, subtraction, multiplication, or division operations. Practice problems ask students to extract ratio components from given contexts (e.g., "In a fruit basket there are 9 bananas, 4 apples, and 3 plums. The ratio of bananas to apples is ___ : ___") but not to calculate. The lesson is foundational, establishing vocabulary and notation for ratios before computational work (ratio scaling, unit rates, solving proportions) begins in subsequent lessons.

title: Representing Ratios with Diagrams
teacher_goals: Coordinate discrete diagrams and multiple written sentences describing the same ratios.
Draw and label discrete diagrams to represent situations involving ratios.
Practice reading and writing sentences describing ratios, for example, "The ratio of these to those is a : b. The ratio of these to those is a to b. For every a of these, there are b of those."
purpose: In this lesson, students use diagrams to represent situations involving ratios and continue to develop ratio language. Previously, students used words to describe ratios in collections of objects. Here, they also draw diagrams to represent ratios and interpret statements about ratios. Both the visual and verbal descriptions of ratios demand careful attention to language. There is not really a right or wrong way to draw a diagram. What matters is that the diagram accurately represents the quantities, makes sense to the student and others, and can be explained. That said, one goal of this lesson is to help students draw useful diagrams efficiently.
student_facing_goal: Let's use diagrams to represent ratios.

task_events.excerpt: There are ___ pencils for every notepad.
There are ___ pencils for every eraser.
For each pint of glue, there are 4 cups of corn flour.

boundary_note: This lesson (6.RP.A.1) is about representing and interpreting ratios through discrete diagrams and ratio language; it deliberately precedes unit-rate and equivalent-ratio work — the narrative explicitly declines to write an association like 2:1 and defers equivalent ratios to later lessons. No addition, subtraction, or explicit multiplication is demanded. The only number-fill demands are partitive "for every" rate statements (one quantity shared into equal groups per unit of another), which fit fair_share_equal_groups rather than the first pass's measure_groups_of_size; both cool-down events were reclassified. Students read these rates off their diagrams rather than running a division algorithm, so the automaton fit is loose. The first pass's two practice events (pretzels/raisins and almonds/raisins, cited on "pages 57-58") do not appear anywhere in the provided pages (46-55, which end at the cool-down on page 55); they were deleted as unconfirmable — the operands could not be checked against any page in this PDF. Retained cool-down operands come from the printed ratio 6:12:3 (erasers:pencils:notepads); the added activity-2 event (Jada, 8 cups corn flour : 2 pints glue -> 4 cups per pint) comes from printed text and a printed sample response on page 51. Building On is empty (none listed on page 47); Addressing 6.RP.A.1 and Building Toward 6.RP.A are transcribed correctly.

title: Recipes
teacher_goals: Draw and label a discrete diagram with circled groups to represent multiple batches of a recipe.
Explain equivalent ratios (orally and in writing) in terms of different-sized batches of the same recipe having the same taste.
Understand that doubling or tripling a recipe involves multiplying the amount of each ingredient by the same number, yielding something that tastes the same.
purpose: This is the first of two lessons that develop the idea of equivalent ratios through physical experiences. A key understanding is that if we scale a recipe up (or down) to make multiple batches (or a fraction of a batch), the result will still be the same in some meaningful way. In this lesson, students see this idea in the context of taste. A mixture containing two batches of a recipe tastes the same as a mixture containing one batch. The fact that two equivalent ratios yield the same taste is a physical manifestation of the equivalence of the ratios. Students notice structure when they see that scaling a recipe requires multiplying the amount of each ingredient by the same factor.
student_facing_goal: Let's explore how ratios affect the way a recipe tastes.

task_events.excerpt: How many of each shape would be in two copies of this flower pattern?
How many of each shape would be in two copies of this flower pattern?
How many of each shape would be in two copies of this flower pattern?
How many batches can you make with 15 cups of flour and 6 teaspoons of vanilla?
How many batches can you make with 15 cups of flour and 6 teaspoons of vanilla?
How much flour and vanilla would you need for 5 batches of cookies?
How much flour and vanilla would you need for 5 batches of cookies?
Think of a recipe that would give a smaller batch of lemonade but still taste the same.
Think of a recipe that would give a smaller batch of lemonade but still taste the same.
How many batches are represented by the diagram?
How many batches are represented by the diagram?
How many batches are represented by the diagram?
Find two more ratios of cups of sparkling water to cups of juice concentrate that would make a mixture that tastes the same.
Find two more ratios of cups of sparkling water to cups of juice concentrate that would make a mixture that tastes the same.
Find two more ratios of cups of sparkling water to cups of juice concentrate that would make a mixture that tastes the same.
Find two more ratios of cups of sparkling water to cups of juice concentrate that would make a mixture that tastes the same.
Write the missing number under each tick mark on the number line.
Write the missing number under each tick mark on the number line.
Elena has 80 unit cubes. What is the volume of the largest cube she can build with them?

boundary_note: No automaton is missing. Every computational demand maps to a vocabulary automaton: batch scale-up is multiplication (coordinate_groups_items), batch counts are quotative division (measure_groups_of_size), the number-line ticks are addition (count_on_from_larger), and the cube volume is rectangular_prism_volume_layer_iteration. Two nuances are worth flagging rather than treating as boundaries. First, the recipe scale-up, batch-count, and equivalent-ratio tasks are modeled with generic arithmetic automata even though the lesson frames them as ratio equivalence under 6.RP.A.1; the ratio-native fit would be scale_ratio_unit, but the arithmetic-automaton scheme is applied consistently across the lesson and captures the operands correctly. Second, Practice 6 ('largest cube from 80 unit cubes') layers a bounded search for the largest n with n^3 <= 80 on top of the volume automaton (n=4 since 4^3=64 <= 80 < 125=5^3). Neither task demands an operation absent from the vocabulary.

title: Grade 6, Unit 2, Lesson 4 — Practice Problems 6–7 (spiral review): parallelogram area by decomposition; whole-number × unit-fraction
student_facing_goal: Find the area of a parallelogram by surrounding it with a square and subtracting the triangle area; complete equations that multiply a whole number by a unit fraction.
teacher_goals: Rehearse parallelogram area by decomposition — surround with a square, rearrange the outer triangles into a rectangle, subtract — carried over from Unit 1, Lesson 4.
Rehearse multiplying a whole number by a unit fraction, n · 1/d = n/d, carried over from an earlier course.
purpose: Distributed-practice (spiral review) problems closing the Grade 6 Unit 2 practice set. Problem 6 (marked 'from Unit 1, Lesson 4') revisits finding parallelogram area by decomposition; Problem 7 (marked 'from an earlier course') revisits whole-number-times-unit-fraction multiplication. Both are review pulled from prior work rather than new lesson content, and reinforce arithmetic and geometric reasoning students draw on elsewhere in the unit.

task_events.excerpt: Find the area of the parallelogram. Show your reasoning.
Find the area of the parallelogram. Show your reasoning.
Find the area of the parallelogram. Show your reasoning.

boundary_note: Pages 88–89 are the tail of the Grade 6 Unit 2 practice-problem set: Problem 6 is labeled 'from Unit 1, Lesson 4' and Problem 7 'from an earlier course', so both are spiral/distributed review, not the current lesson's core content. No CCSS codes, formal lesson title, teacher learning goals, or purpose statement appear on these pages; those live earlier in the teacher guide, so Building On / Addressing / Building Toward are left empty rather than guessed. CORRECTION vs first pass: Problem 7b is 7 · 1/4 = 7/4, NOT 7 · 1/2 = 7/2 — the first pass misread the denominator (page-89 solution confirms 7/4); corrected here. In Problem 6 the operands 7·7=49 and 4·7=28 come from the sample solution's single decomposition (surround with a 7×7 square, rearrange the two outer triangles into a 4×7 rectangle, 49−28=21); a student could decompose differently, and the parallelogram base×height strategy is not the demonstrated method, so no separate geometry event was coded. figure_bound=true wherever operands live only in rendered math or the grid figure that plain-text extraction drops (every product in Problem 6 and every equation in Problem 7 came through as blanks in the text layer); figure_bound=false for 49−28 because 49 and 28 also appear verbatim in the solution prose. Problem 7e (x · 1/y = x/y) is symbolic with no numeric operands and is omitted. Each n · 1/d item is coded fraction/unit_fraction_iteration (iterate the unit fraction 1/d exactly n times); op+kind pairs all validate against the supplied vocabulary (multiplication/multiplication_fact_retrieval, subtraction/take_away_base_ones — 49−28 needs no regrouping — and fraction/unit_fraction_iteration).

title: Defining Equivalent Ratios
student_facing_goal: Let's investigate equivalent ratios some more.
teacher_goals: Generate equivalent ratios and justify that they are equivalent.
Present (in words and through other representations) a definition of "equivalent ratios," including examples and non-examples.
purpose: In this lesson, students work with equivalent ratios more abstractly, both in the context of recipes and by using numbers without a context. They learn and articulate that all ratios that are equivalent to a : b can be generated by multiplying both a and b by the same number. As they connect concrete quantitative experiences to abstract representations, students develop their skills in reasoning abstractly and quantitatively (MP2). They continue to use diagrams, words, or a combination of both for their explanations.

task_events.excerpt: Twice the amount of radish cake?
Twice the amount of radish cake?
Twice the amount of radish cake?

Half the amount of radish cake?

How many batches: 2 ounces of rice flour and 1 tablespoon of green onions

boundary_note: All 15 confirmed events come from Activity 5.2 (Radish Cake) and map cleanly to the vocabulary: recipe scaling by whole and fractional factors to scale_ratio_unit, and quotitive batch-count division to measure_groups_of_size. Seven first-pass events were deleted as unconfirmable — two "practice" items (light-yellow-paint diagram, orange/banana ratio) cited to page 101 and five "cool-down" items (gray/black/white-paint double number line, carnival game) cited to pages 105-106 do not exist; this PDF ends at page 99, and the real cool-down (5.4) asks students to write any ratio equivalent to 4 : 6. The lesson's central conceptual demand — judging and justifying whether two ratios are equivalent (Activity 5.3: is 15 : 12 equivalent to 5 : 3? is 30 : 18?) — is a check for a single common scale factor, which has no dedicated automaton; the positive cases lean on scale_ratio_unit and the non-example (15 : 12) is closest to diagnostic/multiplicative_bound_invalidation. The cool-down (write any equivalent ratio to 4 : 6) and the axolotl extension (compose legs:gills with toes:gills through a common referent) demand a learner-chosen multiplier or referent composition, so they carry no determinate operand to encode.

title: Problem 5: Parallelograms and Triangles (from Unit 1, Lesson 6)
purpose: Not provided in the sourced pages (122–123 are Practice Problems only, lacking the full lesson plan section)
boundary_note: Pages 122–123 show only the Grade 6 Unit 2 Practice Problems section with no lesson-plan metadata (teacher learning goals, standards, lesson purpose). The task is a geometric construction exercise—students draw shapes satisfying area constraints (parallelogram and triangle, each with area 24 square units)—rather than a computational problem. No concrete arithmetic operations with specific operands appear; the solution explicitly notes "Answers vary" (multiple valid base–height pairs satisfy each area requirement). This is a representation/construction task, not a computational one. Full lesson context would be on different pages of the teacher guide.

title: Creating Double Number Line Diagrams
student_facing_goal: Let's draw double number line diagrams like a pro.
teacher_goals: I can create a double number line diagram and correctly place and label tick marks to represent equivalent ratios.
I can explain what the word "per" means.
purpose: In this lesson, students create double number line diagrams from scratch. They see that it is important to use parallel lines, equally-spaced tick marks, and descriptive labels. They are also introduced to using the word per to refer to how much of one quantity there is for every one unit of the other quantity. As students progress through the activities, they learn to draw double number line diagrams with decreasing levels of scaffolding. In the first activity, students start by placing their own tick marks and interpolating the given values down to smaller numbers. Next, they draw a double number line diagram from scratch by following instructions. Finally, in an optional activity, they create a double number line diagram with no guidance. In the optional activity, students revisit a situation previously represented with a discrete diagram and examine how the new representation can help to answer questions about the ratios in the situation. In later lessons, students make their own strategic choice of an appropriate representation to support their reasoning. Regardless of method, students practice attending to precision (MP6) as they indicate the units that go with the numbers in a ratio in both verbal statements and diagrams. Note that students are not expected to use or understand the term "unit rate" in this lesson.

task_events.excerpt: How much yellow water should be used for 1 ml of blue water?
How much yellow water should be used for 11 ml of blue water?
How much yellow water should be used for 8 ml of blue water?
How much corn flour should be used with 10 pints of glue?

How much corn flour per pint of glue does this recipe use?

If you made a large amount of radish cake by mixing 80 ounces of radish with 30 ounces of rice flour, would it taste the same as the original recipe?
How many tablespoons of green onions should you mix with 60 ounces of radish to make a radish cake that tastes the same as the original recipe?
How many tablespoons of green onions should you mix with 60 ounces of radish to make a radish cake that tastes the same as the original recipe?

boundary_note: Verification found no practice-problem section in the source: the PDF ends at page 135 (Lesson 7 Summary + Glossary). Every first-pass task_event cited at pages 136-137 (positions practice_1 through practice_5 - split peas/sesame seeds, fruit punch, pink paint, "three ratios equivalent to 3:11", etc.) had no corresponding source page and was unverifiable; all were deleted as hallucinated (33 events removed, 10 retained). The 10 retained events all confirm against the recipe text and the printed Student Responses on pages 127-133, so every one is figure_bound=false - the operands live in prose (recipe ratios and question stems), not in the double-number-line figures, and pdftotext would catch them. Classification: "per 1 unit" divisions that produce a unit rate (15/5 for yellow-per-1-ml-blue; 8/2 for corn-flour-per-pint) are partitive and map to fair_share_equal_groups (amount per group), which is more defensible than NONE because a partitive-division automaton computes exactly this quotient; "how many batches / how many pints" divisions (80/20, 60/20, 24/4) are quotitive and map to measure_groups_of_size; every scaling of a known ratio to a new quantity is scale_ratio_unit. The optional "Are You Ready for More?" geometry brain-teaser (64/4=16 for the overlapping-square area, pages 130-131) is off the ratio spine of this lesson and is not recorded as a task_event. No signed-number, formal-equation, or unit-rate-terminology operations appear; 6.RP.A.3 is the sole addressed standard, 4.NF the sole building-on, and there is no building-toward code printed.

title: How Much for One?
teacher_goals: Calculate equivalent ratios between prices and quantities and present the solution method (using words and other representations).
Calculate unit price and express it using the word "per" (orally and in writing).
Understand that the phrase "at this rate" indicates that equivalent ratios are involved.
purpose: This lesson introduces students to the idea of unit price. Students use the word "per" to refer to the cost of 1 apple, 1 pound, 1 bottle, and so on, as in "$6 per pound" or "$1.50 per avocado." They also learn that the phrase "at this rate" is used to indicate that the ratios of price to quantity are equivalent. (For example, "Pizza costs $1.25 per slice. At this rate, how much do 6 slices cost?") They find unit prices in different situations and notice that unit prices are useful for comparing prices for other amounts. Students choose whether to draw double number lines or other representations to support their reasoning. They continue to use precision in stating the units that go with the numbers in a ratio in both verbal statements and diagrams. Note that students are not expected to use or understand the term "unit rate" in this lesson.
student_facing_goal: Let's use ratios to describe how much things cost.

boundary_note: All 37 task events verified against book pages 139-150; the source PDF runs 13 pages (139-151), so nothing was unconfirmable. No misread operands: every operand and answer appears in prose or in a printed Student Response/Solution. Three first-pass kind errors corrected: divide(246,12) was long_division but the warm-up is a mental Math Talk whose sample reasoning is 20½·12=246 (missing_factor_known_product_search, matching the other three warm-up items); divide(21,3) (books for $21) and divide(12,1.5) (rice bags for $12) are quotitive "how many items for this amount" and were moved from fair_share_equal_groups to measure_groups_of_size. Cost-per-one divisions stay fair_share_equal_groups; all multiplications are repeat_equal_groups (scaling a unit price). figure_bound was over-flagged in the first pass and is corrected to false everywhere: every operand is stated in prose, so the double number lines carry answers, not operands. No demanded operation falls outside the vocabulary. Deliberately not extracted (out of scope, spiral review on page 151): problem 5 (recipe doubling, from U2 L3) and problem 6 (trapezoidal-prism surface area, from U1 L15), which belong to other lessons and would draw on ratio/geometry automata rather than this lesson's unit-price arithmetic.

title: Constant Speed
student_facing_goal: Let's use ratios to work with how fast things move.
teacher_goals: Calculate the distance that an object travels in 1 unit of time and express it using a phrase like "meters per second" (orally and in writing).
For an object moving at a constant speed, use a double number line diagram to represent equivalent ratios between the distance traveled and elapsed time.
Justify (orally and in writing) which of two objects is moving faster, by identifying that it travels more distance in the same amount of time or that it travels the same distance in less time.
purpose: This lesson introduces the context of constant speed. Through concrete experiences, students continue to explore equivalent ratios and ratios involving 1 unit. They measure the time it takes them to travel a predetermined distance moving slowly and then quickly—and use it to calculate and compare the speed they traveled in meters per second.

task_events.excerpt: Estimate the distance in meters that you traveled in 1 second when moving slowly.
Estimate the distance in meters that you traveled in 1 second when moving quickly.
Lin ran 40 meters and Diego ran 55 meters... How far did each person move in 1 second?
Lin ran 40 meters and Diego ran 55 meters... How far did each person move in 1 second?
Han ran 100 meters in 20 seconds at a constant speed. Is this speed faster, slower, or the same as Lin's?
Train B: distance traveled 0 to 100 meters, elapsed time 0 to 4 seconds. Which train is traveling faster?
Han ran 10 meters in 2.7 seconds... At this rate, how long would it take each person to run 50 meters?
Priya ran 10 meters in 2.4 seconds... At this rate, how long would it take each person to run 50 meters?
A scooter travels 30 feet in 2 seconds at a constant speed. What is the speed of the scooter in feet per second?
Complete the double number line to show the distance the scooter travels after 1, 3, 4, and 5 seconds.
Complete the double number line to show the distance the scooter travels after 1, 3, 4, and 5 seconds.
Complete the double number line to show the distance the scooter travels after 1, 3, 4, and 5 seconds.
Complete the double number line to show the distance the scooter travels after 1, 3, 4, and 5 seconds.
A cargo ship traveled 150 nautical miles in 6 hours at a constant speed. How far did the cargo ship travel in one hour?

boundary_note: No operation in this lesson falls outside the vocabulary. Every demanded computation is either a unit-rate division (total distance shared across a known number of time units to find distance per one unit) or a scaling multiplication (a rate repeated over elapsed time), both of which have automata. Correction to the first pass: the eight rate divisions are partitive fair-sharing, so they map to fair_share_equal_groups; the first pass mistagged them measure_groups_of_size (quotitive), which reads the divisor as a group size rather than a group count. No misread operands were found — all 14 tasks confirmed against pages 156-163.

title: Section F: Let's Put It to Work
purpose: In the final section, students have the opportunity to apply their thinking from throughout the unit.

task_events.excerpt: Find the area of the triangle. Show your reasoning.
Find the area of the triangle. Show your reasoning.
Find the area of the triangle. Show your reasoning.
The total area of the right triangles is 15.5 square units, because 3 + 5 + 7.5 = 15.5.
The area of the given triangle is 9.5 square units, since 25 − 15.5 = 9.5.

boundary_note:
All five task events are confirmed against page 277. Every operand, operation, and answer matches the figure labels (2, 3, 3, 2, 5, 5 on the enclosing 5-by-5 square) and the verbatim solution text; the three right-triangle areas (3, 5, 7.5), their sum (15.5), and the complementary subtraction (25 − 15.5 = 9.5) all check out.

Correction applied to the op field on every event. The first pass reported the surface arithmetic operation (division / addition / subtraction), but a real pair from this vocabulary requires op to name the category the kind belongs to: geometry for triangle_area_half_base_height, and decimal for decimal_addition_by_aligned_units and decimal_subtraction_by_aligned_units. The kinds themselves were correct and are the best fit (right-triangle area as half base times height; decimal arithmetic because 7.5 is a decimal, which the plain addition/subtraction categories do not cover).

Provenance caveat: these computational events come from spiral-review practice problem 6, labeled "from Unit 1, Lesson 10," not from Section F's own material. Section F (page 278) is a Fermi-problem application section that explicitly states there are no section goals and no checkpoint questions, so teacher_goals is empty and no CCSS codes appear on either page, leaving all three standards lists honestly empty. The title was trimmed from the first pass, which appended "Find the Area of the Triangle" and thereby over-narrowed the section to one review problem.

Not broken out: the enclosing-square area step (5 × 5 = 25) is stated in the solution text but was not extracted as a separate multiplication event. No demanded operation fell outside the controlled vocabulary.

title: Representing Ratios with Tables
student_facing_goal: Let's use tables to represent equivalent ratios.
teacher_goals: If I am looking at a table of values, I know where the rows are and where the columns are.
When I see a table representing a set of equivalent ratios, I can come up with numbers to make a new row.
When I see a table representing a set of equivalent ratios, I can explain what the numbers mean.
purpose: In this lesson, students learn to organize equivalent ratios in a table, which is a more abstract tool but also a more flexible one for solving problems.

task_events.excerpt: If someone mixes 36 liters of orange juice and 45 liters of soda water, how many batches would they make?
If someone uses 400 liters of orange juice, how much soda water would they need? [scale factor: 4*?=400]
If someone uses 400 liters of orange juice, how much soda water would they need? [soda = 5*100; solution: 5*100=500]
If someone uses 455 liters of soda water, how much orange juice would they need? [solution: 455=5*91]
If someone uses 455 liters of soda water, how much orange juice would they need? [OJ = 4*91; solution: 4*91=364]

Complete the table so ratios in each row are equivalent to 7:5. Row: 10 fl oz soy sauce (=5*2), find vinegar.
Complete the table so ratios in each row are equivalent to 7:5. Row: 3.5 fl oz vinegar (=7*0.5), find soy sauce.
Complete the table so ratios in each row are equivalent to 7:5. Row: 250 fl oz soy sauce (=5*50), find vinegar.
Complete the table so ratios in each row are equivalent to 7:5. Row: 56 fl oz vinegar (=7*8), find soy sauce.
Complete the last row for a different batch size that hasn't been used so far. [sample: flour = 5*2 = 10 cups]
Complete the last row for a different batch size that hasn't been used so far. [sample: vanilla = 2*2 = 4 tsp]
Complete the table of yellow/red paint batches. [solution row 2: yellow = 5*(1/4) = 5/4]
Complete the table of yellow/red paint batches. [solution row 2: red = 6*(1/4) = 3/2]
Complete the table of yellow/red paint batches. [solution row 3: yellow = 5*(1/2) = 5/2]
Complete the table of yellow/red paint batches. [solution row 3: red = 6*(1/2) = 3]
Complete the table of yellow/red paint batches. [solution row 4: yellow = 5*(3/4) = 15/4]
Complete the table of yellow/red paint batches. [solution row 4: red = 6*(3/4) = 9/2]
How far does the car travel in 14 hours? [solution: multiply 2 by 7 to get 14 hours]
How far does the car travel in 14 hours? [solution: multiply 140 by 7 to get 980 kilometers]
How many liters of olive oil can this orchard produce in a year? [100 lb row: oil = 3*5 = 15 L]
How many liters of olive oil can this orchard produce in a year? [3000 lb row: oil = 15*30 = 450 L]
Label each remaining tick mark with its value. [adults: 2*2 = 4; solution 0,2,4,6,8]
Label each remaining tick mark with its value. [children: 24*2 = 48; solution 0,24,48,72,96]
How many adults are needed if there are 72 children? [adults: 2*3 = 6; solution: 6 adults]

Her heart beats 25 times in 10 seconds... [solution: 25 times in 10 seconds means 150 heartbeats per minute (25*6=150)]
