12 apples for $2.88. What was the cost per apple? [solution: $0.24]
5 tomatoes for $3.10. What was the cost per tomato? [solution: $0.62]

boundary_note: Every operation the lesson demands has a model automaton, so no vocabulary gap. The dominant act is scale_ratio_unit: multiplying both parts of an equivalent ratio by a whole-number, unit-fraction, or decimal factor to produce a table or double-number-line entry. Scale factors are recovered by division (measure_groups_of_size for the explicit "how many batches" count; missing_factor_known_product_search for intermediate factor finds and for the unit-price divisions in practice 6). One rate-total conversion (heartbeats to beats-per-minute, practice 5) reads best as coordinate_groups_items. Six first-pass events were removed as unconfirmed: they asserted the learner computes a value the page supplies as given (cool-down rows 15|6 and 2.5|1 are pre-filled and Q2 is interpretive; practice-3 olives 100 and 3000 are given inputs, only the oil outputs are computed; practice-4 endpoints 8 and 96 are printed number-line labels, not blank ticks). No printed solution shows those six as computations, whereas every retained event either fills a printed blank or appears as an explicit computation in the solution/response.

title: Navigating a Table of Equivalent Ratios
student_facing_goal: Let's use a table of equivalent ratios like a pro.
teacher_goals: Choose multipliers strategically while solving multi-step problems involving equivalent ratios.
Describe (orally and in writing) how a table of equivalent ratios was used to solve a problem about prices and quantities.
Remember that dividing by a whole number is the same as multiplying by an associated unit fraction.
purpose: This lesson develops students' ability to work with a table of equivalent ratios and to compare and contrast different ways of solving equivalent ratio problems.

task_events.excerpt: Find the value of each product mentally. 1/3 · 21
Find the value of each product mentally. 1/6 · 21
Find the value of each product mentally. (5.6) · 1/8
Find the value of each product mentally. 1/4 · (5.6)
Noah bought 4 tacos and paid $6. At this rate, how many tacos could he buy for $15?
Jada's family bought 50 tacos for a party and paid $72. Were Jada's tacos the same price as Noah's tacos?
Lin is paid $90 for 5 hours of work. (What is the meaning of the 18 that appears in the table?)
She used the table to calculate how much she would be paid at this rate for 8 hours of work.
At this rate, how much would Lin be paid for 3 hours of work?
For 2.1 hours of work?
In 2022, 128 gigabytes (GB) of portable computer memory cost $16. Find the cost of 1 gigabyte.
A shop sells bagels for $5 per dozen. At this rate, how much would 6 bagels cost?
How many bagels can you buy for $50?
Priya collected 2,400 grams of pennies in a fundraiser. Each penny has a mass of 2.5 grams. How much money did Priya raise?
Kiran reads 5 pages in 20 minutes. He spends the same amount of time per page.
How long will it take him to read 11 pages?
Mai is making personal pizzas. For 4 pizzas, she uses 10 ounces of cheese. How much cheese does Mai use per pizza?
At this rate, how much cheese will she need to make 15 pizzas?
Clare is paid $90 for 5 hours of work. At this rate, how many seconds does it take for her to earn 25 cents?

boundary_note: No task in this lesson demands an operation absent from the vocabulary. The one composite is practice 4 (Clare's wage): the extracted functor divide(3600,72)=50 is a partitive fair-share division (3,600 seconds shared across 72 quarter-dollars = 50 seconds each), and it does map to an automaton. But the full task chains three sub-operations the single functor under-represents rather than exceeds: a unit-rate step (90 for 5 hours -> 18 per hour, scale_ratio_unit), a time-unit conversion (1 hour -> 3,600 seconds, measurement/unit_conversion_by_iteration), and a count of quarter-dollars in $18 (18/0.25 = 72). Each has an automaton; the reduction to one division loses the chain, so this event should be read as a terminal step, not the whole task.

title: Tables and Double Number Line Diagrams
student_facing_goal: Let's contrast double number lines and tables.
teacher_goals: I can create a table that represents a set of equivalent ratios.
I can explain why sometimes a table is easier to use than a double number line to solve problems involving equivalent ratios.
I include column labels when I create a table, so that the meaning of the numbers is clear.
purpose: In this lesson, students explicitly connect and contrast double number lines and tables. Students consider the ways in which the two representations are alike (for instance, labels are needed and numbers that form equivalent ratios line up) and how they are different. Students examine the relationship between values in different rows in a table. Two features of tables make them more flexible than double number lines. On a double number line, differences between values are represented by lengths. This can support reasoning about relative sizes but can be a limitation for large or small numbers, and a table removes this limitation. On a number line, values are listed in order of size; in a table, pairs of values can be written in any order, allowing more nimble problem solving.

task_events.excerpt: Han can run 100 meters in 20 seconds. He wonders how long it would take him to run 3,000 meters at this rate.
Priya can bike 150 meters in 20 seconds. At this rate, how long would it take her to bike 3,000 meters?
Priya's neighbor has a dirt bike that can go 360 meters in 15 seconds. At this rate, how long would it take her neighbor to go 3,000 meters?
The International Space Station orbits around the Earth at a constant speed. Complete the parts of your representation that you can figure out for sure.
In a sprint to the finish line, a professional cyclist travels 380 meters in 20 seconds. At that rate, how far does the cyclist travel in 3 seconds?
A bread recipe uses 3 tablespoons of olive oil for every 2 cloves of crushed garlic. Complete the table to show different-sized batches of bread that taste the same as the recipe.
Clare travels at a constant speed, as shown on the double number line. At this rate, how far does she travel in 1 hour?

boundary_note: Every demanded operation has an automaton in the vocabulary. Unit-rate scaling up is multiplication/repeat_equal_groups; finding a unit rate is partitive division/fair_share_equal_groups, including the one fractional unit rate (2 cloves shared over 3 tablespoons = 2/3 clove per tablespoon). The lesson's ratio and double-number-line reasoning is expressed through the arithmetic each task actually requires rather than through the ratio-category automata (scale_ratio_unit), consistent with testing whether arithmetic strategies can earn the G6 ratio curriculum. Two first-pass classifications were corrected: Tasks 4 and 7 were tagged measure_groups_of_size (quotitive) but unit-rate finding is partitive, and Task 6 carried an invalid pair (multiplication/scale_ratio_unit, since scale_ratio_unit is a ratio-category automaton). One first-pass event was deleted: a practice_5 air-freshener "how many batches" problem, divide(36,12)=3, that appears on no page of the source PDF (pp. 216-229) and does not match this lesson's practice set (problems 1-4: water/soap, bread/garlic, Clare, Lin/Diego).

title: Solving Equivalent Ratio Problems
teacher_goals: Determine what information is needed to solve a problem involving equivalent ratios. Ask questions to elicit that information.
Understand the structure of a what-why Info Gap activity.
purpose: This lesson gives students further practice in solving equivalent ratio problems and introduces the Information Gap activity structure. The Warm-up familiarizes the class with a process central to Info Gap (asking for missing information and providing it) before students engage in the Info Gap activity in small groups. An optional activity provides additional practice solving equivalent ratio problems if there is time after the Info Gap activity.
student_facing_goal: Let's practice getting information from our partner.

task_events.excerpt: Problem Card 1: Jada should use 13.5 cups of milk.
Problem Card 2: No, Noah does not have enough time. It will take him 75 minutes to finish peeling all the potatoes.
Lin read the first 54 pages from a 270-page book in the last 3 days.
Diego read the first 100 pages from a 325-page book in the last 4 days.
Elena read the first 160 pages from a 480-page book in the last 5 days.
A caterer needs to buy 21 pounds of pasta to cater a wedding. At a local store, 8 pounds of pasta cost $12. How much will the caterer pay for the pasta there?
Lin is reading a 47-page book. She read the first 20 pages in 35 minutes.
Diego can type 140 words in 4 minutes. At this rate, how long will it take him to type 385 words?
Diego can type 140 words in 4 minutes. How many words can he type in 15 minutes?

boundary_note: None. Every retained task maps to an automaton in the vocabulary: partitive/quotitive division for finding unit rates (fair_share_equal_groups, measure_groups_of_size) and scale_ratio_unit for scaling those rates to a target quantity, all under 6.RP.A.3. Verification changes: (a) four first-pass events (the glasses-ratio practice problems cited to pages 246-247 — divide(32,8), multiply(3,4), divide(10,2), multiply(5,9)) were DELETED as unverifiable; the supplied source PDF spans only pages 231-242 (through Practice Problem 4), so those pages are absent. (b) The first-pass practice_4a event divide(1540,140)=11 carried a fabricated operand (1540 = 140x11, nowhere on the page); corrected to divide(385,35)=11, the real quotitive computation, answer 11 confirmed on p.242. (c) Added the two central Activity 14.2 (Info Gap) computations the first pass missed, both confirmed against the milk and potato tables on pages 235-236. (d) The scaling multiplications were reclassified from multiplication/repeat_equal_groups to ratio/scale_ratio_unit as the best fit for a 6.RP.A.3 equivalent-ratio lesson. The optional Are You Ready for More extension (cats:dogs 2:3 becoming 9:11 after 5 more cats; answer 22 cats, 33 dogs, p.238) would demand contextual_linear_equation_construction, which also exists in the vocabulary; it was not extracted as a task event.

title: Part-Part-Whole Ratios
teacher_goals: Comprehend the word "parts" as an unspecified unit in sentences (written and spoken) that describe ratios.
Draw and label a tape diagram to solve problems that involve both ratios and a total amount. Explain (orally) the solution method.
purpose: In this lesson, students encounter situations in which the sum of two or more quantities is meaningful in context. So far, students have worked with ratios of quantities where the units are the same (such as cups to cups) and ratios of quantities where the units are different (such as miles to hours). Students begin by using snap cubes to represent a ratio and see that each cube can represent a variety of amounts. Students use this insight to reason about individual quantities (the parts) in the ratio and the total quantity (the whole). Students then learn to use tape diagrams to represent and reason about such ratios (sometimes called "part-part-whole" ratios) and solve problems. They also see that ratios can be expressed in "parts" rather than in specific units.
student_facing_goal: Let's look at situations where you can add the quantities in a ratio together.

task_events.excerpt: A recipe for maroon paint says, "Mix 5 ml of red paint with 3 ml of blue paint." How many milliliters of maroon paint will there be?
Suppose each cube represents 2 ml. How much red paint is there (5 red cubes)?
Suppose each cube represents 2 ml. How much blue paint is there (3 blue cubes)?
Total maroon paint at 2 ml per cube: 10 ml red + 6 ml blue.
Suppose each cube represents 5 ml. How much red paint is there (5 red cubes)?
Suppose each cube represents 5 ml. How much blue paint is there (3 blue cubes)?
Total maroon paint at 5 ml per cube: 25 ml red + 15 ml blue.
If the original recipe is for one batch of maroon paint, how many batches are in 80 ml of maroon paint? (each batch 5+3 = 8 ml)
Suppose you need 80 ml of maroon paint. How much red paint? (10 batches, 5 ml red per batch)
Suppose you need 80 ml of maroon paint. How much blue paint? (10 batches, 3 ml blue per batch)
Total maroon needed: 50 ml red + 30 ml blue = 80 ml.
Elena makes taco seasoning using 4 parts chili powder, 1 part garlic powder, and 2 parts ground cumin. If Elena uses 20 teaspoons of ground cumin (2 parts), how much per part?
Four parts of chili powder at 10 teaspoons per part.
How much taco seasoning will she have? 40 chili + 10 garlic + 20 cumin teaspoons.
The ratio of students wearing sneakers to those wearing boots is 5 to 6. How many total parts (5+6)?
If there are 33 students, all wearing sneakers or boots (11 parts total), what is the value of each part?
How many are wearing sneakers? Sneakers are 5 parts at 3 students per part.
A recipe for concrete says, "Mix 3 parts gravel with 2 parts sand and 1 part cement." Total parts?
If a project needs 42 gallons of concrete in all (6 parts), how much per part?
Gravel: 3 parts at 7 gallons per part.
Sand: 2 parts at 7 gallons per part.
Cement: 1 part at 7 gallons per part.
Are You Ready for More: 32 teaspoons of chili powder used for the 4 parts, so each part is how much?
Are You Ready for More: 9 teaspoons of garlic powder used for the 1 part, so each part is how much?
Are You Ready for More: 15 teaspoons of ground cumin used for the 2 parts, so each part is how much?
There are 7 parts of ingredients in the recipe; cumin limits to 7.5 teaspoons per part, so 7 x 7.5.
The areas of the kitchen, playroom, and dining room are in the ratio 4:3:2. How many total parts (4+3+2)?
The combined area of these three rooms is 189 square feet (9 parts). How much per part?
Kitchen: 4 parts at 21 square feet per part.
Playroom: 3 parts at 21 square feet per part.
Dining room: 2 parts at 21 square feet per part.
Tape diagram of red paint (3 boxes) and yellow paint (2 boxes), each box worth 3 cups. How many total cups of orange paint?
At the kennel, the ratio of cats to dogs is 4:5. How many total parts (4+5)?
There are 27 animals in all (9 parts). What is the value of each small rectangle?
How many dogs are at the kennel? Dogs are 5 parts at 3 animals per part.
How many cats are at the kennel? Cats are 4 parts at 3 animals per part.
Last month, there were 4 sunny days for every rainy day. How many total units (4+1)?
If there were 30 days in the month (5 units total), how many days were rainy?
The double number line shows $14.50 earned at 2 hours worked. How much does the cashier earn per hour?
At $7.25 per hour, how much does the cashier earn if she works 3 hours?

boundary_note: Every extracted task is whole-number or decimal addition, multiplication, or division, and each maps to an existing automaton: repeat_equal_groups for the "N parts x value-per-part" products, fair_share_equal_groups for the partitive "total shared into a known number of parts" quotients, measure_groups_of_size for the one quotitive quotient (divide(80,8): how many 8-ml batches are in 80 ml), and base_ones_chunking as the neutral default for the additions, since the worked solutions state sums as results without showing a method. No task demands an operation lacking an automaton. The lesson's actual target is not any of these arithmetic automata: it is constructing a part-part-whole tape diagram that coordinates the parts with the whole (nearest ratio-family fits would be construct_referent_ratio_diagram or scale_ratio_unit). The extracted events are the arithmetic substeps downstream of that ratio reasoning. The Warm-up (15.1) is four true/false judgments about a whole number times a unit fraction; those equation-truth tasks were not extracted as arithmetic task events.

title: Solving More Ratio Problems
teacher_goals: Choose and create diagrams to help solve problems that involve ratios and a total amount.
Compare and contrast (orally) different representations of and solution methods for the same problem.
purpose: In this lesson, students use all representations they have learned in this unit—double number lines, tables, and tape diagrams—to solve ratio problems that involve the sum of the quantities in the ratio.
student_facing_goal: Let's compare all our strategies for solving ratio problems.

task_events.excerpt: Aquarium trip: 2 chaperones for every 15 students, 85 total tickets; each part of tape worth 5 tickets, so 15 student-parts give 75 student tickets
Aquarium trip: 2 chaperone-parts, each part worth 5 tickets, so 10 chaperone tickets
Cleaning liquid: 4 parts water to 3 parts vinegar, 28 tablespoons total; 7 parts, so 28 divided by 7 = 4 tablespoons in 1 part
Cleaning liquid: 4 tablespoons per part, 4 parts of water, so 4 times 4 = 16 tablespoons of water
Moving boxes: Andre 4 boxes per half hour, Han 5 per half hour (9 total per half hour), 72 total boxes; 72 = 9 times 8, so 8 half-hour increments
Pizza party: 6 ounces dough per person, 13 people, 6 times 13 = 78 ounces of dough
Pizza party: 4 ounces sauce per person, 13 people, 4 times 13 = 52 ounces of sauce
Beige paint: batch is 4 white, 2 yellow, 1 red; Han used 8 cups yellow at 2 per batch, so 8 divided by 2 = 4 (each tape piece = 4 cups)
Beige paint: 16 cups white, 8 cups yellow, 4 cups red; 16 + 8 + 4 = 28 total cups of beige paint
Card activity: 4 purple and 3 white cards per student (7 per student), 91 total cards; 91 divided by 7 = 13 students
Card activity: 4 purple cards per student, 13 students, 4 times 13 = 52 purple cards given out
Card activity: 3 white cards per student, 13 students, 3 times 13 = 39 white cards given out
Book cost: Andre paid $13 for 3 books, Diego bought 12 books; twelve is 4 groups of 3, so 12 divided by 3 = 4
Book cost: Diego's 12 books cost 4 times Andre's $13, so 4 times 13 = 52 dollars

boundary_note: Every demanded operation in this lesson maps to an existing automaton. The ratio work is scaling by a unit value (scale_ratio_unit) and finding either the number of equal groups (measure_groups_of_size: 72/9, 8/2, 91/7, 12/3) or the value of one part (fair_share_equal_groups: 28/7). One caveat worth naming: the beige-paint total add(16,8,4)=28 is a three-addend sum, but the addition family in this vocabulary is expressed as binary strategies. It is classified base_ones_chunking on the reading that a learner computes a partitioned total by combining place-value chunks across a chain of binary additions; if the model admits only strict binary addition, this is the one spot where a demanded task is a composition rather than a single-automaton match, not an out-of-vocabulary operation. Note also that the first pass omitted several incidental sub-operations the lesson demands but does not foreground (85/17=5 for the aquarium batch count, 4+5=9 and 130/10=13 and 6+4=10 for the sum-of-ratio setups); these were not added here because they were not in the first-pass record to verify, but each sits inside the same division/addition families and none fall outside the vocabulary.

title: A Fermi Problem
student_facing_goal: Let's solve a Fermi problem.
teacher_goals: I can apply what I have learned about ratios and rates to solve a more complicated problem.
I can decide what information I need to know to be able to solve a real-world problem about ratios and rates.
purpose: In this lesson, students apply the strategies for reasoning about situations involving equivalent ratios to solve an unfamiliar, Fermi-type problem. Students must take a problem that is not well-posed and simplify the problem so that it can be solved. To understand the problem, students need to break down a larger question into more-manageable sub-questions, which requires sense making and perseverance (MP1). To solve the problem, they need to make assumptions and approximations, plan an approach, and reason with the mathematics they know. Engineers, computer scientists, physicists, and economists often make simplifying assumptions as they use mathematics to tackle complex problems. This process is central to mathematical modeling (MP4).

task_events.excerpt: There are 30 teachers and 12 adults who are not teachers, so there are 42 adults.
There are 20 classes with 25 students in each class. ... (20 · 25) + 42 = 500 + 42 = 542.
There are 542 people in total, because (20 · 25) + 42 = 500 + 42 = 542.
If each person uses 2 teaspoons, then 542 people use 542 · 2 or 1,084 teaspoons.
There are 16 cups in a gallon, 8 ounces in a cup ... There are 128 ounces in 16 cups.
There are 6 teaspoons in an ounce, so in 128 ounces there are 6 × 128 or 768 teaspoons.

boundary_note: Every arithmetic step captured maps to an existing automaton: addition -> base_ones_chunking; multiplication -> coordinate_groups_items and repeat_equal_groups. No demanded operation in this lesson lacks an automaton. Two corrections applied to the first pass: tasks 5 and 6 were cited to page 285 but the sanitizer/teaspoon arithmetic and the cups-ounces table are on page 286 (page 285 holds only the graphic-organizer diagram and sub-questions 1-2); and their figure_bound flags were changed from true to false because every operand (16, 8, 6, 128) and result (768) appears in plain running text and the "Facts" line, with "6 x 128 or 768" written explicitly, so pdftotext would not miss them. One further sample-response step, 4 . 1/2 = 2 (page 285, sub-question 2), was not extracted; it would map to a multiplication/fraction automaton, so it introduces no genuine boundary. Caveat for the model: all six events come from the teacher-facing sample Student Response, not operations every student must perform. The lesson's real target is Fermi-problem modeling (decomposition into sub-questions, assumptions, estimation, MP1/MP4), which is not an arithmetic automaton and is where the arithmetic-strategy vocabulary honestly stops.

title: Anchoring Units of Measurement
student_facing_goal: Let's see how big different things are.
teacher_goals: Compare (orally) the relative size of different units of measure for one attribute, that is, length, volume, and weight or mass.
Comprehend the approximate size of 1 inch, foot, yard, mile, millimeter, centimeter, meter, kilometer, ounce, pound, ton, gram, kilogram, cup, quart, gallon, milliliter, and liter.
Identify which unit is closest to the length, volume, weight, or mass of a given object, and explain (orally) the reasoning.
purpose: This lesson activates and builds on students' prior knowledge of units of measurement. It anchors students' perception of standard units of length, volume, weight, and mass with familiar objects serving as benchmarks. This work prepares students to reason about ratios and rates in the context of measurement and to perform unit conversions.
boundary_note: This lesson contains no concrete arithmetic computations. It focuses exclusively on measurement unit anchoring through benchmarking, estimation, classification, and reasoning about which units are appropriate for different objects and contexts. All tasks are either estimation activities, unit-classification exercises, or magnitude-comparison reasoning (e.g., identifying whether 500 units refers to feet or yards given a distance context) that require conceptual understanding of relative unit size rather than numerical operations.

title: Measuring with Different-Size Units
teacher_goals: Generalize (orally and in writing) that it takes more of a smaller unit or fewer of a larger unit to measure the same quantity.
Given a measurement in one unit, estimate what would be the same amount expressed in a different unit, and explain (orally) the reasoning.
purpose: This optional lesson further develops students' understanding of standard units of length, volume, weight, and mass through the tactile experiences of measuring objects. The main idea is that it takes more of a smaller unit and fewer of a larger unit to measure the same quantity. Students begin by comparing two non-standard units of length. They determine whether it would take more regular paper clips or more jumbo paper clips to measure the length of a piece of paper. Then, students engage in similar exercises involving various standard units. In groups, they rotate through up to five measurement stations, using two units to measure one or more quantities at each station. By reasoning repeatedly, students notice regularity—that a measurement in a smaller unit results in a greater value than the same measurement in a larger unit.
student_facing_goal: Let's measure things.

task_events.excerpt: About how many teaspoons of salt would it take to fill the graduated cylinder to 100 milliliters?
If you poured 15 teaspoons of salt into an empty graduated cylinder, about how many milliliters would it fill?

deformation.excerpt: If you poured 15 teaspoons of salt into an empty graduated cylinder, about how many milliliters would it fill?

task_events.excerpt: How many milliliters per teaspoon are there?
How many teaspoons per milliliter are there?

boundary_note: This lesson develops standard-unit measurement through hands-on data collection: students measure salt with teaspoons and record volumes in mL, then reason about the resulting values. The four recorded task events all come from the Station 5 follow-up questions on pages 54-55, where the data (4 teaspoons per 20 mL, i.e. 5 mL per teaspoon) and every answer are printed in the Student Response. The whole-number computations map to the model: measurement division to count teaspoons for a target volume (5a, divide(100,5)=20), repeated equal groups to build volume from a teaspoon count (5b, multiply(15,5)=75), and partitive division to find the unit rate mL-per-teaspoon (5c, divide(20,4)=5). The reciprocal unit rate in 5d (teaspoons per milliliter = 4/20 = 1/5) yields a unit fraction with no clean whole-number automaton, so it is left as NONE — the honest boundary where this lesson's arithmetic runs into fraction reasoning. The Lesson-2 practice problems (57 in to cm answer 144.8, 20 qt to L answer 19, 4.5 lb to kg answer 2.04) are deliberately excluded: each requires a conversion constant (2.54, ~0.946, ~0.454) that never appears on the page, so the operands cannot be read from the source and are not recorded.

title: Converting Units
student_facing_goal: Let's convert measurements to different units.
teacher_goals: Choose a strategy to solve problems involving unit conversions. Create a double number line diagram, a table, or another representation as needed.
Explain (orally) how to use a "rate per 1" to solve problems involving unit conversion.
Recognize that when we measure things in two different units, the pairs of measurements are equivalent ratios.
purpose: In this lesson, students convert units of measurement by reasoning about ratios. The goal is to see that when a quantity is measured in two different units, the pairs of measurements form equivalent ratios. Students also begin to think about "rates per 1" in this context and consider how these can help with unit conversion. The reasoning here serves as a transition for understanding unit rates more broadly in future lessons.

boundary_note: Verified every operand, operation, and answer against the source PDF (printed pages 62-75). Key correction: the first pass misread "2 3/4 cups of flour" as "2 1/2" and then fabricated a source arithmetic-error deformation (claiming the source said 2 1/2 x 16 = 44 vs 40). The source is correct — 2 3/4 x 16 = 44 — so the event is fixed to multiply(11/4, 16) = 44 and the deformation is removed; there is no error in the source. Deleted two events that cite page 76, which is not in the provided PDF (it ends at page 75) and cannot be confirmed: divide(28, 35) = 0.8 (baseball bat 28 ounces to kilograms) and multiply(0.8, 1000) = 800 (kilograms to grams). Reset figure_bound to false on every event: all operands appear in prose, and the double-number-line and table figures on pages 66/68/69/73 only restate values already given in the text (the first pass had marked seven events figure_bound=true without cause). Fixed a copy-pasted excerpt on multiply(4, 10) = 40 that had carried warm-up text ("If a fourth of 32 is 8..."); the real source is "88 is 4 times 22, so the weight in kilograms is 4 times 10, which is 40." Kind policy applied for internal consistency: (a) finding a fraction of a whole number (1/4 of 32, 1/2 x 16, etc.) and ratio/unit-rate division (dividing a:b to get a per-1 rate) have no matching automaton -> NONE; this is the Grade 6 boundary where the current arithmetic automata run out. (b) Conversion multiplications by a decimal or fraction rate (1.5 x 10, 0.25 x 10, 8 x 22/10, 75 x 8/5, 2 x 3.8) also -> NONE. (c) coordinate_groups_items is kept only for whole-number x whole-number products (10 x 5, 4 x 10, 3,785 x 3). Removed the measurement kind "unit_conversion_by_iteration" the first pass attached to four divisions (8/5, 5/8, 22/10, 19/5): op=division only pairs with division-category kinds, and the mechanism here is ratio scaling / dividing to a per-1 rate, not iterating a unit — this now agrees with the boundary note instead of contradicting it. Standards transcribe correctly: Building On 5.NF.B.4.a, Addressing 6.RP.A.3.d, no Building Toward listed (lesson-level and every activity 3.1-3.5 agree). Not extracted (outside the verify-what-exists scope, but present in source for a fuller record): three more warm-up fraction-of-number items on pages 63-64 (3/4 of 32 = 24, 3/8 of 32 = 12, 3/8 of 64 = 24) and the "Are You Ready for More?" Moon/Mars extension on page 69.

title: Comparing Speeds and Prices
student_facing_goal: Let's compare some speeds and some prices.
teacher_goals: Explain (orally and in writing) that if two ratios have the same rate per 1, they are equivalent ratios.
Justify (orally and in writing) comparisons of speeds or prices.
Recognize that calculating how much for 1 of the same unit is a useful strategy for comparing rates. Express these rates (in spoken and written language) using the word "per" and specifying the unit.
purpose: In this lesson, students continue to work with ratios in context and reason about rates per 1. They consider situations and questions about speed and prices. Students see that expressing ratios as rates per 1 is an effective way to compare them. For instance, calculating the distance run in 1 hour or 1 minute shows that runners have the same speed, and calculating the price for 1 item shows which shopping deal is better. Students work toward the understanding that when two ratios have the same rate per 1, they are equivalent ratios, and notice that dividing one quantity in a ratio by the other is an efficient way to find a rate per 1.

task_events.excerpt: Tyler ran 4,200 meters in 30 minutes. At what rate did he run?

Are bottles of sparkling water cheaper or more expensive this week? How much cheaper or more expensive? Show your reasoning.
Priya travels 22 meters in 10 seconds. Who was moving faster?
Mai traveled 15 meters in 6 seconds. Who was moving faster?

Store Y: 5 cans for $3.00. Which store offers the best deal?
A 450-square-foot apartment in New York City costs $540,000. What is the price per square foot?
A 2,100-square-foot home in Cheyenne, Wyoming, costs $110 per square foot. How much does this home cost?

boundary_note: All 16 operands and answers were confirmed against the source pages; none were misread and none had to be deleted. The classification changes: (1) The first pass sent eight decimal-quotient divisions to kind NONE on the premise that no automaton converts an integer division into a decimal quotient. That premise overlooks the decimal category, which carries ecuadorian_decimal_long_division — the decimal long-division automaton that covers exactly these (6/8=0.75, 3/2=1.5, 1.40/28=0.05, 5/4=1.25, 22/10=2.2, 15/6=2.5, 2.48/4=0.62, 3/5=0.6). They are now op=decimal / ecuadorian_decimal_long_division. (2) The whole-quotient rate and price divisions (4200/30, 6300/30, 6300/45, 10/10, 540000/450) are partitive unit-rate finds and map to division / fair_share_equal_groups; the first pass read three as measure_groups_of_size (the quotitive reading, wrong here) and one as division / known_fact_retrieval, which is not a valid pair since known_fact_retrieval is an addition automaton. (3) The cool-down subtraction is decimal subtraction, so its op is decimal, not subtraction (decimal_subtraction_by_aligned_units lives in the decimal category). (4) Three figure_bound flags were corrected to false because the operands appear in readable task text, not only in ad graphics ($1.40/28-oz can in the written Q2; 4-for-$5 and $1.69 in the cool-down text). The boundary this lesson actually reaches is not arithmetic but ratio: its stated goal (6.RP.A.2, 6.RP.A.3.b) is the judgment that two ratios are equivalent because they share the same rate per 1. That judgment is a ratio-category operation (scale_ratio_unit / construct_referent_ratio_diagram), and this arithmetic task registry records only the underlying divisions and multiplications, not the equivalence claim that gives them their point.

title: Interpreting Rates
teacher_goals: Calculate and interpret the two unit rates associated with a ratio, i.e., a/b and b/a for the ratio a:b.
Choose which unit rate to use to solve a given problem, and explain the choice (orally and in writing).
Comprehend that the term 'unit rate' (in spoken and written language) refers to a rate per 1.
purpose: This lesson introduces the term unit rate and the two unit rates, a/b and b/a, associated with a ratio a:b. Students learn that each unit rate tells us how much of one quantity in the ratio there is per unit of the other quantity. They also see that, although both unit rates describe the same situation, one or the other might be preferable for answering a given question about the situation. The work in this lesson also reinforces the idea that dividing one number in a ratio by another is an efficient way to find a unit rate. (It is not necessary to start with a table or use another representation as an intermediate step.)
student_facing_goal: Let's explore unit rates.

boundary_note: Grade 6 ratio/rate lesson. Demanded operations are (a) dividing the two terms of a ratio to produce per-1 unit rates and (b) scaling a unit rate by a quantity. Unit-rate divisions are tagged scale_ratio_unit (the ratio-category automaton); the scaling-up steps are tagged repeat_equal_groups (multiplication). The 5.1 Math Talk opens with four true/false judgments about division/fraction/decimal equivalence (e.g., 5÷10=0.5); these demand division but no whole-number division automaton fits the mental fraction/decimal reasoning, so they are marked kind NONE. The third Math Talk statement, '4÷20=2/5', is deliberately false (4÷20=0.2, not 0.4) and carries a deformation. VERIFICATION CORRECTIONS: (1) first-pass misread that statement as '4÷20=3/5' with wrong_answer 0.6 — corrected to 2/5 / 0.4; (2) the cool-down 'gallons per dollar' cell was recorded as divide(1,3), lifting the answer 1/3 into the functor — corrected to divide(2,6); (3) nine task events (practice problems 3–6: polenta 5:2, paint 350 sq ft, typing 208/342 words, corn 20/25 ears) cited pages 107–108, which are NOT present in this source PDF — the file ends at page 106 with only practice problems 1 and 2 — so all nine were deleted as unconfirmable. 24 verified task events remain.

title: Equivalent Ratios Have the Same Unit Rates
teacher_goals: Apply reasoning about unit rates to complete a table of equivalent ratios, and explain (orally and in writing) the solution method.
Explain (orally) that if two ratios are equivalent, they have the same rate per 1.
Generalize that the unit rate is the factor that relates the value in one column to the value in the other column in a table of equivalent ratios.
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is to make it explicit to students that equivalent ratios have the same unit rates. To arrive at this insight, students reason about equivalent ratios and unit rates in a direction opposite of that in prior work.
student_facing_goal: Let's revisit equivalent ratios.

boundary_note: Every computation this lesson demands maps to an existing automaton, so no task hits a vocabulary boundary. The divisions split into three cleanly-typed families: partitive rate-finding (total shared over N units to get a per-unit rate: $/binder, tomatoes/onion, $/oz, m/s speed, lb/kg) reads as fair_share_equal_groups; quotitive count-finding (total measured in whole rate-sized chunks: notebooks, bracelet-hours, seconds, hours, bananas) reads as measure_groups_of_size; and fractional-rate measurement (applesauce pounds via 1.5, gallons via 100/35) reads as measurement_division. Multiplications are all value-times-unit-rate scaling (coordinate_groups_items). Two corrections were required for number integrity: three first-pass events captured the table row in the reverse direction (using the answer as an operand), and the four applesauce events used cross-multiplication intermediates (42, 60, 36, 80) that appear nowhere on the source pages rather than the lesson's own prescribed unit-rate operation (multiply/divide by 1.5, p.120). The warm-up (6.1, Which Three Go Together) is a qualitative rate comparison with no demanded computation and correctly yields no task events. Note: op is recorded at the arithmetic-operation level (division/multiplication); measurement_division sits under the fraction domain in the vocabulary but is a genuine division automaton, so the op/kind pairing is intentional, not a domain mismatch.

title: More Rate Comparisons (Grade 6, Unit 3, Lesson 7)
student_facing_goal: Let's use unit rates like a pro.
teacher_goals: Apply reasoning about ratios and unit rates to compare prices and justify (orally) whether a given price is a good deal.
I can choose how to use unit rates to solve problems.
purpose: In this lesson, students gain fluency in working with unit rates by solving problems without scaffolding. They choose which unit rate to use to solve a problem, divide to find the desired unit rate, and multiply or divide by the unit rate to answer questions. In the main activity, students decide whether to accept shopping deals by comparing unit rates. In the optional activity, students compare distances given in different units of length to determine which animal ran the farthest, requiring multiple conversion rates in sequence.

task_events.excerpt: B. Juice Boxes — Original: 10 for $3.50. Table: 10 boxes, $3.50, $0.35 per box.

boundary_note: The optional Activity 7.3 includes metric-to-imperial conversions that chain multiple rates and so exceed the single-operation functor format: kangaroo 1,073 meters to feet and ostrich 1.15 kilometers to feet. Page 135 spells them out as (1,073) . 100 / 2.54 / 12 = 3,520.34 and (1.15) . (1,000) . 100 / 2.54 / 12 = 3,772.97 (m to cm to in to ft, with km to m for the ostrich). No measurement automaton captures a chained cross-system conversion in one step; these fall at the boundary where converting requires human judgment about which intermediate unit to route through. They are therefore excluded from task_events. Note also that the antelope task multiply(1, 5280) is degenerate: converting 1 mile to feet is recall of the given fact 1 mile = 5,280 feet rather than a computed multiplication, so it is retained as a verified conversion but its multiplicative form is nominal.

student_facing_goal: Let's investigate the Burj Khalifa building.
teacher_goals: Choose a strategy to solve problems involving rates and explain (orally, in writing, and through other representations) the solution method.

purpose: In this lesson, students solve problems that reward finding and making sense of unit rates. All problems use the Burj Khalifa—the tallest building in the world as of 2023—as a context. Solving them requires students to make sense of and talk about rates in terms of the situation, giving students opportunities to apply and deepen their understanding from earlier lessons.

boundary_note: Every computational task fits the arithmetic vocabulary; none demands an operation outside it, so there is no NONE. The lesson is a uniform divide-then-scale rate structure. Divisions that find a rate per 1 (or per equal part of a whole) are partitive and classified fair_share_equal_groups: 828/3 (meters per third of the climb), 138/4 (meters per quarter hour), 330/10 and 310/10 (feet per second), 33/4 (dollars per hour), 4/5 (dollars per can). This corrects the first pass, which split these structurally identical unit-rate divisions between fair_share_equal_groups and missing_factor_known_product_search; here they are all partitive. Divisions that find a count of equal groups are quotitive and stay measure_groups_of_size: 24,348/15 (groups of 15 windows) and 26/4 (groups of 4 tables). Every scaling multiplication is repeat_equal_groups. Two further corrections: the cool-down operands (330 ft, 10 s, 11 s) are in the prose task statement, not a figure, so figure_bound is false, not true. And practice problem 4 (26 tables) prints only its task statement in the source—no solution page—so its answer 65 is confirmed by computation from the page operands (2.5 min per table, 26 tables) rather than read from a printed solution; the 26/4 division is one of two valid decompositions, both reaching 65.

boundary_note:
Pages 137-139 mix two lessons. A single header line and the "Responding to Student Thinking / Points to Emphasize" note reference Grade 6 Unit 3 Lesson 9 "More about Constant Speed" (comparing distance/time rates), but the summary and every practice problem carry the footer "Grade 6 Unit 3 Lesson 7" and the heading "Lesson 7 Practice Problems." The practice content is about unit rates and price/weight/pay comparisons, not constant speed (problems 4 and 5 are marked spiral review from Lessons 4 and 8). So all eleven task_events are transcribed from Lesson-7-labeled practice problems; the curator should decide whether they belong on an L9 record or an L7 one before treating this as canonical.

No student-facing goal, lesson purpose, or CCSS codes (Building On / Addressing / Building Toward) appear in the provided pages — that material sits in front matter not included here, so those fields are left empty rather than guessed. teacher_goals paraphrase the Lesson 9 "Points to Emphasize" note.

Every operand and answer was checked against the rendered page images. The IM PDFs render each computed expression as a math image that plain-text extraction drops, so the solution arithmetic (308÷11=28, 18·28=504, 480÷4=120, 120·10=1,200, 720÷120=6, 9·2000=18,000, 5,500·2.2=12,100, 15,840÷16=990, 10·2=20, 30÷2=15) is legible only in the image, while task-statement and table operands survive as text. figure_bound is true only where an operand exists solely in rendered math: the 1/2- and 3/2-hour amounts in problem 4. The uncle's-rate event divide(30, 3/2)=20 is a single-operation reconstruction — the page reaches $20/hour by halves ($10 per 1/2 hour, doubled) rather than by one division — but both operands (30, 3/2) and the $20/hour result are on the page. One op fix: 5,500·2.2 uses decimal_multiplication_rule, which is a "decimal"-category kind, so op is "decimal", not "multiplication". Every demanded operation maps to a covered registry kind; none require an automaton outside the vocabulary.

title: What Are Percentages?
student_facing_goal: Let's learn about percentages.
teacher_goals: Comprehend the word "percentage" (in written and spoken language) and the symbol "%" (in written language) to mean a rate per 100.
Draw and label a double number line diagram to represent percentages of a dollar and to find corresponding monetary values or percentages.
purpose: This lesson introduces percent and percentage. Students learn that percent means "per hundred" and describes the ratio of a quantity to 100. They begin with gridded 10-by-10 squares, each representing an area of 100 square units, and use "percent of the large square" to describe shaded regions (including values less than, equal to, and greater than 100). They then treat percentages as rates per 100 with money as the main context, expressing coin values as percentages of a dollar (100 cents), and use double number line diagrams to align dollar amounts with percentages and connect the idea to equivalent ratios. Here 100% corresponds only to 100 units of area and to 1 dollar; later lessons let 100% correspond to other values.

task_events.excerpt: Clare has 6 dimes in her pocket. What percentage of a dollar does she have?
Han has 6 quarters in his pocket. What percentage of a dollar does he have?
Which has a greater value: 8 nickels or 40% of a dollar?

What percentage of a dollar is the value of each coin combination? b. 1 nickel and 3 pennies

Three tickets for a train ride cost $18. Complete the table to show the cost of 4, 5, and 6 tickets at the same rate. (cost of 5 tickets)
Three tickets for a train ride cost $18. Complete the table to show the cost of 4, 5, and 6 tickets at the same rate. (cost of 6 tickets)

boundary_note: The lesson's conceptual core—percent as a rate per 100—is developed through coin values and double number line diagrams and does not demand arithmetic missing from the vocabulary; every extracted task is whole-number multiplication or addition of coin values, or a partitive division to find a unit price / a half. The genuine boundary is in the review problems that are NOT part of this lesson's content: Practice 5 (tokens) and Practice 6 (snail) demand multi-step proportional reasoning—first a unit-rate division ($4.40 / 8 = $0.55 per token; 10 cm / 4 min = 2.5 cm/min), then scaling ($6.05 / $0.55 = 11 tokens; 6 min x 2.5 = 15 cm; 19 x $0.55 = $10.45). The component decimal operations do have kinds (decimal_multiplication_rule, ecuadorian_decimal_long_division), so the gap is not the arithmetic itself but the composition "recover the unit rate, then scale it," which is not a single automaton in this vocabulary. Finding an arbitrary percentage of $50 (e.g., 75% of $50 = $37.50) likewise needs decimal multiplication rather than the whole-number coin arithmetic the lesson foregrounds; those cells were left figure-bound and out of the task list.

title: Representing Percentages with Double Number Line Diagrams
teacher_goals: Comprehend a phrase like "A% of B" (in written and spoken language) to refer to the value that makes a ratio with B that is equivalent to A : 100.
Explain (orally) how to use a double number line diagram to solve problems such as A of B is ?, and A of ? is C.
Interpret statements about percentages and identify the quantity that corresponds to 100%.
student_facing_goal: Let's use double number line diagrams to represent percentages.
purpose: In this lesson, students explore percentages of quantities other than 100 and 1 and in a variety of contexts. In all activities, the percentages describe multiplicative comparisons of two quantities, rather than part-to-whole relationships. Students reason about percentages when the value for 100% is known (such as finding 75% of $10) and about the value of 100% when a percentage is known (such as finding a weight given that 10% of that weight is 9 pounds). The percentages in all problems are limited to multiples of 10% and 25%.

task_events.excerpt: After one week, she had saved 50% of the cost of the hat.
After two weeks, she had saved 75% of the cost of the hat.

boundary_note: This lesson centers on percentages as multiplicative comparisons and ratios per 100. The core operations—finding P% of N and finding N when N is P% of M—are not represented by automata in the provided vocabulary, which derives from K–5 arithmetic strategies. Percentage-finding is conceptually distinct from earlier grade-level operations and involves understanding a percentage as a rate per 100 and scaling quantities proportionally. This marks a significant shift in mathematical reasoning introduced at Grade 6. All percentage-computation tasks are marked kind=NONE to indicate this boundary.

title: Representing Percentages in Different Ways
student_facing_goal: Let's use tape diagrams to understand percentages.
teacher_goals: Choose and create representations to solve problems such as A of B is ? and A of ? is C.
Interpret diagrams that represent situations involving percentages and explain (orally) how the quantities are related.
purpose: In this lesson, students use two more familiar representations, tape diagrams and tables, to reason about percentages. In prior grades, students used tape diagrams to reason about fractions and multiplicative comparisons. Here, tape diagrams can serve similar purposes, enabling students to see connections between percentages and fractions.

boundary_note: The lesson demands percentage operations (multiplication by decimals ≤ 1.5, division by decimals, finding one quantity as a percentage of another) that lie beyond the Grade-6 multiplication and division strategy vocabulary. These operations fall into the domain of ratio and proportion reasoning rather than basic computational strategies (equal groups, repeated factors, fair share, measurement division). All tasks marked kind="NONE" to signal this boundary: the formal model has no automaton that directly encodes percentage-scaling computation.

title: Benchmark Percentages
student_facing_goal: Let's contrast percentages and fractions.
teacher_goals: Explain (orally and in writing) how to solve problems involving the percentages 10%, 25%, 50%, and 75% by reasoning about the fractions 1/10, 1/4, 1/2, and 3/4.
Generalize (orally) processes for calculating 10%, 25%, 50%, and 75% of a quantity.
purpose: The goal of this lesson is to help students understand the connection between benchmark percentages and common fractions. In these materials, 10%, 25%, 50%, and 75% are identified as primary benchmark percentages and multiples of 10% as secondary benchmark percentages.

boundary_note: Every operand in this lesson appears in readable text (pdftotext captured all questions, answers, and the Practice 2 table), so no task event is figure-bound. The lesson's core work is computing benchmark percentages of a quantity and the reverse (finding the whole from a percentage part). Two families fall outside the K-6 automaton set and are tagged NONE: (a) 75% forward and 75%/150% reverse via non-unit fractions — 75% of a quantity multiplies by 3/4, and "9 is 75%/150% of what number?" multiplies by 4/3 or 2/3, none of which map to a unit-fraction division or an equal-groups multiplication automaton; (b) "A is what percentage of B" when B does not divide evenly by A (6 is what percent of 10 -> 6/10), a rate computation with no dedicated automaton. The evenly-dividing "A is what percentage of B" cases (15 of 30, 3 of 12, 6 of 24) are tagged measure_groups_of_size, since the operative move is quotitive measurement (how many A's fit in B) followed by taking the reciprocal. Benchmark 50%/10%/25%/20% of a quantity map to fair_share_equal_groups (partition into 2/10/4/5 equal groups, take one); reverse problems with whole-number factors (x2, x4, x10) map to repeat_equal_groups. Fraction-of-fraction (50% of 1/2 mile = 1/2 x 1/2 = 1/4) is tagged area_model_part_of_part.

title: Solving Percentage Problems
student_facing_goal: Let's solve more percentage problems.
teacher_goals: Choose and create a tape diagram, double number line diagram, or table to solve problems involving percentages and explain (orally) the solution method.
Determine what information is needed to solve a problem involving percentages. Ask questions to elicit that information.
purpose: In this lesson, students solve percentage problems with less scaffolding and engage with all three kinds of questions that are commonly asked in situations where A% of B is C: (1) What is A% of B?; (2) What is B if A% of B is C?; (3) What percentage of B is C?. In the Warm-up, students reason about what percentage one whole number is of another whole number, with most problems involving benchmark percentages. In the main activity, an Info Gap, students practice identifying what information they need to solve percentage problems, asking for that information, and solving the problems. Unlike earlier lessons, no blank representations or intermediate prompts are given, so students must choose approaches that seem appropriate.

boundary_note: Percentage calculation fundamentally requires proportional reasoning: expressing quantities as ratios to 100 and solving for missing values in the three canonical question types. The core operations (multiplication, division, subtraction) appear in the vocabulary, and some strategies like fair_share_equal_groups or scale_ratio_unit partially apply. However, the lesson's strategic use of percentage decomposition (finding 10%, then scaling; finding the base when given a part and percentage) and the coordination across multiple representation forms (tape diagrams, double number lines, tables) exceed what individual arithmetic automata capture. The vocabulary has no dedicated percentage or proportional-reasoning kind, so all percentage-finding tasks are marked kind = NONE. This is an honest boundary: the arithmetic automata solve the component calculations, but the proportional-reasoning framework that ties them together lives at a higher conceptual level.

title: Finding This Percent of That
teacher_goals: Calculate any percentage of a value and explain (orally) the solution method.
Generalize a process for finding A% of B and justify (orally) why this can be expressed as (A/100) · B.
student_facing_goal: Let's solve percentage problems like a pro.
purpose: In this lesson, students work toward generalizing the process for finding a percentage of a value and for calculating "A% of B" efficiently. In previous lessons, the numbers were chosen so that they could be calculated mentally or easily represented on a diagram. In this lesson, the numbers are intentionally chosen to be difficult to work with mentally or to represent on a diagram. This is to motivate students to find a simpler way to do the calculation by hand. Students solve a series of problems that involve finding percentages that are not factors of 100. Through repeated reasoning, they notice regularity (MP8); to find P% of x, it is helpful to find 1% of x and then multiply that amount by P. In general, it can be done by computing (P/100) · x.

boundary_note: The lesson's central strategy is to find P% of B by calculating (P/100) · B, which is fundamentally multiplication by a percentage expressed as a decimal or fraction. While the formal vocabulary includes various multiplication strategies (known_product_adjustment, distribute_group_size_split, coordinate_groups_items, etc.), none specifically capture the strategy of "multiply by a percentage to find a percentage of a quantity." Thus, all percentage calculations are coded with kind: NONE, indicating that the lesson's core strategy is not represented in the formal automata vocabulary.

title: Finding the Percentage
student_facing_goal: Let's find percentages in general.
teacher_goals: Calculate the percentage one value is of another value and explain (orally) the solution method.
Generalize a process for finding the percentage that C is of B and justify (orally) why this can be expressed as C/B · 100.
purpose: This lesson is optional because it goes beyond the expectations of grade 6 standards. Students learn to generalize the process for expressing a number as a percentage of another number, or finding what percentage C is of B in a situation where A% of B is C. While students have reasoned about such problems using strategies learned in this unit, developing a general structure that will work with any pair of B and C is not required in this course. Students begin by revisiting fraction and decimal equivalence in the Warm-up. Next, they reason repeatedly about what percentage and what fraction one number is of another number in the context of a situation. They write each fraction in decimal form and then look for regularity in the way the decimals relate to the percentages. Then students generalize the process of expressing one number, C, as a fraction of another, B, as C/B. They see that the decimal equivalent of C/B is related to the numeric value of the percentage by a factor of 100. The percentage C is of B can be found by computing: C/B · 100. The last activity allows students to apply their generalized process to solve similar problems in a new context.

boundary_note: The lesson's core demand is calculating percentages via the formula C/B · 100 (proportional reasoning and ratio comparison), which does not have a dedicated automaton in the curriculum model vocabulary. The operation requires students to find a ratio, express it as a decimal, and scale by 100—a compound operation that transcends single atomic arithmetic. Additionally, Practice_4 involves multiplying by a decimal percentage factor (e.g., 30 × 0.85 = 25.50 to find 85% of a base price), which represents an inverse application where the percentage is given and students find the resulting value. Neither operation—finding what percentage one value is of another, nor calculating the result of applying a known percentage—maps cleanly to the automata vocabulary.

title: Painting a Room
student_facing_goal: Let's see what it takes to paint a room.
teacher_goals: Apply reasoning about rates and percentages to calculate the material, cost, and time needed to complete a painting project, and explain the solution methods.
Determine what information is needed to solve a problem about painting a room.
purpose: In this culminating lesson, students apply and integrate many concepts and skills from the past three units to estimate and calculate various quantities in a home improvement context. Along the way, students reason about areas of two-dimensional figures, solve problems involving ratios and rates, and work with percentages. Though there is a single measurement for the total wall area to be painted, the amount of paint needed will depend on some assumptions and decisions that students make about the work involved.

boundary_note: All 34 task_events confirm against the source pages; no operand misreads were found and none were deleted. The "op" field is set to the vocabulary category that lists the chosen kind, so every (op, kind) is a literal pair from the provided vocabulary; the first pass's op values (e.g. op:"multiplication" with kind:"rectangle_area_unit_iteration", a geometry automaton) were not real pairs and are corrected. Reclassifications: the two "wall minus opening" subtractions were mis-tagged area_preserving_polygon_decomposition (that removes area, so it is not area-preserving) and are now take_away_base_ones (72−21, no regroup) and decompose_base_for_ones (80−15, one regroup); 400−96 and 304−150 borrow across a zero, so borrow_across_zero_cascade; 38+36 is not a known fact (it carries), so base_ones_chunking; the discounted pricing sum/differences are decimal automata. figure_bound was corrected to false for the door (3×7) and window (5×3) products because those dimensions are given in the text bullets, not only the floor-plan figure; the figure-only wall lengths (9, 6.5, 10, 2, 0.5 ft) remain figure_bound. Pages were corrected: activity_2 spans 281–282 (the short-wall/east/south/sum arithmetic prints on 282). Genuine curricular demands surfaced: decimal multiplication at 6.5×8 and 0.5×8 (unit-square tiling breaks on a half-foot side) and throughout the discount pricing; ratio/percent reasoning (scale_ratio_unit) for the 24%-done and proportional-time computations. Boundary reached but not extracted: one of the source's alternative time strategies uses a mixed-number unit rate (1⅓ minutes per square foot, 228×1⅓=304), which would demand fraction multiplication with no whole-number automaton; the extracted path (300×96÷72) avoids it, so no kind is set to NONE. No signed arithmetic, fraction arithmetic, or exponent laws are demanded by the extracted events.

title: Size of Divisor and Size of Quotient
student_facing_goal: Let's explore quotients of different sizes.
teacher_goals: Comprehend the terms "dividend" and "divisor" (in spoken language) to refer to the numbers in a division problem.
Explain (orally) how to estimate quotients, by comparing the size of the dividend and divisor.
Generalize about the size of a quotient, i.e., predicting whether it is a very large number, a very small number, or close to 1.
purpose: This lesson invites students to investigate the relationships between the numbers in a division situation. Students learn that reasoning about the relative sizes of the divisor and the dividend can tell us about the size of the quotient.

task_events.excerpt: Find the value of each expression mentally. 5,000 ÷ 5
Find the value of each expression mentally. 5,000 ÷ 2,500
Find the value of each expression mentally. 5,000 ÷ 10,000
Find the value of each expression mentally. 5,000 ÷ 500,000
A stack of books is 72 inches tall. Each book is 2 inches thick. Which expression tells us how many books are in the stack? 72 ÷ 2

boundary_note: Computation tasks use whole-number division only. All five confirmed task events read operands directly from text (none figure-bound). Activity 1.1 warm-up: only 5,000÷5 is on p.46; the other three expressions (5,000÷2,500; 5,000÷10,000; 5,000÷500,000) are at the top of p.47 — first pass mislabeled these as p.46, now corrected. Activity 1.2's 72÷2 is on p.49 (p.48 holds only the narrative/launch), corrected from p.48. Activity 1.2 Task 1 (stacks of boxes/notebooks/egg cartons/pennies reaching 5 feet) is estimation with figure-assumed thicknesses, so no confirmable division automaton; excluded. Activities 1.3 (All in Order), 1.4 (Cool-down), and Practice Problems 1-3 ask students to estimate/order/classify quotients "without computing" with fractional and decimal operands (30÷1/2, 30÷0.45, 100÷1/100, 50⅓÷50¼, etc.) and never compute exact values, so no division automata for fractions or decimals are required by this lesson's explicit computational demands.

title: Meanings of Division
student_facing_goal: Let's explore ways to think about division.
teacher_goals: Identify or generate a multiplication equation that represents the same relationship as a division expression, and explain (orally) the reasoning.
Interpret a division expression in two ways: as an answer to a "How many groups?" question or to a "How many in each group?" question.
Interpret and create tape diagrams that represent situations involving equal-size groups.
purpose: In this lesson, students revisit the relationship between multiplication and division to solidify their understanding of the meanings of division. Specifically, students recall that we can think of multiplication as a way to quantify equal-size groups, and that we can find a product if we know the number of groups and the size of each group. We can interpret division as a way of finding an unknown factor, which can either be the number of groups, or the size of one group. Students explore these ideas in the context of concrete situations and by using diagrams and equations to support their reasoning. The given quantities are small whole-numbers that are easy to work with mentally, allowing students to focus on the structure that relates the quantities. Understanding this structure will support students in reasoning about division of fractions.

boundary_note: Every operand and quotient in the first pass was verified correct against the source pages (58-67); no digit was misread and no event was deleted. Classification was corrected, not the numbers. The first pass's `NONE` tags on the fraction/decimal tasks conflated operand type with the operation the task actually demands. In Practice 3 (4.5÷3, ½÷4) students only "write a multiplication equation that corresponds to each division equation" — the p66 solutions give the inverse equations (?·3=4.5, ?·4=½), so no fraction or decimal division is executed; these map to division/inverse_fact_decomposition regardless of operand type (10÷5 was likewise moved off fair_share to inverse_fact_decomposition). The one place a fraction division's value is genuinely reasoned about, 12÷½=24 ("how many half-pound bags in 12 pounds"), is measurement division by a unit fraction, which the fraction vocabulary DOES cover (fraction/measurement_division), so it is not a boundary either. 2.5÷5 is a partitive equal-groups situation with only an equation demanded, so fair_share_equal_groups, not NONE. Net result: this lesson reaches no genuine erasure/no-automaton boundary — every demanded operation has a fitting automaton. Two core division tasks omitted by the first pass were restored: the Warm-up 2.1 task 20÷4 (p59) and Practice Problem 1, 20÷5=4 (p66). Not extracted as graded events: the "Are You Ready for More" extension on p62 (how many ½- or 1/5-slices in a block; dividing by 0), which previews true unit-fraction measurement division and a division-by-zero limit, and the worked summary example 24÷3 on pp64-65, which is exposition rather than a student task. Many interpret-and-represent events remain tagged fair_share_equal_groups even though the lesson deliberately holds both the partitive ("how many in each group") and quotitive ("how many groups") readings; those are dual tasks where an agnostic missing_factor reading would be equally defensible, and the partitive tag is kept as the situation's primary reading.

boundary_note:
All demanded operations fall inside the controlled vocabulary. Warm-up multiplications map to multiplication/coordinate_groups_items (8 groups of $5) and fraction/unit_fraction_iteration (n copies of a unit fraction); the whole-number division 40÷5 maps to division/measure_groups_of_size; every "How many ___s are in ___?" task maps to fraction/measurement_division. No task requires an automaton outside the vocabulary, so no kind is set to NONE. Note the op field is set to the automaton family (so unit_fraction_iteration and measurement_division carry op "fraction" even though the surface arithmetic is multiply/divide); this makes each op+kind a real family/kind pair.

Four first-pass operand misreads were corrected against the rendered page images, because IM typesets fractions as MathML that pdftotext drops, so the digits must be read from the page, not the text layer: cool-down 2a is "How many 1/2s are in 3 1/2?" → divide(7/2,1/2)=7 (first pass read the divisor as 1/3, giving the spurious 10.5 it then flagged); cool-down 2c is "How many 1/6s are in 2/3?" → divide(2/3,1/6)=4 (first pass had divide(4/3,1/3)); practice 2 is "How many 1/3s are in 1 2/3?" → divide(5/3,1/3)=5 (first pass had divide(4/3,1/6)=8); practice 4's per-week group size is 3/4 lb → divide(3,3/4)=4 (first pass read 1/4 — the 1/4 marks are the diagram's sub-ticks composing each 3/4-lb week — and got 12). The first pass's original boundary_note described these as "discrepancies preserved from the original document," but they were its own transcription errors; the source is internally consistent on all four.

One genuine source defect remains at Practice Problem 1b. The problem statement clearly reads "8 ÷ 1/3 = ?" (dividing by a unit fraction; correct answer 24, consistent with the lesson summary's 2 ÷ 1/6 = 12), but the document's printed answer key answers 1b as a fair-share reading of 8÷3 ("What is in one group if there are 3 groups in 8? There is 2 2/3 in each group"). The task_event records the operands as printed in the problem statement (divide(8,1/3), answer 24); the mismatched answer-key prose is an inconsistency in the source PDF, not a vocabulary boundary.

purpose: In this lesson, students learn to use tape diagrams to represent and answer "How many groups?" questions. Tape diagrams are more abstract than other visual representations but offer greater flexibility for making sense of problems involving fractions. Students continue to write multiplication and division equations for each situation, engaging in abstract and quantitative reasoning (MP2). As students work with diagrams to show known values and reason about unknown values, they practice communicating with precision (MP6).
student_facing_goal: Let's draw tape diagrams to think about division with fractions.

Every task event is quotitive ("how many groups of a given size fit in a total") division, spanning whole-by-whole, whole-by-fraction, and fraction-by-fraction operands; all are served by division/measure_groups_of_size. Verification against the source corrected eight misread divisors the first pass had guessed or transposed (2a: 3/4 not 1/3; 2c: 3/2 not 2/3; activity-3: 1/2-lb & 2 3/4-lb not 1/4 & 2 1/2; 3/8-in not 1/8-in; cool-down: 3/4 not 4/5 — the 4/5 is the p.120 Lesson-Synthesis teacher demo, not the cool-down; rice summary: 3/4-cup not 1/4-cup; practice-1: 1/4 not 1/5; practice-3: 1/2-day not 1/7-day), and deleted two practice items (divisors 5/6 and 4/5) whose source page 124 is absent from this file. title: What Fraction of a Group?
teacher_goals: Comprehend the phrase "What fraction of a group?" (in spoken and written language) as a variation of the question "How many groups?" that is used when the number of groups is less than 1.
Create a tape diagram to represent and solve a problem asking "How many groups?" in which the number of groups is a fraction less than 1.
Write multiplication and division equations to represent a problem asking "How many times as long?"
purpose: In this lesson, students extend their exploration of "How many groups?" questions to include cases where the number of groups is a fraction less than 1. In such situations, the total amount is smaller than the size of 1 group, so the question becomes "What fraction of a group?" Students notice that they can use the same reasoning strategies as before because each situation still has the same structure of (number of groups) · (size of a group) = total amount. They continue to write multiplication equations of this form and the corresponding division equations.
student_facing_goal: Let's think about dividing things into groups when we can't even make one whole group.
boundary_note: The lesson runs on tape diagrams and measurement-division reasoning rather than a formal invert-and-multiply algorithm; students arrive at fractional quotients (2/3, 3/4, 5/6, 1/10) by partitioning and matching lengths on a diagram. Every demanded computation is measurement division in the fraction family: "how many groups of size b fit in a," or, when a is smaller than b, "what fraction of a group." Each soup and rope comparison also asks students to write a missing-factor multiplication equation (?·b = a) beside the division; that equation represents the same relation, and its unknown is exactly what the division finds, so each comparison is logged once as its division (the first pass did the same for the soup activity but redundantly split the rope rows into multiply/divide pairs, two of which carried misread operands). op was corrected from "division" to "fraction" throughout, because "measurement_division" is a fraction-family automaton and the original op/kind pairs were cross-family mismatches. Practice problems 3 and 7 are multiple-choice interpretation items (match a situation to an equation, answers A and B) with no operand to compute, so they carry no task event.

title: Grade 6, Unit 4, Section C: Algorithm for Fraction Division
teacher_goals: Generalize a process for dividing a number n by a fraction a/b as n · b · 1/a, n · b ÷ a, or n · b/a.
Use the fraction division algorithm to calculate quotients.
purpose: In this section, students develop a general algorithm for dividing a fraction by a fraction, pulling together the thread of reasoning from earlier lessons. First, students use tape diagrams to reason repeatedly about division of a whole number by a unit fraction, looking for structure and regularity. They then extend this reasoning to division by a non-unit fraction, generalizing that to divide a number n by a non-unit fraction a/b is to find how many unit fractions 1/b are in n (find n ÷ 1/b) and then how many groups of a unit fractions can be formed (find n ÷ 1/b ÷ a). For example, to divide 6 by 3/4 is to find how many 1/4 are in 6 (6 ÷ 1/4) and then how many groups of three 1/4 can be formed (6 ÷ 1/4 ÷ 3). Students then extend these generalized steps to divide a fraction by a fraction, concluding that to find n ÷ a/b is to find n · b · 1/a or n · b/a, and finally practice applying this algorithm to calculate quotients.

boundary_note: Correction to the first pass: the model DOES have a live, registered automaton for fraction-by-fraction division, so tasks 6-7 are (fraction, measurement_division), not NONE. run_fraction_action(measurement_division, fraction_pair(A,B,C,D), unit(whole), ...) in strategies/math/fraction_action_pairs.pl (elaborating divaded_fractional_units:measurement_divide_fractions/7, with a passing test) computes both checkpoint quotients exactly by co-measuring both fractions in a shared unit, counting how many group-sizes fit, and naming the leftover as a fraction of the group size. I ran it: 9/8 ÷ 3/2 returned whole_groups(0) + remainder(9/12) = 3/4, and 21/10 ÷ 1/5 returned whole_groups(10) + remainder(1/2) = 21/2. Both match the printed answers on page 179. The genuine boundary is narrower than the first pass claimed and sits in the lesson's stated GOAL, not the operation: the automaton backs the quotient through measurement meaning, whereas Section C's first goal asks students to generalize the literal symbolic invert-and-multiply rule (n · b/a). Per the comment in fraction_action_pairs.pl, that literal invert-and-multiply algorithm as its own operation has no automaton in the repo. Numeric verification against the source: all seven operand/answer pairs confirmed against pages 175-179 (tasks 1-4 from the plain-text solutions; task 5 from the page-176 table image; tasks 6-7 from the page-179 solutions and re-derived by running the automaton). Two kind refinements beyond the fraction-division fix: tasks 1-2 moved from op 'division'/'multiplication' to op 'decimal' so the (op, kind) pair is valid, and tasks 3-4 moved from measure_groups_of_size to fair_share_equal_groups because 'how far in 1 second' is partitive (known number of groups = seconds, unknown group size), not quotitive. No CCSS codes are printed anywhere in this PDF excerpt (pages 175-179 carry the Section C practice problems, the Section C narrative, and the Section C checkpoint), so Building On/Addressing/Building Toward remain empty. Section C Checkpoint item 1 ('Select all expressions that give 6 ÷ 3/2'; answer B, C, D, F) was left out because it is an expression-equivalence task, not a compute-the-quotient event.

title: How Much in Each Group? (Part 2)
student_facing_goal: Let's practice dividing fractions in different situations.
teacher_goals: Interpret a situation (presented in written language or using other representations) involving equal-size groups, and generate mathematical questions that could be asked about it.

purpose: This optional lesson offers students additional practice in finding the amount in one group given the amount in a different number of groups. It also prompts students to interpret, represent, and solve different kinds of division problems. In one activity, students are not explicitly told whether the situations involve finding the number of groups or finding the amount in each group. They decide on an interpretation that would enable them to solve the problem. Students are also required to identify relevant information (from a video, a picture, or written statements) that would help them answer questions. In an optional activity, they invent a situation that can be represented by a given division expression.

boundary_note: All eight retained task events were confirmed operand-by-operand against pages 161-172. The first-pass extraction had misread an operand in every one of the seven contextual divisions: dividend 3/4 read as 1/4 in 9.2; 4/9 read as 2/9 in 9.3-Q2; 1 1/5 read as 1 1/3 in 9.3-Q3; 5/4 read as 5/6 in 9.3-Q4; given 7/8 read as the answer 2/8 in the cool-down; and the blanket division reversed to 1,600 ÷ 17,280,000. Each contextual problem shares one equation structure — (number of groups) · ? = total, solve for the unit — so all are tagged solve_for_unit rather than measurement_division; the lesson narrative (p.166) states the odd/even "groups greater than 1 vs a fraction of a group" distinction does not change the equation, only the diagram. The lone quotitive (measurement) task is the crochet-blanket extension, tagged measure_groups_of_size; its functor answer is the count 10,800, from which the word-problem's reported answer 1/10,800 is the reciprocal. The warm-up (9.1) demands estimating whether each of four quotients (1/2÷1/4, 1÷3/4, 2/3÷7/8, 2 7/8÷2 3/5) is greater or less than 1 — a magnitude-benchmarking comparison with no dedicated automaton in this vocabulary, so it is left out of the events rather than force-fit. Activity 9.4 (1/3 ÷ 1/4) is deliberately open: the lesson offers both a solve_for_unit reading ("1/4 of what number is 1/3?") and a measurement_division reading ("how many 1/4s are in 1/3?"); it is tagged by the primary "how much in one group" reading. The three first-pass practice events cited page 174, which is not present in the provided 12-page PDF (pages 161-172 only); they were deleted as unverifiable, not reclassified.

title: Dividing by Unit and Non-Unit Fractions
student_facing_goal: Let's look for patterns when we divide by a fraction.
purpose: Students use tape diagrams to recall two ideas from grade 5: dividing by a whole number b has the same result as multiplying by the unit fraction 1/b, and dividing by a unit fraction 1/b has the same result as multiplying by the whole number b. They extend the second idea to division by non-unit fractions, seeing it as a two-step process: partition each whole into as many parts as the denominator (multiply by the denominator), then group those parts by the numerator (divide by the numerator). Through repetition they notice a pattern (MP8) and use structure (MP7) so they can find fraction quotients without always drawing diagrams.
teacher_goals: Interpret and critique explanations (in spoken and written language, as well as in other representations) of how to divide by a fraction.
Use a tape diagram to represent dividing by a non-unit fraction a/b and explain (orally) why this produces the same result as multiplying the number by b and dividing by a.
Use a tape diagram to represent dividing by a unit fraction 1/b and explain (orally and in writing) why this is the same as multiplying by b.
