Division by a unit fraction (6÷1/3, 8÷1/4, 12÷1/5, 24÷1/2, the ÷1/10 family, 5÷1/3) maps to unit_fraction_denominator_product_rule (a ÷ 1/b = a·b). Division by a non-unit fraction (6÷3/4, 6÷4/3, 6÷4/6, 12÷3/5, 4÷2/10, 4÷8/10, 5÷3/10, 5÷9/10, 2.5÷5/8, 1÷3/5) maps to measurement_division ("how many a/b fit in n"). The lesson's central pedagogical move — decomposing a ÷ (a/b) into two steps, multiply by the denominator then divide by the numerator (a·b÷a) — is a compound procedure with no single dedicated automaton; measurement_division is the closest fit for the resulting quotient, and the two-step structure lives in the reasoning rather than in one operator. CORRECTION to first pass: Activity 10.3 task 2.a was extracted as divide(6,1/4) because the tape diagram's partition cells are labeled 1/4, but the expression printed on page 188 is 6 ÷ 3/4, with confirmed value 8 on page 189 (6÷1/4 would be 24). Operand corrected from 1/4 to 3/4. The "groups of" warm-up multiplications (12 × 1/b) map to coordinate_groups_items. Two Practice 2.d tasks (4÷2/10=20, 4÷8/10=5, page 193) omitted by the first pass are added and verified.

title: Using an Algorithm to Divide Fractions
student_facing_goal: Let's divide fractions using the rule we learned.
teacher_goals: Coordinate (orally) different strategies for dividing by a fraction.
Find the quotient of two fractions, and explain (orally, in writing, and using other representations) the solution method.
Generalize a process for dividing a number n by a fraction a/b, and justify (orally) why it can be expressed as n · b/a.
purpose: In this lesson, students complete the process of developing a general algorithm for dividing a fraction by a fraction. First, students calculate quotients using the steps they observed previously when dividing a number by a fraction—multiply the number by the denominator of the fraction and divide by the numerator. They compare the results to quotients found by reasoning with a tape diagram and verify that the two methods produce the same quotient. They sum up the steps as an algorithm: To divide a number n by a/b, we multiply n by b/a. Students learn that b/a is the reciprocal of a/b because the two numbers multiply to 1. With an additional strategy at their disposal, students practice calculating quotients involving a wider variety of numbers (whole numbers and fractions). They begin to see the flexibility and efficiency of the algorithm, especially when it is impractical to reason about the division in terms of equal-size groups, with or without using diagrams.

task_events.excerpt: Find the value of each product mentally. 1/8 · 8
Find the value of each product mentally. 1/8 · 8/3
Find the value of each product mentally. 9/8 · 4/3
Find the value of each product mentally. 1 1/8 · 4/9
Partner A: Find the value of each expression by completing the diagram. 3/4 ÷ 1/8. How many 1/8s are in 3/4?
9/10 ÷ 3/5. How many 3/5s are in 9/10?
Calculate at least four quotients. Show your reasoning. 8/9 ÷ 4
Calculate at least four quotients. Show your reasoning. 9/12 ÷ 6/12
Calculate at least four quotients. Show your reasoning. 3 1/3 ÷ 2/9
Calculate at least four quotients. Show your reasoning. 9/2 ÷ 3/8
Calculate at least four quotients. Show your reasoning. 1 2/5 ÷ 3
Calculate at least four quotients. Show your reasoning. 6 1/4 ÷ 10/3

boundary_note: Every demanded operation has a fraction-category automaton; none falls outside the vocabulary. The warm-up demands fraction multiplication (area_model_part_of_part for the fraction-by-fraction products; unit_fraction_iteration for 1/8*8, which reconstructs a whole from eight unit fractions). Every quotient task is fraction division introduced as measurement division ("how many b's are in a"), carried by measurement_division. The lesson's own arc develops the reciprocal/product-rule algorithm (divide by a/b = multiply by b, divide by a), which the vocabulary carries SEPARATELY as unit_fraction_denominator_product_rule; tasks are tagged by their measurement-division framing rather than by whichever computational method a student happens to pick, since the lesson lets students use either. Classification fix from the first pass: op is set to the "fraction" category so each op+kind is a real pair. The first pass paired op "multiplication"/"division" with fraction-category kinds (measurement_division, area_model_part_of_part, unit_fraction_denominator_product_rule), which are not valid pairs; and pairing the whole-number division kind measure_groups_of_size with these fractional operands would misclassify them. Number corrections applied and re-verified against the p.198/p.200/p.203/p.205 answer keys: warm-up item 2 was misread as 1/8*3/3=1/8 and is actually 1/8*8/3=1/3; the fabricated activity-1 task 9/10 divided by 1/5 = 9/2 was deleted (no such expression on p.199-200; the two real expressions are 3/4 div 1/8 = 6 and 9/10 div 3/5 = 3/2); activity-2 item 6 divisor was misread as 10 and is actually 10/3 (25/4 * 3/10 = 15/8; 25/4 div 10 would be 5/8, not the stated 15/8).

title: Grade 6 Unit 4 Practice Problems (problems 6-8)
purpose: Not provided on this page. The page is the tail of a practice-problems set (page 256), not a lesson body, so it carries no purpose statement.
student_facing_goal: Not provided on this page (practice-problems section).

task_events.excerpt: 5/6 · ? = 11⅔ and 11⅔ ÷ 5/6 = ? ; b. 14 gallons. Sample reasoning: 11⅔ ÷ 5/6 = 35/3 · 6/5, which equals 14.
There are 80 kids in a gym. 75% are wearing socks. How many are not wearing socks? [25% = 1/4 of 80 = 20]
The regular fare... is $75 per person. During a promotion, train riders can save $37.50 each. What percentage of the regular fare is the discount? [37.50 is half of 75 → 50%]
Noah wants to save $60. If he has saved $45 so far, what percentage of his goal has he saved? [3/4 of 60 is 45 → 75%]

boundary_note:
Source is a single page (256) from the Grade 6 Unit 4 practice-problems section that closes Lesson 12; it is not a lesson body. No lesson title, student-facing goal, teacher goals, purpose statement, or CCSS codes appear on the page, so those fields stay empty rather than guessed. All four numeric answers were confirmed against the printed solutions: P6 = 14 (35/3 · 6/5 = 210/15), P7 = 20 (1/4 of 80), P8a = 50% (37.50 ÷ 75), P8b = 75% (45 ÷ 60).

Corrections to the first pass: (1) P6 was marked kind NONE, but fraction division has automata; the page prints both the missing-factor equation 5/6·?=11⅔ and the division 11⅔÷5/6, so this is reversible_measurement_division under op fraction. The fraction operands (11⅔, 5/6) were dropped by the PDF text layer — visible only in the rendered math — so figure_bound is true. (2) P7 paired op "multiplication" with unit_fraction_partition, a fraction-category kind; op corrected to fraction. The demanded computation is 1/4 of 80 (25% not wearing socks). (3) P8a/P8b paired op "division" with recalled_result_scaling, a decimal-category kind — a mismatched pair. Both are inverse-percent problems ("what percentage is A of B"). This vocabulary has no automaton that finds the percent one quantity is of another: the forward direction (P7, percent-of-a-whole) maps to unit_fraction_partition, but the inverse has no dedicated kind. Nearest candidates were ecuadorian_decimal_long_division (A÷B as decimal) and ratio/scale_ratio_unit (scaling A:B to per-100), but the printed sample reasoning uses benchmark-fraction recognition ("half of 75", "3/4 of 60 is 45"), not either procedure, so kind is set to NONE with op ratio to name the domain honestly. Problems 7 and 8 are spiral review carried into Unit 4 (P7 from Unit 3 Lesson 12; P8 from Unit 3 Lesson 11); only P6 is native Unit 4 content. No task_events were deleted — all four are confirmed against the page.

title: Rectangles with Fractional Side Lengths
student_facing_goal: Let's explore rectangles that have fractional measurements.
teacher_goals: Calculate one side length of a rectangle given its area and the other side length.
Coordinate (orally) diagrams and equations that represent the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths.
Draw and label a diagram to justify the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths.
purpose: In this lesson, students solve problems about the side lengths and area of rectangles. The lesson begins with two optional activities that activate geometric ideas from grade 5. Students revisit different ways to find the area of a rectangle with fractional side lengths. They verify that the area can be found multiplying the side lengths regardless of whether they are or are not whole numbers. Next, students solve a contextual problem about finding the length of a rectangular tray whose area and width are known. They are also asked to find the number of rectangular tiles—which have fractional side lengths—that can cover the tray completely. Solving these problems prompts students to apply their understanding of division of fractions.

task_events.excerpt: How many squares with side lengths of 1/4 inch can fit in a square with side lengths of 1 inch?
What is the area of a square with side lengths of 1/4 inch? Explain or show your reasoning.
Show that a rectangle that is 3 1/2 inches by 2 1/4 inches has an area of 7 7/8 square inches.
Each of these multiplication expressions represents the area of a rectangle: 2 · 4
Each of these multiplication expressions represents the area of a rectangle: 2 1/2 · 4
Each of these multiplication expressions represents the area of a rectangle: 2 · 4 3/4
Use the diagram that matches 2 1/2 · 4 3/4 to show that the value of 2 1/2 · 4 3/4 is 11 7/8.
Find the length of the tray in inches. The tray has a width of 11 1/4 inches and an area of 50 5/8 square inches.
If the 3/4-inch side of the tiles are placed along the 4 1/2-inch side of the tray, 6 tiles are needed (4 1/2 ÷ 3/4 = 6).
20 tiles needed for the longer side (11 1/4 ÷ 9/16 = 20).
How many tiles would Noah need to cover the tray completely, without gaps or overlaps? (6 · 20 = 120)
Two rectangular picture frames have the same area of 45 square inches... Frame B has a length of 7 1/2 inches. Find the width that you predicted to be shorter.

boundary_note: Verification against the source (pages 228-239) overturned the first pass's central claim: the three flagged 'arithmetic discrepancies' were extraction misreads, not errors in the materials, so all three deformations were removed. (1) The 13.2 rectangle is 3 1/2 by 2 1/4 — not 2 1/2 — and 3 1/2 × 2 1/4 = 7 7/8 exactly. (2) The fourth 13.3 expression is 2 1/2 · 4 3/4 — not 4 1/4 — and equals 11 7/8 exactly; the third is 2 · 4 3/4 = 9 1/2 (first pass had 2 · 4 1/4). (3) The 13.4 tray is 11 1/4 wide with area 50 5/8 — not 11 1/2 and 50 1/8 — so 50 5/8 ÷ 11 1/4 = 4 1/2 exactly. The 13.4 tiles are 3/4 by 9/16 (first pass misread 3/4 as 1/4), and the first pass's tile-area multiply(0.25, 0.5625) task had no counterpart in the source (the tile area is never asked for), so it was replaced by the real number-of-tiles work: 4 1/2 ÷ 3/4 = 6, 11 1/4 ÷ 9/16 = 20, 6 · 20 = 120. Task 2 was corrected from a nonexistent 1/2-inch-square area prompt to the actual page-231 question about a 1/4-inch square (area 1/16). Every demanded operation maps to an automaton: rectangle area with fractional sides (rectangle_area_unit_iteration), counting unit squares that tile a region (area_unit_covering), missing side from area (rectangle_missing_side_from_area), and fraction-by-fraction measurement division (measurement_division). No operation falls outside the vocabulary; no NONE was needed. The provided PDF ends at page 239 (lesson synthesis, cool-down, summary); the practice-problem pages (240+) are not present, so the four practice task_events the first pass cited from pages 241-242 — including divide(11, 3.667), divide(17.5, 3.5), and the off-theme bookcase unit conversion divide(210, 12) — could not be confirmed against any page and were deleted. Standards (5.NF.B.4.b / 6.NS.A.1 / 6.G.A.2) and all header metadata verified correct on page 228.

title: Fractional Lengths in Triangles and Prisms
student_facing_goal: Let's explore area and volume when fractions are involved.
teacher_goals: Calculate the base or the height of a triangle given its area and the other measurement.
Determine the volume of a rectangular prism by counting how many 1/2-inch or 1/3-inch cubes it takes to build, and explain (orally and in writing) the solution method.
Generalize that the volume of a rectangular prism with fractional edge lengths can be found by multiplying the edge lengths.
purpose: This lesson transitions students from thinking about the area of two-dimensional figures with fractional linear measurements to reasoning about the volume of prisms with fractional edge lengths. Students begin by solving problems about triangles, finding area given base and height, and calculating an unknown base or height given the area and the other measurement. Next, students explore the volume of rectangular prisms with non-whole-number edge lengths. They find volume by packing prisms with unit-fraction cubes, quantifying the number of cubes, multiplying by the volume of one cube, and eventually multiplying the edge lengths directly (MP8).

task_events.excerpt: Find the area of Triangle A in square centimeters. Show your reasoning.

boundary_note: Two operation families. (1) Geometry maps cleanly: forward triangle area (1/2·b·h) -> triangle_area_half_base_height; cube/prism volume by multiplying edge lengths -> rectangular_prism_volume_layer_iteration; counting how many small cubes build a larger solid -> compare_solid_volume_by_cube_count. (2) Fraction division has no exact automaton for the triangle 'find the missing base/height from area' tasks (B, C, cool-down, practice 2) because the vocabulary has triangle_area_half_base_height only in the forward direction and no triangle-specific missing-side kind; these are coded measurement_division, which matches the printed 'how many groups of X in Y' student reasoning. The decontextualized symbolic exercises (practice 5a, 5b: 'find the value of each expression') ground no specific grounded strategy and are marked NONE. The contextual bucket problem (practice 6: 5/6 of the whole is 11 2/3, find the whole) is a genuine find-the-whole-from-a-part task and is coded solve_for_unit rather than NONE. Adversarial pass corrected operand misreads that would have poisoned the model: Triangle C area 34/5->54/5 with base 3 3/5=18/5 (not 7/2), so task divide(68/5,7/2) [=3.89] replaced by divide(108/5,18/5)=6; prism table row 5 height 1/2 / volume 4 -> height 3/2 / volume 12; cool-down base 3 2/10 -> 3 2/5=17/5 (task divide(51/10,17/10)=3 was already numerically right); practice 2 area 7 2/7->7 7/8=63/8 and base 5 1/2->5 1/4=21/4, so divide(51/7,5/2) [=2.91] replaced by divide(63/8,21/8)=3; practice 5a divisor 2/9->2/7 with answer 135/14->15/2; practice 5b dividend 17/30->17/20 with answer 68/30->17/5; practice 6 quantities 11 3/8->11 2/3=35/3 and 5/8->5/6 with answer 91/5->14; cool-down 1/3-cube volume 0.037->exact 1/27. Deleted one phantom event ('1/2 by 1 by 4 inch, volume 1', position practice_1 page 251): no such problem exists; page 251 is Lesson Synthesis, a display-and-discuss pair with no printed answer. Metadata fixes: goal 2 cube fraction 1/4->1/3 (the lesson uses 1/2-inch and 1/3-inch cubes; no 1/4-inch cube task appears); standard code 6.N.S.A.1->6.NS.A.1.

title: Volume of Prisms
student_facing_goal: Let's look at the volume of prisms that have fractional measurements.
teacher_goals: Calculate one edge length of a rectangular prism given its volume and the other two edge lengths.
Explain (orally, in writing, and using other representations) how to solve a problem involving the volume of a rectangular prism with fractional edge lengths.
Generalize that it takes more smaller cubes or fewer larger cubes to fill the same volume.
purpose: In this lesson, students reason about the volume of various rectangular prisms, solidifying their understanding that multiplying the edge lengths, or the area of the base and the height of a prism, gives its volume. Students also have an opportunity to reason in the opposite direction: to find an unknown edge length by dividing the volume by other two edge lengths or by the area of the base. Problems about prisms may involve three quantities—area of the base, height, and volume—or four quantities—length, width, height, and volume. To find an unknown length may involve calculating two quotients. For instance, to determine an unknown height may mean dividing the volume by the length and then by the width. It may also mean two different operations, for example, multiplying two known lengths first and then dividing the volume by that product. In tackling problems with increasing complexity, students must make sense of problems and persevere in solving them (MP1).

task_events.excerpt: How many cubes with an edge length of 1 inch fill this box?

boundary_note: Lesson does not demand operations without automata. Multiplication of whole and fractional edge lengths maps to rectangular_prism_volume_layer_iteration and rectangle_area_unit_iteration; counting fractional cubes along each edge maps to measurement_division; total volume computed as cube-count times unit-cube volume, and a fractional part of a known volume, map to recalled_result_scaling. The reverse-direction task shown in the Lesson Summary (finding an unknown edge length from a known volume, e.g. 400 1/2 / (11/2 * 6)) is an illustrative example with no computed answer, so it is not recorded as a task_event; the demanded operation is nonetheless covered by rectangular_prism_missing_dimension_from_volume in the vocabulary.

title: Solving Problems Involving Fractions
teacher_goals: Generate an equation to represent a situation involving fractions, and justify (orally) the operation chosen.
Use operations with fractions to solve problems in a variety of situations, and explain (orally and in writing) the reasoning.
purpose: In this lesson, students apply their understanding of all four operations to represent and solve problems involving fractions. The lesson is optional as it offers further practice on skills from this unit and reinforces concepts of fractions from earlier grades. In all activities, students are prompted to interpret situations or expressions, represent situations and questions mathematically, and estimate answers that would make sense in terms of the situations. Along the way, students practice reasoning abstractly and quantitatively (MP2). The last activity requires students to think about how different constraints limit possible answers to a question, prompting students to make sense of a problem and persevere in solving it (MP1).
student_facing_goal: Let's add, subtract, multiply, and divide fractions.

task_events.excerpt: There was 3/4 liter of water in Andre's water bottle. Andre drank 1/2 of the water. How many liters of water did he drink?
The distance from Han's house to his school is 3/4 kilometer. Han walked 1/2 kilometer. What fraction of the distance did Han walk?
Priya's goal was to collect 1/2 kilogram of cans to recycle. She collected 3/4 kilogram of cans. How many times her goal was the amount of cans she collected?
Mai's class volunteered to clean a park with an area of 1/2 square mile. Before they took a lunch break, the class had cleaned 3/4 of the park. How many square miles had they cleaned before lunch?
Lin's bottle holds 3 1/4 cups of water. She drank 1 cup of water. What fraction of the water in the bottle did she drink?
A box of pencils is 5 1/4 inches wide. Seven pencils, laid side by side, take up 2 5/8 inches of the width. How many inches of the width of the box is not taken up by the pencils?
All 7 pencils have the same width. How wide is each pencil?

boundary_note: Mixed-number / unlike-denominator fraction subtraction (the cool-down's 5 1/4 - 2 5/8, i.e. 21/4 - 21/8) has no dedicated automaton in the vocabulary: the fraction family's co_denominator_* entries are co-denominated ADDITION analogs, not subtraction, and the subtraction family holds only whole-number place-value strategies (borrow_across_zero_cascade, take_away_base_ones, etc.). That cool-down subtraction is therefore left kind=NONE. Note that fraction-times-fraction multiplication does NOT hit this boundary: area_model_part_of_part covers it, so the first pass's claim that fraction multiplication lacks a kind was incorrect. Coverage caveat (not a boundary): Activity 16.3 "Pairs of Problems" (A1-E2, ten fraction/mixed-number computations across all four operations, pp. 275-276) and Activity 16.4 "Making Ornaments" (batch-limiting division, p. 278) contain many more demanded operations that this event set does not capture; the source PDF ends at page 279, and the four practice problems the first pass cited to "page 280" were deleted because that page is absent from the file.

title: Fitting Boxes into Boxes
purpose: In this lesson, students use what they learned about volume and about operations with fractions to solve an optimization problem. Given several shipping boxes with fractional edge lengths, students determine the most efficient and economical way to pack and ship 270 necklaces, each placed in a smaller gift box.
student_facing_goal: Let's use what we learned about fractions to find shipping costs.
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally and using other representations) different ways jewelry boxes could be packed inside larger shipping boxes.
Determine which size shipping box is least expensive, and present (orally and in writing) a justification.
Make simplifying assumptions and determine what information is needed to solve a problem about shipping costs.

task_events.excerpt: record the number of boxes needed to ship all 270 necklaces
record the number of boxes needed to ship all 270 necklaces
record the number of boxes needed to ship all 270 necklaces
record the number of boxes needed to ship all 270 necklaces
Calculate the total cost of shipping for each box size
Calculate the total cost of shipping for each box size
Calculate the total cost of shipping for each box size
using shipping boxes in two different sizes

boundary_note: All eight task events are confirmed against the page 289 sample-response table; operands match the printed tables (270 necklaces throughout; capacities 20, 154, 151, 255 on pp.287 and 289; costs $6.80, $13.45, $18.75 on p.286). Two honest boundaries. (1) The four "shipping boxes needed" events are quotitive measurement division (measure_groups_of_size), but the recorded answers 14, 2, 2, 2 are ceilings of non-integer quotients — 270/20 = 13.5, 270/154 ~= 1.75, 270/151 ~= 1.79, 270/255 ~= 1.06 — so the division automaton yields the raw quotient while the whole-box count needs a contextual round-up the automaton does not itself perform. (2) The gift-box-fit counts 20, 154, 151, 255 are sample responses the lesson explicitly says vary by orientation; on p.287 they are assembled from products of dimensional counts (e.g., small box 3*3*2 = 18 plus 2 in the gap) and from implicit fractional measurement divisions of shipping-box edges by gift-box edges (e.g., 8 5/8 / 2 1/4 ~= 3.83 -> 3), which map to fraction/measurement_division. Because those packing counts are orientation-dependent and non-canonical, they are not emitted as task_events. The 18.75 + 6.80 combination cost was reclassified from addition/column_addition_with_carrying to decimal/decimal_addition_by_aligned_units: the operands are two-place decimals summed by aligned place value, not whole-number columns. Activity 3 (17.3) is optional in the lesson; all emitted arithmetic lives there, with calculator access (p.288), which is why the decimal computations are not hand-algorithm tasks.

title: Using Decimals in a Shopping Context
teacher_goals: Calculate sums and products of decimals in the context of money, and explain (orally and in writing) the calculation strategy.
Estimate sums, differences, products, and quotients of decimals in the context of money, and explain (orally) the estimation strategy.
student_facing_goal: Let's use what we know about decimals to make shopping decisions.
purpose: This lesson activates students' experience with the four operations in the context of planning a party and staying within a budget. It also offers insights into students' understanding of operations on base-ten numbers before new concepts are introduced.

task_events.excerpt: One pack of paper plates and one pack of napkins cost $5.10. ($3.25 + $1.85)
disposable table covers for $0.99 each ... Five table covers cost $4.95. (5 x $0.99)

estimated cost per person (fish): 28 divided by 8 = 3.50
example dessert: cupcakes, 8 needed, $2.99 per 6 (rounded to $3), 2 packs: 2 x 3 = 6
estimated cost per person (cupcakes): 6 divided by 8 = 0.75

computing the cost of 4 pounds of beef at $5.89 per pound (= $23.56)

boundary_note: Every exact computation demanded maps to a decimal or whole-number automaton in the vocabulary. The lesson's estimation moves are only partly covered: rounding a price then multiplying (e.g. $6.69 rounded to $7, then 4x7=28) or dividing is modeled here as fact retrieval or division on the already-rounded operands, which drops the round-then-estimate step itself. The vocabulary has round_then_adjust for addition only; there is no rounding-estimation automaton for multiplication or division. Practice problem 2 (40 divided by 7.50) is quotitive division whose contextual answer is the floor, 5 packs with the remainder dropped, captured by measure_groups_of_size (the raw quotient 5.33 the first pass recorded is not the lesson's stated answer). The practice-3 exact-cost products (2x5.50, 3x4.25) are shown in the guide as repeated addition of equal groups rather than the decimal digit-count rule, so decimal_multiplication_rule is a defensible but not unique fit. No explicit student-error or deformation scenarios appear in the teacher guide.

title: Using Diagrams to Represent Addition and Subtraction

purpose: This lesson is optional because it reviews place-value concepts from earlier grades. The reasoning here reinforces students' understanding of the relationship between base-ten units, preparing students to compute sums and differences of any decimals algorithmically.

title: Adding and Subtracting Decimals with Few Non-Zero Digits
student_facing_goal: Let's add and subtract decimals.
teacher_goals: Add or subtract decimals, and explain the reasoning (using words and other representations).
Explain (orally) that writing additional zeros or removing zeros after the last digit to the right of the decimal point in a number does not change its value.
Interpret diagrams that represent a larger base-ten unit being decomposed into 10 units of lower place value, such as 1 tenth as 10 hundredths.
purpose: In this lesson, students continue to use base-ten diagrams and vertical calculations to add and subtract decimals to thousandths. First, students reason about the zeros in a decimal and whether a statement such as "25 = 25.0" is true. Then, students have an opportunity to practice adding decimals in an optional activity. In the main activity, students recall that a base-ten unit can be decomposed into another unit that is 1/10 its size. For example, 1 tenth can be decomposed into 10 hundredths. They use this idea to subtract a larger value from a smaller value that is in the same base-ten place. Take 0.012 - 0.007 for instance. Decomposing the 1 hundredth in 0.012 as 10 thousandths makes it easier to subtract 7 thousandths. Students see that decimals can also be written in several equivalent ways. Because 0.4 can be viewed as 4 tenths, 40 hundredths, 400 thousandths, or 4,000 ten-thousandths, it can be written as 0.40, 0.400, 0.4000, and so on. The additional zeros at the end of the decimal do not change its value. Students use this idea to subtract a number with more decimal places from one with fewer decimal places (such as 2.5 - 1.028). These calculations encourage students to make use of the structure of base-ten numbers (MP7).

title: Adding and Subtracting Decimals with Many Non-Zero Digits
student_facing_goal: Let's practice adding and subtracting decimals.
teacher_goals: Choose a strategy for adding and subtracting decimals efficiently, especially decimals with multiple non-zero digits.
Interpret a description (in written language) of a real-world situation involving decimals, and write an addition or subtraction problem to represent it.
Use the standard algorithm to add or subtract decimals with multiple non-zero digits, and explain (orally) the solution method.
purpose: This lesson strengthens students' ability to add and subtract decimals, enabling them to work toward fluency. Students encounter longer decimals (beyond thousandths), find unknown addends, and work with decimals in context. They decide which operation (addition or subtraction) to perform and which strategy to use. The lesson reinforces that a decimal can be expressed in different but equivalent ways, and that writing additional zeros after the last digit to the right of the decimal point does not change its value. To solve these problems, students must lean on understanding of base-ten numbers and make use of structure (MP7), reasoning abstractly and quantitatively (MP2).

title: IM-G6-U5-L5: Using Fractions to Multiply Decimals
student_facing_goal: Let's look at products that are decimals.
teacher_goals: Identify and generate multiplication expressions that use whole numbers and decimals such as 0.1, 0.01, and 0.001 to represent the product of two decimals, and find its value.
Use decimal fractions to represent and justify (orally and in writing) how to find the product of two decimals.
purpose: The core lesson operation is decimal multiplication using fractional decomposition and place-value understanding. Students reason from unit fractions (1/10, 1/100, 1/1000) and their decimal equivalents (0.1, 0.01, 0.001), recognize that multiplying by these is equivalent to dividing by 10, 100, and 1,000, then express each decimal factor as a whole number times a power-of-ten fraction and apply the commutative and associative properties to compute products of two decimals.

boundary_note: All nineteen task_events were checked digit-by-digit against the printed pages (133-152); every operand pair and product is confirmed, so nothing was deleted. Corrections to the first pass: (1) figure_bound set to false throughout. Every product's factors are stated in the lesson text (e.g. '(2.4) . (1.3)', 'the 3.1 by 1.4 rectangle', the Practice 1 expressions 'a. 3 . (0.6)'); the area diagrams are pedagogical scaffolds, not the source of any operand, so nothing is operand-bound to a figure. (2) The four warm-up items are reclassified from multiplication/distribute_group_size_split to diagnostic/error_magnitude_estimate_comparison. The task is literally 'choose the best estimate,' carried out by rounding each factor, not by place-value partial products. Their recorded answers (14, 560, 16.6, 6.5) are the CHOSEN ESTIMATES, not exact products: 6.8*2.3 is 15.64, 166*0.09 is 14.94, 3.4*1.9 is 6.46. Flagged explicitly because feeding these estimates to an exact-product automaton would poison the model. (3) The four Practice 1 items are reclassified from distribute_group_size_split to multiplication/coordinate_groups_items: each is a single sub-rectangle area (length x width) inside the 3.4x2.6 decomposition (3.4=3+0.4, 2.6=2+0.6), not a full decompose-and-sum. Recorded answers are the products, which equal the sub-rectangle areas; the source's literal answers are the region letters (a->B, b->C, c->A, d->D), all consistent with those products. (4) Added the 24*13=312 example in activity 7.2 (p.137); it is a given/worked example that students analyze for partial products (12,60,40,200 and 72,240) rather than compute, but it is the anchor multiplication of that activity. The whole- and decimal-product tasks keep multiplication/distribute_group_size_split, which matches the lesson's actual method: decompose both factors by place value, form partial products (sub-rectangle areas), and sum them, i.e. (a+b)(c+d)=ac+ad+bc+bd. The decimal-family kind decimal_multiplication_rule was deliberately NOT chosen: this lesson builds the area/partial-products approach and its 'Building on Student Thinking' explicitly warns against the 'count the decimal places' rule, which IM defers to a later lesson. Not captured as task_events: the second extension question ('estimate how many lis in 1 mile', answer about 3.2) is division-by-estimation with an approximate answer, and Practice 5 is a spiral-review addition item carried over from Unit 5 Lesson 3; neither is a decimal-multiplication task. Standards verified against p.133 (Building On 4.NBT.B.5, 5.NBT.B.7; Addressing 6.NS.B.3; no Building Toward listed) and the per-activity standard headers (7.1 Building On 5.NBT.B.7; 7.2 Building On 4.NBT.B.5; 7.3/7.4/7.5 Addressing 6.NS.B.3).

boundary_note: Every computational demand in Lesson 8 is a multiplication and every one maps to a vocabulary automaton, so no NONE is needed. The lesson's core method is decimal_multiplication_rule (op decimal): multiply the corresponding whole numbers, then place the decimal point so the product has as many decimal places as the sum of the factors' decimal places. This carries the two activities, the cool-down, and practice problems 1, 3, and 4. Two related-but-distinct decimal moves also appear: recalled_result_scaling covers the warm-up mental-math items (recall 20*8=160, scale by 1/10) and practice problem 2, whose task is literally to use the whole-number product 3*65=195 and scale it by 0.0001. The whole-number sub-products the method requires (25*12, 165*7, 3*65) are multi-digit partial-products computations shown with the vertical algorithm, classified multiplication / distribute_group_size_split; the first pass mislabeled them multiplication_fact_retrieval, which is wrong since none is a basic fact. Correction to the first pass: it also paired op 'multiplication' with kind 'recalled_result_scaling', an invalid pair (recalled_result_scaling is a decimal automaton) now fixed to op 'decimal' throughout, and it referenced a 'Section C (Dividing Decimals)' that does not exist in this lesson. Out of scope for this record and excluded: the optional 'Are You Ready for More?' enrichment (a decimal add/subtract chain and a decimal-product chain) on p.160, and spiral-review practice problems 5-6, which are drawn from Unit 5 Lessons 3 and 4, not from this lesson.

title: Using Base-Ten Diagrams to Divide
student_facing_goal: Let's use base-ten diagrams to find quotients.
teacher_goals: Explain (orally, in writing, and using other representations) division of whole numbers and decimals in terms of equal-size groups and decomposition of a larger base-ten unit into 10 units of a lower place value.
Interpret and explain (orally) base-ten diagrams that represent the quotient of a whole-number or decimal dividend by a whole-number divisor.
purpose: This lesson is optional because it revisits grade 5 standards on division of multi-digit numbers. Students recall ways to divide two numbers using strategies based on place value, properties of operations, or the relationship between multiplication and division. Base-ten diagrams, the main representation used here, remind students that a base-ten unit can be decomposed into 10 of a smaller unit in order to divide. The insights from this lesson prepare students to reason more abstractly about division of decimals in upcoming lessons.

task_events.excerpt: Elena used base-ten diagrams to find 372 ÷ 3.
Mai used base-ten diagrams to calculate 62 ÷ 5.
Find the quotient of 511 ÷ 5. Show your reasoning.
Four students share a $271 prize from a science competition. How much does each student get if the prize is shared equally?
To find 53.8 ÷ 4 using diagrams, Elena began by representing 53.8.
To find 33 ÷ 4, Clare drew a diagram and thought about how to put the tens and ones into 4 equal-size groups.

boundary_note: All six retained tasks are partitive fair-share division: an amount is split into a stated number of equal-size groups using base-ten diagrams, decomposing a base-ten unit into 10 of the next-smaller unit whenever a place value cannot be shared evenly (yielding terminating decimal quotients for 62/5, 511/5, 271/4, 53.8/4, and 33/4). Every one fits fair_share_equal_groups, and no operation in the readable pages (170-181) exceeds the provided division automata. Two corrections of note. (1) The first pass also listed seven practice-section tasks cited to pages 183-184 (645/3, 1320/5, 132/5, 13.2/5, 195/3, 356/4, 17.52/4); those pages are NOT in this PDF, which ends at printed page 181 (Lesson Summary). Their operands and answers could not be checked against the source, so the events were removed rather than carried on trust — if the practice section is supplied, they can be re-verified (and the earlier partial_quotient_chunking / missing_factor_known_product_search kinds re-examined). (2) figure_bound was corrected to false on every retained event: each dividend and divisor appears in the running task text, not only in a base-ten diagram, so pdftotext would recover the operands. The base-ten diagrams are illustrations of a method, not the sole source of any operand in this lesson.

title: Using Partial Quotients
student_facing_goal: Let's divide whole numbers.
teacher_goals: Interpret division calculations that use partial quotients, and explain (orally and in writing) what each step in the division represents.
Use partial quotients to divide whole numbers that result in a whole-number quotient, and explain the reasoning (using words and other representations).
purpose: In this lesson, students continue to reason about place value and the base-ten structure in division. Instead of using base-ten representations, they use partial quotients. Students begin by recalling that division can be done in parts, by decomposing the dividend. For instance, we can calculate 1,216 ÷ 4 by calculating 1,000 ÷ 4, 200 ÷ 4, and 16 ÷ 4. These partial quotients can be recorded as a series of equations, as students may have seen in earlier grades. Next, students analyze a method of recording division that also involves partial quotients but is arranged vertically. Students make sense of the steps for subtracting parts of the dividend below the original number and the adding of partial quotients stacked above it. The vertical calculation foreshadows the long division algorithm. Finally, students use this method to divide multi-digit numbers. While the dividends, divisors, and quotients are whole numbers, the challenges here are in using an unfamiliar algorithm and in looking for efficient ways to decompose each dividend.

task_events.excerpt: How might Andre calculate 896 ÷ 4? Explain or show your reasoning.
Find the value of each quotient. Show your reasoning. Use vertical calculations at least once. 1. 1,332 ÷ 9
Find the value of each quotient. Show your reasoning. Use vertical calculations at least once. 2. 1,115 ÷ 5
Find the value of each quotient. Show your reasoning. Use vertical calculations at least once. 3. 432 ÷ 16

Here is an incomplete calculation of 534 ÷ 6. Write the missing numbers (marked with "?") that would make the calculation complete.
Use partial quotients to find 1,032 ÷ 43.

boundary_note: All eight computational task events are whole-number division demands, each best fit by partial_quotient_chunking within the division family; no demanded operation falls outside the controlled vocabulary. Two demonstration/analysis items (warm-up 657 ÷ 3 = 219 on pp.188-189, practice 2 comparing Andre's and Jada's 657 ÷ 3 on p.196) and one conceptual comparison (practice 3, 1,150 ÷ 46, which method is better, no computation demanded on p.196) were not extracted as compute events; that is a defensible scope choice, not a vocabulary gap. Practice 6 (polygon area) and practice 7 (micrometer comparison) are carried over from earlier Unit 5 lessons and are outside this lesson's division focus.

title: Using Long Division
student_facing_goal: Let's use long division.
teacher_goals: Interpret the long division method, and explain (orally) what each step in the division represents.
Use long division to divide whole numbers that result in a whole-number quotient, and multiply the quotient by the divisor to check the answer.
purpose: This lesson introduces students to long division. Students see that in long division the meaning of each digit is intimately tied to its place value, and that it is an efficient way to find quotients.

One task in the source has NO automaton and is intentionally not a task_event: practice problem 1 (page 209) presents 696 ÷ 12 as a place-value comprehension multiple-choice item — "In which decimal place should Kiran place the first digit of the quotient (5)?" (answer B, tens; underlying quotient 696 ÷ 12 = 58). Identifying the place value of a quotient digit is a comprehension probe the vocabulary does not model, distinct from computing a quotient. Corrections applied over the first pass: activity-1 pages 202→203; practice pages for 180·9 and 972÷9 209→210; practice positions renumbered to the real problem numbers (180·9 and 972÷9 are both problem 3; 465÷5, 924÷12, 1107÷3 are all problem 4); practice 4 flagged figure_bound because those operands appear only inside long-division bracket diagrams; and practice problem 2 (917 ÷ 7 = 131) added. All 13 original numeric answers verified by inverse multiplication and were already correct.

title: Dividing Numbers that Result in a Decimal
student_facing_goal: Let's find quotients that are not whole numbers.
teacher_goals: Interpret and explain (orally) a long-division calculation that results in a quotient that is not a whole number.
Use long division to divide whole numbers that result in a quotient with a decimal, and explain (orally) the solution method.
purpose: In this lesson, students learn to use long division to divide two whole numbers that result in a terminating decimal. Students begin by examining a worked example of 62 ÷ 5 calculated using long division. They recognize that the same steps can be used to divide a remainder in the ones place, and that it involves writing one or more zeros in the places to the right of the decimal point to continue the calculation. Students then practice using the algorithm to find quotients.

boundary_note: The lesson targets terminating-decimal quotients via long division; every graded task maps to long_division except the two mental warm-up items (81.2 ÷ 4 uses partial_quotient_chunking, textually confirmed by the synthesis note 'Highlight how partial quotients are used in finding 81.2 ÷ 4'; 1.2 ÷ 4 uses decimal recalled_result_scaling). The 'Are You Ready for More?' extension (10 ÷ 3) introduces a non-terminating repeating decimal for which the model has no automaton (kind NONE); the intended answer is 3.333… and Noah's stopping-at-'3 remainder 1' claim is recorded as a deformation. One source inconsistency: the cool-down task-statement glyph reads ambiguously (looks like 43.5 ÷ 5), but the worked long division on page 220 divides 43.5 by 3 to get 14.5 — a self-consistent calculation (3 × 14.5 = 43.5), so the operand is recorded as 3. Practice problems 5 (decimal subtraction with borrow-across-zero, from Unit 5 Lesson 4) and 6 (decimal-multiplication place-value reasoning, from Unit 5 Lesson 6) are spiral review from other lessons and are excluded from this lesson's task events. Every operand and quotient was checked against the printed worked solutions and verified by multiplication.

title: Dividing a Decimal by a Decimal
student_facing_goal: Let's divide a decimal by a decimal.
teacher_goals: Calculate the quotient of a whole-number or a decimal dividend and a decimal divisor, and explain the reasoning (orally and using other representations).
Generalize (orally and in writing) that multiplying both the dividend and the divisor by the same factor does not change the quotient.
Generate another division expression that has the same value as a given expression, and justify (orally) that they are equal.
purpose: In this lesson, students learn to find the quotient of two decimals. This work requires students to integrate their understanding of place value, division strategies, and the relationship between multiplication and division. Students begin by observing that the value of a quotient does not change when both the divisor and the dividend are multiplied by the same multiple of 10. For example, 8 ÷ 1 and 8,000 ÷ 1,000 both have a value of 8. Then, they write division expressions that have the same value as a given expression. Next, students apply this insight to divide a number by a decimal divisor. They see that they can multiply the divisor by a power of 10 to make it a whole number, which makes it easier to divide, but to maintain the original quotient, the dividend needs to be multiplied by the same power of 10. An optional activity is included to allow students to take a closer look: to compare the calculations for two equivalent division expressions (48.78 ÷ 9 and 4,878 ÷ 900) and to practice explaining why such expressions produce the same quotient. In the last activity, students apply their understanding about equivalent expressions to divide a decimal by another decimal.

task_events.excerpt: Calculate each quotient. Show your reasoning. If you get stuck, think about what equivalent division expression you could write. 3 ÷ 0.12
Calculate each quotient. Show your reasoning. 1.8 ÷ 0.004

boundary_note: Two corrections were made against the source. (1) op+kind fix: the first-pass paired op "division" with kind "ecuadorian_decimal_long_division", but that kind belongs to the "decimal" category in this vocabulary, so (division, ecuadorian_decimal_long_division) is not a real pair. All ten decimal-division events are re-tagged op "decimal"; the two whole-number spiral-review problems (1097÷5, 2106÷25) correctly keep op "division" with kind "long_division". (2) Deletion: the first-pass practice_4 pennies problem (p.236) was removed. The page states "5.1 kilograms" and "2.5 grams" and asks "About how many pennies," a multiple-choice item whose stated answer is C = 2,000 (an order-of-magnitude estimate). The extraction had fabricated the operand 5100 via an unstated kg→g conversion and reported the exact quotient 2040, which contradicts the page. It cannot be represented as a true (operands, division, exact answer) triple — the demanded operation is unit conversion plus magnitude estimation, which no single automaton in the vocabulary models. Two source tasks were correctly omitted by the first pass and remain out: the 13.1 warm-up equivalent-expression selection (no computed quotient) and practice problem 2 (Han's 31.59÷13, where 2.43 is given and the student only justifies place value rather than computing). Substantively, the lesson's conceptual core — multiplying dividend and divisor by the same power of 10 and justifying that this preserves the quotient — is the equivalent-expression setup that precedes the arithmetic; ecuadorian_decimal_long_division captures the final long-division execution but not that transformation or its justification. All twelve retained events were confirmed digit-for-digit against the printed Student Response answers.

title: Solving Problems Involving Decimals
student_facing_goal: Let's solve some problems using decimals.
teacher_goals: Interpret a description or a question (orally and in written language) of a real-world situation involving operations of decimals, and write expressions to represent it.
Use operations with decimals to solve problems in context, and explain (orally, in writing, and using other representations) the solution method.
purpose: In this lesson, students apply their knowledge of operations on decimals, equal-size groups, and rates to solve a variety of contextual problems.
boundary_note: Two demands sit outside the decimal automaton set and carry kind=NONE. The 14.1 warm-up asks for the best estimate of four decimal quotients (124.3÷2, 124.3÷24, 12.43÷80, 1.243÷1.6); no estimate-a-decimal-quotient automaton exists, so the recorded answer is the lesson's chosen estimate (60, 5, 0.15, 0.75), not the exact quotient — the task is selection among estimates, and the page states those estimates as the answers. Practice Problem 2 scales a decimal by a fraction, 6.42 × 50/59, as a multiple-choice estimation (exact ≈5.44, keyed answer B / 5.4 million km²); op=multiplication, kind=NONE. Every remaining event is standard decimal arithmetic and maps to decimal_addition_by_aligned_units, decimal_subtraction_by_aligned_units, decimal_multiplication_rule, or ecuadorian_decimal_long_division — the only decimal-division automaton, applied to all decimal-operand quotients including whole-number divisors (e.g. 82.26÷9, 23.77÷2). Note: none of the operands are figure-only; every value is restated in a task statement or solution prose that pdftotext would capture, so figure_bound is false throughout (the tennis-court measurements 23.77 and 10.97 both appear in the Student Response text on p.249).

title: Making and Measuring Boxes
student_facing_goal: Let's use what we know about decimals to make and measure boxes.
teacher_goals: Compare the measurements of different paper boxes multiplicatively, and determine, by estimation or calculation, how many times as great one set of measurements are as another.
Describe (orally) sources of measurement error, and justify an appropriate level of precision for reporting measurements or results of calculation.
Measure the decimal edge lengths of paper boxes and calculate their surface area.
purpose: This culminating lesson is optional. Students apply their understanding of decimals to record, calculate, and analyze measurements of two- and three-dimensional objects: open-top paper boxes constructed by folding square paper of different sizes. Before folding, students measure the side lengths of square pieces of paper and notice their relative sizes. Then, they make conjectures about how the length and area measurements would compare after the paper is folded into boxes. Next, students measure the edge lengths of the paper boxes and calculate the surface area of each box, applying their knowledge of operations on decimals. Finally, in an optional activity, students test their conjectures by making multiplicative comparisons—dividing the measurements of one box by those of another—and reflect on the accuracy of their predictions.

These operations draw on standard multiplication, addition, and division, but the specific geometric application (surface area from measured edge lengths) and the measurement comparison workflow lack explicit strategy representations.

title: Tape Diagrams and Equations
student_facing_goal: Let's recall how tape diagrams and equations can show relationships between amounts.
teacher_goals: Draw tape diagrams to represent equations of the forms x + p = q and px = q
Interpret (orally and in writing) tape diagrams that represent equations of the form p + x = q or px = q
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is to remind students how tape diagrams and equations can be used to represent operations. There are two roles that tape diagrams (or any diagrams) can play: helping to visualize a relationship and helping to solve a problem. The focus of this lesson is using tape diagrams to represent relationships between values in equations. Students both interpret tape diagrams and create their own. This prepares students to use both diagrams and equations to solve problems in later lessons.

task_events.excerpt: One represents 5 · 2 = 10. ... Label each diagram with the value that represents the total.
The other represents 2 + 5 = 7. ... Label each diagram with the value that represents the total.

boundary_note: This lesson builds fluency in translating between symbolic equations and visual tape diagrams for both addition and multiplication. The central challenge: students must accurately identify which diagram component (individual parts or total) corresponds to each quantity in an equation, and recognize that equivalent relationships can be expressed in multiple ways (e.g., 5 × 2 = 10 and 2 × 5 = 10 represent the same total but diagram differently depending on how groups are arranged).

title: Truth and Equations (IM-G6-U6-L2)
student_facing_goal: Let's see what it means to find a solution for an equation with a variable.
teacher_goals: Comprehend the word 'variable' to refer to a letter standing in for a number and recognize that a coefficient next to a variable indicates multiplication in spoken and written language
Generate values that make an equation true or false and justify orally and in writing whether they are solutions to the equation
Use substitution to determine whether a given number makes an equation true
purpose: Students extend their understanding of equation truth-value from numerical equations (familiar since grade 1) to algebraic equations containing variables. They learn that a variable represents an unknown value, that an equation with a variable is true for some values and false for others, and that a value making an equation true is called a solution. The lesson introduces coefficient notation (e.g., 3x means 3 × x) and students practice using substitution to verify solutions.

task_events.excerpt: If a is 3, b is 4, and c is 5, is the equation a + b = c true or false?
If x is 3, y is 4, and z is 12, is the equation x · y = z true or false?

task_events.task: Find solution to subtraction equation from list
Find solution to multiplication equation from list
Find solution to multiplication equation with decimals from list
Find solution to fraction multiplication equation from list
Find solution to decimal subtraction equation from list
Verify solution to fraction division equation using substitution
task_events.excerpt: Explain how you know that 88 is a solution to the equation (1/8)x = 11

boundary_note: This lesson is about transitioning from evaluating the truth of numerical equations to understanding that equations with variables have truth-values that depend on substituted values. The trickiest misconception: students often conflate an equation being "true" with being "solved" or think equations with false statements are somehow invalid. They also struggle to distinguish between a variable (a placeholder for any value) and a solution (the specific value or values that make the equation true). No specialized arithmetic strategies beyond substitution are required; the cognitive work is entirely about equation structure and logical reasoning.

title: Staying in Balance
teacher_goals: I can compare the process of removing or grouping weights to keep a hanger diagram balanced and the process of subtracting or dividing numbers to solve an equation.
I can explain what a balanced hanger diagram and a true equation have in common.
I can write equations that could represent the weights on a balanced hanger diagram.
purpose: The goal of this lesson is for students to understand why we can generally approach solving p + x = q by subtracting the same value from each side and why we can generally approach solving px = q by dividing each side by the same value. This understanding is accomplished by considering what can be done to a hanger to keep it balanced. Previously, students solved a variable equation by thinking about values for the variable that made the equation true. Here, they use the image of a balanced hanger to reason about things they could "do" to the equation to make the solution easier to see while still keeping both sides of the equation equal (balanced). In the next lesson, students will apply this strategy to solve equations algebraically.
student_facing_goal: Let's use balanced hangers to help us solve equations.

task_events.position: Activity 3.2, Match Equations and Hangers, parts 1–2
task_events.excerpt: Match each hanger diagram to an equation. Complete the equation by writing x, y, or w. 3 · ☐ = 6. Find a solution to each equation.
Diagram A: circle x and box 3 balanced against box 8. Write an equation to represent this relationship. Explain how to find the value of the variable.
Diagram C: four triangles balanced against 10. Write an equation to represent this relationship. Explain how to reason with the diagram to find the value of the variable.
Here is a balanced hanger diagram. Each circle is labeled w. Write an equation that represents the diagram. Find the weight of one circle. Explain your reasoning.

boundary_note: This lesson grounds equation-solving in the balanced-hanger model: doing the same operation to both sides keeps an equation true. Students struggle most with applying operations asymmetrically (only one side) and understanding why dividing the hanger's total relates to finding the unknown's value, especially when answers are non-whole numbers.

title: Practice Solving Equations
student_facing_goal: Let's solve equations by doing the same to each side.
teacher_goals: Explain and critique (orally and in writing) strategies for solving equations.
Solve equations of the form x + p = q or px = q and explain (in writing) the solution method.
purpose: In this lesson, students consolidate their equation solving skills. In the first activity, they use partially solved equations to identify solutions. In the second activity, students match equations to their solutions found algebraically. Students then study groups of solved equations and generalize about a solution method by noticing regularity through repeated reasoning (MP8). In the last activity, students solve a variety of equations that look different but still share the same two basic structures: x + p = q or px = q. Students may choose any strategy to solve the equations, including drawing diagrams to reason about unknown quantities, looking at the structure of the equation, or doing the same thing to each side of the equation. Students choose strategies for specific problems. This will help students develop flexibility and fluency in writing and solving equations.

task_events.position: Activity 4.1 (Math Talk: Dividing by Fifths), item 1
Activity 4.1 (Math Talk: Dividing by Fifths), item 3
Activity 4.3 (Row Game: Solving Equations Practice), Row 1
Activity 4.3 (Row Game: Solving Equations Practice), Row 2
Activity 4.3 (Row Game: Solving Equations Practice), Row 7

boundary_note: Students practice solving equations of the form x + p = q and px = q by performing the same operation on both sides. Common errors include applying the inverse operation incorrectly (especially with fractions and decimals) and failing to verify solutions by substitution back into the original equation.

title: Represent Situations with Equations
student_facing_goal: Let's use equations to represent and solve real-world problems.
teacher_goals: Interpret and coordinate sentences and equations that represent a situation.
State explicitly (orally and in writing) what the chosen variable represents in an equation.
Use equations in the form x + p = q or px = q to solve real-world problems and explain (orally) the solution method.
purpose: In this lesson, students apply what they have learned about variables and equations to represent and solve real-world problems. Students must decontextualize the situation and represent it symbolically, and then interpret the results of any calculation in context.

task_events.excerpt: Jada finishes a 200-page book by reading 25 pages a day. Solve 25n = 200.

task_events.task: Solve addition equation for miles before Friday
task_events.excerpt: Elena biked 5 miles Friday and 20 miles total for the week. Solve x + 5 = 20.

task_events.task: Solve multiplication equation for amount per item
task_events.excerpt: Jada divides 5 cups of food equally among 20 cats. Solve 20x = 5.

task_events.task: Solve subtraction equation for score difference
task_events.excerpt: Kiran scores 223 fewer points than Mai who scores 409. Solve 409 - 223 = z.

task_events.task: Solve addition equation with mixed numbers
task_events.excerpt: New cable is 6 feet, which is 2 5/6 feet longer than old. Solve x + 2 5/6 = 6.

task_events.task: Solve division equation for number of groups
task_events.excerpt: Noah cuts 12-inch tape into 1.5-inch pieces. Solve 1.5n = 12.

boundary_note: This lesson challenges students to recognize that different situations embody different structures—additive (parts to whole), multiplicative (scaling), comparative (differences)—and match those structures to equation forms rather than relying on keyword strategies alone. Common pitfall: students select equations based on surface language ('times,' 'altogether') without understanding the actual quantitative relationship, or they solve correctly but fail to interpret the solution in context with appropriate units and meaning.

title: Percentages and Equations
student_facing_goal: Let's use equations to find percentages.
teacher_goals: Use equations to solve problems involving percentages and explain (orally) the solution method.
Write equations of the form px = q or equivalent to represent situations where the amount that corresponds to 100% is unknown.
purpose: In this lesson, students apply what they have learned about solving equations of the form px = q to situations that involve percentages. Students learned about what percentages are and how to solve percentage problems in an earlier unit. However, they did not generalize the reasoning process for finding the value that corresponds to 100% when a percentage and its value is known, or finding B in "A% of B is C" given A and C. Students have an opportunity to generalize that process in this lesson.

task_events.excerpt: 40% of a number n is 58. What is that number? Equation: 0.4n = 58
60% of c is 43.2. What is the value of c? Equation: 0.6c = 43.2
38% of e is 190. What is the value of e? Equation: 0.38e = 190
If 125% of the recommended time is 75 minutes, then 25% or one-fifth of that time is 75÷5=15, and 100% is 15·4 = 60.
If 88% of the recommended time is 132 minutes, then 88/100·m=132 or 0.88m=132. Dividing 132 by 0.88 gives 150.
Noah raised $54 to support the animal shelter, which is 60% of his fundraising goal. What is Noah's fundraising goal? Equation: 0.6x=54, solution: x=90

boundary_note: The central challenge is the inverse percentage problem: students can usually find "what is 40% of 200?" but struggle with "if 40% of something is 80, what is it?" This lesson demands they recognize the equation form p/100·x=q and solve by division or equivalent reasoning. Students frequently reverse operations, forget to account for the percentage as a decimal/fraction, or misidentify which quantity is the 100% reference.

title: Write Expressions with Variables
student_facing_goal: Let's use expressions with variables to describe situations.
teacher_goals: Explain (orally) how to write an expression that represents a situation with an unknown amount.
Write an expression with a variable to generalize the relationship between quantities in a situation.
purpose: Students write expressions with one variable to represent situations in which a calculation can be carried out repeatedly with different numbers. Students build on prior work writing expressions to represent operations and relationships. They practice interpreting situations, writing expressions with a variable, explaining how parts of the expression match the situation, and completing tables with both numerical and algebraic expressions.

task_events.excerpt: Another esport player scored p fewer points than Priya. Write an expression that can be used to find their score.
How many stickers did Noah sell if he collected $127.50? (Solve 0.5s = 127.50)
Clare is 12 years old. She said this equation, x + 5 = 12, can be used to find how old her cousin is. Do you agree?
Diego has 27 comic books. How many comic books does Han have? Write an equation that represents the question and use it to find the answer.
Two-fifths of the vegetables in Priya's garden are tomatoes. If Priya's garden has 6 tomatoes, write an equation to find total vegetables.
A school paid $31.25 for each calculator. The school spent $500 on calculators. How many did the school buy? (Solve 31.25c = 500)

boundary_note: Students often write expressions correctly but fail to verify them with specific values, or they miss the directionality of relationships — for instance, writing p for "p fewer than 473" instead of "473 - p." This lesson develops the critical habit of checking expressions against the problem context by substituting known values and confirming the answer makes sense.

title: Equal and Equivalent
teacher_goals: Explain (in writing) that some pairs of expressions are equal for one value of their variable but not for other values.
Justify (using words or other representations) whether two expressions are "equivalent," or equal to each other for every value of their variable.
purpose: In this lesson, students are introduced to equivalent expressions—expressions that always have the same value. Students begin by using tape diagrams to justify whether two numerical expressions are equal. They then use tape diagrams to represent two expressions with variables when given a value for the variable. Through repeated reasoning, they distinguish between equivalent expressions (which have the same value for all values of the variable) and expressions that may be equal for some but not all values of a variable. Students identify simple equivalent expressions using structure and properties of operations.

task_events.excerpt: Diagrams show a length of 3 for x + 2 and 3 for 3x.
Draw tape diagrams that represent x + 2 and 3x when x is 3.
Do 4x and 15 + x have the same value when x is 5?

task_events.answer: x=1: 4 and 2; x=2: 5 and 4; x=3: 6 and 6; x=4: 7 and 8
task_events.excerpt: Draw tape diagrams that represent x + 3 and 2x when x is 1, 2, 3, and 4.

task_events.answer: b=1: both equal 3; b=2: both equal 6; b=3: both equal 9
task_events.excerpt: Show that 2b + b and 3b have the same value when b is 1, 2, and 3.

boundary_note: This lesson asks students to distinguish between expressions that are equal at one or more specific values (e.g., x + 2 and 3x both equal 3 when x = 1) and expressions that are equivalent (equal for all values of the variable, e.g., x + 3 and 3 + x). Students often confuse these categories—they may think that because two expressions match for one test value, they must be equivalent. The core insight is that equivalence requires testing the full logical structure (using properties of operations) rather than spot-checking one value.

title: Comparing Triangle Areas (Practice Problem from Unit 1, Lesson 9)
purpose: Not visible in excerpt (practice problems page only)

task_events.answer: Triangle Q has a greater area: 48.75 square inches vs. Triangle P: 48 square inches
task_events.excerpt: Triangle P has a base of 12 inches and a corresponding height of 8 inches. Triangle Q has a base of 15 inches and a corresponding height of 6.5 inches. Which triangle has a greater area?

boundary_note: This problem requires students to apply the triangle area formula A = ½ × base × height to two triangles with different dimensions and compare results. Students often misconceive that a taller height always yields a larger area, or conflate base and height. The problem's value lies in revealing that area depends multiplicatively on both dimensions; a smaller base paired with a moderate height can rival a larger base with a smaller height.

title: The Distributive Property, Part 2
boundary_note: The PDF file provided contains only page 120 (the closing/back page with "Responding to Student Thinking" guidance). The complete lesson content—including student-facing goal, teacher learning goals, lesson purpose, CCSS standards, and task events—is not present in this file. Page 120 shows example answers (0.8a, 5(y + 2)) and a Press Pause note about distributive property mastery, but the full lesson structure required for a complete teacher digest is unavailable. The file appears to be a fragment; the complete lesson pages (typically 115–120 or similar) are needed.

title: Solving Linear Equations (Practice Problems)
purpose: Not found in provided PDF. This page contains practice problems for solving linear equations with inverse operations.
boundary_note: This practice page focuses on solving linear equations with inverse operations. Students often misunderstand the balance principle—applying operations to only one side of the equation. Fraction coefficients (1/8, division by 5/3) present heightened difficulty; students may confuse the inverse operation when division or fraction multiplication is involved.

title: Illustrative Mathematics Grade 6 Unit 6 Lesson 12 — INSUFFICIENT DATA
purpose: Lesson content not found in pages 68-69 (practice problem section only).
student_facing_goal: Not found in rendered pages.
boundary_note: Pages 68-69 contain practice problems sourced from Unit 3 Lesson 12 and Unit 5 Lesson 8, not Lesson 12 itself. Lesson structure (goals, purpose, standards) not visible in the provided PDF pages.

title: Grade 6 Unit 6 Lesson 13 (full lesson title not available—PDF contains only practice problems page 56)
student_facing_goal: Not available—PDF page 56 contains practice problems only; lesson narrative not included in provided file.
purpose: Not available—PDF page 56 contains practice problems only; lesson purpose not included in provided file.

x = 7; students draw a tape diagram with 4 groups labeled x and a total of 28.

boundary_note: Cannot determine from page 56 alone. The provided PDF contains only the practice problems section; the full lesson narrative, learning goals, and instructional context are not included in this file. To build a complete teacher digest, the full IM-G6-U6-L13 lesson document (pages 1–56) is needed.

title: Grade 6, Unit 6 — Practice Problems (sourced from Unit 3)
purpose: Practice application of percentage reasoning to concrete contextual quantities. (Note: Full lesson plan with Learning Goals and standards is not present in this PDF; this page contains two worked practice problems sourced from earlier lessons.)

task_events.excerpt: A water bottle contains 16.9 fluid ounces. Andre drinks 80% of the bottle. How many fluid ounces does Andre drink?
One cup of milk has 25% of the recommended daily allowance of calcium [1,200 mg]. How many milligrams of calcium are in 1 cup of milk?

boundary_note: This PDF does not contain a full lesson plan—only two practice problems from a practice-problem set. The mathematical work is percentage as multiplicative scaling: students must recognize that "80% of X" and "25% of Y" require multiplication by the decimal form of the percentage (0.80 and 0.25) to find the part. Common misunderstandings: confusing percentage as a quantity versus a multiplicative operator; computational errors in decimal multiplication; and failure to match units in the final answer.

title: Equivalent Exponential Expressions
teacher_goals: Describe (orally) the values that result from evaluating expressions in which a fraction is raised to a power.
Determine whether a given value is a solution to an equation that includes an exponent.
Evaluate expressions that have a variable, an exponent, and one other operation for a given value of the variable, carrying out the operations in the conventional order.
student_facing_goal: Let's investigate expressions with variables and exponents.
purpose: In this lesson, students encounter expressions and equations that involve variables and exponents. Students first evaluate expressions for given values of their variables. Then students are presented with equations that contain a variable and a list of possible solutions. To identify which value makes each equation true, students may use substitution, consider the structure of the equations, or apply their understanding of exponents and operations.

task_events.excerpt: Find the values of 3^x and (1/3)^x for each value of x. What patterns do you notice?
Find the value of each expression for the given value of x. [Examples: 3x^2 when x is 10; (3x)^2 when x is 4; x^2/4 when x is 4]
Find a solution to each equation in the list. (Numbers in the list may be a solution to more than one equation, and not all numbers in the list will be used.)

boundary_note: This lesson is fundamentally about evaluating exponential expressions with variables and learning when a value satisfies an exponential equation via substitution, not algebraic manipulation. The key student error is misapplying order of operations: multiplying the coefficient by the variable value first (e.g., computing 3·10 then squaring) rather than squaring the variable first. Students also struggle to internalize that fractions raised to increasing powers decrease in value, which contradicts their intuition from whole-number bases where values grow. Additionally, students must manage the distinction between equivalent forms like 3x^2 and (3x)^2, which evaluate to dramatically different results when x is a fraction.

title: Two Related Quantities, Part 1
student_facing_goal: Let's use tables, equations, and graphs to describe relationships.
teacher_goals: Comprehend the terms 'independent variable' and 'dependent variable' (in spoken and written language).
Create a table, graph, and equation with two variables to represent the relationship between quantities.
purpose: This lesson is the first of two that apply new understanding of algebraic expressions and equations to represent relationships between two quantities. Students use tables, graphs, and equations that represent these relationships and make connections across these representations. In the first activity, students analyze a relationship that can be defined by addition or subtraction. The context, about the difference of two measurements, is one students encountered earlier in the unit. Here, they write equations that relate two quantities and examine the graphs that can represent the relationship. In the second activity, students revise and extend their understanding of equivalent ratios through a paint-mixing scenario, writing equations that represent a multiplicative relationship between two quantities. Students learn that relationships between two quantities can be described by two different but related equations with one quantity—the dependent variable—affected by changes in the other quantity, the independent variable.

task_events.answer: When Han is 6, neighbor is 11; when 12, neighbor is 17; when 18, neighbor is 23; when 20, neighbor is 25; general rule: neighbor = h + 5
task_events.excerpt: Complete the table to show their ages.
What equations can we write to represent the relationship between Han's and his neighbor's ages?

task_events.answer: Graph A corresponds to n = h + 5 (adding 5 to horizontal gives vertical). Graph B corresponds to h = n − 5 (subtracting 5 from first coordinate gives second).
task_events.excerpt: Which graph represents the relationship between Han's age and his neighbor's age? How do you know?
Complete the table to show different combinations of red and yellow paint that will make the shade of orange.
Lin writes this equation to figure out the amount of yellow paint needed if red paint amount is known: y = 3/2 · r

task_events.answer: Graph B (displays points (1/2, 1), (1, 2), (3/2, 3) showing 1 soda to 1/2 water ratio)
task_events.excerpt: Which graph represents the relationship between cups of baking soda and cups of water?

task_events.answer: A and D: w = (1/2)b and b = 2w
task_events.excerpt: Select all equations that can represent the relationship between b (baking soda) and w (water).

boundary_note: The lesson establishes how the same relationship between two quantities can be expressed as two different but related equations, depending on which variable is treated as independent. Students often fail to recognize that equations like y = (3/2)r and r = (2/3)y represent the same multiplicative relationship viewed from different perspectives—this distinction between roles (independent vs. dependent) is the load-bearing concept.

title: Two Related Quantities, Part 2
student_facing_goal: Let's use equations and graphs to describe stories with constant speed.
teacher_goals: Create a table, graph, and equation to represent the relationship between distance and time for an object moving at a constant speed.
Identify (in writing) the independent and dependent variable in an equation.
Interpret (orally and in writing) an equation that represents the relationship between distance and time for an object moving at a constant speed.
purpose: In this lesson, students use tables, graphs, and equations to represent the relationships between time and distance, in the context of objects moving at a constant rate. They use their representations to compare rates and consider how each of the representations would change if the independent and dependent variables were switched.

task_events.answer: Wheelchair B is faster (2.6 m/s vs 2.5 m/s)
task_events.excerpt: Wheelchair A can travel 25 meters in 10 seconds. Wheelchair B can travel 13 meters in 5 seconds. Which wheelchair can travel at a faster rate?
How many seconds will it take each wheelchair, moving at a constant speed, to travel 195 meters?
Complete each table to show how far each team's robot traveled during the competition.

task_events.answer: Diego: d = 3t; Elena: d = 2.75t; Andre: t = (2/7)d or d = 3.5t
task_events.excerpt: Write an equation with variables to represent the relationship between distance and time for each robot

task_events.answer: 1/4 minute per meter (time for robot to travel 1 meter)
task_events.excerpt: What does the coefficient 1/4 tell us about the situation?
What point on the graph would represent the time it takes the robot to travel 7.5 meters?

boundary_note: This lesson hinges on students understanding that a linear distance-time relationship can be expressed multiple ways depending on which variable they treat as independent (input) vs. dependent (output). The most common pitfall is confusing which variable should be independent and writing equations backward; students also frequently overlook that the coefficient in an equation like d = 3t represents a concrete unit rate (3 meters per minute) that they can check against data and graphs.

title: More Relationships
teacher_goals: Coordinate (orally and in writing) graphs, tables, and equations that represent the same relationship
Create an equation and a graph to represent the relationship between two variables
Describe and interpret (orally and in writing) a graph that represents a nonlinear relationship between independent and dependent variables
purpose: This optional lesson offers additional opportunities to examine multiple representations (equations, graphs, and tables) of relationships between two quantities in situations involving constant area, constant volume, and doubling relationships. Students connect reasoning about geometric relationships to prior work with exponents, developing the ability to recognize and compare different types of relationships and their graphical representations.
student_facing_goal: Let's use graphs and equations to show relationships involving area, volume, and exponents.

task_events.excerpt: What is the width of the banner if the length is: a. 6 feet? b. 4 feet? c. 9 feet?
w = 36/ℓ, where w is the width and ℓ is the length
Complete the table with pairs of values that will make the volume 225 in³.
The equation n = 2^d matches the data. All the data values in the table make the equation true.
