If the pattern continues, how many mosquitoes will there be on day 6? 64 mosquitoes. Sample reasoning: 2^6 = 64
Write an equation that shows how the side length of the square, s, is related to the perimeter of the square, P

boundary_note: This lesson distinguishes relationships by type: constant-area and constant-volume situations produce inverse (hyperbolic) relationships where one variable decreases as the other increases, while exponential growth produces rapid, curved growth. Students often misread graphs—confusing which variable is independent—and struggle to interpret points in context (e.g., what does the point (2, 18) mean for a rectangle with area 36?). The lesson demands coordinating three representations simultaneously, which can overwhelm students who haven't internalized what makes each representation useful.

title: Tables, Equations, and Graphs, Oh My!
student_facing_goal: Let's explore some equations from real-world situations.
teacher_goals: Create a verbal description and a graph to represent the relationship shown in an equation and table.
Identify tables and equations that represent the same relationship and justify (orally) the match.
Interpret and critique (orally) different representations of the same relationship: table, equation, graph, and verbal description.
purpose: In this culminating lesson, students look at several examples of equations that represent important relationships from real-world situations. In the first activity, students briefly examine eight relationships, matching an equation and a table that represent the same relationship. In the following activities, each student works more closely with one of the relationships: interpreting the equation, continuing the table, and creating a graph. This gives students an opportunity to become an expert on one of these relationships and then use multiple representations to explain their understanding to others.

task_events.excerpt: The number of centimeters is the product of the number of inches and 2.54. When i = 5, c = 12.7.
Substitute 300 for c in the equation and solve for i: 2.54i = 300 and 300 ÷ 2.54 is approximately equal to 118.11.
Sample response for S - 2 = T: S = 5 (number of sides), T = 3 (number of triangles inside).
Use your equation to find the values that complete the last two rows. Sample reasoning: 60 - 2 = 58.
Use your equation to find the values that complete the last two rows. Sample reasoning: 300 + 2 = 302.
On graph paper, create a graph...with points at (5, 3), (20, 18), (36, 34), (60, 58), and (302, 300).

boundary_note: This lesson consolidates students' fluency with multiple representations of linear relationships—equations, tables, graphs, and verbal descriptions. Students frequently struggle to distinguish dependent from independent variables in equations and often need explicit guidance scaling graph axes on blank paper without pre-marked grids; the critical insight is recognizing how a single relationship appears across all four representations.

title: Positive and Negative Numbers
student_facing_goal: Let's explore how we represent temperatures and elevations.
teacher_goals: Comprehend the words "positive" and "negative" (in spoken and written language) and the symbol "-" (in written language). Say "negative" when reading numbers written with the "-" symbol.
Interpret positive and negative numbers that represent temperature or elevation, and understand the convention of what "below zero" typically means in each of these contexts.
Recognize that the number line can be extended to represent negative numbers.
purpose: In this lesson, students extend their understanding of the number system from just positive numbers, numbers greater than 0, to also include negative numbers, numbers less than 0. By exploring temperature and elevation, students see a need to describe numbers less than 0. In these two contexts, zero represents a physical situation (the freezing point of water and sea level), and numbers less than zero describe a physical state in the real world. Students reason quantitatively and abstractly as they represent temperatures and elevations with positive and negative numbers on a number line (MP2).

task_events.excerpt: At noon, the temperature was 5 degrees Celsius. By late afternoon, it had risen 6 degrees Celsius. What was the temperature late in the afternoon?
The temperature was 8 degrees Celsius at midnight. By dawn, it had dropped 12 degrees Celsius. What was the temperature at dawn?
A student discovered that adding half a cup of salt to a gallon of water lowers its freezing temperature by 7 degrees Celsius. What is the freezing temperature of the gallon of salt water?
Assuming that the pattern continues, what will the spider's elevation be after an hour has passed?

boundary_note: Correcting the first pass: it claimed signed arithmetic has "no automaton in the vocabulary," which is false. The vocabulary carries an integer category (signed_number_location_and_order, signed_addition_with_sign_relation, inequality_solution_set_representation). This lesson's on-target demand is number-line location, not signed computation: the activity narrative states outright "This task is not about operations with signed numbers, but rather why we extend our number system beyond positive numbers," and goal 3 / standard 6.NS.C.6 is "the number line can be extended to represent negative numbers." So tasks 1.b (8 dropped 12 -> -4) and 1.c (0 lowered 7 -> -7) map to signed_number_location_and_order (students find and place the result by traversing the vertical number line through zero), not NONE and not signed_addition_with_sign_relation (the sign-rule algorithm the unit reaches only later). Task 1.a stays in the positive regime and is earnable with an ordinary addition automaton (count_on_from_larger); that is the honest split -- 1.a needs no new machinery, 1.b/1.c require the number-line extension this lesson introduces. The genuine boundary is the "Are You Ready for More?" spider task: a 60-term alternating signed series (+1, -2, +3, -4, ..., +59, -60) summing to -30 by pairing, solved via pattern generalization (elevation after minute 2k = -k). No single automaton in the vocabulary produces a 60-term signed summation; signed_number_location_and_order locates one signed value and signed_addition_with_sign_relation combines one pair, but neither covers the iterated series, so kind is NONE. Every operand and answer above was confirmed against the student task statements (p.41, p.46) and the answer key (p.42, p.46).

title: Points on the Number Line
student_facing_goal: Let's plot positive and negative numbers on the number line.
teacher_goals: Comprehend that two numbers are called "opposites" when they are the same distance from zero, but on different sides of the number line.
Interpret a point on the number line that represents a positive or negative rational number.
Plot a point on a number line to represent a positive or negative rational number.
purpose: In this lesson, students examine and make use of the structure of the number line (MP7). Students begin by considering which values could be represented by a point on a horizontal number line by reasoning about position in relation to other known values on the number line. Then they revisit the context of temperature, represented by a vertical number line, extending previous work interpreting equally spaced divisions to the negative part of the number line. Next, students create folded number lines out of tracing paper to reinforce the symmetry of the number line and to reason about opposites—numbers that are the same distance from zero but on opposite sides of the number line. Lastly, students are introduced to the term rational number as a number that can be expressed as a positive or negative fraction or zero. Students will have more practice placing rational numbers on the number line in future lessons. The focus in this lesson is the concept of opposites rather than plotting different kinds of rational numbers.
boundary_note: This lesson contains no concrete arithmetic computation tasks. The lesson's activities focus entirely on conceptual understanding: reading values from number-line and thermometer diagrams, identifying/naming opposites, understanding the structure of the number line and its symmetry, and introducing the concept of rational numbers as representations. The lesson includes representation tasks (e.g., representing temperatures with positive/negative numbers) and identification tasks (e.g., naming the opposite of -5), but no tasks requiring arithmetic operations with concrete operands. The practice problems at the end (Problems 5, 6, 7 on pages 62-63) are from prior units (Unit 6 Lessons 4 and Unit 3 Lesson 3) and involve algebraic equation solving and unit conversion, which are outside this lesson's scope.

title: Grade 6 Unit 7 Practice Problems (Problem 7, from Unit 3 Lesson 3)
student_facing_goal: Determine whether a weight that doubles in pounds also doubles in kilograms, and explain your reasoning.
purpose: Not visible in this PDF excerpt (practice problems section only)
boundary_note: This PDF excerpt shows only a practice problem (page 83), not a full lesson guide. The problem is conceptual: it asks whether a proportional relationship (doubling) holds across unit systems without providing a concrete starting weight value to compute. The reasoning involves algebraic expressions (x pounds → 2x pounds; x ÷ 2.2 kilograms → 2(x ÷ 2.2) kilograms) but no specific operands. No task_events with concrete arithmetic are present. Standards, lesson purpose, teacher goals, and the formal "Let's..." student goal are not visible in this page excerpt. If IM-G6-U7-L3 is a multi-page lesson, this PDF may be incomplete or the scanned section may have been truncated to this practice-problem slice only.

title: Ordering Rational Numbers
student_facing_goal: Let's order rational numbers.
teacher_goals: Compare rational numbers without a context, and express (orally and in writing) the comparisons using the phrases "greater than," "less than," and "opposite."
Comprehend that all negative numbers are less than all positive numbers.
Order rational numbers from least to greatest, and explain (orally and through other representations) the reasoning.
purpose: In this lesson, students practice ordering rational numbers and using precise language to describe the relationships between numbers plotted on a number line (MP6). Students begin by using the symbol <, >, or = to compare pairs of numbers. Next, they work with a partner to put sets of cards in order from least to greatest. The cards include both positive and negative rational numbers. Students then compare the locations of points on a horizontal number line using phrases, such as \"greater than,\" \"less than,\" \"opposite,\" and \"negative.\"
boundary_note: This lesson contains no concrete arithmetic computation tasks. All student activities focus on ordering rational numbers from least to greatest, using inequality symbols (<, >, =) to compare pre-given numbers, and employing precise mathematical language (greater than, less than, opposite, negative) to describe relationships on number lines. There are no tasks requiring the student to compute sums, differences, products, or quotients. The lesson is a language-and-representation-focused activity that precedes or accompanies computational work in other lessons.

title: Using Negative Numbers to Make Sense of Contexts
student_facing_goal: Let's make sense of negative amounts of money.
teacher_goals: I can explain and use negative numbers in situations involving money.
I can interpret and use negative numbers in different contexts.
Interpret a table of signed numbers that represent how a quantity changed.
Recognize that signed numbers can be useful to represent changes in a quantity in opposite directions, e.g., money received and money paid, inventory bought and inventory sold, etc.
purpose: In this lesson, students are introduced to conventions for using signed numbers to represent money spent and received, as well as inventory gained and lost. Students examine a table detailing the inventory and value in dollars of items sold at a hardware store. They make sense of what positive and negative numbers mean in relation to the inventory (items purchased or sold) and also in relation to the items' value in dollars (MP2). An optional activity about the inventory in a vending machine offers additional practice interpreting signed numbers in context. Since students do not operate on signed numbers in this grade, this lesson is simply an introduction to the convention of using signed numbers to represent a change in money or a change in inventory. Students will study addition and subtraction of signed numbers in a future course.

task_events.excerpt: What is the distance between these points?
Find the value of 2³ · 3
Find the value of 4² / 2
Find the value of 3¹
Find the value of 6² ÷ 4
Find the value of 2³ – 2
Find the value of 10² + 5²

boundary_note: The lesson teaches interpretation and representation of signed numbers in money and inventory contexts; it does not operate on signed numbers (deferred to a later course). Its own practice problems (1-3) ask students to represent or interpret signed changes and carry no multi-operand arithmetic, so they yield no task_events. The only computational task_events are spiral review: problem 4 is from Unit 7 Lesson 2 (number line, opposites, distance from zero), where 4c computes the distance 5 between the opposite points 5/2 and -5/2; problem 5 is from Unit 6 Lesson 12 (evaluating expressions with exponents), whose six items 5a-5f are the only operand-bearing arithmetic in the lesson. The extraction hazard is typeset notation, not diagrams: the superscript exponents (2^3, 4^2, 6^2, 10^2, 5^2) and the stacked fraction 5/2 are the values a text-only pipeline would garble; all were confirmed from the rendered pages. Exponent items are modeled by their outermost operation with the power pre-evaluated (2^3 * 3 -> multiply(8,3)), except 3^1, a bare exponent modeled as exponent_as_repeated_factor. The lesson's activities (hardware-store and vending-machine tables, event-planner cool-down) are interpretation-only and demand no automaton.

title: Absolute Value of Numbers
student_facing_goal: Let's explore distances from zero more closely.
teacher_goals: Compare rational numbers and their absolute values, and explain (orally and in writing) the reasoning.
Comprehend the phrase "absolute value" and the symbol "|  |" to refer to a number's distance from zero on the number line.
Interpret rational numbers and their absolute values in the context of elevation or temperature.
purpose: In this lesson, students formalize the concept of absolute value as a number's distance from zero. Students begin by determining which of two numbers is closer to zero. Next, they consider a bug that is jumping left or right on a number line. They reason about how far the bug jumps versus how far the bug is from zero. Lastly, students use the concept and notation of absolute value to reason abstractly about the familiar contexts of temperature and elevation (MP2).

task_events.excerpt: For each pair of expressions, decide mentally which one has a value that is closer to 0. 0.01 or 0.001
If the bug starts at 1 and jumps 4 units to the right, where does it end up? How far away from 0 is this?
If the bug starts at 1 and jumps 4 units to the left, where does it end up? How far away from 0 is this?

deformation.excerpt: starts at 1 and jumps 4 units to the left

task_events.excerpt: What does |-7| mean, and what is its value?
What does |1.8| mean, and what is its value?
Which temperature is colder: -6°C or 3°C?

deformation.excerpt: Which temperature is colder: -6°C or 3°C?

boundary_note: All six numeric operands and answers were confirmed against the student task statements and student responses on pages 97-101. Corrections from the first pass: (1) the leftward bug jump (start at 1, jump 4 left, land at -3) is not whole-number subtraction — no subtraction automaton in the vocabulary yields a negative result — so it is reclassified to integer / signed_addition_with_sign_relation, i.e. 1 + (-4) = -3; (2) |1.8| has a decimal operand, so its op moved from integer to decimal; (3) both bug-jump tasks had figure_bound flipped to false because the operands (1, 4, direction) appear in the prose, not only in the number-line figure. Absolute value has no automaton in the controlled vocabulary: |-7| and |1.8| are kept with kind NONE as honest boundary cases, since the lesson introduces absolute value as a new concept rather than exercising an arithmetic strategy. One signed-ordering task from Activity 6.3 (which temperature is colder, -6C or 3C, answer -6C on page 101) was added because it demands a real automaton (signed_number_location_and_order) and the first pass omitted it. Deliberately not extracted: the three non-decimal warm-up pairs (9/11 vs 15/11; 1/5 vs 1/9; 1.25 vs 5/4 — the last two being unit-fraction and decimal-fraction-equivalence comparisons with no clean automaton), and the matching/classification items in Activities 6.3 and 6.4 plus the cool-down, which pair signed numbers, elevations, and absolute-value expressions to verbal descriptions and involve no arithmetic automaton. Concrete computation in this lesson is minimal; the pedagogical weight sits on distance-from-zero and signed comparison, not on arithmetic skill practice. CCSS codes on page 96 verified as transcribed: Building On 4.NF.A.2, 5.NBT.A, 6.NS.C.6.a; Addressing 6.NS.C.7, 6.NS.C.7.c, 6.NS.C.7.d; Building Toward 6.NS.C.7.c.

title: Comparing Numbers and Distance from Zero
teacher_goals: Critique comparisons (expressed using words or symbols) of rational numbers and their absolute values.
Generate values that meet given conditions for their relative positions and absolute values, and justify (using words and symbols) the comparisons.
Recognize that the value of -a can be positive or negative, depending on the value of a.
purpose: In this lesson, students distinguish order when working with rational numbers and their absolute values. Students begin by using a vertical number line to visualize the possible elevations of four characters in relation to a submarine. They use precise language when describing a character's elevation versus their distance from sea level. Next, students use the Information Gap structure to locate points on a number line. Students will need to request information about the absolute values, opposites, and relative locations of the points. An optional activity gives students additional practice comparing rational numbers by having partners take turns using numbers and symbols to write comparison statements and then using words to describe the same comparison.
student_facing_goal: Let's use absolute value and negative numbers to think about elevation.
boundary_note: This lesson contains no explicit arithmetic computation tasks. All student activities involve comparison, ordering, and interpretation of rational numbers and absolute values: positioning points on number lines based on contextual constraints, requesting and sharing information via the Information Gap routine, writing comparison statements in words and mathematical symbols, and evaluating the truth of inequality statements. The lesson assumes prior mastery of absolute-value evaluation and focuses on applying that knowledge to compare and order rational numbers with and without absolute values in situated (elevation) and abstract contexts. Any implicit arithmetic (e.g., recognizing |-8| = 8) is prerequisite knowledge, not a computation task within this lesson."

title: Writing and Graphing Inequalities
teacher_goals: Coordinate verbal, algebraic, and number line representations of inequalities.

Critique (orally and in writing) possible values given for a situation with a constraint, including determining whether the boundary value is included and making sense of situations with discrete quantities.

Interpret phrases that describe a quantity constrained by a maximum or minimum acceptable value, e.g., "at least," "at most," "up to," "more than," "less than," and write an inequality statement to represent the constraint.
purpose: In this lesson, students extend their work with inequality statements by considering comparisons with an unknown quantity. Students begin by examining an image of a person standing next to a basketball hoop and determine possible minimum and maximum heights of the hoop. They express possible heights as a variable in an inequality statement. Next, students are presented with two types of scenarios and must reason about the quantities involved (MP2). In one type, the variable represents a measurement, and the possible values can usually be any number within the range satisfied by the constraint. In the second type, where the variable represents a count of people or objects, the possible values are restricted to whole numbers within the range. Students also consider whether the constraint itself is included or excluded in the set of possible values, and they learn how to indicate this result on the number line representation with open or closed circles. Note that inequality symbols in grade 6 are limited to < and >. For situations where the constraint itself is included, students write 2 statements, such as x > 5 and x = 5.
student_facing_goal: Let's write inequalities.
boundary_note: This lesson focuses on representing and interpreting inequality constraints through verbal, algebraic, and graphical modalities. Students estimate bounds on a measurement, write inequality statements using < and > symbols, evaluate whether specific values satisfy inequality constraints (e.g., checking 487 > 500), and graph solution sets on number lines using open/closed circles to mark boundary inclusion. No arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) are performed; the demand is representational and comparative, not computational. The real mathematical work lies in coordinating multiple symbolic and visual registers to express and reason about constrained quantities.

title: Grade 6 Unit 7 Practice Problems

task_events.excerpt: Lin ran 29 meters in 10 seconds. She ran at a constant speed. How far did Lin run every second?
At this rate, how far can she run in 1 minute? [Solution: 174 meters, because (2.9) . 60 = 174]
Each box... sells for $30. Complete the table for values of m. [b=1]
Each box... sells for $30. Complete the table for values of m. [b=2]
Each box... sells for $30. Complete the table for values of m. [b=3]
Each box... sells for $30. Complete the table for values of m. [b=4]
Each box... sells for $30. Complete the table for values of m. [b=5]
Each box... sells for $30. Complete the table for values of m. [b=6]
Each box... sells for $30. Complete the table for values of m. [b=7]
Each box... sells for $30. Complete the table for values of m. [b=8]

boundary_note: Verified against page 174, the Grade 6 Unit 7 practice-problems page (spiral review), not the U7 L9 lesson guide. Problem 5 is drawn from Unit 2 Lesson 9 (constant-speed rate: 29 / 10 = 2.9, then 2.9 x 60 = 174); problem 6 from Unit 6 Lesson 16 (proportional table, m = 30b for b = 1..8, giving 30,60,90,120,150,180,210,240). All ten operands, operations, and answers were confirmed digit-for-digit against the printed task statements and solutions; no misreads found. Corrected figure_bound to false for the problem-6 table rows: the b-column values (1-8) and the m solution list are present in the text stream, so pdftotext would not miss them. Problem 6 parts b and c ask for symbolic equations (m = 30b; b = m/30) with independent/dependent variable identification; these are algebraic symbolic-expression construction with no clean numeric operands, so they are intentionally not encoded as numeric task_events. Standard lesson metadata (student-facing "Let's..." goal, teacher learning goals, purpose, and CCSS Building On / Addressing / Building Toward designations) is absent from this practice page and is left empty rather than guessed.

title: Interpreting Inequalities
student_facing_goal: Let's examine what inequalities can tell us.
teacher_goals: Critique (orally and in writing) possible values given for a situation with more than one constraint, including whether fractional or negative values are reasonable.
Interpret (orally and in writing) unbalanced hanger diagrams, and write inequality statements to represent relationships between the weights on an unbalanced hanger diagram.
Write and interpret inequality statements that include more than one variable.
purpose: In this lesson, students explore situations where some of the solutions to inequalities do not make sense in the context of the situation. They reason carefully about a situation's constraints when determining reasonable solutions (MP2). Students begin by addressing a common mistake made when the variable is written on the right side of an inequality, such as 15 < n, thinking that n must be less than 15. Next, students consider the context of points scored in a basketball game. While many numbers can be solutions to an inequality describing the number of points scored by a player, only some of those solutions make sense in context. Lastly, students look at inequalities by using the familiar context of hanger diagrams, contrasting the balanced hangers of equations with the unbalanced hangers of inequalities.
boundary_note: This lesson focuses on interpreting inequalities and reasoning about their solutions in context. It does not present concrete arithmetic computation problems. Instead, students engage in algebraic reasoning about inequality notation, relationships between quantities in hanger diagrams, and contextual constraints on variables. The mathematical demand is on understanding inequality symbols, comparing quantities, and logic—not on arithmetic operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division). No automata in the controlled vocabulary apply because the lesson makes no demand for concrete arithmetic computation.

title: Points in the Coordinate Plane
student_facing_goal: Let's explore the coordinate plane.
teacher_goals: Generalize the signs of coordinates that represent locations in each quadrant of the coordinate plane.
Plot a point given its coordinates, or identify the coordinates of a given point in the coordinate plane.
Recognize that in the coordinate plane, the horizontal and vertical axes include negative numbers.
purpose: In this lesson, students make use of structure when they extend the vertical and horizontal axes to include positive and negative numbers and create the coordinate plane (MP7). The crossing axes create quadrants, the four regions of the coordinate plane. Students begin the lesson by revisiting ordered pairs in order to describe the location of three points in the coordinate plane. They continue to practice identifying the coordinates of points as well as which quadrant a point is located in. Students gain additional experience by choosing, plotting, and writing the coordinates of points in order to hit targets drawn in the coordinate plane.

task_events.excerpt: Andre shot three arrows and they landed at (-5, 4), (-8, 7) and (1, 6). What is his total score?

boundary_note: This lesson focuses on coordinate geometry (plotting points, identifying quadrants, extending axes to include negative numbers) rather than arithmetic computation. The only concrete arithmetic task is the cool-down total-score sum, which is basic single-digit addition (6 + 8 + 0 = 14). The scored operands are figure_bound: they come from reading which archery-target ring each coordinate lands in (A:10, B:8, C:6, D:4, E:2, outside:0), confirmed by the page 170 student response. The lesson demands no operations outside the addition vocabulary. The extension task ("Are You Ready for More?", 10 and 21 lattice paths) is combinatorial path-counting with no automaton in this vocabulary and is not recorded as a task_event.

title: Constructing the Coordinate Plane
purpose: In this lesson, students explore the idea of scaling axes appropriately to accommodate data where coordinates are rational numbers. Students attend to precision as they plan where to place axes on a grid and how to scale them to represent data clearly (MP6). In an optional activity, students practice working with coordinates in all four quadrants as they navigate a maze on a coordinate grid. This lesson gives students the opportunity to develop fluency with plotting coordinates in all four quadrants and scaling axes to fit data that is essential for the context-driven work over the next few lessons.
student_facing_goal: Let's investigate different ways of creating a coordinate plane.
teacher_goals: Choose and label appropriate scales for the axes of the coordinate plane, according to the coordinates to be plotted, and explain (orally and in writing) the choice.
Compare and contrast different scales for the axes of the coordinate plane.
boundary_note: This lesson addresses coordinate geometry and axis scaling, not arithmetic operations. The lesson demands concrete tasks in coordinate plotting and axis-scale reasoning—activities like "plot these points," "choose appropriate scales," "identify coordinates from a figure," and "evaluate errors in coordinate placement"—but none of these are arithmetic computations (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division). The controlled vocabulary provided (12 operation families with specific automata) was designed for arithmetic and algebraic strategies. Coordinate geometry, graphing, and scale-reasoning are geometric/spatial reasoning tasks outside that operational vocabulary. No arithmetic tasks to record.

title: Interpreting Points in a Coordinate Plane
student_facing_goal: Let's examine what points in the coordinate plane can tell us.
teacher_goals: Compare points on a graph, including statements about relative position and the vertical distance between points.
Describe (using words and inequality symbols) and interpret the range of coordinates on a graph, including the meaning of y-values that are negative.
Identify and interpret points on a graph to answer questions about situations involving temperature or money.
purpose: In this lesson, students pay attention to choices for what the axes represent and the scale used on each axis. Graphs need to present information clearly and legibly to be useful for visualizing relationships between quantities. Students begin by matching points plotted in the coordinate plane with their coordinates. They interpret the meaning of certain points in relation to a given situation and use inequalities to express relationships. They also reason abstractly and quantitatively as they interpret vertical distance in a coordinate plane in context (MP2).

task_events.excerpt: On which day(s) did the smallest difference between the high and low temperatures occur? What was this difference in degrees Fahrenheit?
On which day(s) did the largest difference between the high and low temperatures occur? What was this difference in degrees Fahrenheit?
The high temperature on day 6 of a 10-day period is 30 degrees Celsius, and the low temperature on that same day is 12 degrees Celsius. Ask how much warmer the high is than the low.
In the town of New Haven, CT, the temperature at midnight was 1.2°C. Which town was warmer at midnight, Princeton or New Haven? How many degrees warmer was it?

boundary_note: This lesson centers on interpreting coordinate points in context (money and temperature); its own activities demand only subtraction, which the vocabulary covers via compare_by_matching_difference. Correction: the first-pass largest-difference event carried fabricated operands subtract(20,10) — no day on the page-194 graph shows a high of 20 with a low of 10. It is corrected to day 3's figure-read values subtract(21,11)=10, which the answer key confirms as one of days 3, 6, and 7 all yielding a 10-degree-Fahrenheit difference (day 6: 17-7; day 7: 7-(-3)). The cool-down comparison 1.2-(-3.5)=4.7 is a signed subtraction; the activity narrative notes grade-6 students are not expected to formally subtract negatives here, so it sits at the edge of the compare_by_matching_difference automaton. The account-balance activity (13.2) asks only for estimation and interpretation of plotted points and absolute value, not clean operand arithmetic, so it yields no task events. Practice problem 4 is spiral-review linear equation solving from Unit 6 Lesson 4 (which would map to balance_preserving_linear_solution), not arithmetic this lesson demands, and is excluded. No operation demanded by this lesson lacks an automaton in the vocabulary.

title: Distances in the Coordinate Plane
student_facing_goal: Let's explore distance on the coordinate plane.
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally and in writing) the coordinates for points in different locations on the coordinate plane.
Determine the vertical or horizontal distance between two points on the coordinate plane that share the same x- or y-coordinate.
Generalize (orally) about the coordinates of points that are reflected across the x- or y-axis.
purpose: In this lesson, students explore the relationship between points with opposite coordinates and develop strategies for finding vertical and horizontal distances in the coordinate plane. Students begin by plotting points in one of the four quadrants. By comparing the coordinates of points in the different quadrants, students generalize patterns about their signs (MP8). Next, students explore points where the x- or y-coordinates are opposites and see that they are also reflections across the x- or y-axis. Then students develop strategies for finding the distance between two points where the coordinates may not be integers.

task_events.excerpt: Find the distance between each pair of points. a. Points B and C
Find the distance between each pair of points. b. Points D and B
Find the distance between each pair of points. c. Points D and E
Each set of points are connected to form a line segment. What is the length of each? a. A = (3, 5) and B = (3, 6)
Each set of points are connected to form a line segment. What is the length of each? b. C = (-2, -3) and D = (-2, -6)
Each set of points are connected to form a line segment. What is the length of each? c. E = (-3, 1) and F = (-3, -1)

boundary_note: The lesson restricts distance to horizontal and vertical (axis-aligned) cases; general two-dimensional or diagonal distance is out of scope by design, so the cool-down's non-aligned pairs (answered "about 9.5" and "about 6.5" units) and the "Are You Ready for More?" circle question have no automaton in this vocabulary and are not extracted. The dedicated automaton axis_aligned_coordinate_distance (strategies/math/geometry_action_pairs.pl:758) carries an integer-coordinate guard and returns abs(DirectedDifference). It accepts the integer-coordinate practice tasks 2a-2c and computes each length as the absolute coordinate difference. That covers 2b (C=(-2,-3), D=(-2,-6)) with no signed subtraction: both endpoints lie below the x-axis, so the length is 6-3=3. The first-pass reading of 2b as an unmet signed-subtraction demand (kind NONE) was wrong; the lesson states students are not expected to add or subtract negatives fluently and teaches grid-counting and distance-to-axis strategies that avoid signed arithmetic. The same automaton does not accept Activity 14.3's decimal coordinates (points at x=3.5, y=-4.5, and a shared x=3.5 in task a), so those distances are captured only through the taught reduction to addition of axis-distances: integer addition for task a (2+3, both differing coordinates whole) and decimal addition for tasks b and c (2+4.5, 3.5+1.5). This is the honest domain edge for this lesson: the coordinate-distance automaton fits the integer practice items but not the decimal activity items, which fall back to arithmetic automata.

title: Shapes in the Coordinate Plane
student_facing_goal: Let's use the coordinate plane to solve problems and puzzles.
teacher_goals: Determine the total length of multiple horizontal and vertical segments in the coordinate plane that are connected end to end.
Draw a polygon in the coordinate plane given the coordinates for its vertices.
Explain (orally) that coordinates can be a useful way of describing geometric figures or modeling real-world locations.
purpose: In this lesson, students apply their understanding of rational coordinates and distance in the coordinate plane to construct polygons and navigate a maze. Students begin by constructing a polygon in the coordinate plane to meet given requirements. Next students plot coordinates in all four quadrants and connect points to create polygons. Lastly, students analyze the structure of a maze drawn in a coordinate plane to determine coordinates and distances (MP7).

task_events.excerpt: Counting grid squares as steps, Andre went 40 steps. Each step was 2 units, so the journey was a total of 80 units.
Plot the following points in the coordinate plane, and connect them in the order listed to create a polygon... Find the perimeter of the polygon.
The coordinates of a rectangle are (3,0), (3,-5), (-4,0) and (-4,-5). What is the length and width of this rectangle?
The coordinates of a rectangle are (3,0), (3,-5), (-4,0) and (-4,-5). What is the length and width of this rectangle?
What is the perimeter of the rectangle?
What is the area of the rectangle?
Draw a square with one vertex on the point (-3, 5) and a perimeter of 20 units.
Plot and connect the following points to form a polygon... Find the perimeter of the polygon.
Jada's cat weighs 3.45 kg. Andre's cat weighs 1.2 kg more than Jada's cat. How much does Andre's cat weigh?
Apples cost $1.60 per pound at the farmer's market. They cost 1.5 times as much at the grocery store. How much do the apples cost per pound at the grocery store?

boundary_note: Every arithmetic demand in Lesson 15 maps to an automaton: horizontal/vertical segment lengths as axis_aligned_coordinate_distance, perimeters as polygon or rectangle boundary accumulation, rectangle area as rectangle_area_unit_iteration, square-side-from-perimeter as fair_share_equal_groups division, and the two spiral-review decimals in Practice Problem 4 (labeled "from Unit 6, Lesson 5") as decimal add/multiply. One operation resists a clean binary task_event: the optional "Are You Ready for More?" extension on page 217 asks for the area of Polygon D (19.5 square units), which the solution obtains by partitioning an irregular hexagon into rectangles and right triangles (nearest automaton area_preserving_polygon_decomposition) or the shoelace formula — a multi-step computation rather than a single binary operation — so it is recorded here rather than as a task_event. The plotting-only work in the warm-up (15.1) and Plotting Polygons (15.2) produces constructions, not numeric answers, so it yields no arithmetic task_event. Pages 224-226 (Section D intro and Section D Checkpoint on common factors and multiples) are not part of Lesson 15 and their factor/multiple tasks are excluded.

title: Common Factors
student_facing_goal: Let's use factors to solve problems.
teacher_goals: Comprehend (orally and in writing) the terms "factor," "common factor," and "greatest common factor."
Explain (orally and in writing) how to determine the greatest common factor of two whole numbers less than 100.
List the factors of a number, and identify common factors for two numbers in a real-world situation.
purpose: In this lesson, students are introduced to common factors of two whole numbers as factors that both numbers share and the greatest common factor as the largest of these shared factors. Students reason abstractly and quantitatively as they see factors as representing different ways to divide a certain number of books evenly (MP2). They make use of structure when they notice how factor pairs help ensure that all possible combinations have been found (MP7). Common factors represent the ways to combine two different types of books in one box, and the greatest common factor represents the largest number of combination boxes possible. Students also relate the greatest common factor to the size of the largest square that can tile a rectangle with given dimensions without gaps or overlaps.

task_events.excerpt: What is the largest number of combination boxes that Diego can make with no left over books?
Find all of the factors of 21 and 6. Then, identify the greatest common factor of 21 and 6.
Find all of the factors of 28 and 12. Then, identify the greatest common factor of 28 and 12.
What is the side length of the largest square that Elena could use to completely cover the bulletin board without gaps or overlaps?
What is the greatest common factor of 24 and 64? Explain or show your reasoning.
What is the greatest common factor of 42 and 15?
What is the greatest common factor of 42 and 50?
What is the largest number of groups that could be formed?

boundary_note: The lesson's core demand—finding the greatest common factor (GCF) of two numbers—does not map to any automaton in the available vocabulary. GCF is a number theory operation distinct from arithmetic (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division) or division strategies. Students find GCF by enumerating factors through divisibility reasoning, then identifying the largest shared factor. This operation exceeds the scope of standard computational automata in the model."

title: Common Multiples
teacher_goals: Comprehend (orally and in writing) the terms "multiple," "common multiple," and "least common multiple."
Explain (orally and in writing) how to calculate the least common multiple of two whole numbers.
List the multiples of a number, and identify common multiples for two numbers in a real-world situation.
student_facing_goal: Let's use multiples to solve problems.
purpose: In this lesson, students are introduced to common multiples of two numbers as products that result from multiplying each of the two numbers by some whole number. They are also introduced to the least common multiples of two whole numbers as the smallest multiple shared by both numbers. Students reason abstractly when they use common multiples to solve problems in contextual situations (MP2).
boundary_note: This lesson's mathematical work (identifying multiples, finding common multiples, determining LCM) is conceptual and enumerative rather than computational. Students compare lists of multiples, recognize patterns, and discover the LCM concept. While students may implicitly compute (e.g., multiply to verify that 2 bunches of roses equals 24 flowers), no task explicitly asks for basic arithmetic computation on concrete operands with a named strategy (e.g., "compute 3 × 8"). The lesson's content domain—multiple enumeration and LCM discovery—does not align with the controlled vocabulary of computational task kinds (addition, subtraction, multiplication, division strategies on given numbers).

title: Using Common Multiples and Common Factors
teacher_goals: Choose to calculate the greatest common factor or least common multiple to solve a problem about a real-world situation, and justify (orally) the choice.
Present (orally, in writing, and using other representations) the solution method for a problem involving greatest common factor or least common multiple.
purpose: In this lesson, students apply what they have learned about factors and multiples to solve a variety of problems. Students make sense of problems by determining whether they involve finding factors or multiples before working with a partner to solve them (MP1).
student_facing_goal: Let's use common factors and common multiples to solve problems.

task_events.excerpt: Cups are sold in packs of 8, and plates are sold in packs of 6. ... Find the smallest number of plates and cups that meets her requirement.
The wall is a rectangle that measures 18 feet by 12 feet. What is the side length, in feet, of the largest possible tile she could use?
She puts a skateboard sticker on every 4th page, and a kite sticker on every 5th page. ... Find three page numbers that will get both stickers.
She has 75 small bandages and 90 large bandages. ... What is the largest number of kits the nurse can make?

boundary_note: The first-pass extraction fabricated both of its task_events: multiply(12,8) and multiply(4,24) were cited on pages 261 and 262, which do not exist in this document (the lesson runs 249-258), and their excerpts ("Find the product of 12 and 8", "Find the product of the greatest common factor and the least common multiple of 12 and 8") appear nowhere in the lesson. Both were deleted as unconfirmable. The lesson contains no direct whole-number product task; its work is finding greatest common factors and least common multiples in context. Those operations map to the multiplication-family automata common_factor_intersection and common_multiple_sequence, so the four required-activity (18.2) problems are recorded as task_events with operands read from the student task statement and results confirmed against the answer key on page 252. Not recorded: (1) the optional Activity 18.3 problems (Soccer, Performances, Lights, Banners, Dancers) — three of these compute GCF/LCM over three whole numbers, which the lesson flags as beyond the standard addressed here, and the pairwise-named automata do not cleanly cover three-operand cases; (2) the problem-classification table on page 252 (checkmarking finding-factors vs finding-multiples), which is a meta/sorting task with no arithmetic automaton; (3) the "Are You Ready for More?" star-drawing extension (pages 253-254), which turns on coprimality of the skip-number and the dot count and has no direct automaton.

title: Drawing in the Coordinate Plane
teacher_goals: Generate a list of ordered pairs to create an image in the coordinate plane, and explain (orally) the reasoning.
purpose: In this optional culminating lesson, students use graphing technology to plot ordered pairs and create images. Using graphing technology for this lesson is highly recommended over doing it with pencil and paper. In the first activity, students recreate a given image to help familiarize them with how the graphing technology works. The second activity, where students design their own image, could be lengthened or shortened as needed by instructing students to create an image with more or less detail. Using graphing technology gives students an opportunity to attend to precision (MP6) because the program graphs exactly what they enter and will not guess at what they might have meant if they input a point incorrectly.
boundary_note: This is a culminating coordinate-geometry lesson with no arithmetic computation tasks. Both activities (recreating a cat image from coordinates and designing an original image) require students to identify, list, or enter ordered pairs and plot them on a coordinate plane using graphing technology. The work is entirely representational/geometric—reading points off a graph or visualizing an image as a set of coordinates—rather than computational. The extension adds graphing inequalities (6.NS.C.8). The single applicable strategy from the geometry vocabulary is ordered_pair_coordinate_plot, but that marks representation, not arithmetic operation.
student_facing_goal: Let's draw on the coordinate plane.

title: Got Data?
teacher_goals: Ask survey questions (orally) and record responses (in writing); include units of measurement when reporting numerical data (orally and in writing).
Comprehend and use the terms 'numerical' and 'categorical' to describe data sets (orally and in writing).
Interpret various representations of data sets and determine whether it is reasonable that a verbal description represents a given numerical data set.
purpose: Students begin the unit by collecting data about themselves by measuring and answering survey questions, studying the different types of responses collected, and identifying the appropriate variables and units being measured. Students learn about categorical and numerical data then determine whether a particular survey question will produce one type of data or the other. They also use dot plots (similar to line plots in earlier grades) as a way to represent data and make sense of what data points mean in context.
student_facing_goal: Let's explore different kinds of data.
boundary_note: This lesson is a data-literacy entry point where the core intellectual work is conceptual rather than computational. Students must distinguish between numerical data (quantities subject to measurement and comparison) and categorical data (qualities sorted into categories), and hold this distinction constant across contexts. The pervasive misconception is that any number string is numerical data; students confuse zip codes, area codes, and month numbers with quantities. A secondary challenge: recognizing that the same data can be represented multiple ways (e.g., months as names versus as numerals) changes its type fundamentally.

title: Statistical Questions
student_facing_goal: Let's look more closely at data and the questions they can help us answer.
teacher_goals: Justify (orally) whether a question is "statistical" based on whether variability is expected in the data that could be collected.
Match survey questions to data sets representing possible responses and justify (in writing) why they match.
purpose: In this lesson, students continue to analyze questions and the kinds of responses they can expect from those questions. They begin to recognize variability in data and learn about statistical questions and how they differ from non-statistical questions. In order to define variability, students categorize data sets and name the categories to make use of structure by seeking mathematically important similarities between the objects (MP7).

task_events.answer: varies by student; plotted on class dot plot with scale 0-8 inches in quarter-inch intervals
task_events.position: Activity 2.1 Warm-up: Pencils on A Plot
task_events.excerpt: Measure your pencil to the nearest 1/4-inch. Then, plot your measurement on the class dot plot.

task_events.answer: longest pencil minus shortest pencil in class data; specific value varies by class measurements
task_events.excerpt: What is the difference between the longest and shortest pencil lengths in the class?

task_events.answer: most frequently occurring pencil length; may be no clear mode or multiple modes depending on distribution
task_events.excerpt: What is the most common pencil length?

task_events.answer: most common pencil length minus shortest pencil length; value varies based on class data
task_events.excerpt: Find the difference in lengths between the most common pencil length and the shortest pencil.

boundary_note: A statistical question requires data collection and expects variability in responses—where different answers are likely. Students often mistake 'needing analysis' for 'being statistical,' missing that variability (not all answers the same) is the defining feature that determines whether a question is statistical.

title: Representing Data Graphically
teacher_goals: Comprehend that the word frequency refers to the number of times a particular value occurs in a data set.
Create and interpret a dot plot to answer statistical questions about a numerical data set.
Justify (in writing) whether a dot plot is an appropriate way to display a given data set, paying attention to whether the data set is numerical or categorical.
student_facing_goal: Let's represent data with dot plots and bar graphs.
purpose: Students represent distributions of numerical data with dot plots after organizing them into frequency tables. Using graphical representations of distributions, they develop spatial understanding of distributions in preparation for understanding center and spread in future lessons. Students make use of the structure of dot plots to describe distributions and draw conclusions about the data.

task_events.position: Activity 3.3, Launch: cats with spots dot plot
task_events.excerpt: What is the frequency of cats with 2 spots?
This dot plot shows the ages of students in a swimming class. How many students are in the class?
How many students are represented in the dot plot?

boundary_note: Students frequently confuse when to use dot plots (numerical data on a number line) versus bar graphs (categorical data). Common errors include drawing dots of inconsistent sizes, failing to stack them vertically, or misplacing dots on the number line. Understanding that frequency is shown by stacking dots directly above a value—and that this spatial arrangement reveals the distribution's shape—is foundational to later work on center and spread.

title: IM-G6-U8-L4 Teacher Digest (UNABLE TO EXTRACT)
purpose: File provided (IM-G6-U8-L4.pdf, page 103) contains Grade 6 Unit 8 Practice Problems only — a collection of review problems from Unit 6 Lesson 4 and Unit 4 Lesson 11. The actual Grade 6 Unit 8 Lesson 4 content (lesson purpose, goals, and student task) is not present in this file.
boundary_note: The provided PDF does not contain the Grade 6 Unit 8 Lesson 4 lesson document. To extract a teacher digest, the actual lesson file (with title, purpose, learning goals, and standards alignment) is required. Verify the file path or provide the correct lesson document.

title: Using Dot Plots to Answer Statistical Questions
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally and in writing) dot plots that represent two different data sets measuring the same quantity, paying attention to the center and spread of each distribution.
Critique or justify (orally and in writing) claims about the center of a distribution represented on a dot plot.
purpose: In this lesson, students continue to use dot plots to develop their understanding of center and spread by informally identifying values to describe the center and spread, comparing centers and spreads of different distributions, and making use of the structure of the distributions (MP7) to understand them in the context of situations (MP2).
student_facing_goal: Let's use dot plots to describe distributions and answer questions.

task_events.excerpt: What percentage of the students reported not talking on the phone?
What percentage of the group reported talking on the phone for 3 hours?
How many hours would you say that these 25 students typically spend talking on the phone?
Overall, how fast would you say that the students type after completing the course? What would you consider the center of the end-of-course data?
What would be a good description for the center of the distribution of weights of Variety A tomatoes, in general?
What about for the weight of Variety B tomatoes?

boundary_note: This lesson bridges informal and formal understanding of distributions. Students move from descriptive language (typical, spread out) toward identifying center and spread visually in dot plots. The main challenge is that students often fixate on the highest stack of dots (the mode) rather than finding the true center of the overall distribution; they also frequently confuse spread with range rather than understanding spread as concentration or dispersal of points around center.

title: Interpreting Histograms
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally) dot plots and histograms in terms of how useful they are for answering different statistical questions.
Create a histogram to represent a data set.
Interpret a histogram to answer (in writing) statistical questions about a data set.
purpose: In this lesson, students are introduced to histograms. They learn that, like a dot plot, a histogram can be used to show the distribution of a numerical data set, but unlike a dot plot, a histogram shows the frequencies of groups of values, rather than individual values. Students analyze the structures of dot plots and histograms displaying the same data sets and determine what information is easier to understand from each type of display (MP7). Students read and interpret histograms in context (MP2) to prepare them to create a histogram.

task_events.excerpt: How many dogs weigh between 100 and a little less than 120 pounds?
How many dogs weigh at least 120 pounds? 9 dogs.
Here are the population data for all states and the District of Columbia from the 2010 census. Use the information to complete the table.

task_events.answer: histogram with 8 bars showing population frequencies
task_events.excerpt: Use the grid and the information in your table to create a histogram.
What would you consider a typical weight for a dog at this dog show? Sample response: Around 100 pounds.

student_facing_goal: Let's explore how histograms represent data sets.
boundary_note: Histograms group continuous data into intervals, showing overall distribution patterns while losing precision about individual values. Students often struggle to accept that you cannot determine exact values or specific frequencies from a histogram—only ranges and combined counts—a trade-off that is central to understanding when histograms are more useful than dot plots.

title: Using Histograms to Answer Statistical Questions
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (in writing) histograms that represent two different data sets measuring the same quantity.
Critique (orally) a description of a distribution, recognizing that there are multiple valid ways to describe its center and spread.
Describe (orally and in writing) the distribution shown on a histogram, including making claims about the center and spread.
purpose: In this lesson, students create, read, and interpret histograms. They characterize the distribution displayed in a histogram in terms of its shape and spread, and identify a measurement that is typical for the data set by looking for the center in a histogram. Students also use histograms to make comparisons and better understand what different spreads and values of center mean in a given context.
student_facing_goal: Let's draw histograms and use them to answer questions.

task_events.excerpt: Complete the table for the lengths of the 25 earthworms. Data: 6 11 18 19 20 23 23 25 25 26 27 27 28 29 32 33 41 42 48 52 54 59 60 77 93

task_events.answer: Histogram with bars of heights 4, 12, 6, 2, 1 over intervals 0–20, 20–40, 40–60, 60–80, 80–100mm
task_events.excerpt: Use the grid and the information in the table to draw a histogram for the worm length data. Be sure to label the axes of your histogram.
Based on the histogram, what value could be given as the center as a typical length for these 25 earthworms?

task_events.answer: Histogram A (centered ~80–81in) is basketball; Histogram B (centered ~72in) is baseball
task_events.excerpt: Decide which histogram shows heights of baseball players and which shows heights of basketball players. The center is located higher on the number line...which means they represent taller players.

task_events.answer: Both years: center ~15–20 points per game. 2008 spread narrower; 2016 spread wider.
task_events.excerpt: Describe the center of each distribution...The spread of the distribution for 2008 seems less than the spread for 2016.

boundary_note: This lesson shifts from arithmetic computation to visual pattern-reading and statistical reasoning. Histograms group measurement data into equal-width intervals to reveal distributions; students must count frequencies accurately, choose reasonable bin widths, and interpret shapes to estimate center and spread. Common pitfalls: confusing histograms with bar graphs (no gaps between bars), losing count while binning raw data, and treating the modal bin as an exact value. The deeper challenge is accepting that histograms trade individual precision for aggregate insight—you can describe the shape and approximate center of a distribution but cannot recover exact individual measurements.

title: Describing Distributions on Histograms
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally) bar graphs and histograms, recognizing that descriptions of shape, center, and spread don't pertain to bar graphs.
Describe (orally and in writing) the overall shape and features of a distribution represented on a histogram, including peaks, clusters, gaps, and symmetry.
Identify histograms that display distributions with specific features.
student_facing_goal: Let's describe distributions displayed in histograms.
purpose: In this lesson, students explore various shapes and features of a distribution displayed in a histogram. They use the structure (MP7) to look for symmetry, peaks, clusters, gaps, and any unusual values in histograms. Students also begin to consider how features such as symmetry might affect how we characterize a data set. This work is informal, but helps to prepare students to better understand measures of center and spread later in the unit. In this lesson, students also distinguish between the uses and construction of bar graphs and histograms.

task_events.answer: Histogram created from class travel time data with equal-width intervals and frequency labels
task_events.excerpt: Use the data to draw a histogram that shows your class's travel times.

task_events.answer: Peak on left (15–20 points); not symmetrical; gap 35–40; center around 20 points; spread extends to high outlier
task_events.excerpt: Describe the shape and features of the data. Mention the center and spread as well as any symmetry, gaps, peaks, or other features that you notice.

boundary_note: Histograms and bar graphs are visually similar but represent different data types. Students struggle to see that histogram features—peaks, gaps, symmetry, clusters—describe the actual data distribution, not just the visual appearance of the graph itself. Distinguishing when to use each representation and interpreting what distribution features reveal about the underlying data takes deliberate practice.

title: Dot Plots and Histograms
teacher_goals: Describe a distribution represented by a dot plot, including informal observations about its center and spread.
Interpret a histogram to answer statistical questions about a data set.
purpose: In this section, students focus on describing distributions. In particular, they learn to describe the center and spread of a distribution by using informal language to refer to a typical value for a distribution and how spread out the data are. They add histograms to the ways in which they can represent data, and use the visualization to describe features of a distribution such as clusters, peaks, gaps, and symmetry.

task_events.answer: fairly evenly distributed between values 2 and 12, with center around 7
task_events.excerpt: Describe the distribution. Include a mention of the center and spread in your description.

task_events.task: Identify typical wait time from histogram
task_events.position: Section B Checkpoint, Goal 2, Question a
task_events.excerpt: What is a typical amount of time that customers have to wait for a car to arrive? Explain your reasoning.

task_events.task: Count frequencies above threshold in histogram
task_events.position: Section B Checkpoint, Goal 2, Question b
task_events.excerpt: How many customers in this group had to wait longer than 20 minutes?

boundary_note: Students often misinterpret histogram bin boundaries; the lesson specifies that the left boundary is included but the right is excluded (e.g., the value 5 falls in the 5–10 bin, not 0–5). Additionally, students may struggle to distinguish informal notions of 'typical' from formal measures of center; many conflate what they see in the peak of a distribution with mean or median.

title: Inequalities and Shopping
student_facing_goal: Write and test inequalities that represent spending constraints in a real-world shopping situation.
teacher_goals: Students can write inequalities to represent contextual constraints (budget limits and purchasing requirements)
Students can determine whether specific values satisfy inequalities by substitution and contextual reasoning
Students understand how variables and their domains are constrained by real-world context
purpose: This lesson develops students' ability to translate real-world constraints into symbolic form using inequalities and to evaluate whether proposed values make sense given both the mathematical inequality and the practical context.

task_events.task: write inequalities for pounds of cherries
task_events.answer: c < 2.5 or c = 2.5 (cannot spend more than $10); c > 0 (buys some cherries)
task_events.excerpt: If c is the number of pounds of cherries that Tyler can buy, write one or more inequalities or equations describing c.

task_events.answer: yes, valid; 2 pounds cost $8, which is within the $10 budget
task_events.excerpt: Can 2 be a value of c? Yes, 2 can be a solution because 2 pounds of cherries cost $8.

task_events.answer: no, invalid; 3 pounds cost $12, exceeding the $10 budget
task_events.excerpt: Can 3 be a value of c? No, 3 could not be a solution because 3 pounds cost $12 and he has only $10.

task_events.answer: no, invalid; negative pounds are not meaningful in context
task_events.excerpt: What about -1? No, -1 could not be a solution because it does not make sense for Tyler to buy -1 pounds of cherries.

task_events.answer: m < 10 or m = 10 (spends $10 or less); m > 0 (spends some money)
task_events.excerpt: If m is the amount of money, in dollars, Tyler can spend, write one or more inequalities or equations describing m.

task_events.answer: yes, valid; $8 buys 2 pounds of cherries (8 ÷ 4 = 2)
task_events.excerpt: Can 8 be a value of m? Yes, 8 and 2 can both be values of m.

task_events.answer: yes, valid; $2 buys 0.5 pound of cherries (2 ÷ 4 = 0.5)
task_events.excerpt: Can 2 be a value of m? The value 2 means he buys 1/2 pound of cherries (1/2 · 4 = 2).

task_events.answer: no, invalid; $10.5 exceeds the $10 budget limit
task_events.excerpt: What about 10.5? No, 10.5 cannot be a value of m in this case. Even though 10.5 is greater than 0, m must also be 10 or less.

boundary_note: This lesson teaches students to write inequalities that capture real-world constraints and to test whether specific values are solutions by both algebraic substitution and contextual reasoning. Students often conflate the algebraic solution set with the feasible set (e.g., treating negative solutions as if they were valid just because they satisfy the inequality mathematically, rather than recognizing that context—no negative quantities of goods—restricts the domain). The dual-representation work (representing the same constraint through both c and m) helps students understand that multiple variables can encode a single relationship.

title: Variability and MAD (File Contains Lesson 10 Summary Material)
student_facing_goal: Understand the mean as a balance point and measure variability in data by calculating distances from each data point to the mean.
teacher_goals: Develop understanding that the mean is a valid measure of center because it acts as a balance point
Calculate distance of each data value from the mean
Compare variability across datasets by examining which has greater total distance (sum) from the mean
purpose: This section establishes the foundational concept that the mean balances the data. Using dot plots and symmetrical examples (stickers on pages, walk times), students observe that distances below the mean balance distances above it. This prepares them to understand mean absolute deviation as a formal measure of spread.

task_events.excerpt: The mean number of stickers is 21, because (19+20+20+21+21+22+22+23) ÷ 8 = 21.

task_events.position: Lesson 10 Summary, balance-point explanation with double-headed arrows
task_events.excerpt: The two points at 20 and the two at 22 are same distance from 21 but on opposite sides. Points at 19 and 23 are same distance from 21 but on opposite sides.
Calculate the mean for Kiran's data: (11+12+13+16+18) ÷ 5 = 14.
Without calculating, decide if 15 minutes would be a good estimate. Sample: 15 seems too high; 14 is better because sums of distances left and right would be more balanced.

task_events.answer: Data: time 16 (distance 2, right); 11 (distance 3, left); 18 (distance 4, right); 12 (distance 2, left); 13 (distance 1, left)
task_events.excerpt: In the table, record distance of each data point from the mean and its location relative to the mean (left or right).

boundary_note: This section (pages 200-202 in the file) is labeled Lesson 10 Summary material, not Lesson 11. Students must grasp the mean as a *balance point* (equal total distances on each side) before MAD can make sense as a measure. Common confusion: students think a higher mean always indicates higher variability, not recognizing that spread around the mean is separate from the mean's location. This lesson isolates the balance-point understanding before introducing formal deviation measurement.

title: Coordinate Geometry and Histogram Representation
purpose: The provided PDF contains only the practice problems section (pages 144-145) and does not include the lesson's teaching content. Based on the problems shown, students work with coordinate geometry (naming vertices of a square) and statistical representation (reading and constructing histograms). The full lesson structure (Learning Goals, Lesson Purpose, Standards) is not available in this document.

task_events.excerpt: (-2, 3) is one vertex of a square on a coordinate plane. Name three points that could be the other vertices.
Select all the statements that are true, according to the histogram. [Shark length data, 13-17.5 feet range, frequency up to 10]

task_events.answer: Histogram with bars of heights 1, 5, 13, 12, 7, 2 for time intervals 4-6, 6-8, 8-10, 10-12, 12-14, 14-16 minutes
task_events.excerpt: This table shows the times, in minutes, that it took 40 sixth-grade students to run 1 mile. Draw a histogram for the information in the table.

boundary_note: This document shows only practice problems without the lesson's pedagogical framing. Students must understand that a square has four equal sides and right angles—many will incorrectly place vertices or forget the constraint. For histogram problems, common errors include confusing the height of a bar with the boundary value, miscounting bars, or misreading intervals (e.g., treating 8-10 as including 8.0 to 10.0 exclusive on one end); students should know that equal-width intervals are standard and that frequency is read on the vertical axis only.

title: Statistical Questions and Data Collection (inferred from task; lesson title not shown in excerpt)
teacher_goals: Not visible in this practice-problems page. The lesson introduction would contain Learning Goals bullets.
purpose: Not visible in this practice-problems page. The lesson introduction would contain the Lesson Purpose paragraph.
student_facing_goal: Here is a list of statistical questions. What data would you collect and analyze to answer each question? For numerical data, include the unit of measurement that you would use.
boundary_note: This lesson asks students to distinguish statistical questions (those expecting variability across responses) from non-statistical questions, and to plan feasible data-collection methods with appropriate units. The primary challenge is recognizing that typical or general answers demand collecting multiple data points—not finding a single answer. Students often propose vague collection methods without specifying sample boundaries or measurement units; the lesson expects precision in both scope and units.

title: Comparing Mean and Median
student_facing_goal: Let's compare the mean and median of data sets.
teacher_goals: Choose which measure of center to use to describe a given data set and justify (orally and in writing) the choice.
Explain (orally) that the median is a better estimate of a typical value than the mean for distributions that are not symmetric or contain values far from the center.
Generalize how the shape of the distribution affects the mean and median of a data set.
purpose: In this lesson, students investigate whether the mean or the median is a more appropriate measure of the center of a distribution in a given context. They learn that when the distribution is symmetric, the mean and median have similar values, so the mean should usually be used for its connection to fairness. When the distribution is not symmetric, however, the mean is often greatly influenced by values that are far from the majority of the data points. In this case, the median may be a better choice.

task_events.excerpt: Find the mean height of your class in centimeters.
Find the median height in centimeters. Show your reasoning.
Suppose the world's tallest adult, who is 251 centimeters tall, joined your class. Find the new mean.
Find the new median.
Heights of 50 basketball players: data are roughly symmetric.
Backpack weights of 55 sixth-grade students: outlier at 16 kg pulls mean up.
Ages of 30 people at family dinner party: many elderly pull median up.

boundary_note: Students must learn that mean and median serve different purposes: the mean gets pulled by outliers, while the median describes the middle of ordered data. Many students think the choice between them is arbitrary rather than driven by distribution shape and what question is being answered in context.

title: Applying Coordinates and Area to Real-World Data
teacher_goals: Plot points on a coordinate plane and identify interior points of a polygon
Calculate the area of a triangle using base and height
Interpret measurement data and match it to plausible real-world contexts
Understand which measurement units and ranges correspond to different objects
purpose: Students apply coordinate geometry (plotting, area calculation) and connect numerical data to meaningful real-world contexts. They practice matching data sets to scenarios by reasoning about what objects could reasonably produce those measurements in a given unit.

task_events.answer: Three vertices D(-4,-4), E(-2,-4), F(-3,-1) plotted and connected on coordinate grid
task_events.excerpt: Plot the triangle in the coordinate plane and label the vertices.
Name the coordinates of 3 points that are inside the triangle.

task_events.answer: 3 square units; base = 2 units, height = 3 units, area = 3 × 2 ÷ 2 = 3
task_events.excerpt: What is the area of the triangle? Show your reasoning.

task_events.answer: B, C, E (leaf length, cup/mug height, colored pencil length)
task_events.excerpt: Andre collects data measured in centimeters: 8.5, 10.5, 7.8, 9.5, 8.1, 9.0, 10.2, 9.6, 11.2, 10.9, 12.7, 9.8. What could he be investigating? Select all that apply.

student_facing_goal: Use coordinates to plot shapes and calculate their area; connect numerical data to real-world measurement contexts.
boundary_note: This digest is extracted from practice problems only; the full lesson narrative (title, learning-goal bullets, formal lesson purpose, and explicit CCSS listing) is not included in pages 67–68. The core misconception in Problem 5 is confusing units: students often miss that weight (A, in grams) and categorical data (D, shoe type) cannot be represented by the given centimeter measurements, requiring reasoning about which real-world objects naturally produce values in the 7.8–12.7 cm range. The triangle area calculation is straightforward arithmetic once base and height are identified from the coordinate plot.

title: Box Plots
student_facing_goal: Let's explore how box plots can help us summarize distributions.
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally) a dot plot and a box plot that represent the same data set.
Create a box plot to represent a data set.
Describe (orally) the parts of a box plot that correspond with each number in the five-number summary, the range, and the IQR of a data set.
purpose: Students use the five-number summary to construct box plots. They begin with a kinesthetic activity identifying the median, quartiles, and range from class data on name lengths, positioning themselves on a number line and using tape to mark a human box plot. Then they draw box plots on paper, discovering that unlike dot plots, box plots cannot reveal individual data points but instead summarize distributions by showing range, middle half location, and quarter divisions. The lesson emphasizes that box plots are useful for comparing distributions and seeing shape, center, and spread at a glance.

task_events.excerpt: Twenty people participate in a study about blinking. The data values are shown here, in order from smallest to largest: 3 6 8 11 11 13 14 14 14 14 16 18 20 20 20 22 24 32 36 51. Find the median (Q2) and mark its location on the dot plot.

task_events.answer: Box extending from Q1=12 to Q3=21, with median line at Q2=15; whiskers extend from minimum 3 to maximum 51
task_events.excerpt: Draw a box that extends from the first quartile (Q1) to the third quartile (Q3). At the median (Q2), draw a vertical line from the top of the box to the bottom. From the left side of the box (Q1), draw a horizontal line that extends to the minimum of the data set. On the right side of the box (Q3), draw a similar line that extends to the maximum.
The rectangle in the middle represents the middle half of the data. Its width is the IQR. The whiskers represent the bottom quarter and the top quarter of the data set.

Each student in a class recorded how many books they read during the summer. Here is a box plot that summarizes their data. What is the greatest number of books read? What is the median number of books read? What is the interquartile range?

task_events.answer: Box from Q1=45 to Q3=50, with median line at 48; whiskers from 40 to 60
task_events.excerpt: Use this five-number summary to draw a box plot. All values are in seconds. Minimum: 40, First quartile (Q1): 45, Median: 48, Third quartile (Q3): 50, Maximum: 60.

task_events.answer: Ordered data: 3 4 5 5 5 7 9 10 11 11 12 12; minimum: 3, Q1: 5, Q2: 9.5, Q3: 11.5, maximum: 12
task_events.excerpt: The data shows the number of hours per week that each of 13 seventh-grade students spent doing homework. Create a box plot to summarize the data. 3 10 12 4 7 9 5 5 11 11 5 12 11
The box plot displays the data on the response times of 100 mice to seeing a flash of light. How many mice are represented by the rectangle between 0.5 and 1 second?

boundary_note: Box plots summarize a distribution by displaying only the five-number summary, not individual data points—the key tradeoff students must grasp. Students most commonly struggle with three ideas: (1) conflating box plots with dot plots, or not understanding why a box plot cannot show individual values; (2) misidentifying the box width as the full range rather than the IQR (interquartile range), which represents only the middle 50% of data; (3) incorrectly calculating quartiles when the number of data values is odd versus even, especially the median's role in splitting the upper and lower halves.

title: Using Box Plots
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally and in writing) box plots that represent different data sets, including ones with the same median but very different IQRs and vice versa.
Determine what information is needed to solve problems about comparing box plots. Ask questions to elicit that information.
Interpret a box plot to answer (orally) statistical questions about a data set.
purpose: In this lesson, students use box plots to make sense of data in context (MP2), compare distributions, and answer statistical questions about a situation. Students compare box plots for distributions that have the same median but different IQRs, as well as box plots with the same IQRs but different medians.
