After reviewing the distributive property, the first activity explains why either of two equations could represent a diagram. The second activity matches equations to diagrams and solves them. The goal is for students to see and understand two approaches to solving this type of equation.

task_events.excerpt: Select all the expressions equivalent to 2(x + 3). A. 2·(x + 3) ... C. 2·x + 2·3
Explain why either of these equations could represent this hanger: 14 = 2(x + 3) or 14 = 2x + 6. Find the weight of one circle.
Identify which of the following equations matches the diagram: 2(x + 5) = 16. Then explain how to figure out the weight of a piece labeled with a variable by reasoning about the equation.
Solve the equation 3(x + 4.5) = 36. If you get stuck, use the diagram.

boundary_note: Balanced hangers justify two solution methods for p(x + q) = r: divide by p first, then subtract q; or distribute p first, then solve. Students commonly confuse 2(x + 5) with 2x + 5, misread the hanger as 2 + x instead of 2x, or doubt that both approaches yield the same answer. The concrete hanger grounding is essential to prevent confusion between multiplicative grouping and sequential operations.

title: Dealing with Negative Numbers
teacher_goals: Generalize (orally) that doing the same thing to each side of an equation generates an equivalent equation.
Solve equations of the form px + q = r or p(x + q) = r that involve negative numbers, and explain (orally and in writing) the solution method.
purpose: Students extend the "do the same thing to each side" method to equations involving negative numbers. After a warm-up comparing rational number operations, they solve straightforward equations with negative coefficients and constants, then work with a partner to create equivalent equations by applying sequences of transformations. The central idea: doing the same operation to both sides maintains equality and preserves solutions, even when the moves are counterproductive toward isolation, and even when negative numbers are involved.
student_facing_goal: Let's show that doing the same to each side works for negative numbers too.

task_events.excerpt: Solve each equation. Be prepared to explain your reasoning. 1. x + 6 = 4
Solve each equation. Show your work, or explain your reasoning. 1. -3x - 5 = 16

boundary_note: Students often stumble on arithmetic with negative numbers—especially subtraction like x − (−1)—and doubt the solving method works when negative coefficients appear. Deeper misunderstanding: many expect that doing something "unhelpful" to both sides should break the equation, not realizing that maintaining equality always preserves solutions whether or not the move progresses toward isolating the variable.

title: Different Options for Solving One Equation
teacher_goals: Critique (orally and in writing) a given solution method for an equation of the form p(x + q) = r
Evaluate (orally) the usefulness of different approaches for solving a given equation of the form p(x + q) = r
Recognize that there are two common approaches for solving an equation of the form p(x + q) = r, i.e., expanding using the distributive property or dividing each side by p
student_facing_goal: Let's think about which way is better when we solve equations with parentheses.
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is to practice solving equations of the form p(x + q) = r, and to notice that one of the two ways of solving may be more efficient depending on the numbers in the equation.

task_events.answer: Andre's method yields x = 14 (correct); Noah's and Elena's methods contain algebraic errors
task_events.excerpt: Three students each attempted to solve the equation 2(x - 9) = 10, but they got different solutions.
Solve each of these equations twice, one time using each method: 3(x + 2) = 21
Solve each of these equations once. Choose whichever method you think will be easier: 1/4(4 + x) = 4/3; -10(x - 1.7) = -3; 5.4 = 0.3(x + 8)

boundary_note: This lesson teaches two equally valid pathways for solving equations of the form p(x + q) = r: dividing the coefficient into both sides first, or distributing first. The instructional work is not about computing solutions but about recognizing which method is more efficient for specific numbers. Students often fail to see p(x + q) as a single quantity and instead attempt to isolate variables mechanically; they also make errors distributing (forgetting to multiply all terms) or treating fractional results as failures. The lesson's core tension is strategic: a student who can solve the equation mechanically might not yet know when to choose the divide-first method (e.g., 3(x + 2) = 21) over distribute-first (e.g., 100(x + 0.06) = 21).

title: Calculating Sale Price with Percentage Discount

task_events.excerpt: A store is having a 25% off sale on all shirts. Show two different ways to calculate the sale price for a shirt that normally costs $24.

boundary_note: This practice problem centers on percentage reasoning: students compute a 25% discount in multiple ways (subtract the discount amount vs. find the complement/remaining percentage). The three presented methods all yield $18, emphasizing strategic flexibility. The vocabulary gap: none of the provided operation types capture finding a percentage/fraction of a quantity, so this is marked as "concept."

title: Solving Problems about Percent Increase or Decrease
student_facing_goal: Let's use tape diagrams, equations, and reasoning to solve problems with negatives and percents.
teacher_goals: Solve word problems leading to equations of the form px + q = r or p(x + q) = r
I can solve story problems about percent increase or decrease by drawing and reasoning about a tape diagram or by writing and solving an equation
purpose: This lesson is an opportunity for students to revisit percentages of and percentage change to solve word problems. Minimal scaffolding is provided, so students will need to make sense of the problems and perhaps attempt different solution pathways. Now, they can choose to use their deeper understanding of tape diagrams and writing and solving equations.

task_events.excerpt: Find the number of minutes Mai walked on the first day. Explain or show your reasoning.
Find what the temperature was at midnight. Explain or show your reasoning.
If Diego pays $18.40 for a pair of shoes, what was their original price before the sale and without the coupon?
how many pairs of flip flops (any color) did they sell?
how many pairs were on display originally?
how many pairs of shoes did they have at the start?
If their final time is 3.96 minutes, what was their beginning time? Show or explain your reasoning.

boundary_note: Students solve multi-step percent-change problems by writing and solving equations, combining percentage increases or decreases with fixed monetary adjustments. The core difficulty is correctly modeling the sequence of operations and accurately identifying which quantity represents the 'whole' in the percentage calculation when a base amount changes partway through the problem.

title: Grade 7 Unit 6 Lesson 13: Practice Problems
purpose: Practice problems reviewing tape diagrams for solving linear equations in context, signed number operations, and equation solving with integers and decimals.

task_events.excerpt: How much does Andre's neighbor pay him each week to mow the lawn?

boundary_note: This PDF contains only practice problem pages, not a full lesson with learning goals or standards. The core struggle: students interpreting double-negative subtraction (c - (-3)) and recognizing when a tape diagram represents a linear equation 5(x-2) = 40. The lesson has no automaton for tape-diagram-to-equation translation; that inference lives in student reasoning, not symbolic derivation.

title: Distinguishing Proportional from Non-Proportional Linear Relationships; Interpreting and Matching Equations to Contexts
teacher_goals: Distinguish proportional relationships (constant unit rate) from non-proportional linear functions (with fixed fees or intercepts)
Interpret real-world situations and match them appropriately to linear equations, recognizing when a simple proportional model fails
Execute signed-integer addition and solve problems requiring symbolic reasoning about linear structures
purpose: This lesson solidifies the critical distinction between proportional and non-proportional linear relationships. Students learn that while both have constant rates of change, only proportional relationships pass through the origin; non-proportional linear functions include fixed costs that make them unsuitable for certain equation forms. Students also practice recognizing when real-world contexts do or do not fit standard equation templates like 3x = 27.
student_facing_goal: Solve problems involving signed integers in sequence contexts; determine whether cost-and-quantity relationships are proportional; identify which word problems can be solved by given linear equations

task_events.task: Determine which sequences of four football plays sum to at least 10 yards (minimum threshold for keeping the ball)
task_events.excerpt: Select all the sequences of four plays that result in the Bulldogs getting to keep the ball.

task_events.task: Test whether the cost-to-quantity relationship for sandwich delivery is proportional by comparing unit rates
task_events.answer: No—unit rate is $6.67 per sub for 3 subs but $6.50 per sub for 4 subs
task_events.excerpt: Is the relationship between the number of turkey subs delivered and the amount charged proportional? Explain how you know.

task_events.task: Calculate the marginal cost (cost of one additional sandwich unit)
task_events.excerpt: How much does the store charge to deliver 1 additional turkey sub?

task_events.task: Identify which word problem cannot be modeled by the equation 3x = 27
task_events.answer: C (Diego's pizza pricing involves a fixed delivery fee, so cost is not purely proportional to quantity)
task_events.excerpt: Which question cannot be answered by the solution to the equation 3x = 27?

boundary_note: The central misconception: students assume any regular linear pattern is proportional without verifying that unit rates remain constant. In Problem 5, both prices increase regularly with quantity, but the unit rates diverge ($6.67 vs $6.50), revealing a fixed fee ($2 delivery charge). For Problem 6, students often try to force contexts into simple proportional equations without recognizing that fixed-cost structures demand a different model. This lesson demands careful verification of proportionality claims, not just visual pattern recognition."

title: Grade 7, Unit 6 Practice Problems (Page 84)
student_facing_goal: Not present in extracted PDF. Task statements read: 'Solve each equation mentally' (Problem 1) and 'Complete the magic squares so that the sum of the three numbers in each row, each column, and each diagonal are all equal' (Problem 2).
purpose: Not present in extracted PDF. This page contains practice problems referencing content from Unit 5, Lesson 15 and Unit 5, Lesson 3.

task_events.answer: Three completed grids with equal row/column/diagonal sums: [0,7,2; 5,3,1; 4,-1,6], [1,2,6; 8,3,-2; 0,4,5], [3,-2,5; 4,2,0; -1,6,1]
task_events.excerpt: Complete the magic squares so that the sum of the three numbers in each row, each column, and each diagonal are all equal.

boundary_note: The extracted PDF contains only a practice problems page (page 84), not a full IM lesson with Launch, Activities, and Synthesis. Standard lesson components—Learning Goals, Lesson Purpose, and CCSS standards (Building On/Addressing/Building Toward)—are absent. Problem 1 reviews mental solving of linear equations with fractions and negative coefficients; Problem 2 asks students to use constraint reasoning (equal sums) to fill magic squares. Students often struggle with the signs and magnitudes when multiplying or dividing both sides by negative numbers or fractions in Problem 1; for magic squares, common errors arise from not setting up or tracking the sum constraint correctly across all rows, columns, and diagonals.

title: Interpreting Inequalities
teacher_goals: Critique (orally) a solution method for a problem involving an inequality.
Identify the inequality that represents a situation, and justify (in writing) the choice.
Present (orally, in writing, and using other representations) the solution method for a problem involving an inequality, and interpret the solution.
purpose: In this lesson, students identify which inequality, from several options, matches a given situation. They make sense of what each part of the inequality represents in the context. Then they write a question about the situation that could be answered by solving the inequality. Finally, students solve the inequality and create a visual display explaining their solution. As students create their displays and examine others' displays, they construct and critique arguments (MP3).

task_events.excerpt: 1/2 x + 5 ≤ 25; solve: 1/2 x ≤ 20; x ≤ 40. Up to 40 small trees can be planted.
12 - 3x ≥ -37; solve: -3x ≥ -49; x ≤ 16 1/3. Hike time: up to 16 1/3 minutes to bottom.
212 - 1/500 x < 195; solve: -1/500 x < -17; x > 8,500. At elevations > 8500 ft, boiling point < 195°F.
3x + 10 ≤ 30: time for x small cranes (3x) + centerpiece (10) ≤ available time (30). Solution: x ≤ 6 2/3.

boundary_note: Student-facing goal: Let's write inequalities. The core work is interpretation, not just procedural solving—students must explain what the variable and each term represent in context, then connect solutions back to real constraints. Students most often succeed at algebraic steps but struggle to articulate what x ≤ 40 means in the problem's situation (e.g., "we can plant up to 40 trees"). Inequalities with variables in non-standard positions (like 212 - 1/500 x < 195) and those requiring attention to inequality direction when multiplying by negative numbers are also common trouble spots.

title: Modeling with Inequalities
student_facing_goal: Let's look at solutions to inequalities.
teacher_goals: Critique (orally) the solution to an inequality, including whether fractional or negative values are reasonable.
Determine what information is needed to solve a problem involving a quantity constrained by a maximum or minimum acceptable value. Ask questions to elicit that information.
Write and solve an inequality of the form px + q > r or px + q < r to answer a question about a situation with a constraint.
purpose: In this lesson, students consolidate what they have learned over the last few lessons about writing and solving inequalities. They should now consider whether fractional values or negative values make sense in context as solutions to an inequality. For instance, if they find that x < 7, but x represents the number of students who can go on a trip, then they should realize that x cannot be 3.25, nor can x be -2. The main activity uses the Information Gap routine. To obtain all the necessary information, students need to persevere in asking questions and to communicate precisely (MP6). The last activity is optional because it provides an opportunity for additional practice with writing an inequality to represent a situation and interpreting the solution in context.

task_events.excerpt: The situation can be represented with -1.65x + 50 > 15, where x is the number of loads.
The situation can be represented with 14 + 2w ≤ 65, where w is the width of the frame.
Solving the inequality 129 + 21x > 420 we get x > 13.86.
48b + 185 ≤ 2,000 (or equivalent), where b is the number of identical boxes
The correct inequality is 3.5x + 15 ≤ 120. The variable x represents the number of songs.

boundary_note: Students often accept mathematically valid solutions (like x = 13.86 sandwiches) without checking if they make sense in context (you can only order whole numbers, and 14 costs too much). This lesson trains the critical reasoning: solve the inequality first, then filter solutions for what's realistic in the situation.

title: Subtraction in Equivalent Expressions
teacher_goals: Explain (orally, in writing, and using other representations) how the distributive and commutative properties apply to expressions with negative coefficients.
Justify (orally and in writing) whether expressions are equivalent, including rewriting subtraction as adding the opposite.
purpose: In this lesson, students begin working with equivalent expressions that involve negative numbers. They apply what they have learned about rewriting subtraction as adding the opposite. This enables students to apply properties of addition to generate equivalent expressions. For example, 6x − 5 + 2x can be rewritten as 6x + (−5) + 2x and then rearranged as 6x + 2x + (−5) using the commutative property of addition. Then students use a similar strategy to make sense of the distributive property of multiplication over subtraction. As students rewrite an expression in different ways, they make use of structure (MP7).
student_facing_goal: Let's find ways to work with subtraction in expressions.

task_events.position: 18.1 Math Talk: Adding and Subtracting (Warm-up), Problem 1
task_events.excerpt: Find the value of each expression mentally. 64 − 9
Write an expression with three terms that is equivalent to 7 2/4 + 3 5/6 − 1 3/4 and uses only addition.
Use the distributive property to write an expression that is equivalent to 1/2(8y + −x + −12). The boxes can help you organize your work.
Select all the expressions that are equivalent to 4 − x.
Use the distributive property to write an expression that is equivalent to 5(−2x − 3).

boundary_note: The lesson's core idea is that subtraction equals adding the opposite, which unlocks rearrangement via the commutative property of addition. Students' most common mistake is separating the negative sign from its term when reordering—they keep the − sign where it was instead of moving it with the number, producing equivalent-looking but incorrect expressions like a + c − b instead of a + c + (−b).

title: Expanding and Factoring
student_facing_goal: Let's use the distributive property to write expressions in different ways.
teacher_goals: Apply the distributive property to expand or factor an expression that includes negative coefficients, and explain (orally and using other representations) the reasoning.
Comprehend the terms "expand" and "factor" (in spoken and written language) in relation to the distributive property.
purpose: In this lesson, students practice using the distributive property to write equivalent expressions when there are rational coefficients. Students learn the terms factor and expand to describe applying the distributive property in opposite directions: changing a sum of products to a product of a factor and a sum, or vice versa. Some expressions students factor have a variable as the common factor instead of a constant, showing how the distributive property is the basis for combining like terms. As students apply the distributive property, they make use of structure (MP7).

task_events.excerpt: Write as many expressions as you can that represent the area of this rectangle (width 6 + 3x, height 10).
-3(5 - 2y) is equivalent to -15 + 6y
5a - 30 factors to 5(a - 6)
Expand k(4 - 17) to get 4k - 17k
Expand -⅓(-2x + 4y) to write an equivalent expression
Calculate 3 · 15 + 4 · 15 - 5 · 15 using common factor 9 (or rewrite as (3 + 4 - 5) · 15)
Expand 5(4x - 2y + 3z) to get 20x - 10y + 15z

boundary_note: This lesson introduces expand and factor as inverse operations using the distributive property, with special emphasis on handling negative coefficients and recognizing variables as common factors. The most frequent errors: students mishandle sign distribution (e.g., -2(3x - 5) becomes -6x - 10 instead of -6x + 10), or fail to recognize when a variable is a common factor (e.g., 3x - 2x = (3 - 2)x). The reasoning students must develop here—that factoring reverses expanding—is foundational for later polynomial work; the formal connection to combining like terms (e.g., 3a + 2a = (3 + 2)a) becomes explicit only retrospectively.

title: Combining Like Terms (Part 1)
student_facing_goal: Let's see how we can tell that expressions are equivalent.
teacher_goals: Apply properties of operations to justify (orally and in writing) that expressions are equivalent.
Generate an expression with fewer terms that is equivalent to a given expression.
Interpret different methods for determining whether expressions are equivalent, and evaluate (orally) their usefulness.
purpose: In this lesson, students use properties of addition to justify that expressions are equivalent. As students explain how they know two expressions are equivalent for all values of the variables, they attend to precision (MP6). Students practice writing equivalent expressions with fewer terms. The given expressions do not include any parentheses. The last activity is optional because it provides an opportunity for additional practice adding and subtracting signed numbers in the context of writing equivalent expressions.

task_events.excerpt: Diego and Jada are both trying to write an expression with fewer terms that is equivalent to: 6j + 4d - 2j + 3d

task_events.position: Activity 20.3 (Making Sides Equal), Set A, Q1
task_events.excerpt: Replace each ? with a term to make: 6x + ? = 10x
Write each expression with fewer terms. Show your work or explain your reasoning. 10x - 2x

boundary_note: This lesson focuses on combining like terms to write simpler equivalent expressions. The central misconception to watch: students often believe two expressions are equivalent because they equal the same value when they substitute a single test number (e.g., both expressions equal 10 when x = 1), but true equivalence must hold for all values. The lesson insists students use algebraic properties (distributive, associative, commutative) to justify equivalence, not just numerical testing.

title: Combining Like Terms (Part 2)
teacher_goals: Critique (in writing) methods for generating equivalent expressions with fewer terms.
Identify equivalent expressions, and justify (orally and in writing) that they are equivalent.
purpose: In this lesson, students analyze expressions that include parentheses and write equivalent expressions with fewer terms. First, students analyze some worked examples that contain errors and identify why the expressions are not equivalent. Then they match expressions that are equivalent. As students contrast different strategies for rewriting the same expression, they are critiquing the reasoning of others (MP3). The last activity in this lesson is optional because it provides an opportunity for additional practice working with parentheses in expressions.
student_facing_goal: Let's see how to use properties correctly to write equivalent expressions.

task_events.excerpt: Some students are trying to write an expression with fewer terms that is equivalent to 8 – 3(4 – 9x).
Write an equivalent expression with fewer terms. Explain or show your reasoning. (16x + 5) – 4(3 + 2x)

boundary_note: The lesson's central challenge is correctly applying the distributive property and handling the subtraction sign in front of parentheses. Students commonly distribute only to the first term inside parentheses, or forget that a negative sign applies to all terms being subtracted, leading to incorrect simplifications like 8 - 3(4 - 9x) = 20 - 45x instead of the correct -4 + 27x.

title: Applications of Expressions
student_facing_goal: Let's use expressions to solve problems.
teacher_goals: Determine which order for applying multiple coupons gives the better discount and explain (orally and in writing) the reasoning.
Justify (orally, in writing, and using other representations) that two different sequences of calculations give the same result.
purpose: In this culminating lesson, students look at several real-world situations that can be represented by an expression with a variable. First, students write expressions to represent two different methods for performing a real-world calculation. They use the expressions to show why the two methods are equivalent. Then students are presented with two different coupons to a store (a 20% off coupon and a $30 off coupon). They apply their understanding of expressions, equations, and inequalities to decide in which order the coupons should be applied to save more money on a purchase.

task_events.excerpt: 20% off is $80. Then $30 off of $80 is $50.
$30 off is $70. Then 20% off of $70 is $56.

task_events.answer: Apply 20% off coupon first; saves $6 on any purchase amount
task_events.excerpt: Comparing 0.8x − 30 to 0.8x − 24, shows the resulting cost will always be $6 less if you use the 20% off coupon first.

boundary_note: The core insight is that the order of applying two successive discounts matters—applying the percentage discount first then the fixed amount always saves a fixed $6 more, independent of purchase price. Students typically believe order doesn't matter (Mai's reasoning) or depends on the purchase amount (Kiran's reasoning). The lesson demands algebraic reasoning with expressions and the distributive property to justify a general claim, moving beyond testing specific dollar amounts.

title: Relationships of Angles
student_facing_goal: Let's examine some special angles.
teacher_goals: Comprehend that the word 'degrees' (in spoken and written language) and the symbol ° (in written language) refer to the amount of turn between two different directions.
Determine the angle measures of pattern blocks, and explain (orally) the reasoning.
Recognize 180° and 360° angles, and identify when adjacent angles add up to these amounts.
purpose: In this lesson, students gain hands-on experience composing, decomposing, and measuring angles. Students connect vocabulary such as right angles and straight angles with their degree measures, as well as the description of going all the way around a point as 360 degrees. Students fit pattern blocks around a point using these relationships to determine the unknown angles on the remaining pattern blocks. Students use the structure of the blocks completely going around a point with no gaps to solve for angles (MP7).

task_events.excerpt: Angle DAB (or angle BAD) is obtuse. It measures 110° because 60 + 50 = 110.
What is the measure of Angle a? How do you know? (90°, because it is a right angle.)
What is the total measure of a + b + c? (270°, because 90 + 90 + 90 = 270.)
What is the total measure of a + b + c + d? (360°, because 4 · 90 = 360.)

If you trace three copies of the hexagon so that one vertex from each hexagon touches the same point, as shown, they fit together without any gaps or overlaps. Use this to figure out the degree measure of the angle inside the hexagon pattern block. 120°. Sample reasoning: It takes 3 blocks to go around a point, and 360 ÷ 3 = 120.
Green triangle: All 3 angles measure 60°. Sample reasoning: It takes 6 to go around a point, and 360 ÷ 6 = 60.
180°. Sample response: It is the same size as three green triangles put together.

Tyler and Priya were both measuring angle TUS. Priya thinks the angle measures 40 degrees. Tyler thinks the angle measures 140 degrees. Sample response: I agree with Priya, since the angle clearly measures less than 90 degrees. I think Tyler measured from the wrong end of the protractor.

This pattern is composed of a square and some regular octagons. Find the measure of one of the angles inside one of the octagons. 135°. Sample reasoning: The angles around a point add up to 360°, then 2 octagon angles must be 360 – 90, or 270°. Since all of the octagon angles are the same, each angle is 270 ÷ 2 or 135°.

boundary_note: Angles measure turn, not ray length—a distinction many students conflate. Students often struggle to internalize why exactly three hexagons fit around a point (their 120° interior angles divide 360° evenly) and why this matters; they also commonly misread protractors or confuse angle names (DAB vs. BAD). The lesson assumes Grade 4 mastery of protractors and basic angle addition.

title: Adjacent Angles
teacher_goals: Comprehend the terms "complementary" and "supplementary" (in spoken and written language) as they pertain to pairs of angles.
Explain (orally and in writing) how to find an unknown angle measure, given adjacent complementary or supplementary angles.
Generalize (orally) that when a straight angle or a right angle is decomposed, the measures of the resulting angles add up to 180° or 90°, respectively.
purpose: In this lesson, students are introduced to the terms complementary and supplementary for describing specific angle relationships. They practice finding an unknown angle given the measure of another angle that is complementary or supplementary.
student_facing_goal: Let's look at some special pairs of angles.

task_events.excerpt: For the angles making a side of the rectangle, Clare measured 70 degrees on one of her pieces. Predict the angle measure of her other piece.
For the angles making a corner of the rectangle, Priya measured 53 degrees on one of her pieces. Predict the angle measure of her other piece.
Use the protractor in the picture to find the measure of angles BCA and BCD.
Explain how to find the measure of angle ACD without repositioning the protractor.
Use the protractor in the picture to find the measure of angles LOK and LOM.
Explain how to find the measure of angle KOM without repositioning the protractor.
Angle BAC is a right angle. Find the measure of angle CAD.
Point O is on line RS. Find the measure of angle SOP.
Point F is on line CD. Find the measure of angle CFE.
Angle SPR and angle RPQ are complementary. Find the measure of angle RPQ.

boundary_note: Students frequently confuse complementary (90°) and supplementary (180°) angles, and struggle to identify adjacent angle pairs from diagrams or protractor readings. The core insight—that specific adjacent angle pairs always compose to 90° or 180°—requires first measuring to discover the pattern, then recognizing the relationship as a tool for finding unknowns. Without understanding that adjacency matters, students apply the property incorrectly to non-adjacent angle pairs.

title: Grade 7 Unit 7 Practice Problems: Rate Conversion (Problem 6)
purpose: This is a practice problem from an earlier lesson (Unit 2, Lesson 3) embedded in Unit 7 practice set. The PDF excerpt contains only the problem and solution, with no lesson context, learning goals, or purpose statement.
student_facing_goal: Describe the weight gain in days per pound.

task_events.excerpt: Lin's puppy is gaining weight at a rate of 0.125 pounds per day. Describe the weight gain in days per pound.

boundary_note: Students must find the reciprocal of the given rate (1 ÷ 0.125 = 8), inverting both the quantity and its unit. The core challenge is recognizing that "days per pound" means inverting the ratio from "pounds per day." Students often struggle to see rate inversion as a multiplicative operation and may instead try additive transformations or misinterpret which quantity should be in the numerator.

title: Solving for Unknown Angles
student_facing_goal: Let's figure out some missing angles.
teacher_goals: Coordinate (orally and in writing) diagrams and equations that represent the same relationship between angle measures.
Solve multi-step problems involving complementary, supplementary, and vertical angles, and explain (orally) the reasoning.
purpose: In previous lessons, students solved single-step problems about supplementary, complementary, and vertical angles. In this lesson, students apply these skills to find unknown angle measures in multi-step problems. In the Information Gap activity, students ask questions about the angle relationships and solve for missing measures in a diagram. Then they see that they can represent angle problems with equations. Students also work to construct arguments about angles and discuss them with their partners (MP3).

task_events.excerpt: Given angle relationships in diagram, find angle b where c = 56, a = 90 - 56 = 34, and b = 180 - 34 - 56 = 90
Multi-step problem involving vertical and supplementary angles: z = 63, d = 180 - 63 = 117, c = 63, a = 90, b = 180 - 90 - 63 = 27
Match five angle relationship diagrams (showing vertical, supplementary, straight, and right angle relationships) to corresponding equations
Given AOB = 40° and DOF = 120°, where vertical angles are congruent: AOC = 120°, so 40° + BOC = 120°, thus BOC = 80°
COD and DOF are supplementary angles: COD + 120° = 180°, therefore COD = 60°

boundary_note: This lesson advances students from single-step to multi-step angle problems by connecting geometric diagrams to algebraic equations. Students often misidentify which angles are related in complex figures (especially non-adjacent supplementary angle pairs) or incorrectly translate between diagram and equation representations.

title: Using Equations to Solve for Unknown Angles
student_facing_goal: Write equations to represent angle relationships in a diagram and find the values of unknown angles.
teacher_goals: Solve multi-step problems involving complementary, supplementary, and vertical angles.
Write an equation to represent the relationship between angles in a given diagram.
purpose: This checkpoint assesses whether students can translate visual geometric relationships formed by intersecting lines into algebraic equations and solve those equations to find unknown angle measures.

task_events.excerpt: a + 27 = 90 (sample response to part a); a = 63 (solution to part b)

task_events.position: Part b, vertical angle at intersection
Part b, supplementary angles on a line
task_events.excerpt: b + c = 180 (sample response to part a); c = 153 (solution to part b)

boundary_note: Students must recognize which angles form complementary (90°), supplementary (180°), or vertical (equal) relationships from a diagram of intersecting lines, write equations expressing those relationships, and solve the equations. The core challenge is the translation from visual geometry to algebraic form—students frequently misidentify which angles relate, write equations that don't capture the geometric constraint, or make solving errors. Vertical angles are typically recognized as a property (b = 27°) rather than solved via equation.

title: Building Polygons (Part 1)
teacher_goals: I can show that the 3 side lengths that form a triangle cannot be rearranged to form a different triangle.
I can show that the 4 side lengths that form a quadrilateral can be rearranged to form different quadrilaterals.
purpose: This lesson is the first in a series of lessons in which students create shapes with given conditions. Students experiment with making polygons of various numbers and combinations of side lengths using cardboard strips and metal fasteners. The goal is for students to make sense of the different shapes that are possible under given constraints about side lengths, including whether only one shape is possible or no shape is possible. Students do not try to formulate general rules about what side lengths are possible; in the next lesson, they formulate such a rule for triangles.
student_facing_goal: Let's build shapes.
boundary_note: The lesson's central finding—that three side lengths uniquely determine a triangle but four side lengths do not uniquely determine a quadrilateral—contradicts students' intuition that specifying all dimensions fixes a shape. Students often assume their quadrilateral is wrong when it differs from a classmate's version with identical side lengths; the lesson reveals both are valid. This geometric rigidity versus flexibility emerges from physical construction before any abstract reasoning about constraints.

title: Using Equations to Solve for Unknown Angles
teacher_goals: Determine whether a given equation represents the relationship between angles shown in a diagram.
Solve an equation that represents a relationship between angle measures, and explain (in writing and using other representations) the reasoning.
Write an equation of the form px + q = r or p(x + q) = r to represent the relationship between angles in a given diagram.
student_facing_goal: Let's figure out missing angles using equations.
purpose: In this lesson, students practice writing and solving equations of the form px + q = r in the context of finding unknown angle measures. Students examine the structure of angle relationships as they compare a diagram to possible equations that could be used to represent the angle relationships. The angles used connect to earlier work with supplementary, complementary, and vertical angles, giving additional opportunities to build fluency with these concepts.

task_events.excerpt: Diego's equation: x + 35 = 180. Solution: 145.
Elena's equation: 35 + w + 41 = 180. Solution: 104.
Elena's equation: w + 35 = 90. Solution: 55.
Elena's equation: 2w + 35 = 90. Solution: 27.5.
Both equations. w = 32 and x = 58.
2w + 76 = 180. Subtract 76 from 180 and then divide by 2. Solution w = 52.
Angle DCE measures 148°. Find the value of x. Solution: 16.
Line ℓ is perpendicular to line m. Find x and w. Solution: x = 29 and w = 42.

boundary_note: Students must recognize angle relationships in geometric diagrams (supplementary angles sum to 180°, vertical angles are equal, complementary angles sum to 90°) to correctly set up the equation. Common errors include misidentifying which angles in the diagram are related, incorrectly ordering terms in the equation, and arithmetic mistakes when solving. The lesson assumes prior fluency with basic linear equation solving.

title: Triangles with 3 Common Measures
student_facing_goal: Let's contrast triangles.
teacher_goals: Describe, compare, and contrast (orally and in writing) triangles that share three common measures of angles or sides.
Justify (orally and using other representations) whether triangles are identical copies or are "different" triangles.
Recognize that examining which side lengths and angle measures are adjacent can help determine whether triangles are identical copies.
purpose: In this lesson, students examine sets of triangles in which all the triangles share 3 common measures of angles or sides. Students learn to recognize when triangles are "identical copies" that are oriented differently on the page, and when they are different triangles (meaning triangles that are not identical copies). This prepares them for trying to draw more than one triangle given 3 measures, in the next lesson.
boundary_note: This lesson trains students to see that the adjacency and order of side and angle measures determine triangle congruence, not just having three equal measures. The core misconception: students assume any three matching measures make identical triangles, without recognizing that two triangles with sides 5, 7 and a 30° angle can be different if the angle's position relative to the sides differs. Students often miss subtle size differences when the same three measures are arranged in different orders, and may not recognize that tracing, flipping, and rotating are the only ways to verify identical copies. Reasoning is purely structural (Can the triangles overlay?) — no procedural operation automaton applies.

title: Drawing Triangles (Part 1)
teacher_goals: Draw triangles with two given angle measures and one side length, one given angle measure and two side lengths, or three side lengths
Justify whether 3 measures of angles or sides determine a unique triangle or more than one triangle, or if no triangle is possible
purpose: This checkpoint assesses student understanding of triangle construction and the conditions under which given measurements determine triangle uniqueness. Students draw triangles from constraints and justify whether the given measures produce one triangle, multiple triangles, or no triangle at all.

task_events.answer: A triangle with sides 3 in, 4 in, 6 in
task_events.excerpt: Draw a triangle with side lengths 3 in, 4 in, and 6 in.

task_events.answer: No. These 3 side lengths make a unique triangle.
task_events.excerpt: Can you draw a different triangle with these same lengths? Explain how you know.

task_events.answer: Yes. The angle position between the two sides vs. adjacent to one side produces different triangles.
task_events.excerpt: Priya and Han each draw a triangle with side lengths 2 inches and 5 inches and angle 30°. Could they have drawn different triangles?

boundary_note: This lesson centers on triangle construction constraints and the conditions for uniqueness. The core insight: SSS (three side lengths) always produces a unique triangle, but SSA (two sides and an angle) is ambiguous—students often miss that the angle's location relative to the sides matters critically. Students most commonly assume any three measurements uniquely determine a triangle, overlooking the ambiguous case where an angle's position can yield two distinct triangles.

title: Drawing Triangles (Part 2)
teacher_goals: Draw triangles with two given side lengths and one angle measure or with three given angle measures, and describe (orally) how many different triangles could be drawn with the given conditions.
Use drawings to justify (in writing) whether two given side lengths and one angle measure determine one unique triangle.
purpose: In this lesson, students continue their work on drawing triangles with specified angle and side measures. Students focus on drawing a triangle given two side lengths and an angle, and given three angles. They continue to gain experience with a compass, ruler, and protractor. They continue to notice from their drawings when the conditions determine one triangle, more than one, or none, and they increase their precision of language as they describe the conditions to each other (MP6).
student_facing_goal: Let's draw some more triangles.

task_events.excerpt: If you connect the ends of the sides you drew to make a triangle, is the third side longer or shorter than 5 centimeters?
One angle measures 40°, one side measures 4 cm, and one side measures 5 cm.
Two sides measure 6 cm, and one angle measures 100°.

task_events.answer: many possible triangles (scaled copies of one another)
task_events.excerpt: One angle measures 50°, one measures 60°, and one measures 70°.

task_events.answer: no—angles sum to 210 degrees, exceeding 180 degrees
task_events.excerpt: One angle measures 50°, one measures 60°, and one measures 100°.
A triangle has sides of length 7 cm, 4 cm, and 5 cm. How many unique triangles?

task_events.answer: no—angles sum to 220 degrees, exceeding 180 degrees
task_events.excerpt: Is it possible to make a triangle that has angles measuring 90 degrees, 30 degrees, and 100 degrees?

boundary_note: Students discover that certain combinations of sides and angles determine a unique triangle, while others produce multiple triangles or no triangle at all. The critical misconception is that students assume more measurements guarantee uniqueness; they must learn that the position of an angle relative to given sides, not the number of pieces of information, determines how many solutions are possible.

title: Slicing Solids
student_facing_goal: Let's see what shapes you get when you slice a three-dimensional object.
teacher_goals: Categorize images of planes intersecting pyramids and prisms, and describe (orally) the categories.
Comprehend that the term "cross-section" (in spoken and written language) refers to the two-dimensional face that results from slicing a three-dimensional figure.
Describe, compare, and contrast (orally and in writing) different cross-sections that could result from slicing the same pyramid or prism.
purpose: This lesson introduces the idea that slicing a three-dimensional figure with a plane results in a two-dimensional cross-section. Slicing a fruit or vegetable, dipping it in paint, and stamping it on paper helps students make sense of the two-dimensional face that is created by the slice. Given two-dimensional representations of how objects are sliced, students practice visualizing the three-dimensional figures and the resulting cross-sections.
boundary_note: This lesson is purely spatial-visualization work with no computational tasks. Students must mentally rotate and slice solids to predict resulting 2D cross-sections—a challenge that the arithmetic model does not address. The physical demonstration (slicing fruit/vegetable and painting the cross-section) is essential; without it, students struggle to visualize the abstraction. Common error: confusing cross-sections or failing to predict that different slice angles produce different 2D shapes.

title: Grade 7 Unit 7 Lesson 12: Practice Problems
teacher_goals: Find unknown angle measures using properties of intersecting lines and angle relationships
Recognize and select equivalent algebraic expressions representing percentage decrease
Verify linear equation solutions and identify procedural errors through substitution
purpose: Students practice with angle relationships at intersecting lines, equivalent expressions for percent change, and linear equation solving with error detection and verification.

task_events.excerpt: Segments AB, EF, and CD intersect at point C, and angle ACD is a right angle. Find the value of g.
Select all the expressions that are the result of decreasing x by 80%.

task_events.answer: x = 11/8 is incorrect; Andre should distribute 4 first to get 4x + 3 = 7, yielding x = 1
task_events.excerpt: Andre solves 4(x + 3/4) = 7, obtaining x = 11/8. Kiran suspects an error in Andre's work.

boundary_note: This practice page reviews angle relationships at intersecting lines, equivalent algebraic representations of percentage decrease, and linear equation solving. Students often struggle with visualizing angle composition at intersections, recognizing the equivalence of 0.2x, (1-0.8)x, and x - 0.8x as expressions for an 80% decrease, and avoiding procedural errors like subtracting before distributing in equations. Problem 5 explicitly trains error detection through substitution checking.

title: Lesson 9 Practice Problems (IM Grade 7 Unit 7)
student_facing_goal: No explicit student-facing goal ("Let's...") provided on this practice page.
purpose: This page contains practice problems; no Lesson Purpose paragraph is printed.

task_events.answer: Triangle c (90° and 100°) is impossible because 90 + 100 = 190°, exceeding 180°.
task_events.excerpt: A triangle where one angle measures 90° and another angle measures 100°
A triangle has an angle measuring 90°, an angle measuring 20°, and a side that is 6 units long. The 6-unit side is in between the 90° and 20° angles. How many unique triangles can you draw like this?

task_events.answer: Part a: any x > 0 (e.g., x = 1); Part b: any x < 0 (e.g., x = -1)
task_events.excerpt: Find a value for x that makes −x less than 2x. Find a value for x that makes −x greater than 2x.

boundary_note: This practice page tests three linked ideas: the triangle-angle-sum constraint (why certain angle pairs fail), congruence via ASA (Angle-Side-Angle produces a unique triangle), and inequality reasoning. Students often confuse angle constraints—assuming any two angles suffice—and may struggle to see why ASA guarantees uniqueness. Problem 3 bridges into earlier algebra work; students must test or reason symbolically about when one expression dominates another.

title: Surface Area of Right Prisms
teacher_goals: Calculate the surface area of a prism, and explain (in writing) the solution method.
Comprehend that surface area and volume are two different attributes of three-dimensional objects and are measured in different units.
Interpret different methods for calculating the surface area of a prism, and evaluate (orally and in writing) their usefulness.
purpose: In this lesson, students find surface areas of prisms, and they see that the structure of a prism allows for shortcuts in adding up the areas of the faces. They see that if the prism is sitting on its base, then the vertical sides can be unfolded into a single rectangle whose height is the height of the prism and whose length is the perimeter of the base. The purpose of the lesson is not to come up with a formula for the surface area of a prism, but to help students see and make use of the structure of the prism to find surface area efficiently.
student_facing_goal: Let's look at the surface area of prisms.

task_events.excerpt: The surface area is 234 cm². Explanations vary. Sample responses: Adding the areas of all the faces: There are two pentagonal bases that can each be decomposed into two rectangles and a right triangle.
The surface area is 270 cm². Possible strategy: The area of the base is 27 cm². The perimeter of the base is 24 cm, so the combined area of the sides is 216 cm².

boundary_note: This lesson develops the insight that prisms can be unfolded into nets: lateral faces form a single rectangle (perimeter of base × height) plus two identical bases, enabling efficient calculation without computing each face separately. Students commonly struggle to see why this net method works universally and often confuse surface area (square units) with volume (cubic units), despite the lesson's explicit emphasis on distinguishing these two different measures of three-dimensional objects.

title: Section B: Drawing Polygons with Given Conditions
teacher_goals: Draw triangles with two given angle measures and one side length, one given angle measure and two side lengths, or three side lengths
Justify whether 3 measures of angles or sides determine a unique triangle, more than one triangle, or if no triangle is possible
purpose: During these lessons students think about what conditions are needed to determine a unique figure, in preparation for future work with congruence in grade 8 and high school. Students experiment with creating polygons using fixed side lengths, generalize the triangle inequality rule (sum of any 2 side lengths must be greater than the third), and explore when given angle and side combinations create unique triangles versus multiple possible triangles.
student_facing_goal: Determine what combinations of angle and side measures create unique triangles, and justify your reasoning by constructing triangles under different conditions.
boundary_note: This is a conceptual geometry lesson focused on understanding the triangle inequality and conditions for uniqueness, not computational arithmetic. Students work through construction and justification; no specific numerical computations are present on pages 105-107. The key learning involves recognizing that three side lengths, or certain angle-side combinations, determine unique triangles while others do not—foundational reasoning for grade 8 congruence work. IM's formal triangle congruence criteria (SSS, SAS, ASA) are not taught at this stage; students develop intuition through construction.

title: Applying Volume and Surface Area
teacher_goals: Solve problems involving the volume and surface area of children's play structures.
Apply reasoning about surface area and volume of prisms as well as proportional relationships to calculate how much material to build something will cost and explain the solution method.
purpose: In this second lesson on applying surface area and volume to solve problems, students solve real-world problems of increased complexity that require them to choose which of the two quantities is appropriate for solving the problem, or whether both are appropriate for different aspects of the problem. They use previous work on ratios and proportional relationships, thus consolidating their knowledge and skill in that area. Students engage in aspects of mathematical modeling as they determine whether to use surface area or volume and consider how the constraints of the situation affect the solution.
student_facing_goal: Let's explore things that are proportional to volume or surface area.

task_events.excerpt: The volume of the play structure is 4,960 in³, because the area of the base is 248 in² and 248 · 20 = 4,960.
The surface area of the play structure is 2,216 in², because the perimeter of the base is 86 in and 86 · 20 + 248 · 2 = 2,216.
The total cost is $52.98. The foam will cost $39.68 because 4,960 · 0.008 = 39.68. The vinyl will cost $13.30, because 2,216 · 0.006 = 13.30.
819 cubic inches of sand. The volume of the sand that is already there is 11,460 in³ since 1,146 · 10 = 11,460. Since 14 bags were used, each bag must have 819 cubic inches inside.

One way to think about it is that it took 14 bags to fill it to a depth of 10 inches. To fill it another 3 inches, it will take (3/10) · 14 = 4.2. Since we can't buy part of a bag, we round up to 5 bags.
One way to think about it is that the larger sandbox's dimensions are all multiplied by 1.5, so the area is multiplied by 2.25, because 1.5 · 1.5 = 2.25.
The daycare manager needs to purchase a total of 14 bags of sand. At $3.25 each, that would cost 14 · 3.25 = $45.50.
2 bottles of paint. 75/40 = 1.875

boundary_note: This lesson demands that students distinguish volume (amount of material to fill a 3D space) from surface area (amount to cover the outside), then apply that distinction strategically within real-world constraints. Students frequently confuse which measure applies to a context; the lesson teaches discrimination through problems where both quantities matter for different parts of the solution. Cost calculations then reinforce that volume and surface area scale differently (volume by the cube of a linear scale factor, surface area by the square), requiring students to reconsider their reasoning when dimensions change.

title: Grade 7 Unit 7 Practice Problems (page 50)
purpose: Not provided on this page

task_events.answer: How many days can pass before the water tank needs to be refilled?
task_events.excerpt: Write a question that could be answered by solving the equation 10 − 0.035d = 4.
Is 100 a solution of 10 − 0.035d > 4? Write a question that solving this problem could answer.

boundary_note: This page is a practice worksheet consolidating linear-equation formulation and distributive-property expansion from Unit 6. Students must both interpret equations in context (water tank scenario) and manipulate algebraic expressions with negative and fractional coefficients. Critical student errors: (1) sign mishandling when distributing negative leading coefficients; (2) misinterpreting what "a solution" means for an inequality (does the value make the inequality true?); (3) forgetting to distribute to all terms, especially when a fraction or negative is involved. The typical IM lesson structure (title, learning goals, purpose, standards) is not present on this page—only practice problems and solutions.

title: Grade 7 Unit 7 Lesson 21 — Algebraic Expression Equivalence and Proportional Relationships (Practice Problems)
purpose: NOT_VISIBLE_ON_PAGES_65-66
student_facing_goal: NOT_VISIBLE_ON_PAGES_65-66

task_events.excerpt: Complete the equation: 5x − 2.5 + 6x − 3 = _____(2x − 1). Solution: 5.5
3x − 2x + 0.5x vs 1.5x → Equivalent

task_events.answer: Not equivalent; change column B to x + 4
task_events.excerpt: 3(x + 4) − 2(x + 4) vs x + 3 → Not equivalent
Table A (x: 2, 3, 4, 5; y: 8, 12, 16, 20) matches equation 3: y = 4x
Table B (x: 3, 6, 7, 10; y: 4.5, 9, 10.5, 15) matches equation 1: y = 1.5x
Table C (x: 2, 4, 6, 12; y: 5/2, 5, 15/2, 15) matches equation 2: y = 1.25x

boundary_note: This practice page emphasizes recognizing algebraic equivalence—particularly the relationship between expanded forms and factored forms involving non-integer coefficients. Students commonly overlook the need to combine like terms before factoring (mistaking 11x − 5.5 for a form that doesn't factor cleanly), and they often make sign errors when distributing. In Problem 4, the trickiest non-equivalence is part (b): 3(x + 4) − 2(x + 4) simplifies to (x + 4), not the given x + 3. The shift to proportional relationships in Problem 5 requires extracting the multiplicative constant from a table, where reversed ratios (y/x instead of the constant itself) are a persistent error.

title: Mystery Bags
teacher_goals: Compare outcomes for different experiments, predict which experiment is more likely to produce a desired result, and justify (orally and in writing) the prediction.
Describe (orally) how we can use the outcomes from previous experiments to help determine the relative likelihood of future events.
purpose: To introduce the unit on probability, students play a game to collect data about what is inside bags and then decide which bag is most likely to give a desired result. The process of using previous results from repeated trials to inform the likelihood of future events is one way to estimate probabilities that will be revisited later (MP8).

task_events.answer: Round 1 Green: 13/16 successful matches; Round 2 Blue: 8/16; Round 3 Red: 4/16
task_events.position: Activity 1.2, main game — all three rounds
task_events.excerpt: At the end of the round, record each person's score in the table. At the end of a round, everyone in the group should record everyone's points from that round.

task_events.answer: Table tennis balls: 2 even out of 10 draws; Golf balls: 7 even out of 10; golf balls offer better chance
task_events.excerpt: Jada chooses 10 table tennis balls from the tank: 1 3 5 1 3 2 4 1 5 3. Golf balls: 1 4 5 2 6 2 2 1 4 8. To win a prize, Jada must get a ball with an even number.

student_facing_goal: Let's make predictions based on what we know.
boundary_note: Students commonly select bags based on a single lucky round instead of aggregating results across all draws. The core reasoning move—comparing relative frequencies to infer likelihood—is probabilistic inference, not arithmetic, and students must learn to distinguish patterns in small samples from trends in aggregate data.

title: Chance Experiments
teacher_goals: Comprehend and use the terms 'impossible,' 'unlikely,' 'equally likely as not,' 'likely,' and 'certain' (in spoken and written language) to describe the likelihood of an event.
Interpret percentages, fractions, and decimals that represent the likelihood of events.
Order a given set of events from least likely to most likely, and justify (orally) the reasoning.
purpose: In this lesson students investigate chance experiments. A chance experiment is something that can be repeated with results that are based on chance. The result of a chance experiment is called an outcome. They use language like 'impossible,' 'unlikely,' 'equally likely as not,' 'likely,' and 'certain' to describe the likelihood of a chance event. An event is a set of one or more outcomes in a chance experiment. Students make sense of situations and sort them into these categories based on their intuition about how often they expect an event to occur. In some cases, a value is assigned to the likelihood of an event using a fraction, decimal, or percentage chance. By comparing informal categories early and numerical quantities later, students are attending to precision when sorting the scenarios. Later, students will connect this language to more precise numerical values on their own.
student_facing_goal: Let's investigate chance.

task_events.answer: From least to greatest: light bulb still works (2/5 = 40%), car needs major repairs (0.7 = 70%), business makes money (75%)
task_events.excerpt: Order from least to greatest likelihood: business (75%), light bulb still works (2/5), car needs major repairs (0.7)

boundary_note: This lesson teaches students to name likelihood using qualitative language (impossible through certain) and connect that language to quantitative measures (fractions, decimals, percentages). Key insight: when an event has more favorable outcomes out of the total possible outcomes (e.g., 4 out of 6 vs. 2 out of 6), it is more likely. Common error: students expect randomness to be predictable in the short run, or they conflate 'equally likely as not' with exactly 50% rather than a rough middle category. This is foundational reasoning prior to formal probability definition in the next lesson.

title: What Are Probabilities?
teacher_goals: Generalize (orally) the relationship between the probability of an event and the number of possible outcomes in the sample space for an experiment in which each outcome in the sample space is equally likely.
List (in writing) the sample space of a simple chance experiment.
Use the sample space to determine the probability of an event, and express it as a fraction, decimal, or percentage.
purpose: In this lesson students begin to assign probabilities to events happening from chance experiments. They understand that the greater the probability, the more likely the event will occur. They define an outcome as a possible result for a chance experiment. They learn that the sample space is the set of all possible outcomes, and they understand that a process is called random when the outcome of an experiment is based on chance. If there are n equally likely outcomes for a chance experiment, they reason abstractly to find that the probability of each of these outcomes is 1/n (MP2).
student_facing_goal: Let's find out what's possible.

task_events.excerpt: Han rolls a standard number cube once. Sample space: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. There are 6 outcomes.
What is the probability of spinning the current day? (1/7, because today is 1 day out of 7 possible days of the week)
What is the probability that Noah gets a number greater than 5? (15/16, because there are 15 outcomes in the event and 16 outcomes)
Determine the probability a fifth person gets the same outcome as person 1. Answer: 1/15 since there is one out of 15 possible things.

boundary_note: The core is calculating probability as favorable_outcomes / total_outcomes for equally likely events. Students commonly fixate on the numerator ('I can win 2 ways!') without weighting it proportionally against the full sample space—and they assume sample spaces are always knowable before an experiment rather than needing to be discovered through repeated sampling.

title: Estimating Probabilities through Repeated Experiments
student_facing_goal: Understand that probability measures the likelihood of an event, expressed as a number from 0 to 1, and learn to calculate probabilities when all outcomes are equally likely.
teacher_goals: Develop student understanding that probability measures likelihood on a scale from 0 (impossible) to 1 (certain)
Teach students to identify and list all possible outcomes (sample space) for a chance experiment
Help students calculate the probability of a single outcome using the formula: probability = 1/n when outcomes are equally likely
Connect probability language to real-world chance situations
purpose: This lesson introduces foundational probability concepts through the formal definitions of probability, sample space, and equally likely outcomes. Students learn that probability can be calculated systematically when all outcomes have the same likelihood.

task_events.excerpt: On TUESDAY, the mother writes the word on a piece of paper and cuts it up so that each letter is on a separate piece of paper. She mixes up the papers and picks one. What is the probability that she will choose the piece of paper with the letter Y?

task_events.answer: A, D, E, F, H, I, M, N, O, R, S, T, U, W, Y (15 letters)
task_events.excerpt: What letters are possible to teach using this method? (There are 15.)

task_events.answer: Any 4 of: B, C, G, J, K, L, P, Q, V, X, Z
task_events.excerpt: What are 4 letters that can't be taught using this method?
List the sample space for each chance experiment. a. flipping a coin
b. selecting a random season of the year

task_events.answer: Sunday, Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday, Friday, Saturday
task_events.excerpt: c. selecting a random day of the week
A computer randomly selects a letter from the alphabet. a. How many different outcomes are in the sample space?
b. What is the probability the computer produces the first letter of your first name?
What is the probability of selecting a random month of the year and getting a month that starts with the letter 'J'?

boundary_note: This lesson teaches theoretical probability using equally likely outcomes and the sample space formula P(event) = 1/n. Students frequently struggle to systematically enumerate ALL outcomes without duplication; the cool-down exploits this by asking them to find letters in weekday names, a non-obvious sample space. Common misconception: confusing the size of the sample space with the probability of an outcome. The lesson is purely formal (theoretical probability based on logical enumeration), not empirical; actual "repeated experiments" would belong to a follow-up lesson to test whether observed frequencies approach theoretical probabilities.

title: More Estimating Probabilities
student_facing_goal: Let's estimate some probabilities.
teacher_goals: Describe (orally and in writing) reasons why the relative frequency from an experiment may not exactly match the actual probability of the event.
Recognize that sometimes the outcomes in a sample space are not equally likely.
Use the results from a repeated experiment to estimate the probability of an event, and justify (orally and in writing) the estimate.
purpose: In this lesson students compare the results from running actual trials of an experiment to the calculated expected probabilities. They also use their data to see that additional trials usually produce more accurate results as minor differences even out after many trials. In the first activity, students spin four different spinners to see that the outcomes in a sample space may not be equally likely, and they examine the spinners to construct arguments about why some outcomes are more likely than others. In the next activity, students draw blocks out of a bag repeatedly and use the relative frequency to estimate the probability of getting a green block.

task_events.excerpt: Spin your spinner 10 times, and record your outcomes.
What fraction of your 10 spins landed on the number 3?

task_events.answer: Spinner A: 1/4; Spinner B: 1/12; Spinner C: 1/12; Spinner D: 1/4
task_events.excerpt: For each spinner, what is the probability that it lands on the number 3? Explain or show your reasoning.

task_events.answer: Spinner A: 2/3; Spinner B: 11/12; Spinner C: 11/12; Spinner D: 3/4
task_events.excerpt: For each spinner, what is the probability that it lands on something other than the number 3?

task_events.answer: varies; one block drawn per trial, color recorded
task_events.excerpt: Take 1 block out of the bag and record whether or not it is green.
I think the probability is 7/12 since we got 7 green blocks after 12 trials.
Andre picks a block out of a bag 60 times and notes that 43 of them were green. What should Andre estimate for the probability of picking out a green block from this bag?

A textbook has 428 pages numbered in order starting with 1. You flip to a random page in the book in a way that is equally likely to stop at any of the pages. What is the probability that you turn to page 45?
What is the probability that you turn to an even-numbered page?
If you repeat this experiment 50 times, about how many times do you expect you will turn to an even-numbered page?

boundary_note: Students often conflate experimental probability (what happened in their trials) with theoretical probability (what should happen based on the sample space). The key insight here is that unequal sections or unequal sample outcomes mean equal likelihoods do not hold. A major misconception: students may think that if they spun a spinner 10 times and never saw a particular outcome, the probability of that outcome is 0 — they need to understand that small sample sizes produce unreliable estimates. With more trials, frequencies stabilize toward theoretical values.

title: Estimating Probabilities Using Simulation
teacher_goals: Comprehend that the term 'simulation' (in written and spoken language) refers to a chance experiment used to represent a real-world situation.
Describe (orally and in writing) a simple chance experiment that could be used to simulate a real-world event.
Perform a simulation, and use the results to estimate the probability of a simple event in a real-world situation (using words and other representations).
purpose: This lesson introduces the idea of simulation. Groups of students use and create different simulations, chance experiments that are designed to approximate the probability of a real-world event. Students build on their work with calculating relative frequencies of experiments to calculate frequencies using a simulation. In this lesson, the outcomes students are tracking are from an experiment designed to represent the outcome of some other experiment that would be harder to study directly. Students see that a simulation depends on the experiment used in the simulation being a reasonable stand-in for the actual experiment of interest (MP4).
student_facing_goal: Let's simulate real-world situations.

task_events.answer: Track cumulative fraction over 15 simulation trials; e.g., day 3: 2/3 ≈ 0.67
task_events.excerpt: Calculate the total number of days and the cumulative fraction of days that Diego has had to wait so far.
After doing the simulation 4 times, a group finds that Diego had to wait 3 times. What is an estimate for the probability Diego has to wait?
After doing the simulation 20 times, this group finds that Diego has had to wait 15 times. What is an estimate for the probability?
Based on the graph, estimate the probability that Diego will have to wait more than 1 minute to cross the crosswalk.

task_events.answer: Map 30% probability to integers 1-15 out of 1-50; if number falls in that range, create rainy day
task_events.excerpt: In a video game, the chance of rain each day is always 30%. Explain how you could use a random integer between 1 and 50 to simulate the weather.

boundary_note: Students use simulations to estimate probabilities of events hard to observe directly, performing many trials, computing cumulative relative frequency, and recognizing that convergence requires substantial data collection. Common misconceptions: treating small samples (3 out of 4 trials) as definitive probability; designing simulations where the simulation outcomes fail to match real-world probabilities; underestimating how many trials are needed to stabilize an estimate.

title: Lesson 6 Practice Problems (Unit 8, page 122)
purpose: Not provided in PDF

task_events.answer: A circle divided into sections: 3/4 blue and labeled 'rain', 1/4 white and labeled 'no rain'
task_events.excerpt: Draw a spinner you could use to simulate this probability.

task_events.answer: Put 4 marbles in a bag: 3 blue (rain) and 1 white (no rain). Draw repeatedly.
task_events.excerpt: Describe another way you could simulate this probability.

task_events.answer: A standard die has only 6 outcomes and cannot produce all 10 required by the experiment.
task_events.excerpt: An experiment will produce one of 10 different outcomes with equal probability for each. Why would using a standard number cube to simulate the experiment be a bad choice?

task_events.answer: Selecting action figures is not a chance experiment; people buy favorites, so random simulation does not apply.
task_events.excerpt: Selecting a hero action figure to buy is not a chance experiment. People will buy their favorites, so it is not done at random.

student_facing_goal: Not provided in PDF (practice problems only)
boundary_note: This is a practice section testing three critical ideas: (1) matching simulation outcome counts to experiment requirements, (2) ensuring fair/random selection mechanisms, and (3) recognizing when real-world contexts violate the randomness assumption. Students often conflate "we can draw randomly from a bag" with "this random model matches what actually happens"—a fundamental confusion between the formalism and its application domain.

title: Keeping Track of All Possible Outcomes
student_facing_goal: Interpret or create a list, table, or tree diagram to represent sample spaces of compound events and use them to calculate probabilities; design and justify simulations of compound probability situations.
teacher_goals: Interpret or create a list, table, or tree diagram that represents the sample space of a compound event, and use the sample space to write the probability for an event.
Describe a multi-step experiment that could be used to simulate a compound event in a real-world situation, and justify that it represents the situation.
purpose: This checkpoint assesses students' ability to enumerate compound sample spaces systematically and apply the probability formula (favorable outcomes / total outcomes). It also evaluates whether students can design valid simulations that accurately represent given probability conditions.

task_events.excerpt: A cube is labeled so that when it is rolled, it shows only 1, 2, or 3 with equal chances of each one coming up. This cube is rolled, then 2 different coins are flipped. a. How many outcomes are in the sample space?
What is the probability that the cube shows 2, and the coins land showing the same face? Explain your reasoning.

boundary_note: Students often fail to enumerate sample spaces completely and systematically—missing outcomes (undercounting to 6 or 8) or double-counting. For the probability calculation, students must locate favorable outcomes from a tree/list structure and apply favorable/total reasoning. The simulation design task (Item 2) requires students to map a 25% game probability to a 50% coin outcome (requires two coins, both heads); students commonly select simulations that don't match the probability structure. Note: CCSS standards and full lesson purpose are not visible on these checkpoint pages; refer to the lesson introduction for complete context.

title: Multi-step Experiments
student_facing_goal: Let's look at probabilities of experiments that have multiple steps.
teacher_goals: Choose a method for representing the sample space of a compound event, and justify (orally) the choice.
Use the sample space to determine the probability of a compound event, and explain (orally, in writing, and using other representations) the reasoning.
purpose: In this lesson, students continue writing out the sample spaces for chance experiments that have multiple steps and also begin using those sample spaces to calculate the probability of certain events. Students must reason quantitatively and abstractly to make sense of the situations and represent the sample spaces mathematically.

task_events.excerpt: What is the probability of getting: Green (G) and 3? There is only 1 outcome that has green and 3, and there are 20 equally likely outcomes in the sample space.
A number cube is rolled and a coin is flipped. What is the probability of getting tails and a 6?
Suppose you roll two number cubes. What is the probability of getting: Both cubes showing the same number?

Imagine there are 5 cards. On one side they look the same and on the other side they are colored red, yellow, green, white, and black. What are the outcomes in the sample space? How many outcomes are there?
You get a white card and a red card (in either order)? There are 2 outcomes that have those 2 cards (RW and WR), and the outcomes in the sample space are equally likely.
You get a blue card? Answer: 0, because there are no blue cards in the deck. If the outcome is impossible, then its probability is 0.

Lin plays a game that involves a standard number cube and a deck of ten cards numbered 1 through 10. If both the cube and card have the same number, Lin gets another turn. What is the probability that Lin gets another turn?

boundary_note: This lesson marks the shift from simple one-step probability to compound (multi-step) experiments. The core work is careful enumeration: students must distinguish that rolling a 2 then a 3 differs from rolling a 3 then a 2, and they must consistently represent all branches (trees, tables, lists). Common pitfalls include undercounting outcomes when dice/coins are involved, confusing "with replacement" and "without replacement" (the Pick a Card activity), and incorrectly assigning probability to impossible events. Students are also developing the principle that P(event) = (# favorable outcomes) / (# total equally likely outcomes), a foundational move in discrete probability that requires both counting precision and reasoning about what "equally likely" means in a compound situation.

title: Designing Simulations
teacher_goals: Describe a multi-step experiment that could be used to simulate a compound event in a real-world situation, and justify (orally and in writing) that it represents the situation.
Perform a simulation to estimate the probability of a compound event, and explain (orally and in writing) how the simulation could be improved.
purpose: In this lesson, students see that the probability of compound events can also be estimated using simulations. The culmination activity has each group design a simulation for a different real-world situation, strategically choosing tools like number cubes, spinners, or blocks to model chance experiments while practicing precise mathematical communication about how their simulation is conducted and what outcomes represent.

task_events.excerpt: Find the value of each expression mentally. [Four expressions all equal 3.6; exact expressions not readable from PDF]

task_events.position: Activity 2, simulation table and group response
task_events.excerpt: I estimate the probability to be 1/3 since 3 of our group's 9 simulations had at least 2 white offspring.
After doing this 10 times, 1 of them had this result, so the probability should be about 0.1.
After doing this 10 times, this never happened. Since it is possible, the estimate should be between 0 and 0.1.
After doing this 10 times, this happened 6 times. The dealership should be concerned since having defective brakes is a big problem.
After doing this 10 times, this happened 7 times. There is a probability of about 0.7 that we will get the prize.
After doing this 10 times, this happened once. There is a probability of about 0.1 that the hospital will not have enough space for the animals.

boundary_note: The core challenge is understanding that a simulation works only when the simulated events preserve the probabilities of the real-world events. Students often struggle to match simulation tools (coins, spinners, number cubes, bags) to probability values and may incorrectly assume that simulations give exact answers rather than estimates that improve with more trials.

title: Proportional Relationships: Area and Perimeter of Squares
student_facing_goal: Investigate whether the relationship between the area and perimeter of a square is proportional, and explain your reasoning using a coordinate graph.
teacher_goals: Understand that not all relationships between geometric quantities are proportional
Recognize that a proportional relationship must pass through the origin on a coordinate graph
Connect algebraic and graphical representations of relationships
purpose: This practice problem asks students to examine the relationship between area and perimeter of a square by plotting ordered pairs on a coordinate graph and determining whether the relationship is proportional. Students learn that while both area and perimeter relate to the side length of a square, their relationship to each other (area vs. perimeter) is non-linear and therefore not proportional.

task_events.excerpt: On a graph of the area of a square vs. its perimeter, a few points are plotted: (8,4), (12,9), (16,16), (20,25)
