task_events.answer: Any two additional points satisfying area = (perimeter/4)², such as (4,1), (24,36), (28,49)
task_events.excerpt: Add at least 2 more ordered pairs to the graph.

task_events.answer: No, there is not a proportional relationship.
task_events.excerpt: There is not a proportional relationship between area and perimeter. Sample reasoning: When graphed, the ordered pairs do not lie on a line that passes through the origin.

boundary_note: Students often expect all relationships between quantities derived from the same geometric object to be proportional. This lesson reveals a critical limitation: while both perimeter and area increase with side length, the area grows quadratically (as the square of perimeter/4) and therefore fails the proportionality test—the line does not pass through the origin. Recognizing this requires students to distinguish between algebraic structure (which quantities relate linearly?) and geometric intuition.

title: Larger Populations
student_facing_goal: Let's compare larger groups.
teacher_goals: I can explain why it may be useful to gather data on a sample of a population.
When I read or hear a statistical question, I can name the population of interest and give an example of a sample for that population.
purpose: This lesson introduces the idea of using data from a sample of a population when it is impractical or impossible to gather data from every individual in the population under study. A population includes all the individuals related to a question, and a sample is a part of the population. Students consider how information from students in their class might apply to larger populations, then identify possible samples from populations given a statistical question. They must also make sense of a problem in which the population is too large to collect data from and use samples to solve it.

task_events.answer: Varies by student; example: preferred name 5 letters, last name 8 letters
task_events.excerpt: How many letters are in the name you prefer to be called? How many letters are in your last name?

task_events.answer: Difference in means: 1.1 letters; expressed as multiple of MAD: approximately 0.4
task_events.excerpt: The mean number of letters in our last names was larger by 1.1 letters, which is about 0.4 times the MAD. This means that although the last names had a higher mean number of letters, the two groups were not very distinct.

task_events.answer: Fall mean: 23 dollars; Spring mean: 24 dollars
task_events.excerpt: Find the mean amount of money made in the fall and in the spring. Solution: 23 dollars in the fall, 24 dollars in the spring

task_events.answer: Fall MAD: 2.8 dollars; Spring MAD: 2.6 dollars; Difference in means expressed as multiple: 0.36 MADs
task_events.excerpt: The MAD for the fall data is 2.8 dollars. The MAD for the spring data is 2.6 dollars. Express the difference in means as a multiple of the larger MAD. Solution: 0.36 MADs, since 1 ÷ 2.8 ≈ 0.36

boundary_note: This lesson is fundamentally about developing vocabulary and understanding why samples are useful, not about computing statistics. The core misconception students have is assuming that data from a small sample will always match the whole population exactly, or conversely, dismissing a sample because it looks different. Students often struggle to distinguish between a representative sample and a biased one. The lesson uses mean and MAD calculations as tools to make comparisons, but these are secondary to the primary goal of reasoning about populations and samples. Students at this level may conflate "sample" with "small group" rather than understanding it as a deliberate method for gathering information about a larger population when complete data is unreasonable to collect.

title: Section C: Sampling
teacher_goals: Describe methods to obtain a random sample from a population, and explain why it is representative of the population.
Explain why samples are necessary and describe a sample and population for a given statistical question.
purpose: In this section, students examine samples taken from a larger population and use randomness to select a sample that is less biased than other methods. They begin by comparing two small populations based on their measures of center and variability. Then students consider much larger populations to motivate the need to sample to obtain data. This leads to considering how some samples may be more representative of the population than others and the idea that random selection is more likely to produce representative samples.
boundary_note: This lesson introduces sampling as a foundational statistical idea. Students move from comparing two complete small populations (gymnastics and volleyball team heights shown in dot plots) to understanding why sampling becomes necessary for large populations. The critical misconception is that convenience samples (like asking friends) represent populations well; the key learning is that random selection reduces bias and produces more representative samples. The lesson is conceptual rather than computational—students explain why sampling matters and discuss methods, not calculate numerical summaries.

title: Estimating Probabilities through Repeated Experiments
teacher_goals: Describe (orally and in writing) patterns observed on a table or graph that show the relative frequency for a repeated experiment.
Generalize (orally) that the cumulative relative frequency approaches the probability of the event as an experiment is repeated many times.
Generate possible results that would or would not be surprising for a repeated experiment, and justify (orally) the reasoning.
purpose: In this lesson students roll a number cube many times and calculate the cumulative fraction of the trials for which an event occurs to see that in the long run this relative frequency approaches the probability of the event. Students must examine the structure of the repeated trials to recognize the connection between long-run frequency and probability (MP8). They also see that the relative frequency of a chance event will not usually exactly match the actual probability.

task_events.answer: 0.5, 0.75, 0.33, 0.67, 0.25 located at specified positions between 0 and 1
task_events.excerpt: Locate and label these numbers on the number line: 0.5, 0.75, 0.33, 0.67, 0.25

task_events.answer: Sample space {1,2,3,4,5,6}; P(win)=2/6=1/3; cumulative fractions show convergence toward 1/3
task_events.excerpt: Mai plays a game in which she only wins if she rolls a 1 or a 2 with a standard number cube. What is the probability Mai will win?

task_events.answer: Not necessarily heads up; the probability of heads on the next flip remains 1/2 regardless of prior outcomes
task_events.excerpt: You've flipped the coin 3 times, and it has landed heads up once. The cumulative fraction of heads is currently 1/3.

task_events.answer: Probability fiction ≈ 4/10 or 0.4; estimate of DVDs per 100 patrons ≈ 20
task_events.excerpt: A librarian is curious about the habits of the library's patrons. He records the type of item that the first 10 patrons check out from the library.

task_events.answer: Approximately 9-10 ducks with red marks (0.06 × 160); approximately 3 or fewer with blue marks (0.02 × 160)
task_events.excerpt: A carnival game has 160 rubber ducks floating in a pool. The person playing the game takes out one duck and looks at it.

task_events.answer: No; 10 trials is too small a sample to establish that the probability differs from 1/2
task_events.excerpt: Lin wants to know if flipping a quarter really does have a probability of 1/2 of landing heads up, so she flips a quarter 10 times.

task_events.answer: a) Approximately 5 times (1/4 x 20); b) Approximately 20 times (1/4 x 80)
task_events.excerpt: A spinner has four equal sections, with one letter from the word MATH in each section. You spin the spinner 20 times. About how many times do you expect it will land on A?

task_events.answer: Approximately 0.65 (reading from graph of fraction of wins after 40 spins)
task_events.excerpt: A spinner is spun 40 times for a game. Here is a graph showing the fraction of games that are wins under some conditions. Estimate the probability of a spin winning this game based on the graph.

boundary_note: This lesson teaches the Law of Large Numbers: experimental probability approaches theoretical probability only after many trials. The central misconception is that students believe small samples (e.g., 10 coin flips) can prove or disprove a theory, or that prior experimental results influence future independent events. Students must work through repeated data collection to experience viscerally that convergence is gradual and uncertain at small sample sizes.

title: Practice Problems: Surface Area of a Staircase
purpose: Teacher-facing lesson materials (purpose, learning goals, standards) not present on this practice-problems page. This page contains Problem 5 from the lesson's practice set.
student_facing_goal: What is the surface area of a staircase where each horizontal step is 10 inches long and 25 inches wide, and each step is 5 inches above the previous one?

task_events.excerpt: A small staircase is made so that the horizontal piece of each step is 10 inches long and 25 inches wide. Each step is 5 inches above the previous one. What is the surface area of this staircase?

boundary_note: This staircase surface-area problem requires spatial decomposition of a 3D composite figure into rectangular faces, then summing their areas. Students often struggle with visualizing which surfaces are exposed (and thus count) versus which are internal, and with tracking all faces systematically—a coordination burden beyond the arithmetic vocabulary alone.

title: Grade 7 Unit 8 Practice Problems (pages 69–70) — Surface Area
purpose: These are practice problems; the lesson purpose is not shown in these pages.

task_events.excerpt: About how many square inches of wood does she need to build this birdhouse before the circular hole is drilled? [Rectangular prism: 7.5 in × 5.5 in × 10.25 in with 9.25 in height]
Select all the situations where knowing the surface area of an object would be more useful than knowing its volume. [Roof tiles, greenhouse windows, candy wrapper paper, couch cover fabric]

boundary_note: This PDF excerpt contains only practice problems (pages 69–70), not a full lesson. The complete lesson page with learning goals, lesson purpose, and CCSS standards alignment is not included in this document. Problem 5 requires students to decompose a 3D figure into rectangular faces and sum their areas, accounting for a subtracted circular hole. Problem 6 is conceptual, asking students to discriminate contexts where surface area (not volume) matters — a reasoning task with no numerical computation. Neither task includes explicit instructional structure typical of an IM lesson opening.
student_facing_goal: LESSON OPENING NOT PRESENT IN THESE PRACTICE PROBLEM PAGES.

title: More about Sampling Variability
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally) a distribution of sample means and the distribution of the population.
Generalize that an estimate for the center of a population distribution is more likely to be accurate when it is based on a larger random sample.
Interpret (orally and in writing) a dot plot that displays the means of multiple samples from the same population.
purpose: Students explore how sample size affects the variability and accuracy of sample means as estimates for a population mean by examining dot plots composed of means from multiple samples.
student_facing_goal: Let's compare samples from the same population.

task_events.excerpt: Calculate the mean reaction time for your sample. (Sample response: 0.43 seconds)
For each show, use the dot plot to estimate the population mean. Trivia the Game Show: 58 years old
Science Experiments YOU Can Do: 12 years old
Learning to Read: 6 years old
Which dot plots are likely to represent means from samples of size 10? Dot Plot 2 (same center, less variability)

boundary_note: This lesson develops the principle that larger sample sizes produce less variable sample means, yielding more accurate population estimates. Students frequently confuse what each dot in a distribution plot represents—a single data point versus the mean of an entire sample.

title: Comparing Populations Using Samples
student_facing_goal: Let's compare different populations using samples.
teacher_goals: Calculate the difference between the mean or median of two samples from different populations, and express it as a multiple of the MAD or IQR.
Interpret a pair of box plots, including the amount of visual overlap between the two distributions.
Justify (orally and in writing) whether there is likely to be a meaningful difference between two populations based on a sample from each population.
purpose: In this lesson, students compare two groups based on samples from each group, building on earlier activities where they compared populations using measures of center and variability. Students learn that a small difference between sample measures of center, relative to their variability, does not mean the populations are meaningfully different, but a large difference—relative to variability—suggests the populations likely differ. The lesson establishes a decision rule: if sample measures of center are at least 2 MADs (or 2 IQRs) apart, there is likely a meaningful difference between the populations.

task_events.excerpt: All samples have mean absolute deviation about 2.8 pounds. Express difference between greatest (9.2) and least (5.2) sample means as multiple of MAD: 4 ÷ 2.8 ≈ 1.43
Region 1 median 0.64% carbon (IQR 0.05%), Region 2 median 0.47% (IQR 0.03%). Difference in medians 0.17%. Express as multiple of larger IQR: 0.17 ÷ 0.05 = 3.4
Students mean 5.7 movies (MAD 0.76), Teachers mean 2.7 movies (MAD 0.9). Difference in means 3 movies. Express as multiple of larger MAD: 3 ÷ 0.9 ≈ 3.33. Is there meaningful difference? Yes.

boundary_note: This lesson establishes a quantitative decision rule for comparing populations via samples: the difference in means (or medians) must be at least 2 MADs (or 2 IQRs) apart to conclude a meaningful difference likely exists. Students often fail to account for sampling variability and may think any difference is meaningful, or they misinterpret visual overlap in dot plots and box plots as contradicting a real difference between populations.

title: Comparing Populations with Friends
student_facing_goal: Let's ask important questions to compare groups.
teacher_goals: Coordinate (orally) visual displays of data with descriptions of shape, measures of center, and measures of spread.
Determine what information is needed to solve problems about using samples to compare populations. Ask questions to elicit that information.
Explain (orally and in writing) whether two populations are likely to be meaningfully different using center and spread.
purpose: Students continue to practice comparing populations by using samples from each population. Outside the classroom, people who wish to compare groups will not typically have all of the useful statistics presented to them in a nice package, so they must determine what information to gather, then work through the comparison process. In this lesson, students are paired so that one student is presented with a situation and question while the other student has information to help answer the question. By this part of the unit, students should be more precise in their language about statistics involving samples and populations.

task_events.answer: Yes, meaningful difference; medians are more than 2 IQRs apart (112.5 vs 65, difference = 47.5)
task_events.excerpt: The medians are more than 2 IQRs apart (112.5 - 65 > 2 · 22.5), so there is a meaningful difference between the flavors.

task_events.answer: No, not a meaningful difference; means differ by 3.5, which is less than 2 × MAD (2 × 7 = 14)
task_events.excerpt: The difference in means is less than 2 MADs apart (83 - 79 < 2 · 7).

task_events.answer: Median must be > 97,500 or < 17,500 to be meaningfully different (2 IQRs from 87,500)
task_events.excerpt: The median salary for Summit Slope Systems must be greater than 2 IQRs above or less than 2 IQRs below Acme's median
The difference in means is 2.57 faulty products per day (since 6.57 - 4 = 2.57), which is greater than twice the MAD
What are the values for these same characteristics for the data in the dot plot?

task_events.answer: Male humpbacks: 44.6 feet; Female humpbacks: 50.9 feet
task_events.excerpt: Estimate the median lengths of male and female humpback whales based on these samples.

boundary_note: This lesson teaches a critical judgment in statistics: when a difference between population samples is large enough to matter. Students learn to use the informal rule "2 × spread" (either 2 IQRs or 2 MADs) as a heuristic for meaningful difference. The key mistake is treating differences in raw numbers as automatic evidence of real population difference without accounting for variability. Students often neglect to check spread or fail to recognize that a difference of, say, 3 units might be negligible if the data varies by 10 units within each group.

title: Memory Test
teacher_goals: Describe (orally) connections between sampling and probability
Generate a random sample and use it to make inferences (in writing) about the population
Justify (orally and in writing) whether a given method produces a random sample
student_facing_goal: Let's put it all together.
purpose: This lesson is optional. It gives students a chance to use the material they have learned in the unit with the final goal of comparing two populations, but it may be shortened or skipped due to time constraints. In this lesson, students apply what they have learned about probability, sampling, and comparing populations to analyze two data sets. Half of the class works with one data set while the other half of the class works with another. Students choose their own tools for selecting a sample at random (MP5) and calculate the mean, MAD, and a proportion to summarize their sample (MP2). Then students compare their results with a partner that had the other data set to construct an argument for whether there is a meaningful difference between the sets (MP3).

task_events.excerpt: When you select the first value for your random sample, what is the probability that it comes from Springfield Middle School? (20/100 = 1/5 = 0.2)
If you take a random sample of size 10, how many scores would you expect to be from Springfield Middle School? (Expect around 2 scores)
Estimate this measure of center for your population based on your sample. (The mean is 44.6)
Calculate the measure of variability for your sample that goes with the measure of center. (The MAD is 10.92)

boundary_note: Students integrate sampling, probability calculation, and the two-MAD rule for comparing populations. Key struggles: (1) understanding sampling variability—random samples from identical populations naturally yield different statistics, not due to error; (2) misinterpreting the comparison threshold—failing to reach 2 MADs means insufficient evidence of difference, not proof the populations are identical.

title: Grade 7, Unit 8, Lesson 21 (Practice Problems page)
purpose: Visible page (193) contains practice and review problems. The actual Lesson Purpose, Learning Goals, and CCSS standards information appears on earlier pages not provided in this excerpt.

task_events.excerpt: After the discount, how much would the book cost? Solution: $15.30, because 18 – 2.7 = 15.3
Match each expression in the first list with an equivalent expression. A. 6(x + 2y) - 2(y - 2x) matches 2. 10(x + y)
Angles C and D are complementary. The ratio of measure C to D is 2:3. Find each angle. Solution: C: 36°, D: 54°

boundary_note: Page 193 contains practice problems involving proportional reasoning (discounts with decimals), algebraic simplification under the distributive property, and angle relationships using ratio constraints. Problems reference earlier units (Unit 6 Lesson 21, Unit 7 Lesson 2), indicating spiraled review within the Unit 8 problem set. The full lesson introduction with explicit Learning Goals, Lesson Purpose, and CCSS alignment codes is not visible on the provided page; it appears earlier in the lesson materials.

title: Cost of a Meal
teacher_goals: Comprehend the term "spreadsheet" as a computer program in which data is arranged in rows and columns of a grid and can be manipulated and used in calculations.
Create formulas in a spreadsheet to perform repeated calculations.
Use a spreadsheet to calculate the cost per serving of all ingredients in a recipe.
purpose: In this optional lesson, students gain experience using a spreadsheet program to perform repeated calculations. After exploring how spreadsheets work, students calculate the cost per serving of all the ingredients in a recipe. This can be a recipe students bring from home or a recipe provided by the teacher. Students make use of structure as they explore the syntax of how formulas are written in spreadsheet programs (MP7). Students reason quantitatively while deciding what formula they need for each column in their spreadsheet (MP2). This lesson relies on skills developed in Unit 2.

task_events.excerpt: Type each formula into the cells of a spreadsheet program and press enter. Record what the cell displays. =40-32
=14/7. Division is represented with the slash character in spreadsheet programs.
=0.5*6. Multiplication is represented with the asterisk symbol in spreadsheet programs.
Predict what will happen if you type the formula =A1*C1 into cell C2. Cell C2 displays the number 16 because it is multiplying the value in cell A1 times the value in cell C1 and 8 × 2 = 16.

Delete the formula in cell A1 and replace it with the number 100. Cell C2 updates to display the number 200, even though nothing was done to that cell, because it is still multiplying the values in cell A1 and C1, and A1 is now 100 instead of 8, so 100 × 2 = 200.

Copy cell C2 and paste it into cell D2. Cell D2 displays the number 15.3 because it is multiplying the value in cell B1 times the value in cell D1, and (5.1) × 3 = 15.3. When the formula in C2 was copied and pasted into D2, it moved one cell to the right, so the letters in the formula automatically adjusted.

task_events.position: Activity 1.2, Student Task Statement, item 4; Student Response sample spreadsheet
task_events.excerpt: Type a formula into the first empty cell below your last ingredient in column K to calculate the total cost per serving for all of the ingredients in your recipe. Sample formula: =sum(K2:K9) resulting in Total: $1.43

boundary_note: This lesson is optional and introduces spreadsheet tools as a means to automate multi-step calculations involving proportional reasoning (7.RP.A.3). The pedagogical core is unit conversion and cost calculation: given recipe quantities and market prices in different units, students reason about what operations belong in each column and how to chain them. Students often struggle with the structural setup (which columns need formulas vs. manual data entry), with understanding relative cell references (how A1, B1 adjust when copied), and with tracking units across multiple conversion steps. The lesson does not drill fraction or ratio arithmetic itself; it treats spreadsheet formula-writing as the vehicle for practicing proportional reasoning.

title: Costs of Running a Restaurant
student_facing_goal: Let's explore how much running a restaurant costs.
teacher_goals: Create an equation to represent certain expenses for a restaurant, and interpret (orally and in writing) the solution.
Determine whether or not a relationship is proportional and explain (orally) the reasoning.
Determine whether or not a restaurant is making a profit using estimates of ongoing expenses, number of meals sold, average price per meal, and average cost per meal.
purpose: In this optional lesson, students apply expressions and signed numbers to the context of balancing projected income and expenses for a restaurant. Then students perform a cost analysis on using reusable or disposable plates and forks. Through this lesson, students make sense of situations involved in running a restaurant (MP1). As students make decisions about running a restaurant, they construct arguments and critique others' reasoning (MP3). This lesson relies on skills developed in Unit 5, Unit 6, and Unit 8.

task_events.excerpt: Estimate how many customers you think your restaurant will serve per day. Explain your reasoning.
write an equation for the total cost, d, of using disposable plates and forks for every customer for n days
Use your equation to predict the cost of using disposable plates and forks for 1 year. Explain any assumptions you make with this calculation.
How much would it cost to buy enough reusable plates and forks for your predicted number of customers per day?
write an expression that represents the total cost, r, of buying and washing reusable plates and forks after d days
If 10.75d + 876 = 8095.70, then d = 671.6. How many days can the reusable plates and forks be used for the same cost that you calculated for using disposable plates and forks for 1 year?

task_events.answer: Yes, the restaurant is making a profit
task_events.excerpt: students predict whether or not a restaurant will make a profit but do not initially have enough information to do so
The restaurant needs to serve at least 647 meals each week to make a profit.

boundary_note: This lesson hinges on distinguishing proportional relationships (y = kx, passing through the origin) from linear but non-proportional relationships (y = mx + b with b ≠ 0). Students must interpret the y-intercept as a fixed/startup cost and the slope as a per-unit variable cost, then use these meanings to make economic decisions. A common misconception is that all linear equations represent proportional relationships. The lesson also demands careful unit coordination (mixing per-meal, per-day, and per-year costs) and interpretation of equation solutions in context—interpreting 671.6 days as 'about 1 year 10 months' requires sense-making beyond symbol manipulation.

title: Restaurant Floor Plan
teacher_goals: Choose an appropriate scale and create a scale drawing for a restaurant floor plan.
Use proportional reasoning to solve problems about the area or volume of different elements of a floor plan and explain (orally) the solution method.
purpose: In this optional lesson, students create a scale drawing of the floor plan for a restaurant and solve problems involving proportional reasoning about the area or volume of different elements within the floor plan.
student_facing_goal: Let's design the floor plan for a restaurant.

task_events.answer: Table A: 3 customers; Table B: 3–4 customers; Table C: 4–5 customers
task_events.excerpt: Restaurant owners say it is good for each customer to have about 300 in² of space at their table. How many customers would you seat at each table?
It is good to have about 15 ft² of floor space per customer in the dining area. How many customers would you like to be able to seat at one time?
What size and shape dining area would be large enough to fit that many customers?

task_events.answer: rectangle 15 cm wide × 20 cm long at scale 1 cm = 2 ft
task_events.excerpt: Select an appropriate scale, and create a scale drawing of the outline of your dining area.

task_events.answer: Table A: 1.25 cm square; Table B: 2 cm × 1 cm; Table C: 1.75 cm diameter circle
task_events.excerpt: Using the same scale, what size would each of the tables from the first question appear on your scale drawing?

task_events.answer: circle with radius 30 cm centered at food pickup point (representing 60 ft maximum distance)
task_events.excerpt: Decide where the food pick-up area will be, and draw it on your scale drawing. Next, show the limit of how far away tables can be positioned from this place.

task_events.answer: refrigerator 86.25 ft² (67.5 ft² rect + 18.75 ft² triangle); freezer 48.75 ft² (30 ft² rect + 18.75 ft² triangle)
task_events.excerpt: The walk-in refrigerator covers an area of 86.25 ft² because it can be decomposed into a rectangle with an area of 67.5 ft² and a triangle with an area of 18.75 ft².
Let's assume that the refrigerator and freezer shown in the drawings are also 7 ft tall, like the ones given in the example. That means their volumes are 603.75 ft³ and 341.25 ft³
In the example, the refrigerator costs $0.21 per cubic foot to operate for one month because 150 ÷ (10 · 10 · 7)
The refrigerator in the drawing would cost about $126.79 to operate for one month because 603.75 · 0.21 = 126.7875

boundary_note: The lesson combines scale drawing construction with proportional reasoning about area and cost in a realistic context. Students must maintain consistent scale across all elements and recognize that multiple valid floor plans can satisfy the spacing constraints. Key teaching point from the lesson: students should understand there is no single "correct" answer. Common pitfalls: choosing impractical scales, confusing actual vs. scaled distances, and failing to apply the inverse scale factor when translating measurements from the drawing back to actual dimensions.

title: How Crowded Is This Neighborhood?
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast the density of uniformly distributed dots in squares.
Create an equation and a graph that represent the proportional relationship between the area of a square and the number of dots enclosed by the square.
Interpret the constant of proportionality in models of housing per square kilometer or population of people per square kilometer.
purpose: This optional lesson involves a sequence of four activities that prepare and introduce students to the concept of population density. The first three activities introduce population density as an application of proportional reasoning. The final activity is optional because it compares the population densities of large cities without a visual representation of the populations. The way the lesson develops helps students make sense of the somewhat abstract idea of density in concrete terms: they start by comparing the number of dots distributed in squares and move on to houses in different neighborhoods, finally comparing the number of people living in different cities. Unlike speed or unit pricing, density is not likely to be familiar to students, so it provides an opportunity to make sense of an unfamiliar situation by thinking about familiar quantities in a new way (MP1). As students move through different representations of density they expand their quantitative reasoning (MP2).
student_facing_goal: Let's see how proportional relationships apply to where people live.

task_events.answer: 16, 64, 16, 64 dots per square inch for squares A, B, C, D respectively
task_events.excerpt: Complete the table with information about each square. [Table requests: area of the square, number of dots, dots per square inch]

task_events.answer: Points (4, 64) and (4, 32) plotted; equations d = 16a and d = 8a; constants of proportionality are 16 and 8
task_events.position: Activity 4.2: Dot Density with a Twist
task_events.excerpt: For each square, plot a point that represents its values of a and d. Draw lines from (0,0) to each point. For each line, write an equation that represents the proportional relationship.

task_events.answer: 800 houses per square kilometer (first image, 48 houses in 0.06 sq km); 112.5 or 125 houses per square kilometer (second image, 9-10 houses in 0.08 sq km)
task_events.excerpt: For each neighborhood, find the number of houses per square kilometer.

task_events.answer: NYC: p = 7a with constant 7 thousand people per square kilometer; LA: p = 3a with constant 3 thousand people per square kilometer
task_events.excerpt: Write an equation for the line that passes through (0,0) and A. What is the constant of proportionality? Write an equation for the line that passes through (0,0) and B.

boundary_note: Density is counterintuitive: students often conflate total quantity with concentration, expecting 'more objects = denser.' This lesson scaffolds understanding by moving from visible dot arrays to real-world satellite photos to abstract city data. The critical insight—that the constant of proportionality IS the density measure, not just an arithmetic result—often remains procedural unless explicitly connected back to interpretation.

title: Fermi Problems
teacher_goals: Calculate a rough estimate for quantities that are difficult or impossible to measure directly and explain (orally) the reasoning.
Choose an appropriate level of accuracy when reporting estimates of quantities.
Make simplifying assumptions to solve problems about estimating quantities.
purpose: Students tackle three optional estimation activities where they calculate their exact age, estimate lifetime heartbeats, and estimate hair strand count. Each task requires deciding how precise to report the answer. These Fermi problems exemplify mathematical modeling (MP4) by requiring simplifying assumptions, research, and choices about which quantities matter. They emphasize precision (MP6) by having students think carefully about how to appropriately report estimates and describe quantities accurately.

task_events.excerpt: I am 13 years, 256 days, 22 hours, and 46 minutes old right now.
13 × 365 × 24 × 60 ≈ 6.8 million minutes; 6.8 million × 75 beats/min ≈ 500 million heartbeats.
Circle diameter 28 cm → radius 14 cm → area π × 14² ≈ 600 cm². 600 cm² × 150 hairs/cm² ≈ 90,000 strands.

boundary_note: Fermi problems teach that precision in reporting must match the uncertainty in estimation: reporting a heartbeat count as 524,344,566 falsely suggests certainty when 500,000,000 is honest. Students often struggle to distinguish between exactness and meaningfulness—a number is definitely calculable but should be communicated at an order of magnitude that reflects the simplifying assumptions made.

title: More Expressions and Equations
teacher_goals: Create expressions and equations to represent a linear relationship with two or more related quantities in context.
Determine the solution to an equation of the form px + q = r and explain (orally) the solution method.
Interpret expressions, equations, and solutions that represent a linear relationship with two or more related quantities.
purpose: In this optional lesson students write algebraic expressions to represent situations and solve equations. They are presented with problems that can be solved using grade 7 mathematics, but are often left for future grades when students have access to a wider variety of algebraic tools. Each of the situations described in the activities involve two or more unknown quantities and multiple relationships or actions. Initially students are walked through the steps of writing an expression to describe a situation, using properties to rewrite the expression with fewer terms, writing an equation to represent the situation, solving the equation, and considering the reasonableness of solutions. As they proceed through the lesson, the supports are slowly removed and students work on their own to reason through the problem (MP2). This lesson relies on skills developed in Unit 6.

task_events.excerpt: Write an expression that represents the total amount of money collected from ticket sales on both nights.
Over these two nights, they collected a total of $1,651 in ticket sales. What was the cost of each type of ticket?
Profits are calculated by subtracting costs from income. Use properties to write an equivalent expression with fewer terms.
The souvenir stand sells all these items and makes a total profit of $953.25. How many of each item does the souvenir stand sell?
Write an equivalent expression with as few terms as possible.
If Jada uses 5540 inches of yarn for the entire scarf, what length of yarn does she use for a single crochet stitch?

boundary_note: Students learn to express one unknown quantity in terms of another (e.g., student ticket = adult ticket − 2) and use those relationships to build and solve multi-step equations involving multiple related quantities. Common errors include failing to maintain relationships consistently when simplifying expressions and checking solutions only by substituting into simplified expressions rather than verifying against the original context.

title: Measurement Error
teacher_goals: Comprehend that percent error is greater when the actual values are smaller than the measured values for an object.
Determine the largest possible percent error of an estimated measurement.
Generalize (orally) a process for calculating the maximum percent error of measurements that are added or multiplied together.
student_facing_goal: Let's check how accurate our calculations are.
purpose: The activities address measurement error, building from a single measurement to added measurements and two- and three-dimensional measurements. Students look for patterns using repeated reasoning (MP8) as activities increase in difficulty; each can stand alone. Students attend to precision (MP6) by evaluating certainty when reporting answers. The lesson relies on skills from Unit 4 and Unit 6.

task_events.excerpt: The percent error if x is as small as possible is 0.1/5.3 ≈ 2%
For the longer pencil, the percent error is smaller. The biggest it can be is 0.1/17.7 ≈ 0.6%
If both boards have maximum length, the total length would be 1.05(x + y). If they are both minimum, the total length would be 0.95(x + y). So the maximum percent error would be 5%.
If they are both maximum, the area would be 1.05²xy = 1.1025xy. If they are both minimum, the area would be 0.95²xy = 0.9025xy. So the maximum percent error would be 10.25%.
The largest percent error occurs with the smallest measurement: 0.5 ÷ 9.5 ≈ 0.05 (or about 5%); 0.5 ÷ 11.5 ≈ 0.04 (or about 4%); 0.5 ÷ 24.5 ≈ 0.02 (or about 2%)
The smallest the actual volume could be is 9.5 × 11.5 × 24.5 = 2,676.625. The percent error in the case of the smallest one is (3,000 − 2,676.625) ÷ 2,676.625, or about 12%.

If the actual dimensions are x, y, and z, then the minimum measured volume would be (0.99)³xyz ≈ 0.97xyz and the maximum measured volume would be (1.01)³xyz ≈ 1.03xyz. So the largest percent error in the volume is about 3%.

boundary_note: Students frequently assume percent errors behave additively or multiplicatively like the quantities themselves: that two 5% errors in added measurements yield 10% error (not 5%), or in multiplied measurements yield 25% error (not 10.25%). The critical insight is that maximum percent errors occur when actual measurements fall below measured values. Measurement precision becomes a larger percentage of smaller quantities—a 1mm tolerance is 2% of a 5cm measurement but 0.6% of a 17.7cm measurement.

title: Deforestation at Scale
student_facing_goal: Let's compare the forested area over time.
teacher_goals: Calculate the area of forested land from a map.
Describe the result of deforestation and reforestation on a model of development.
Make simplifying assumptions to solve problems about estimating quantities.
purpose: In this optional lesson, students use maps to compare the forested area of a location at two different times. They begin by remembering strategies to calculate the area of a polygon. They then are presented with two maps of the same location, before and after development. Students compare the forested areas in each map and discuss the deforestation and reforestation in the area. Students make sense of the problem by making a plan for how to find the area of the forested land (MP1). They construct and critique arguments when comparing plans and solutions (MP3). This lesson relies on skills developed in Unit 1 but can be approached in more ways if taught after later units.

task_events.position: After figure diagram showing room shape with labeled dimensions
task_events.excerpt: Calculate the area of the actual room. Be prepared to explain your reasoning.
What is the forested area, in square feet, shown in the first map?
What is the forested area, in square feet, shown in the second map?

task_events.answer: About 2,895,000 square feet of forested land was removed
task_events.excerpt: How much deforestation happened during this time?

boundary_note: Students must correctly scale areas from drawings (applying the square of the linear scale factor, not just the linear factor) and decompose irregular map regions into simpler polygons. The major challenge is accepting that different decomposition methods yield similar but slightly different answers—all potentially reasonable—while maintaining spatial reasoning about what regions qualify as forested land on an actual map.

title: Measuring Long Distances over Uneven Terrain
student_facing_goal: Let's measure long distances over uneven terrain.
teacher_goals: Choose a method and appropriate measuring tools to measure the length of a path.
Compare and contrast (orally and in writing) methods for measuring long distances.
Compare measurements of a path and represent (in writing) the difference between measurements as a percentage.
purpose: In this optional lesson, students brainstorm ideas about how to measure long distances, possibly over uneven terrain. As students work in groups to try out the accuracy and effectiveness of different methods, they construct arguments and critique the reasoning of others (MP3). Some of the methods involve proportional reasoning. Students use appropriate tools when they plan and try out methods of measurement (MP5).

task_events.excerpt: Use your method to measure the length of the path at least two times.
If group A's measurement is 50 m and group B's measurement is 51 m, then group B's measurement is 2% larger than group A's since 51 ÷ 50 = 1.02.

boundary_note: The lesson explores measurement methods that trade precision for feasibility: tape measures are accurate but cumbersome; stride-counting is fast but vulnerable to variation in individual gait and terrain. Students commonly underestimate how small errors in technique (uneven stride length, imprecise path-following) accumulate dramatically over longer distances.

title: Building a Trundle Wheel
student_facing_goal: Let's build a trundle wheel.
teacher_goals: Create a trundle wheel and use it to calculate the length of the classroom.
Explain (orally and in writing) how a trundle wheel is used to measure long distances.
purpose: In this optional lesson, students build a trundle wheel, a device used to measure walking distances. First, they learn about a trundle wheel and discuss how such a device works and when it is the appropriate measuring tool. Then students use paper plates to make a usable trundle wheel and practice using it to measure distances in the classroom. Students can either use same-sized or different-sized paper plates to explore how diameter and circumference affect the precision of distance measurement.

task_events.excerpt: Plate diameter is 26 cm.
Circumference is 26π cm or about 81.7 cm.
12 clicks
About 9.8m because: 12 · 81.7 = 980.4 cm

boundary_note: This lesson applies circle geometry (circumference = π × diameter) to practical measurement and requires understanding that circumference determines distance traveled per rotation. Students commonly misunderstand that smaller wheels produce more rotations for the same distance, or they miscalculate by failing to use the circumference scaling relationship to correct for wheel-size differences across groups.

title: Using a Trundle Wheel to Measure Distances
teacher_goals: Calculate the distance of a path using the circumference and number of rotations of a trundle wheel.
Compare measurement calculations and express differences between measurements as a percentage.
Critique (orally) methods for measuring a long distance.
purpose: In this optional lesson, students use their trundle wheels to re-measure the distance they measured previously. By returning to the earlier problem with new tools, students make sense of and persevere in solving the problem (MP1). After students have had experience using their trundle wheels, they can connect their observations of how the wheel works with the distance computations and discuss the sources of errors in a more meaningful way. Students compare their previous measurements with their new measurements using the trundle wheel. This comparison allows them to refine their model by reconciling how to report the different measurements from each way of measuring (MP4).
student_facing_goal: Let's use our trundle wheels.

task_events.position: Activity 1.1, Student Task Statement question 1; Student Response table
task_events.excerpt: Diameter of wheel: 25 cm ... 63 clicks ... computation 25π · 63 ... 4,945.5 cm, 49.5 m
Decide what distance you will report to the class. Sample response: Distance to report: 50 m
Group B's measurement is 2% larger than Group A's since 51 ÷ 50 = 1.02.

boundary_note: This lesson connects physical measurement (rolling a wheel and counting rotations) to distance calculation using the formula distance = circumference × rotations. The main challenge is managing measurement variability: students take three measurements and get three different results, forcing a decision about what distance to report. Students often lose track of rotation counts during long-distance measurement and struggle to understand why repeated measurements differ. The lesson treats reporting precision realistically—to the nearest meter rather than centimeter, appropriate given the tool's actual accuracy. Sources of error (wobbling wheel, curved path, counting mistakes) remain practical rather than formally modeled.

title: Designing a 5K Course
teacher_goals: Calculate the distance of a path using the circumference and number of rotations of a trundle wheel.
Create a scale drawing of a 5K course and present (using words and other representations) the map and course details.
Use proportional reasoning to calculate the number of laps of a course that is equal to 5 kilometers.
purpose:
Student-facing goal: Let's map out the 5K course.

Lesson purpose: In this optional lesson, students design a 5K course and use their trundle wheels to measure distances. They draw a scale drawing of the course on a map or a satellite image of the school grounds, give instructions where the start and finish of the course should be, and decide how many laps are necessary to complete the race. Students use their experience with the smaller path to design a path of the required length (MP7). After planning their course, students use their trundle wheels to measure the course they planned and adjust their plan as necessary. As students measure their course, they decide the appropriate level of precision based on the tools they are using (MP6). This lesson may take 2 days.

task_events.excerpt: One time around the course is about 500 meters.
A person would have to go around the course 10 times to walk 5 kilometers.
One time around the course is actually 625 m.
One has to go around the course 8 times to complete the race.

boundary_note: The lesson's core reasoning demand is the inverse relationship between lap length and the number of laps needed to walk 5 km; students must recalculate laps when measured distances differ from estimates, and many confuse the direction of change (longer laps = fewer laps).

title: Moving in the Plane
student_facing_goal: Let's describe ways figures can move in the plane.
teacher_goals: Describe (orally and in writing) a rotation of a shape using informal language, e.g., 'turn left,' 'quarter turn,' etc.
Describe (orally and in writing) a translation of a shape using informal language, e.g., 'slide,' 'move,' 'shift,' etc.
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is to introduce students to translations and rotations of plane figures and to have them describe these movements in everyday language. Expect students to use words like 'slide' and 'turn.' In the next lesson, they will be introduced to the mathematical terms. The term 'transformation' is not yet used and will be introduced later in a later lesson.

task_events.excerpt: Turn counterclockwise 90 degrees (or one quarter of a full turn)
To get from Position 4 to Position 5, the shape rotates 180 degrees.

boundary_note: This lesson introduces translations and rotations through informal description before formal terminology arrives. Students commonly confuse perspective—whether describing from an observer's viewpoint or the shape's own orientation—and struggle to express rotation direction (clockwise/counterclockwise) and amount (degrees) with consistency. The lesson depends entirely on informal language and visual pattern work; there is no formal coordinate system or algebraic work yet.

title: Naming the Moves
teacher_goals: Describe (orally and in writing) the movement of shapes informally and formally using the terms 'clockwise,' 'counterclockwise,' 'translations,' 'rotations,' and 'reflections' of figures.
Identify corresponding points before and after a transformation.
Know the difference between translations, rotations, and reflections.
purpose: In this lesson, students are introduced to the terms translation, rotation, and reflection and begin to describe these transformations with greater precision. Students are also introduced to the terms clockwise and counterclockwise. Students then use this language to identify the individual moves on various figures. Students engage in mathematical practice 6 (precision) as they experiment with ways to describe moves precisely enough for another to understand their meaning.
boundary_note: This is a vocabulary and classification lesson, not computationally focused. Students learn to identify and describe three rigid transformations (translation, rotation, reflection) through visual observation and precise language. The major misconception: students often confuse reflections with rotations when figures are not on the same frame, or when they measure the acute angle between shapes instead of tracing to verify corresponding point mappings. Tracing paper is essential for differentiating these moves, especially distinguishing a reflection from a 180° rotation.

title: Grid Moves
student_facing_goal: Let's transform some figures on grids.
teacher_goals: Describe (orally) the moves needed to perform a translation, rotation, or reflection.
Draw and label the image of figures that result from translations, rotations, and reflections on a square or isometric grid.
purpose: Students build on prior knowledge of translations, rotations, and reflections by applying these moves to figures on both square grids and isometric grids. They label transformed points using prime notation (P → P') and develop flexibility in performing rigid transformations using tracing paper or grid-based reasoning. The isometric grid (composed of equilateral triangles meeting at vertices) introduces a new structure where transformations work differently from square grids, particularly for rotations at 60-degree increments. GeoGebra provides a digital transformation tool for classrooms using the digital curriculum."
boundary_note: This lesson is purely geometric construction, not numerical computation. Students draw and describe transformations but don't solve equations or measure quantities. Common misunderstandings: students struggle to visualize reflections over non-horizontal/non-vertical lines, confuse rotation direction, and are often surprised that no 90-degree angles exist on the isometric grid (only 60-degree multiples). Tracing paper is essential for students who lack strong spatial visualization.

title: Making the Moves
student_facing_goal: Let's draw and describe translations, rotations, and reflections.
teacher_goals: Draw a transformation of a figure using information given orally.
Explain (orally) the sequence of transformations that takes one figure to its image.
Identify (orally and in writing) the features that determine a translation, rotation, or reflection.
purpose: In the previous lesson, students were introduced to the terms "translation," "rotation," and "reflection." In this lesson, students understand that a translation is determined by two points that specify the distance and direction of the translation; a rotation is determined by a point, angle of rotation, and a direction of rotation; and a reflection is determined by a line. These moves are called "transformations" for the first time, and students draw images of figures under these transformations and study sequences of these transformations that take one figure to another. A subtle language shift occurs: in the previous lesson, one shape "moves" to the other as if the original shape has agency; now the transformation "takes" one shape to the other, centering the transformation itself as the object of study. Students using print may use tracing paper; digital students have access to applets to perform transformations. Students are likely starting to think strategically about which transformations will take one figure to another, identifying properties of the shapes that indicate whether a translation, rotation, reflection, or sequence of these will achieve this goal (MP7).
boundary_note: This lesson develops students' ability to precisely describe the three rigid transformations (translation, rotation, reflection) and to identify them visually. A major challenge is that incomplete descriptions are common: students forget to specify distance and direction for translations, or the direction (clockwise/counterclockwise) for rotations, or which line for reflections. Students also need to see that multiple transformation sequences can produce the same image, requiring strategic thinking about which combination most efficiently maps one figure to another.

title: Coordinate Moves (Grade 8, Unit 1, Lesson 5)
teacher_goals: Determine (orally and in writing) coordinates that represent the image of a polygon in the coordinate plane after a transformation
Draw and label a diagram of a line segment rotated 90 degrees clockwise or counterclockwise about a given center
Generalize (orally and in writing) the process to reflect any point in the coordinate plane over the x-axis or y-axis
purpose: Students use coordinates to describe figures and their images under transformations. Reflections over the x-axis and y-axis have structure captured by coordinates: reflecting (2, 5) over the x-axis yields (2, -5) because distance from the axis stays the same but position reverses. Coordinate structure helps visualize how transformations affect points, introducing MP7 (discovering patterns coordinates obey).

task_events.excerpt: If the point (13, 10) were reflected using the x-axis as the line of reflection, what would be the coordinates of the image? What about (13, -20)? (13, 570)?
Without graphing, predict the coordinates of the image of point R if point R were reflected using the y-axis as the line of reflection. ... What are the coordinates of R'?

task_events.answer: A(0, 3) rotated 90° counterclockwise around B(4, 2) yields C(3, -2)
task_events.excerpt: Rotate segment AB 90° counterclockwise around center B. Label the image of A as C. What are the coordinates of C?

task_events.answer: B(4, 2) rotated 90° counterclockwise around A(0, 3) yields D(1, 7)
task_events.excerpt: Rotate segment AB 90° counterclockwise around center A. Label the image of B as D. What are the coordinates of D?
Rotate segment AB 90° clockwise around (0, 0). Label the image of A as E and the image of B as F. What are the coordinates of E and F?

boundary_note: Reflections in the coordinate plane follow a learner-friendly rule: the sign of one coordinate flips, the other stays constant. Rotations are harder—both coordinates change, and the rule depends on the rotation center. Students often confuse whether to measure distance from a different axis or lose track of which coordinate changes when. The coordinate structure makes reflections transparent but can mask the deeper geometry of rotation.
student_facing_goal: Let's transform some figures and see what happens to the coordinates of points.

title: Describing Transformations
teacher_goals: Create a drawing on a coordinate grid of the image of a polygon using verbal descriptions of a sequence of transformations.
Identify what information is needed to perform a sequence of transformations on a polygon. Ask questions to elicit that information.
purpose: Prior to this lesson, students have studied and classified different types of transformations (translations, rotations, reflections). They have practiced applying individual transformations and sequences of transformations to figures both on and off of a coordinate grid. In this lesson, they focus on communicating precisely the information needed to apply a sequence of transformations to a polygon on the coordinate grid. They must think carefully about what information they need and request this information from their partner in a clear, precise way. They also explain why they need each piece of information. The coordinate plane plays a key role in this work, allowing students to communicate precisely about the locations of polygons and how they are transformed.
student_facing_goal: Let's transform some polygons in the coordinate plane.

task_events.excerpt: The point (-4, 1) is rotated 180° counterclockwise using center (-3, 0). What are the coordinates of the image?

boundary_note: Students must understand that the order of transformations matters—applying the same transformations in different sequences yields different results. They often struggle to ask for and articulate precisely what information is needed for each transformation type: translation distance and direction, rotation center and angle and direction, or the line of reflection.

title: No Bending or Stretching
teacher_goals: Comprehend the phrase 'rigid transformation' to refer to a transformation where all pairs of corresponding distances and corresponding angle measures in the figure and its image are the same.
Draw and label a diagram of the image of a polygon under a rigid transformation, including calculating side lengths and angle measures.
Identify (orally and in writing) a rigid transformation using a drawing of a figure and its image.
purpose: In this lesson, students compare the side lengths and angle measures of a figure and its image under a translation, rotation, or reflection. They observe that the corresponding sides are the same length and the corresponding angles have the same measure, and learn that a transformation with this property is called a rigid transformation. In order to observe this property, students create a measuring tool using available materials (MPS). Then, students identify which figure is the image of an original under a rigid transformation and make arguments to explain their reasoning (MP3).
student_facing_goal: Let's compare measurements before and after translations, rotations, and reflections.

task_events.excerpt: Find the length of this segment to the nearest 1/8 of a unit.
Find the length of this segment to the nearest 0.1 of a unit.
Estimate the length of this segment to the nearest 1/8 of a unit.

task_events.answer: Polygon A image has sides measured in grid units: sides labeled 1, 2, 1, 3, 2 units (or similar grid unit lengths)
task_events.excerpt: Translate Polygon A so point P goes to point Q. In the image, write the length of each side, in grid units, next to the side.
Rotate Triangle B 90° clockwise using R as center of rotation. In the image, write the measure of each angle in its interior.

task_events.answer: Pentagon C image has sides 1.8, 2.4, 2.4, 2.4, 3 grid units and angles 90°, 150°, 90°, 120° (rounded to nearest tenth for non-whole sides)
task_events.excerpt: Reflect Pentagon C across line ℓ. In the image, write the length of each side, in grid units, next to the side. You may need to make your own ruler with tracing paper or a blank index card. In the image, write the measure of each angle in the interior.

task_events.answer: Triangle CFG (or triangle DBE can be ruled out because side lengths differ from ABC)
task_events.excerpt: Here is a grid showing triangle ABC and two other triangles. You can use a rigid transformation to take triangle ABC to one of the other triangles. Which other triangle?

task_events.answer: Trapezoid A'B'C'D' with corresponding side lengths 6, 4 units and angles 130°, 50° matching original ABCD
task_events.excerpt: Trapezoid A'B'C'D' is the image of trapezoid ABCD under a rigid transformation. Label all vertices on trapezoid A'B'C'D'. On both figures, label all known side lengths and angle measures.

boundary_note: This lesson's core insight is that rigid transformations (translations, rotations, reflections) preserve all side lengths and angle measures; students must actively measure and compare figures to discover this invariant. Common pitfalls: students may struggle to measure diagonal segments accurately using hand-made rulers or tracing paper, confuse size preservation with position (expecting transformed figures to be in the same location), or believe that rotating or reflecting a figure somehow changes its measurements.

title: Rotation Patterns
teacher_goals: Draw and label rotations of 180 degrees of a line segment from centers of the midpoint, a point on the segment, and a point not on the segment.
Generalize (orally and in writing) the outcome when rotating a line segment 180 degrees.
Identify (orally and in writing) the rigid transformations that can build a diagram from one starting figure.
student_facing_goal: Let's rotate figures in a plane.
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is for students to observe properties of figures that have been rotated 90 degrees or 180 degrees. They generalize that a line segment rotated 180 degrees about a point is either parallel to the original or lies along the same line. They also identify rigid transformations on a triangle involving rotations of multiples of 90 degrees. Students explain that lengths on composite figures must be equal using the property that corresponding side lengths are preserved under rigid transformations.

task_events.answer: The segment is attached at point D and is an extension of segment CD (collinear, twice as long)
task_events.excerpt: Rotate segment CD 180° around point D. Draw its image and label the image of C as A.

task_events.answer: The segment is parallel to segment CD, above point E
task_events.excerpt: Rotate segment CD 180° around point E. Draw its image and label the image of C as B and the image of D as F.

task_events.answer: The image lines up with itself; endpoints are switched (D now where C was, C now where D was)
task_events.excerpt: Rotate segment CD 180° around its midpoint, G. What is the image of C?

task_events.answer: Yes. Segments AC, CE, EG, and GA all have the same length because rigid transformations preserve side lengths.
task_events.excerpt: Do segments AC, CE, EG, and GA all have the same length? Explain your reasoning.

task_events.answer: No. If triangle CDE were a 180° rotation of triangle ABC, then segment AB would be parallel to segment DE.
task_events.excerpt: Triangle ABC is rotated 180° around point C. Will the image line up with triangle CDE? Explain how you know.

boundary_note: The lesson centers on the three outcomes of 180-degree rotation depending on the center: parallel segments (center not on segment), collinear segments (center on or at endpoint), or identical segments with endpoints switched (center at midpoint). Students commonly believe rotating around the midpoint will move the segment; the key insight is that the segment stays in place while endpoints trade positions. Understanding these properties is essential for later work with composite figures and the Pythagorean theorem.

title: Moves in Parallel
student_facing_goal: Let's transform some lines.
teacher_goals: Draw and label rigid transformations of lines and parallel lines and explain (orally and in writing) the relationship between the original and its image under the transformation.
Generalize that a rotation by 180 degrees about a point of two intersecting lines moves each angle to the angle that is vertical to it.
Justify (orally) that vertical angles are congruent using informal arguments about 180-degree rotations of lines.
purpose: The focus of this lesson is for students to observe the effects of rigid transformations on lines and parallel lines. Parallel lines do not meet and are the same distance apart along their entire length, and a rigid transformation does not change these features. Students use the structure of parallel lines to conclude that the image of a set of parallel lines is also a set of parallel lines under a rigid transformation. Students also investigate 180-degree rotations of points on a line and a set of intersecting lines, using arguments about 180-degree rotations to justify that vertical angles have the same measure.

task_events.answer: A, C, B', D, B, C', A'
task_events.excerpt: What is the order of all seven points? Explain or show your reasoning.
What is the measure of angle A'OB'?
Use the diagram to find the measures of each angle. Explain your reasoning. a. angle ABC
b. angle EBD
c. angle ABE
What is the length of side B'C'? Explain how you know.
What is the measure of angle B? Explain how you know.
What is the measure of angle C? Explain how you know.
The point (-4, 1) is rotated 180° counterclockwise using center (0, 0). What are the coordinates of the image?

boundary_note: Students observe that parallel lines stay parallel after transformations, but struggle to explain *why* — the conceptual work is understanding that rigid transformations preserve geometric properties. The heart of the lesson is using 180-degree rotations to justify vertical angle congruence; this requires reasoning about how points map to their images and preserving angle measures, not arithmetic. Many students have difficulty visualizing 180-degree rotations dynamically or linking that visualization to the proof of vertical angles.

title: Composing Figures
teacher_goals: Draw and label images of triangles under rigid transformations and describe properties of the composite figure created by the images
Generalize that lengths and angle measures are preserved under any rigid transformation
Identify side lengths and angles that have equivalent measurements in composite shapes and explain why they are equivalent
student_facing_goal: Let's use reasoning about rigid transformations to find measurements without measuring.
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is for students to apply the property that rigid transformations preserve angle measures and side lengths. Students create composite shapes using translations, rotations, and reflections of polygons and observe that side lengths and angle measures do not change. They apply the property that the image of a line segment under a 180° rotation about a point not on the line is parallel to the original segment to explain why a figure must be a parallelogram. Students use the structure of rigid transformations to apply these properties to various figures.

task_events.excerpt: Reflect triangle ONE across segment ON. Label the new vertex M. What is the measure of angle MON?
What is the measure of angle MOE? 60° because ∠MOE is found by putting together ∠MON and ∠NOE, both 30° angles.
How long is segment OT? How do you know? 5 units because it is the image of ON after reflection so has the same length.
What is the measure of angle TOE? 90° because ∠TOE is found by putting together ∠TOM and ∠MOE.

task_events.answer: AF and BC; AB and CD
task_events.excerpt: Find two pairs of line segments in the diagram that are the same length, and explain how you know they are the same length.
Find two pairs of angles in the diagram that have the same measure, and explain how you know they have the same measure.
Point A has coordinates (3, 4). After a translation 4 units left, a reflection across the x-axis, and a translation 2 units down, what are the coordinates of the image?

boundary_note: This lesson asks students to apply rigid transformations to build composite figures and use properties of these transformations to find unknown side lengths and angle measures without direct measurement. The central challenge is that students often struggle to identify which angles and segments correspond between an original figure and its transformed images, and they may not yet trust that a 180° rotation guarantees parallelism of opposite sides—reasoning that requires understanding that rotation preserves distance and angle, which makes corresponding sides parallel.

title: What Is the Same?
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally and in writing) side lengths, angle measures, and other features of shapes using rigid transformations to explain why a shape is or is not congruent to another
Describe (orally and in writing) two figures that can be moved to one another using a rigid transformation as congruent
Explain that congruent figures have equal corresponding side lengths, angle measures, and areas
purpose: Students explore what it means for shapes to be "the same" by examining mirror images, comparing pairs of figures to construct arguments about sameness, learning that two figures are congruent if a sequence of translations, rotations, and reflections moves one to the other, and investigating how area and perimeter relate to congruence using tools like rulers, protractors, and tracing paper.
student_facing_goal: Let's decide whether shapes are the same.

task_events.excerpt: The perimeter of Rectangle R is 10 units since 3 + 2 + 3 + 2 = 10
its area is 6 square units since 2 × 3 = 6
The point (-2, -3) is rotated 90° counterclockwise using center (0, 0). What are the coordinates of the image?

boundary_note: Congruence rests on rigid transformations, not visual resemblance or measurement matching alone. Students routinely conflate "same area and perimeter" with "congruent," failing to see that rectangles with identical perimeters can have completely different side pairings (a 2×5 and 3×4 rectangle both have perimeter 14 but are not congruent).

title: Congruent Polygons
student_facing_goal: Let's decide if two figures are congruent.
teacher_goals: Critique arguments (orally) that two figures with congruent corresponding sides may be non-congruent figures.
Justify (orally and in writing) that two polygons on a grid are congruent using the definition of 'congruence' in terms of rigid transformations.
purpose: In this lesson, students find rigid transformations that show two figures are congruent and consider why two figures are not congruent. As students compare features of two shapes on a coordinate plane, they observe that having corresponding equal lengths is not enough to determine that two figures are congruent. Students use differences in features, such as angle measures, side lengths, perimeters, or the order of congruent sides, to construct arguments that two figures are not congruent. Students also use the structure of the coordinate plane to compare features of two figures and identify rigid transformations precisely.

task_events.excerpt: Find the side lengths of pentagon ABCDE

task_events.answer: Angle F: 59°, Angle G: 94°, Angle H: 117°, Angle I: 108°, Angle J: 162°
task_events.excerpt: Find the angle measures of pentagon FGHIJ

task_events.answer: Hexagon A area = 8 square units; Hexagon B area = 6 square units
task_events.excerpt: Hexagon A has greater area (8 square units) than Hexagon B (6 square units)

boundary_note: Equal corresponding side lengths are necessary but not sufficient for congruence; students commonly overlook that angle measures, side order, and successful rigid transformation sequences all matter. This lesson uses deliberate counterexamples where polygons share identical side lengths but differ in angles or areas, building geometric reasoning (not arithmetic) that congruence demands both matching measurements and a valid transformation sequence.

title: Congruence
teacher_goals: Determine whether shapes are congruent by measuring corresponding points.
Draw and label corresponding points on congruent figures.
Justify (orally and in writing) that congruent figures have equal corresponding distances between pairs of points.
student_facing_goal: Let's find ways to test congruence of interesting figures.
purpose: In this lesson, students use rigid transformations and compare features to decide whether shapes with curves are congruent. Unlike polygons, curved shapes may not be defined by a set of vertices, so more care is needed to determine congruence. Students begin the lesson by constructing mathematical questions about an image of ovals, helping them make sense of applying congruence to curved shapes. Students then identify corresponding points on curved figures and compare distances between those points. The focus is that the distance between any pair of corresponding points of congruent figures must be the same. Because there are too many pairs of points to consider, this fact is mainly a criterion for showing that two figures are not congruent—only one example of different distances between pairs of corresponding points is enough to conclude that two figures are not congruent.

task_events.answer: Top pair: 3 units × 2 units; bottom pair: approximately 2.5 units × 2 units
task_events.excerpt: For the top two shapes, they can be surrounded by rectangles that measure 3 units by 2 units. For the bottom two shapes, they measure about 2½ units by 2 units.

task_events.answer: AD = A'D'; BC = B'C'; AE = A'E' (all equal in length)
task_events.excerpt: The lengths are the same between segment AD and segment A'D'. They are also the same between BC and B'C' as well as between AE and A'E'.

task_events.answer: Left face: eyes 1 unit apart; right face: eyes approximately 2 units apart
task_events.excerpt: The eyes are one unit apart. For the figure on the right they are almost 2 units apart.

task_events.answer: Both angles ABC and A'B'C' measure approximately 110 degrees
task_events.excerpt: Measure angles ABC and A'B'C' to check your answer. Both angles measure about 110 degrees.

boundary_note: This lesson extends congruence from polygons to curved shapes, which lack a fixed set of vertices and require systematic measurement of corresponding points. Students often mistakenly assume that if all component parts are individually congruent, the whole figures must be congruent; they must discover that corresponding parts must maintain the exact same distances. Finding even one pair of corresponding points with unequal distances proves non-congruence.

title: Alternate Interior Angles
student_facing_goal: Let's explore why some angles are always equal.
teacher_goals: Calculate angle measures using alternate interior, vertical, and supplementary angles to solve problems.
Justify (orally and in writing) that alternate interior angles made by a transversal connecting two parallel lines are congruent using properties of rigid motions.
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is for students to connect rigid transformations with the congruence of angles created by a set of parallel lines cut by a transversal. Students identify angle measures on an image and describe what they notice about the angle measures using precise language (MP6). Then students use 180° rotations to explain why a pair of alternate interior angles are congruent (MP3). Students may connect these arguments to ones they made in a previous lesson when they justified that vertical angles are congruent. Finally, students use their arguments to generalize that for any pair of parallel lines cut by a transversal, the two pairs of alternate interior angles are congruent.

task_events.excerpt: Find the measure of angle JGH. Explain or show your reasoning.

task_events.answer: Seven unknown angles with measures 63° and 117° at two intersection points, determined using vertical and supplementary angle relationships
task_events.excerpt: With your partner, find the seven unknown angle measures in the diagram. Explain your reasoning.
Parallel lines ℓ and m are cut by two transversals that intersect at a point. Two angles are marked in the figure. Find the measure x of the third angle.
The diagram shows two parallel lines cut by a transversal. One angle measure is shown. Find the values of a, b, c, d, e, f, and g.

boundary_note: This lesson establishes alternate interior angle congruence via 180° rotation about the midpoint of the transversal segment. The key misconception is that students treat alternate interior angles as congruent by visual appearance or confuse them with corresponding angles, rather than understanding that congruence depends on the lines being parallel and can be justified through rigid transformation.

title: Adding the Angles in a Triangle
student_facing_goal: Let's explore angles in triangles.
teacher_goals: Describe (orally) that a straight angle can be decomposed into 3 angles to construct a triangle
Justify (orally and in writing) that the sum of angles in a triangle is 180 degrees using properties of rigid motions
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is for students to observe that the sum of the interior angle measures of a triangle is 180 degrees.

task_events.excerpt: Mentally estimate the measures of the other two angles. The angle combinations are: 40°, 50°, 90°; 40°, 60°, 80°; 50°, 50°, 80°; 20°, 20°, 140°; 20°, 40°, 120°.
Tyler has 3 right angles. Can he use them to make a triangle? No. Sample reasoning: 3 right angles sum to more than 180 degrees, since 3 · 90 = 270.

task_events.answer: Isosceles: B and C each 40° or 70°; Right triangle: one angle 50° and one angle 90°.
task_events.excerpt: In triangle ABC, the measure of angle A is 40°. Give possible measures for angles B and C if triangle ABC is isosceles; if triangle ABC is right.
For each set of angles, decide if there is a triangle: a. 60, 60, 60  b. 90, 90, 45  c. 30, 40, 50  d. 90, 45, 45  e. 120, 30, 30

boundary_note: Students discover empirically that all triangles have interior angles summing to exactly 180° by cutting angles, matching congruent triangles, and testing angle combinations. The core misconception is that students believe any three positive angles can form a triangle, or that the property applies only to special cases (equilateral, isosceles) rather than universally.

title: Parallel Lines and the Angles in a Triangle
student_facing_goal: Let's see why the angles in a triangle add to 180 degrees.
teacher_goals: Create diagrams using 180-degree rotations of triangles to justify (orally and in writing) that the measure of angles in a triangle sum up to 180 degrees.
Generalize that the sum of angles in a triangle is 180 degrees using rigid transformations or the congruence of alternate interior angles of parallel lines cut by a transversal.
purpose: In this lesson, students apply properties of rigid transformations to show that the sum of the interior angles of any triangle is 180°. Students first use rigid transformations to explore the sum of angles in a triangle drawn on a grid by rotating the triangle so that angles corresponding to its three interior angles form a straight line, demonstrating the sum equals 180°. However, this argument depends on grid lines to show the three angles form a straight angle. Students then complete the argument without using a grid by using the structure of parallel lines cut by a transversal, corresponding parts of congruent triangles, and straight angles to generalize that the sum of angles in any triangle is 180°.
