Students often misread negative slope or struggle to connect the -1 slope to the contextual meaning: as one piece grows by a foot, the other shrinks by a foot. The challenge is not computational but interpretive—understanding why the relationship is linear and what the slope represents in the story.

title: Slope of Linear Equations (from practice problems, U4 p. 43)
teacher_goals: Identify slope sign (positive vs. negative) from a graph
Calculate slope exactly using two coordinate points
Interpret slope as the rate of change in context
purpose: Students practice determining the sign of slope visually and then computing slope exactly using the formula (rise/run) with coordinates from a graph.

task_events.answer: line ℓ: positive; line m: negative
task_events.excerpt: Decide which line has a positive slope, and which has a negative slope.

student_facing_goal: Consider these graphs of linear equations. Decide which line has a positive slope, and which has a negative slope. Then calculate each line's exact slope.
boundary_note: Students often confuse slope sign with direction (rising = positive, falling = negative) before computing—correct visual intuition is good, but they must then use the coordinate formula consistently. Common error: reversing numerator and denominator (run/rise instead of rise/run), or misidentifying which coordinates to use when two or more points are marked on each line.

title: On Both of the Lines
teacher_goals: Create a graph that represents two linear relationships in context, and interpret (orally and in writing) the point of intersection.
Interpret a graph of two equivalent lines in context.
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is to introduce students to the graphical interpretation of multiple equations written in the form y = mx + b. The equations arise in the context of distance and time in order to anchor student understanding of the shared equation as the point in time when distances are equal. Students are also introduced to a pair of situations represented by the same equation to illustrate how situations can arise with infinitely many solutions.
student_facing_goal: Let's use lines to think about situations.

task_events.excerpt: Write an equation representing the relationship between the ant's distance from the ladybug's starting point and the amount of time that has passed.
Graph the relationship between distance and time for Jada's bike race. Make sure to label and scale the axes appropriately.
Elena travels the entire race at a steady 6 meters per second.
Andre starts with $15 and deposits $5 per week.
Write the equation for Andre's savings, and graph it alongside Noah's. What does the intersection point mean in this situation?

boundary_note: The lesson is about interpreting systems of two linear equations graphically, especially understanding what the intersection point represents in the original context (the common value at a specific time or condition). Students frequently confuse intersection coordinates with data points or fail to connect the x- and y-coordinates back to the situation, e.g., mistaking the x-value for y or neglecting the contextual meaning of each coordinate.

title: Lesson 3 Practice Problems — Linear Equations and Graphing
purpose: This document contains practice problems only; the lesson introduction (with Learning Goals and Purpose) is not included in this file.

task_events.excerpt: In this hanger, the weight of the triangle is x and the weight of the square is y... If x is 6, what is y?
Complete the table with values for x or y that make this equation true: 3x + y = 15
Create a graph, plot these points, and find the slope of the line that goes through them.

boundary_note: This file contains practice problems, not the full lesson body. The substantive work involves reasoning about linear equations through multiple representations (hanger diagrams, symbolic form, tables, graphs). Common student pitfalls: sign errors when solving equations with variables on both sides; incomplete table entries when substituting into linear equations; difficulty interpreting negative slope from a graph.

title: Grade 8 Unit 4 Practice Problems (page 41)
purpose: This PDF file contains a practice-problems page, not a lesson page. The content shown references earlier Unit 3 lessons, not Lesson 13 of Unit 4.

task_events.excerpt: Select all of the given points in the coordinate plane that lie on the graph of the linear equation 4x - y = 3.

The visible problem (selecting points on 4x - y = 3) is marked as drawing from Unit 3, Lesson 13, so it is not the Lesson 13 task itself. To build a teacher digest, the actual lesson body document (with Learning Goals, purpose statement, and CCSS standards listed) is required. The second problem on this page (shopping carts) is cut off and cannot be fully extracted.

title: Angles in Isosceles Triangles
student_facing_goal: Investigate the relationship between angles in isosceles triangles using variables x and y.
purpose: PDF shows only a practice problems page (page 42) with a truncated Student Task Statement. The full lesson content—including learning goals, lesson purpose, standards alignment, and complete problem statement—is not visible in the provided file.

task_events.excerpt: Triangle A is an isosceles triangle with two angles of measure x degrees and one angle of measure y [incomplete]

boundary_note: The PDF supplied contains only a practice problems page with a truncated problem about isosceles triangle angles. No solutions, numerical answers, or teaching context (learning goals, standards, lesson purpose) are visible. To build a complete teacher digest, the full IM-G8-U4-L14 lesson page is needed—specifically the Lesson Purpose, Learning Goals, and complete Student Task Statement with solution.

title: Situations with Non-negative Solutions
teacher_goals: Identify which real-world quantities can only be zero or positive
Distinguish between mathematical domain constraints and contextual constraints
Recognize when negative values are physically or definitionally impossible
purpose: Students evaluate contextual situations to determine which quantities are constrained to zero or positive values, deepening their understanding of when negative numbers are meaningful and when they are not.
student_facing_goal: Select all the situations for which only zero or positive solutions make sense.

task_events.excerpt: Select all the situations for which only zero or positive solutions make sense. [Six options evaluate: Arctic temperature (A), burning candle height (B), canyon elevation (C), students in school (D), bank balance (E), oven temperature (F)]

boundary_note: This lesson asks students to recognize that some real-world quantities are constrained to nonnegative values by definition or physical law, while others can meaningfully be negative depending on context and reference point. Common confusion: students often treat all quantities the same way, or fail to recognize that bank accounts can go negative (debt) and elevation can go below sea level. The correct answer (B: burning candle, D: student count, F: oven temperature on a hot day) passes only when students internalize that height, count, and certain temperature measurements have built-in nonnegative constraints, whereas temperature in Celsius, elevation, and account balance can all sensibly be negative.

title: IM-G8-U4-L16: Solving Problems with Systems of Equations
teacher_goals: Calculate the solution to a system of equations in context, and present (using words and other representations) the solution method.
Create a system of equations to solve a problem in context.
Critique (orally) peer solutions to a system of equations.
purpose: In this lesson students work in groups as they apply what they have learned to solve three problems with different structures and create a new problem similar in structure to one of the systems they solved. Groups trade problems, prepare well-explained solutions, and take turns sharing their solutions with the class. While groups share, ask other students to interpret particular aspects of the presentation, such as the slope of a graph, the coefficient of a variable, or the solution to a system, in terms of the context of the problem.
student_facing_goal: Let's solve some gnarly problems.

task_events.excerpt: Two friends live 7 miles apart. One rides at 0.2 miles per minute, and the other rides at 0.15 miles per minute. At what time will the two friends meet?
Bracelets with the school mascot on them cost $1 each, and shirts with the school crest cost $10 each. They sold 100 items and made $307. How many bracelets did they sell?

Jada earns $7 per hour mowing lawns. Andre gets paid $5 for the first hour of babysitting and $8 per hour for additional hours. What is the number of hours they both can work and earn the same amount?
On a different Saturday, two friends set out on bikes at 8 a.m. and met up at 8:30 a.m. If one was riding at 10 miles per hour, how fast was the other riding?

boundary_note: Students must identify when a problem involves two relationships between the same quantities, then translate that structure into a system. The algebraic solving is now routine; the real work is articulating the relationships and interpreting the solution back into context (e.g., translating a time offset into a clock time, or explaining what a quantity means in the original problem).

title: Inputs and Outputs
student_facing_goal: Let's make some rules.
teacher_goals: Describe (orally) how input-output diagrams represent rules.
Identify a rule that describes the relationship between input-output pairs, and explain (orally) a strategy used for figuring out the rule.
purpose: This lesson is the beginning of students' introduction to functions. While students will not use the word "function" until later, the purpose of this lesson is to focus students on the idea of input and output pairs connected by a rule.

task_events.excerpt: Input 3/4 → add 1, then multiply by 4 → output 7
Input 2.35 → name the digit in the tenths place → output 3
Input 1/7 → divide 1 by the input → output 7
Fill in the table for this input-output rule: divide by 2 and add 1. Input 0 → output 1
Write 1 if the input is odd; write 0 if the input is even. Input -3 → output 1

boundary_note: This lesson introduces functions through input-output pairs and rules without yet using the word "function." The central difficulty: students must learn that a single input-output pair cannot determine a rule uniquely, and that some rules have restricted domains (e.g., division by zero is undefined). Students often mistake "write 7" as a different rule from one that merely outputs 7 for a given input, and struggle to recognize that rules can restrict which inputs are allowable.

title: Introduction to Functions
teacher_goals: Comprehend the structure of a function as having one and only one output for each allowable input.
Describe (orally and in writing) a context using function language, e.g., '[The output] is a function of [the input]' or '[The output] depends on [the input].'
Identify (orally) rules that produce exactly one output for each allowable input and rules that do not.
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is to introduce students to the term "function." A function is a rule that produces a single output for a given input. A key aspect of functions is that for each input, there is one and only one output.
student_facing_goal: Let's learn what a function is.

task_events.excerpt: Make a new list containing the squares of all these numbers. [1, -3, -1/2, 3, 2, 1/4, 0.5]
50 multiply by 60 → 3,000
Calculate the output for each rule when you use -6 as the input. [subtract 7; square the input; divide by 3; divide the input into 3]

Diego's history teacher writes a test with 26 questions. The test is worth 123 points and has two types of questions: multiple choice worth 3 points each and essays worth 8 points each. How many essay questions are on the test?

Compare the outputs of the two functions when the inputs for each function is 4. The relationship between the circumference of a circle and its radius is represented by C = 2πr. The perimeter in centimeters is given by P = 6w.

boundary_note: This lesson is primarily conceptual and linguistic, asking students to identify, classify, and describe functions using precise language rather than perform arithmetic. The central misconception is conflating "multiple inputs map to the same output" (which is allowed) with "the same input producing multiple outputs" (which violates the function definition). Students also often fail to recognize that restricting domain affects which pairs of numbers can be inputs—for instance, -6 cannot be the side length of a geometric figure, so certain function rules become invalid at negative inputs.

title: Equations for Functions
student_facing_goal: Let's find outputs from equations.
teacher_goals: Calculate the output of a function for a given input using an equation in two variables, and interpret (orally and in writing) the output in context.
Determine (orally and in writing) the independent and dependent variables of a function, and explain (orally) the reasoning.
purpose: Students begin to make connections between representations of functions (input-output diagrams, tables, descriptions of rules) and algebraic equations. The lesson introduces the use of independent and dependent variables in the context of functions: the independent variable represents the input, and the dependent variable represents the output. Students learn that the same relationship sometimes allows either variable to be chosen as the input, depending on what question they want to answer.

task_events.answer: s=8 yields A=64; s=2.2 yields A=4.84; s=12¼ yields A=150 1/16
task_events.excerpt: Fill in the table of input-output pairs for the given rule. Write an algebraic expression for the rule in the box in the diagram.
The circumference, C, of a circle with radius, r. For each equation, find the output when the input is 5.
The distance in miles, d, that someone would travel in t hours if they drove at 60 miles per hour. Find output when input is 5.
The output that results from tripling the input and subtracting 4. Find output when input is 5.
The volume of a cube, v, given its edge length, s. Find output when input is 5.

task_events.answer: q=4 yields d=115; q=10 yields d=100
task_events.excerpt: Given 0.1d + 0.25q = 12.5, if Jada has 4 quarters, how many dimes does she have?

task_events.answer: d=25 yields q=40; d=30 yields q=38
task_events.excerpt: Given 0.1d + 0.25q = 12.5, if Jada has 25 dimes, how many quarters does she have?
The value v of your quarters (in cents) is a function of n, the number of quarters you have. Find the output when the input is 10.

boundary_note: Many students confuse which variable is independent versus dependent, or assume either variable in a relationship can serve as the input. The lesson clarifies that some relationships allow choice—you can ask "dimes as a function of quarters" or vice versa—while others do not, because some functions fail the reversal test: squaring has one output per input, but taking a square root does not have a unique output (both 4 and −4 square to 16).

title: Tables, Equations, and Graphs of Functions
teacher_goals: Determine whether a graph represents a function, and explain (orally) the reasoning.
Identify the graph of an equation that represents a function, and explain (orally and in writing) the reasoning.
Interpret (orally and in writing) points on a graph, including a graph of a function and a graph that does not represent a function.
purpose: In this lesson, students work with graphs of functions in addition to tables, equations, and descriptions. They learn the conventions of graphing the independent variable (input) on the horizontal axis and the dependent variable (output) on the vertical axis, and that each coordinate point represents an input-output pair. By matching contexts to graphs and reading information from graphs and tables, students become familiar with different representations and draw connections between them.
student_facing_goal: Let's connect equations and graphs of functions.

task_events.excerpt: How long did it take Kiran to run 6 meters? (From table: when d = 6, t = 2.0)
Estimate when he had run 19.5 meters. It took Kiran 6 seconds to run 18 meters and 6.9 seconds to run 21 meters.
For d = 60t, when the input is t = 1 hour, the output is d = 60 miles. Point (1, 60).
For A = πr², when r = 1, the area is π cm². Point (1, π).
What is the input to the function when the output is 5? On the graph, plot this point and label its coordinates.

boundary_note: The critical conceptual shift in this lesson is recognizing that the choice of which variable is independent and which is dependent determines whether a relationship is a function. Students often misclassify non-functions as functions by failing to apply the vertical line test correctly, especially when context is present. The lesson's central struggle: a relationship can fail the function test in one direction (e.g., time is not a function of distance when you run around a track and return) but satisfy it in the other (distance IS a function of time). Understanding this context-dependence and the axis-labeling conventions is essential to preventing persistent misconceptions.

title: More Graphs of Functions
student_facing_goal: Let's interpret graphs of functions.
teacher_goals: Describe (orally and in writing) a graph of a function as increasing or decreasing over an interval, and explain (orally) the reasoning.
Interpret (orally and in writing) a graph of temperature as a function of time, using language such as input and output.
purpose: In this lesson, students begin to analyze graphs of functions and use them to answer questions about a context. Students also look at what happens over intervals of input values and learn that graphs can be viewed as dynamic objects that tell stories. In the temperature activity, students connect specific features of the graph with specific features of the contextual situation, for example, the highest point on the graph with the highest temperature of the day and when it was attained (MP2). In the activity about garbage production, students investigate what happens over ranges of input values. The graph tells us how much garbage was produced at certain times, and we can also determine if the amount of garbage was increasing or decreasing over a specific time. As students learn to interpret graphs in terms of a context and use them to answer questions, they learn an important skill in mathematical modeling (MP4).

task_events.excerpt: Approximately when is the temperature highest?
Find another time that the temperature is the same as it is at 4:00 p.m.
When the input for the function is 8, what is the output?
What was Diego's speed at the 5-kilometer mark in the race?
According to the graph, where was Diego when he was going the slowest during the race?

boundary_note: Students learn to interpret graphs as dynamic stories about functional relationships, connecting visual features to contextual meaning. A common pitfall: students conflate identifying where a graph reaches its maximum with reading specific input-output values, or mistake increasing intervals for higher absolute values.

title: Practice: Solve Linear Equations (referenced from Unit 4, Lesson 6)
student_facing_goal: Solve each equation, and check your answer.
purpose: Practice problem set (full lesson purpose and instructional context not available in this PDF excerpt).
boundary_note: This PDF contains only a practice problem, not full lesson materials. The three equations spiral in algebraic complexity: (a) requires distribution and fraction simplification on one side; (b) requires collecting like terms on both sides with negatives; (c) involves fractional coefficients and requires clearing fractions or careful arithmetic. Students commonly lose track of signed terms when moving them across the equals sign, skip distribution into negative expressions, and make errors when combining fractions on one side.

title: Connecting Representations of Functions
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally) representations of functions, and describe (orally) the strengths and weaknesses of each type of representation.
Interpret multiple representations of functions, including graphs, tables, and equations, and explain (orally) how to find information in each type of representation.
purpose: In this lesson, students compare two functions represented in different ways (graph and table, graph and equation, and, optionally, table and verbal description). In each case, students use the different representations to find outputs for different inputs. Each representation has strengths and weaknesses. Comparing them in the Lesson Synthesis helps students make decisions about how to use these representations strategically in the future.
student_facing_goal: Let's connect tables, equations, graphs, and stories of functions.

task_events.excerpt: The first function gives the temperature in City A at 3:00 p.m., which is about 54.5°F.
The volume of a cube with edge length 3 cm is 27 cm³, since 27 = 3³.
The output of the cube volume function is 8 cm³ when the input is 2 cm, since 8 = 2³.
Elena has travelled 4.6 miles, because 5 × 0.92 = 4.6.
we solve the equation 0.92t = 7.4 to find that t is approximately 8.04
the area of a circle with radius 1.2 inches is about 4.52 square inches

boundary_note: This lesson centers on the principle that any functional relationship can be expressed as an equation, graph, table, or verbal description, and that mathematicians strategically choose representations for different purposes. Students often conflate different representations as separate ideas rather than recognizing them as different views of one function. Common errors: difficulty reading coordinates from graphs accurately, errors in substituting values into equations, and assuming that comparing linear functions is what "functions" means (the lesson deliberately includes nonlinear functions to broaden this understanding).

title: Linear Functions
student_facing_goal: Let's investigate linear functions.
teacher_goals: Comprehend that any linear function can be represented by an equation in the form y = mx + b, where m and b are the rate of change and initial value of the function, respectively.
Coordinate (orally and in writing) the graph of a linear function and its rate of change and initial value.
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is for students to connect linear relationships to functions and to understand that any linear function can be represented by y = mx + b. In the first activity, students see that a proportional relationship between two quantities can be viewed as a function. In subsequent activities, students investigate and make connections between linear functions as represented by graphs, descriptions, and the equation y = mx + b.

task_events.answer: M = 7t (or t = M/7 depending on variable choice)
task_events.excerpt: Jada earns $7 per hour mowing her neighbours' lawns. Write an equation that represents the function.
To convert feet to yards, we multiply the number of feet by 1/3. Write an equation that represents the function.

The amount of water in gallons, C, in Tank C is a function of time in minutes, t, and can be represented by the equation C = 800 - 7t. Is it filling up or draining out?
The graph of the function for the amount of water in gallons, D, in Tank D at time t is shown. Is it filling up or draining out? How do you know?

task_events.excerpt: Who is saving money at a faster rate? Explain how you know.
How much will Noah save over the course of a year if he does not make any withdrawals?

task_events.task: Identify graph for negative linear context

Write an equation for each line.

boundary_note: Students learn to represent linear functions with equations y = mx + b, where m is the rate of change and b is the initial value (y-intercept). The main conceptual stumbling block: students often reverse independent and dependent variables or confuse the meaning of m and b in context. They may also assume a negative y-intercept means the function is decreasing, when the direction depends on the sign of the slope (m), not the intercept.

title: Linear Models
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally and in writing) different linear models of the same data, and determine (in writing) the range of values for which a given model is a good fit for the data.
Create a model of nonlinear data using a linear function, and justify (orally and in writing) whether the model is a good fit for the data.
purpose: In this lesson, students use linear functions to model real-world situations. They develop linear models from data, make predictions, and discuss the reasonableness of their models. Students encounter situations where linear models fail (moon illumination) or only work within bounded intervals (garbage recycling), developing critical judgment about when linear functions are appropriate approximations and what restrictions the model requires.
student_facing_goal: Let's model situations with linear functions.

task_events.excerpt: Since it burns about 2 inches every hour, it will burn out between 5 and 6 hours after it was lit.

task_events.answer: Day 13 for 50% illuminated; Day 26 for 100% illuminated
task_events.excerpt: If the illumination increases by 4% every day, then 11 days after Day 2, it reaches 50%. Then 13 days after that, the illumination reaches 100%. This gives a prediction of Day 13 for 50% and Day 26 for 100%.

If we model this with the linear function that goes through (0, 0) and (20, 10.5), we would predict that the length would be growing at a rate of 10.5/20 centimeters per minute. After 95 minutes, this would give a prediction of about 50 centimeters.
Yes. After 48 months, they sold about 20.5 thousand games. From Month 12 to Month 36, the rate of games sold was about 11/24 thousand games per month.
Use this information to predict how many miles a customer would need to drive so the monthly charge is $100.

boundary_note: This lesson teaches model selection and the limits of linear approximation. Students must develop judgment about when a linear model is appropriate and over what domain it remains valid. The critical insight is that real-world phenomena (moon cycles, organism growth, resource trends) often follow nonlinear patterns—a model that fits one time interval may fail badly in another. Students commonly assume that any roughly linear-looking data can be modeled with a single line forever, rather than recognizing that models require domain restrictions and comparative evaluation. This requires reasoning about the relationship between formal mathematics and informal reality; no arithmetic automaton covers it.

title: Piecewise Linear Functions
teacher_goals: Calculate the different rates of change of a piecewise linear function using a graph, and interpret (orally and in writing) the rates of change in context.
Create a model of a nonlinear function using a piecewise linear function, and describe (orally) the benefits of having more or less segments in the model.
purpose: This lesson builds on prior work with linear models for data. In some situations where we wish to quantify change, we want to use different constant rates over different time intervals, so we can model the situation with a piecewise linear function. Students look at temperature data, which change at different rates throughout the day. The rates for these intervals are almost constant. They also use piecewise linear graphs to find information about real-life situations. The focus is to study the graphs qualitatively and compute and compare different rates of change, rather than necessarily finding equations for the piecewise linear functions.
student_facing_goal: Let's explore functions built out of linear pieces.

task_events.excerpt: The endpoints of the four segments are (1991, 16), (1996, 22), (1999, 21.5), (2011, 26), and (2013, 25), so we can find slopes of 6/5, 1/4, 2/12, and -1/2

It took 12 minutes to drain 24 gallons, giving a rate of 2 gallons per minute

boundary_note: This lesson teaches students to model situations with functions made of line segments, each with its own rate of change, and to calculate and interpret those rates in context. The chief difficulty is connecting the slope value (a number) to the rate of change in a real situation (e.g., understanding that a slope of -2 means draining at 2 gallons per minute), and distinguishing between steepness of the line and the direction or magnitude of change.

title: Filling Containers
purpose: The provided page (84) contains only the answer key and teacher guidance section. The lesson purpose statement is not visible. Teacher notes emphasize helping students connect graphs of functions to the physical shapes of containers being filled.

boundary_note: Page 84 is the end-of-lesson answer key and teacher guidance section. The full lesson materials (student-facing goal, learning goals, lesson purpose, standards, and main tasks) are not included in this page excerpt. Core challenge visible here: students must interpret a graph of a function (distance vs. time) and connect it back to the shape of a container being filled — integrating function reasoning with geometric visualization.

title: Graphing Lines and Interpreting Slope
teacher_goals: Understand slope as a rate of change describing the relationship between two variables
Connect negative slope to real-world constraints (here: how length and width trade off when perimeter is fixed)
purpose: This task uses the constraint of a fixed rectangle perimeter (24 units) to develop understanding of linear relationships. Students plot points satisfying a linear equation, calculate slope from the graph, and interpret what the slope means in the context of a real constraint: as width increases by one unit, length must decrease by one unit to maintain the same perimeter.

task_events.answer: Plot points (4,8), (2,10), (5,7); sketch line through them representing 2x + 2y = 24
task_events.excerpt: Create a graph, plot the points, and sketch the line.
What is the slope of the line you graphed?

task_events.answer: Slope of −1 means for rectangles with perimeter 24, every extra unit of width requires one less unit of length
task_events.excerpt: What does this slope tell you about the relationship between lengths and widths of rectangles with perimeter 24 units?

boundary_note: Students often read slope values correctly from a graph but fail to connect the number to what it means in context. The key conceptual leap—recognizing that a slope of −1 encodes an inverse trade-off under a binding constraint (fixed perimeter)—requires translating between the abstract slope and the concrete quantities. Watch for students who state "−1" without connecting it to the rectangle constraint, or who reason that "negative slope means the line goes down" rather than "width and length are inversely related under fixed perimeter."

boundary_note: This practice set reinforces function notation through tabular input-output work and equation writing. Problem 3 demands evaluation of a linear rule at negative integers and fractional inputs—students frequently make sign errors with negative coefficients (4 × −3) and struggle converting fraction arithmetic to decimal form (8/5 + 10/5 = 3.6). Problems 1–2 are purely conceptual (equation equivalence and parallel systems) and do not appear in task_events. The full lesson Learning Goals, Purpose, and Standards are not included in this practice-problems excerpt.

The core work is two-variable linear systems solved via substitution method, with students constructing equations from contextual constraints (commerce problem), then performing symbolic manipulation. Students often struggle with keeping track of variable meanings across equations and correctly distributing when substituting an expression; they may also incorrectly combine unlike terms or forget to back-substitute to find the second variable.

title: Finding Cone Dimensions
teacher_goals: Calculate the value of one dimension of a cone, and explain (orally and in writing) the reasoning.
Compare volumes of a cone and cylinder in context, and justify (orally) which volume is a better value for a given price.
Create a table of dimensions of cones, and describe (orally) patterns that arise.
purpose: As they did with cylinders in a previous lesson, in this lesson students compute volumes given radii and heights and find the radius or height given a cone's volume and the other dimension by reasoning about the structure of the volume formula (MP7). Next, they apply their understanding about the volumes of cylinders and cones to decide which popcorn container and price offers the best deal. This activity offers students the chance to reason about volume, out-of-scale images, and price per unit calculations, using reasoning to critique which container is the best deal (MP3).
student_facing_goal: Let's figure out the dimensions of cones.

task_events.task: Solve for cone radius given volume and height: 64π = (1/3)πr² · 3
task_events.excerpt: 64π = 1/3 πr² · 3. What does the radius of this cone have to be?

task_events.task: Calculate cone volume from radius and height: V = (1/3)π(4)²(3)
task_events.excerpt: radius (units) 4, height (units) 3, volume of cone

task_events.task: Find radius from base area: πr² = 144π
task_events.excerpt: area of the base (square units) 144π

task_events.task: Calculate and compare cone vs. cylinder volume and price per unit
task_events.answer: cylinder better value: 120.58 cm³/$ vs. cone 106.07 cm³/$
task_events.excerpt: A movie theater offers two containers... Which container is the better value?

boundary_note: Students work backward from cone volume to find missing dimensions, which requires solving equations involving π and fractions—especially the 1/3 coefficient that expresses why cone volume is one-third of a cylinder's. The main stumbling block is keeping track of which dimension is unknown and managing the algebra; students should anchor to the warm-up fraction strategies (27 = 1/3 h) and recognize that the cone formula V = 1/3 πr²h has the same structure as the cylinder formula, just divided by 3.

title: Scaling One Dimension
teacher_goals: Create a graph and an equation to represent the function relationship between volume and height for cylinders with the same radius and justify (orally) that the relationship is linear.
Interpret (in writing) a point on a graph representing the volume of a cone as a function of its height, and explain (orally) how changing one dimension affects the other.
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is for students to see some examples of linear functions, in this case proportional ones, that arise out of geometry. In an earlier lesson, students considered the relationship between the volume of water inside a graduated cylinder and the height of the water. In that example, the height was a function of the volume, and the relationship was proportional.
student_facing_goal: Let's see how changing one dimension changes the volume of a shape.

task_events.excerpt: At the end of the trip, how far did the truck drive, and how much gas did it use?
If a truck traveled half this distance at the same rate, how much gas would it use?
If a truck traveled double this distance at the same rate, how much gas would it use?
Write an equation that represents the relationship between V and s.

task_events.answer: Volume doubles from 60 cm³ to 120 cm³ when edge length doubles from 4 to 8
task_events.excerpt: What happens to the volume if you double the edge length s?
Write an equation that represents the relationship between V and h. Use 3.14 as an approximation of π.

task_events.answer: When h is halved, volume is halved (39.25h is half of 78.5h)
task_events.excerpt: What happens to the volume if you halve the height, h?
Use the labeled point to find the radius of these cones. Use 3.14 as approximation for π.
Write an equation that relates the volume V and height h.
Complete this table for volume of cylinders with the same radius but different heights.

boundary_note: This lesson establishes that volume is a linear function of one dimension (height or edge length) when other dimensions are held fixed. The core reasoning is proportional: when you scale a dimension by factor k, volume scales by the same factor k. Students typically struggle to explain WHY this happens geometrically, treating it as a memorized pattern rather than as a consequence of the volume formula structure.

title: Scaling Two Dimensions
student_facing_goal: Let's change more dimensions of shapes.
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally) graphs of linear and nonlinear functions.
Create an equation and a graph representing the volume of a cone as a function of its radius, and describe (orally and in writing) how a change in radius affects the volume.
Describe (orally and in writing) how changing the input of a certain nonlinear function affects the output.
purpose: This lesson is optional since it contains work beyond the scope of the grade. The main purpose of this lesson is to understand that if two of the dimensions of a three-dimensional figure are scaled by the same factor, the volume scales by the square of that factor. A secondary purpose is to see some interesting examples of nonlinear functions arising from geometry.

task_events.excerpt: When the edge length of the square base is tripled, the area of the base is multiplied by 9. This makes the volume equation, V = 99s²
Write an equation that expresses the relationship between V and r. Use 3.14 as an approximation for π.
Predict what happens to the volume if the value of r is tripled.
when the radius triples from 1 cm to 3 cm, the volume changes from 7.33 cm³ to 65.94 cm³
Complete this table, giving three possible examples. r (m): 1, 2, 4
Another cone has quadruple the radius and the same height. How many times larger is the new cone's volume?

boundary_note: The critical insight is that when two linear dimensions scale by factor a, volume scales by a². Students often assume volume scales linearly (doubling dimensions doubles volume), missing the quadratic relationship. This manifests as confusion when reading or constructing nonlinear function graphs: the volume curves upward because it depends on the square of the radius or edge length, unlike the linear relationship between height and volume. This lesson is beyond typical Grade 8 scope and requires fluency with squaring, substitution into quadratic expressions, and interpretation of nonlinear graphs.

title: Estimating a Hemisphere
student_facing_goal: Let's estimate volumes of hemispheres with figures we know.
teacher_goals: Calculate the volumes of a cylinder and cone with the same radius and height, and justify (orally and in writing) that the volumes are an upper and lower bound for the volume of a hemisphere of the same radius.
Estimate the volume of a hemisphere by using the formulas for volume of a cone and volume of a cylinder, and explain (orally) the estimation strategy.
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is to introduce students to working with spheres by using shapes they are now familiar with—prisms, cones, and cylinders—to estimate the volume of a hemisphere. The work done in this lesson prepares students to reason about the formula for the volume of a sphere in a future lesson. Students fit a hemisphere inside a cylinder and use the cylinder's volume to estimate the hemisphere's volume, then do the same with a cone that fits inside the hemisphere, establishing that the hemisphere's volume must lie between the cone's and cylinder's volumes.

task_events.excerpt: What is the volume of the box?
What is the volume of the new box?
Calculate the volume of the cylinder.
What is the volume of the cone?
Estimate the volume of the hemisphere by calculating the average of the volumes of the cylinder and cone.

boundary_note: This lesson uses nesting of familiar shapes—a cone inside a hemisphere, a hemisphere inside a cylinder—to establish upper and lower bounds for volume estimation. Students commonly misunderstand why dimensions scaling by a factor of 2 increases volume by a factor of 8, and struggle to justify which shape gives an upper bound versus a lower bound.

title: The Volume of a Sphere
teacher_goals: Calculate the volume of a sphere, cylinder, and cone which all have a radius of r and height of 2r, and explain (orally) the relationship between their volumes.
Create an equation to represent the volume of a sphere as a function of its radius, and explain (orally and in writing) the reasoning.
purpose: Students recognize that the volume of a sphere with radius r is 4/3 πr³ through a visual demonstration in which a sphere fits snugly inside a cylinder (same radius, height equals diameter), and a cone with matching base and height pours into the remaining space. By observing that the sphere and cone together fill the cylinder, students compute volumes and reason about the relationship, then derive the general formula algebraically by manipulating the volume expressions for related solids.

task_events.excerpt: The radius of the cylinder is 5 units. What is the height of the cylinder? 10 units. The top of the sphere touches the top of the cylinder, so the diameter of the sphere is the height of the cylinder.
What is the volume of the cylinder? 250π cubic units, because V = πr² · 10
What is the volume of the cone? (250/3)π cubic units, because V = (1/3)πr² · 10

What is the volume of the sphere? Explain your reasoning. (500/3)π cubic units. Subtracting the volume of the cone from the volume of the cylinder gives the volume of the sphere: 250π - (250/3)π = (500/3)π.
Let the radius of the cylinder be r units. What is the height of the cylinder in terms of r? 2r, because the diameter of the sphere is the height of the cylinder
What is the volume of the cylinder in terms of r? 2πr³, because V = πr² · 2r
What is the volume of the cone in terms of r? (2/3)πr³, because V = (1/3)πr² · 2r
What is the volume of the sphere in terms of r? (4/3)πr³, because V = 2πr³ - (2/3)πr³
The volume of the sphere is what fraction of the volume of the cylinder? 2/3
Here is a sphere with radius 4 feet. What is the volume of the sphere? Express your answer in terms of π. (256/3)π cubic feet, because V = (4/3)π(4)³

A spherical balloon has a diameter of 4 feet. Approximate how many cubic feet of air this balloon holds. Use 3.14 as an approximation for π, and give a numerical answer. 33.49 cubic feet, because V = (4/3)π(2)³

boundary_note: This lesson moves from concrete observation (sphere inside cylinder with cone filling the gap) to the abstract formula V = (4/3)πr³. The core challenge: students see only one specific demonstration yet must believe the ratio holds for all spheres. Common confusion arises when students treat the two computational approaches (using the 2/3 ratio directly vs. subtracting cylinder minus cone) as separate facts rather than equivalent expressions, and when they struggle to understand why a sphere volume formula derived from one physical case generalizes to all spheres. The lesson also requires students to manipulate and compare symbolic volume expressions—a skill many eighth-graders find slippery.

title: Cylinders, Cones, and Spheres
teacher_goals: Calculate the value of the radius of a sphere with a given volume using the structure of the equation, and explain (orally) the solution method.
Determine what information is needed to solve a problem involving volumes of cones, cylinders, and spheres. Ask questions to elicit that information.
purpose: In this lesson, students use the formula for the volume of a sphere to solve various problems. The Warm-up begins with an opportunity to analyze common errors that people make when using this formula. The main activity is an Information Gap in which students must reason about a cone and sphere with matching dimensions and ask precise questions to get the information they need to solve the problem (MP6).
student_facing_goal: Let's find the volume of shapes.

The volume of this sphere with radius r is V = 288π... 288π = (4/3)r³π. What is the value of r for this sphere?

The volume of the sphere is 288π cm³. Students can calculate this value by finding the radius of the cone and then using the volume formula for a sphere, or they can use the fact that the volume of a sphere is twice that of a cone with the same dimensions.

task_events.excerpt: Some information is given about each sphere. Order them from least volume to greatest volume. Sphere A has a radius of 4. Sphere B has a diameter of 6. Sphere C has a volume of 64π. Sphere D has a radius double that of sphere B.

boundary_note: This lesson introduces the sphere volume formula (V = 4/3 πr³) and applies it to solve problems. The most common errors are using r² instead of r³, forgetting to include π as a factor, or confusing the sphere formula with cylinder/cone formulas. The lesson's central challenge—the Information Gap activity—pushes students beyond rote formula application toward mathematical reasoning about which dimensions are sufficient to determine volume, requiring them to ask precise questions about unknown measurements.

Solve problems involving cones, cylinders, and spheres.
