task_events.excerpt: Write a new equation that makes a system with infinite solutions together with 2x + 6y = 10.

task_events.answer: One line that is the same for both equations
task_events.excerpt: Describe the graph of the new system.

boundary_note: Page 112 contains only practice problems, not the lesson's instructional materials. The visible tasks focus on exponent properties (product of different bases with same exponent) and system equivalence (recognizing when two linear equations represent the same line, thus infinite solutions). Missing: lesson title, Learning Goals, Lesson Purpose, and Common Core standards—these appear on earlier pages not included in this PDF.

title: Estimating with Scientific Notation
teacher_goals: Generalize (orally and in writing) a process of multiplying and dividing numbers in scientific notation.
Use scientific notation and estimation to compare quantities and interpret (orally and in writing) results in context.
purpose: In this lesson, students perform operations with numbers expressed in scientific notation, use numbers expressed in the form of a single digit times an integer power of 10 to estimate very large or very small quantities, and express how many times as much one quantity is than the other. Next, students use the Info Gap structure to compare planetary diameters and masses. Students will need to request information about the diameters and masses of certain planets as well as calculate how many of one will equal the other. The focus is on estimation strategies and interpreting their results in context (MP2). An optional activity allows for additional practice multiplying and dividing large and small numbers in context.
student_facing_goal: Let's multiply and divide with scientific notation to answer questions about animals and planets.

task_events.answer: 4 × 10^39 (all three calculator outputs represent the same value)
task_events.excerpt: Diego used a calculator and his display read 4E39. Priya got 40 × 10^38. Clare got 4e + 39. What do these mean?

task_events.answer: approximately 10^13 (10 trillion) times as many ants
task_events.excerpt: Which creature is least numerous? ...There are about 10^13, or 10 trillion, times as many ants as blue whales because 5×10^16 ÷ 4.7×10^3 ≈ 10^13.
Which is more massive, the total mass of all humans or total mass of ants? The total human mass is about 3 times as massive as the total ant mass.
The Sun is about 100 times the width of Earth because (1.392×10^6)÷(1.28×10^4) ≈ 10^2 = 100.
Estimate how many times larger is 6.1 × 10^7 than 2.1 × 10^-4. Answer: about 300 billion times larger.

boundary_note: This lesson requires students to coordinate two separate calculations—multiplying coefficients and powers of 10 (via exponent addition)—when they multiply or divide scientific notation. Students frequently mix up exponent rules (multiplying instead of adding) or misread technology notation like "4E39," and often struggle to estimate accurately when comparing vastly different quantities.

title: Adding and Subtracting with Scientific Notation
teacher_goals: Students apply scientific notation to real-world contexts requiring addition and multiplication of large numbers
Students convert numbers to scientific notation and perform arithmetic operations
Students reason about relative magnitudes and place value when working with powers of 10
purpose: This lesson applies scientific notation to authentic biomass and scale contexts, requiring students to multiply large numbers by powers of 10 and add quantities across different scales. The work bridges notation fluency to computational reasoning in realistic problem-solving.

task_events.excerpt: What is the total mass of 1 human, 1 cow, and 2 chickens? Explain or show your reasoning.
What is the total mass of all the zooplankton on the planet? Explain or show your reasoning.

boundary_note: Students must convert standard numbers to scientific notation and perform operations across different magnitudes. The most common error is treating exponent arithmetic like multiplication (adding exponents when actually adding quantities in different powers of 10) rather than first rewriting to a common base or recognizing that 466 kg is simpler in standard form than trying to force it into scientific notation. The zooplankton task requires recognizing that (1 × 10^20) × (5 × 10^-8) = 5 × 10^12, demanding fluency with exponent rules under multiplication.

title: Is a Smartphone Smart Enough to Go to the Moon?
teacher_goals: Use scientific notation to compare quantities in context, and describe (orally) how using scientific notation helps with making comparisons between very large and very small quantities.
I can use scientific notation to compare different amounts and answer questions about real-world situations.
purpose: In this culminating lesson, students use scientific notation as a tool for making comparisons. Students compare older computer hardware to newer computer hardware using various digital media as a form of measurement. Students begin by comparing the storage, processor speed, and memory on various electronic devices to the 1966 Apollo Guidance Computer used to make the calculations that put humans on the moon. They then compare floppy drives to modern technology by measuring how many floppy drives it would take to store a high-definition film.

task_events.excerpt: Can store 2.6 × 10² times as much as Apollo.
A floppy drive holds 750 kilobytes, which is 750 × 10³ bytes. As a fraction of a gigabyte (10⁹ bytes), divide 750×10³/10⁹
About 500 1-page essays. Sample reasoning: Because 750 ÷ 1.5 = 500.
About 10,000 floppy disks. Sample reasoning: (8 × 10⁹ bytes per movie) / (7.5 × 10⁵ bytes per floppy) ≈ 1 × 10⁴
0.72 seconds. A 2-hour film is 120 minutes or 7,200 seconds. Therefore, 7.2 × 10³ / 10⁴ = 0.72 seconds.

boundary_note: This lesson applies exponent rules and scientific notation to real-world comparisons of computing hardware from 1966 to 2023. Teachers should watch for unit-conversion errors (especially KB/MB/GB mistakes) and help students understand what abstract ratios represent—why a 550× processor speed improvement, though invisible, actually mattered historically."

title: Grade 8 Unit 7 Practice Problems (page 139)
purpose: Practice page presenting problems on scientific notation, volume calculations, and equation solving from multiple prior units.
student_facing_goal: Rewrite quantities using powers of 10; calculate the volume of a sphere and compare by scaling; solve linear equations.

task_events.excerpt: There are about 37 trillion cells in an average human body.
The Milky Way contains about 300 billion stars.
A sharp knife is 23 millionths of a meter thick at its tip.
The wall of a certain cell in the human body is 4 nanometers thick. (A nanometer is one billionth of a meter.)
A fully inflated basketball has a radius of 12 centimeters. Your basketball is inflated only halfway. How many more cubic centimeters of air does your ball need to fully inflate?

boundary_note: Note: The provided PDF is a practice page rather than a full lesson page; it lacks explicit learning goals, lesson purpose, and CCSS standard listings. Students converting between named quantities (trillions, billionths) and exponential form often miscount orders of magnitude; basketball volume requires scaling reasoning (how volume scales with radius) and substitution into the sphere formula.

title: The Areas of Squares
student_facing_goal: Let's investigate the areas of squares.
teacher_goals: Calculate the area of a tilted square on a grid by using decomposition, and explain (orally) the solution method.
Find the area of a tilted square on a grid by using methods like 'decompose and rearrange' and 'surround and subtract.'
Find the area of a triangle.
purpose: Students learned previously how to find the area of a square given its side length. In this lesson, students investigate the areas of squares presented in different ways. The work of this lesson is intentionally focused on setting students up to see the geometric connection between a square's area and its side length, which will happen in a following lesson.

task_events.answer: Figure B has the larger shaded region: Figure A is 4 square units and Figure B is 5 square units.
task_events.position: Student Task Statement with figures A and B
task_events.excerpt: Which shaded region is larger? Explain your reasoning.
The total area of the 5 shapes is 25 square units.
Find the area of each shaded square (in square units).
Rotated diamond-shaped square on grid with shading.
Tilted square with sides labeled 7 and 3 in a surrounding rectangle.
Find the area of square ACEG.
To get the area of the shaded square ABCD, take the area of the outside square and subtract the areas of the four triangles: 121 − 48 = 73 units².

boundary_note: The central challenge is that students often misidentify the side length of a tilted square by reading adjacent grid units without accounting for the diagonal tilt. The surround-and-subtract strategy (enclose the tilted square in an axis-aligned square, subtract the four corner triangles) is more reliable than trying to count diagonal units directly. Students must grasp that the area formula A = s² still holds for tilted squares even when s is not aligned to the grid, preparing them for the Pythagorean theorem in later lessons.

title: Side Lengths and Areas
student_facing_goal: Let's investigate some more squares.
teacher_goals: Comprehend the term "square root of n" (in spoken language) and the notation √n (in written language) to mean the side length of a square whose area is n square units.
Estimate the side length of a square by comparing it to squares with known areas, and explain (orally) the reasoning.
purpose: Students are formally introduced to square roots and square root notation. They begin by finding areas and side lengths of squares on a grid with whole number dimensions, reasoning that two squares with the same area must have the same side length. They then investigate squares whose side lengths are not whole numbers, using comparison to known squares to estimate unknown side lengths. Finally, they work with a geometric construction on a coordinate plane, where rotating a square about the origin reveals its side length as a point on the number line, leading to the introduction of square root notation.

task_events.answer: Square A: side 5 units, area 25 sq units; Square C: side 6 units, area 36 sq units; Square B: area 25 sq units, side 5 units
task_events.excerpt: What is the side length of Square A? What is its area?

task_events.answer: Square D: 20 sq units; Square E: 29 sq units; Square F: 34 sq units
task_events.excerpt: Find the areas of Squares D, E, and F.

task_events.answer: Squares E and F have side lengths between 5 and 6 units because areas between 25 and 36 sq units
task_events.excerpt: Which of these squares must have a side length greater than 5 but less than 6?

task_events.answer: Approximately 28 square units (radius about 5.3, area about 5.3²)
task_events.excerpt: Use the circle to estimate the area of the square shown here. Explain your reasoning.

task_events.answer: √100 = 10 means a square with area 100 has side length 10
task_events.excerpt: What does it mean when we write √100 = 10 in terms of squares and side lengths?

task_events.answer: Area between 70 and 80 square units is reasonable; side length between 8 and 9 gives area between 64 and 81
task_events.excerpt: Mai estimates the area of the square to be somewhere between 70 and 80 square units. Do you agree?

boundary_note: The core conceptual shift is working backward: from area to side length, not forward. Students often believe that if an area falls halfway between two perfect squares, the side length is also halfway between—but squaring is nonlinear. The lesson builds intuition through careful comparison and estimation before formalizing the square root symbol. Students commonly struggle with irrational side lengths (like √29) that do not land neatly on a grid; the tracing-paper verification and circle-rotation construction help make this intuition concrete.

title: Square Roots
student_facing_goal: Let's find some side lengths of squares.
teacher_goals: Create a table and graph that represents the relationship between side length and area of a square, and use the graph to estimate the side lengths of squares with non-integer side lengths.
Determine the exact side length of a square and express it (in writing) using square root notation.
purpose: In this lesson students use different strategies to approximate the value of square roots when a drawn square is not given. Students begin by finding the areas of tilted squares, writing their exact side lengths, and approximating those side lengths using tracing paper. Next, they create a table and graph of side lengths and areas and use them to verify the accuracy of their tracing paper estimates. Lastly, students work in pairs to find the areas of two squares with an area of 50 square units, requiring them to express the side length as √50.

task_events.answer: Square A: √29 (exact), approximately 5.5 units; Square B: √18 (exact), approximately 4.2 units; Square C: √13 (exact), approximately 3.6 units
task_events.excerpt: Find the area of each square and estimate the side lengths using your geometry toolkit. Then write the exact length for the sides of each square.
Complete the tables with the missing side lengths and areas.
Use the graph to approximate √45.
Find the area of each square and estimate their side lengths. Both have area 50 square units.
Write the exact value of the side length of a square. For non-whole results, estimate.

boundary_note: This lesson introduces square root notation as the inverse of squaring, bridging informal geometric estimation to symbolic representation. The key stumbling block is that students often confuse √a with a÷2 or treat the notation as two separate operations rather than a single number that squares to a; the lesson uses concrete figures, graphs, and tables to build the conceptual understanding that √a is the positive number whose square equals a.

title: Rational and Irrational Numbers
student_facing_goal: Let's learn about irrational numbers.
teacher_goals: Comprehend the term 'irrational number' (in spoken language) to mean a number that is not rational and that √2 is an example of an irrational number.
Comprehend the term 'rational number' (in written and spoken language) to mean a number that can be expressed as a positive or negative fraction.
Determine whether or not a given rational number is a solution to the equation x² = 2 and explain (orally) the reasoning.
purpose: In this lesson, students build on previous work with square roots to learn about irrational numbers. Students recall the definition of rational numbers, numbers that can be expressed as positive or negative fractions, as they search for a number x such that x² = 2.

task_events.position: Activity 4.1, Math Talk: Positive Solutions, Student Task Statement
task_events.excerpt: Solve each equation mentally. x² = 9/4

task_events.position: Activity 4.2, Three Squares, Student Task Statement with figure
task_events.excerpt: For each square: 1. Label the area. 2. Label the side length. 3. Write an equation that shows the relationship between the side length and the area. [Tilted square with area 2 shown in grid]

task_events.position: Activity 4.3, Looking for a Solution, Student Response
task_events.excerpt: Are any of these numbers a solution to the equation x² = 2? ... 7/5. No, because (7/5)² = 49/25 (49/25 < 2, since 2 = 50/25)
Find some more rational numbers that are close to √2. Sample: 10/7, because (10/7)² = 100/49

boundary_note: This lesson asks students to accept that √2 is irrational by having them repeatedly fail to find a rational solution to x² = 2, not by offering a rigorous proof. Many students expect eventually to locate a fraction that works; the key pedagogical move is helping them recognize that this exhaustive search is evidence enough to trust that irrational numbers exist.

title: Exponent Equivalence: Negative Exponents
purpose: Not provided in the PDF excerpt (practice problem only).

task_events.excerpt: Select all the expressions that are equivalent to 10^(-6).

boundary_note: This practice problem surfaces a critical misunderstanding: students often conflate the negative sign as applying to the base (making −1/1,000,000) rather than to the exponent. The task demands fluency with three equivalent representations of negative exponents—fractional form, reciprocal power form, and expanded product form—and depends on understanding that a negative exponent denotes a reciprocal, not a negative quantity. This is the bridge between exponent rules (product and quotient rules) and working with reciprocals in algebraic expressions.

title: Reasoning about Square Roots
teacher_goals: Comprehend that -√n represents the opposite of √n.
Determine a solution to an equation of the form x² = n and represent the solution as a point on the number line.
Identify the two whole number values that a square root is between and explain (orally) the reasoning.
purpose: This lesson continues the transition of student understanding from a geometric characterization of square roots as side lengths, to an algebraic characterization of square roots as specific points on the number line.
student_facing_goal: Let's approximate square roots.

task_events.excerpt: Value of square root lies between two consecutive whole numbers. For √7, which two?

task_events.position: Activity 6.3, Solutions on a Number Line (problems 1-2)
task_events.excerpt: x² = 3, y² = 16, z² = 30. Plot x, y, z on number line. Plot -√2 on number line.

task_events.answer: √60. Since 6² = 36 and 8² = 64, only 60 lies in that range
task_events.excerpt: Which numbers greater than 6 and less than 8? √7, √60, √80

boundary_note: This lesson moves square roots from geometric objects (side lengths of squares) to algebraic numbers (points on the number line and solutions to x² = n). Students must learn to approximate irrational values by testing perfect squares—a skill demanding reasoning about bounds, not computing exact decimals. Common errors: students wrongly connect exponent magnitudes to square root positions (e.g., thinking √7 is between 2 and 4), or assume that if √n lies between two integers, it must be exactly halfway between them.

title: Finding Side Lengths of Triangles
teacher_goals: Comprehend the term Pythagorean Theorem as the equation a² + b² = c², where a and b are the leg lengths and c is the hypotenuse length
Describe patterns in relationships between side lengths of right and non-right triangles
Determine exact side lengths in coordinate grids and express using square root notation
purpose: In this lesson students investigate relationships between side lengths of right and non-right triangles, leading to the Pythagorean Theorem. Students begin by comparing aspects of 4 triangles to prime pattern-thinking. They then notice patterns between side lengths and areas of squares, learning that legs are sides forming the right angle and hypotenuse is opposite it. By lesson's end, students see that for right triangles only, given legs a and b and hypotenuse c, the side lengths relate as a² + b² = c².
student_facing_goal: Let's find triangle side lengths.

task_events.answer: Triangle D: a=2, b=√2, c=√10; Triangle E: a=2, b=1, c=√5; Triangle F: a=2, b=√5, c=√5
task_events.excerpt: Complete the tables for these three triangles: [D, E, F shown on coordinate grids with sides labeled]

task_events.answer: Triangle E: a²=4, b²=1, c²=5; sum: 4+1=5
task_events.excerpt: [Table showing a² and b² columns] Triangle E: a²=4, b²=1, c²=5

task_events.answer: Triangles E and Q satisfy a²+b²=c²; Triangles D, F, P, R do not
task_events.excerpt: Students may notice that a² + b² = c² for Triangles E and Q and that they are also right triangles.

task_events.answer: Triangle J: c=4; Triangle K: c=√50
task_events.excerpt: Find the missing side lengths. Be prepared to explain your reasoning. [Triangle J: a=√8; Triangle K: a=√10, b=√40]

task_events.answer: Triangle A: true, 4²+2²=(√20)²; Triangle B: false
task_events.excerpt: For each of the following triangles, determine if a² + b² = c², where c is the longest side.

boundary_note: The lesson is fundamentally about discovering that for right triangles only, the sum of the squares of the two legs equals the square of the hypotenuse. Students most often confuse the formula's scope (applying it to non-right triangles) and make computational errors: confusing exponents with multiplication by 2, or adding side lengths before squaring rather than squaring first then summing. The lesson explicitly teaches identification of legs versus hypotenuse and emphasizes the relationship holds only for right triangles.

title: Grade 8 Unit 8 Practice Problems (Lesson 4 excerpt, page 84)
purpose: Not provided in practice problems excerpt.

task_events.excerpt: Decide whether each number in this list is rational or irrational.
Which value is an exact solution of the equation m² = 14?

boundary_note: This practice set assesses rational/irrational classification, exact solutions to quadratic equations, and exponent rules. Students frequently confuse irrational numbers with approximations and may compute decimals rather than recognize exact values like √14. They often struggle applying power rules to composed exponentials and combining terms with unlike bases, conflating the product rule with improper simplifications.

title: Finding Unknown Side Lengths
teacher_goals: Calculate unknown side lengths of a right triangle by using the Pythagorean Theorem, and explain (orally) the solution method.
Label the legs and hypotenuse on a diagram of a right triangle.
Understand that only right triangles have a hypotenuse, always opposite the right angle.
student_facing_goal: Let's find missing side lengths of right triangles.
purpose: In this lesson, students practice applying the Pythagorean Theorem to find unknown side lengths of right triangles. They begin by examining four equations of the type they will encounter later in the lesson, noting any similarities and differences. Students continue by studying a series of triangles and determining which side of each right triangle is the hypotenuse, reinforcing the idea that only right triangles have a hypotenuse and refining students' precision of language. In the last activity, students practice finding the unknown leg or hypotenuse given drawings or a written description.

task_events.excerpt: Find c. [Triangle with a = √10, b = √40]
Find b. [Triangle with a = √8, hypotenuse = √26]
A right triangle has sides of length 2.4 cm and 6.5 cm. What is the length of the hypotenuse?
A right triangle has a side of length 1/4 and a hypotenuse of length 5/3. What is the length of the other side?
Find the value of x in the figure. [Triangle with altitude, sides labeled √34, √18, base segments 5 and x]
A right triangle has sides of length 3, 4, and x. Find x if it is the hypotenuse.
A right triangle has sides of length 3, 4, and x. Find x if it is one of the legs.

boundary_note: This lesson develops students' ability to correctly identify the hypotenuse (always opposite the right angle, always the longest side) and to apply the Pythagorean Theorem mechanically to solve for unknown sides. The central difficulty is that students frequently misidentify the hypotenuse in triangles shown in non-standard orientations and make algebraic errors when setting up or rearranging a² + b² = c² for different unknowns; continued practice over subsequent lessons is expected.

title: Section B: The Pythagorean Theorem
teacher_goals: Calculate the distance between two points in the coordinate plane by using the Pythagorean Theorem.
Explain an area-based algebraic proof of the Pythagorean Theorem.
Use the Pythagorean Theorem to calculate unknown side lengths of right triangles and to solve problems within a context.
student_facing_goal: Understand and apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find unknown side lengths and distances.
purpose: Work in this section prepares students to understand and apply the Pythagorean Theorem. First, students investigate properties of right and non-right triangles by looking for patterns between their side lengths and the squares of their side lengths. Some students may notice that for right triangles only, a² + b² = c², where a and b are the legs of the right triangle and c is the longest side, or hypotenuse. This is formally introduced as the Pythagorean Theorem. Students are guided through an area-based algebraic proof of the Pythagorean Theorem, and an optional activity provides an opportunity to experience a transformations-based proof. Students then spend time applying the Pythagorean Theorem in various contexts to find unknown side lengths of right triangles. They also consider the converse of the Pythagorean Theorem, and use it to determine if given triangles are right triangles or not. Finally, students put together all they have learned and use the Pythagorean Theorem to find the distance between two points (or the length of the segment connecting two points).

building_on: Standard codes not explicitly listed in provided excerpt
addressing: 8.G.B.6 (inferred: Explain a proof of the Pythagorean Theorem)
8.G.B.7 (inferred: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to right triangles)
8.G.B.8 (inferred: Apply the Pythagorean Theorem to find distance in coordinate systems)
building_toward: Standard codes not explicitly listed in provided excerpt

task_events.position: Section B Checkpoint, Goal 1, part a
task_events.excerpt: Find the exact value of x. [Right triangle with legs 7 and √32, hypotenuse x]

task_events.position: Section B Checkpoint, Goal 1, part b
task_events.excerpt: An envelope measures 3½ inches tall by 5 inches wide. Kiran wants to use it to mail a pencil that measures 6 inches long. Will it fit diagonally?
Find the distance between the two points. [Coordinate plane showing (-3, 4) and (5, -2)]

boundary_note: This lesson introduces the Pythagorean Theorem as both an algebraic formula and a geometric principle. Students frequently confuse the theorem's application to right triangles only, incorrectly apply it to any triangle, and treat it mechanically without understanding why a² + b² = c² represents a meaningful geometric relationship. The area-based proof requires students to connect algebraic manipulation to visual decomposition, which many find conceptually challenging.

title: Applications of the Pythagorean Theorem
student_facing_goal: Let's explore some applications of the Pythagorean Theorem.
teacher_goals: Describe (orally) situations that use right triangles, and explain how the Pythagorean Theorem could help solve problems in those situations.
Use the Pythagorean Theorem to solve problems within a context, and explain (orally) how to organize the reasoning.
purpose: This lesson gives students the opportunity to use the Pythagorean Theorem as a tool to solve different application problems (MP2). In the first activity, students make sense of a problem involving the distance and speed of two children walking and riding a bike along different sides of a triangular region. In the second activity they find internal diagonals of rectangular prisms.

task_events.position: Activity 11.1 Math Talk: Square Roots, Problem 1
task_events.excerpt: Mentally find the value of each expression to the nearest half. √24
Using the Pythagorean Theorem, Tyler travels √16,400 meters going 5 meters per second, which will take him approximately 25.6 seconds since √16,400 / 5 ≈ 25.6.
Prism K has a diagonal of √77 units. Prism L has a diagonal of √75 units. Prism K is longer.
An 11.5 m support pole is attached to a vertical utility pole. The base of the support pole is 4.5 m from the base of the utility pole. How high up the utility pole does the support pole reach?

boundary_note: Students must recognize real-world situations involving right triangles and apply the Pythagorean Theorem to solve them. Key misconception: students often use naive reasoning (e.g., 'the figure with the largest edge or volume must have the longest diagonal'), and they make computational errors by miscopying intermediate values in multi-step calculator work—the lesson explicitly warns against errors like writing 14,600 instead of 16,400 for 100² + 80².

title: More Applications of the Pythagorean Theorem
teacher_goals: Generate sets of three numbers that satisfy the Pythagorean Theorem.
Identify what information is needed to solve a problem using the Pythagorean Theorem. Ask questions to elicit that information.
student_facing_goal: Let's solve problems using the Pythagorean Theorem.
purpose: This lesson offers students additional practice solving problems that use the Pythagorean Theorem. The first activity has students investigating Pythagorean triples. The main activity of the lesson uses the Information Gap routine, which requires students to make sense of problems and use precise language when asking for or giving information (MP1, MP6).

task_events.excerpt: A Pythagorean triple is a set of three integers a, b, and c where a² + b² = c². An example is 3, 4, and 5 because 3² + 4² = 5². Find other Pythagorean triples.
The length across the diagonal of the gym is √9,556, or approximately 97.8 feet. Tyler would need 8.1 strings, so the school should buy 9 strings of light.
The ladder will reach √60 feet up the wall, or approximately 7.7 feet.
The height of a cone is 12 cm and its slant height is 13 cm. What is the diameter of the base of the cone? 10 cm

boundary_note: Students learn to spot right triangles hidden within complex figures—like diagonals across rectangles or height/slant-height relationships in 3D objects—and apply the Pythagorean theorem to solve for missing lengths. The biggest stumbling blocks are failing to recognize the right triangle exists in the first place, confusing which side is the hypotenuse, and handling irrational answers or deciding when to round.

title: Finding Distances in the Coordinate Plane
teacher_goals: Calculate the distance between two points in the coordinate plane by using the Pythagorean Theorem and explain (orally) the solution method.
Generalize (orally) a method for calculating the length of a line segment in the coordinate plane using the Pythagorean Theorem.
purpose: In this lesson, students continue to apply the Pythagorean Theorem by finding distances between points in the coordinate plane.

task_events.answer: 5, 6, 8, 9, 11 units (pairs e, a, b, d, c respectively from closest to farthest)
task_events.excerpt: Order the following pairs of coordinates from closest to farthest apart. Be prepared to explain your reasoning.
Find the distances between the three points shown.
Have each person in your group select one of the sets of coordinate pairs shown. Then calculate the distance between these two coordinates.

task_events.answer: √17 units and √18 units; segment f is longer
task_events.excerpt: Calculate the exact lengths of segments e and f. Which segment is longer?
Find the distance (in units) between each pair of points.

boundary_note: This lesson bridges simple axis-aligned distances (coordinate subtraction) to the Pythagorean Theorem for arbitrary points. Students commonly misapply distance as the sum of horizontal and vertical changes (|Δx| + |Δy|) rather than √(Δx² + Δy²), and struggle computing legs when coordinates are negative. The key conceptual move is recognizing that any two points form a right triangle via auxiliary horizontal and vertical line segments.

title: Unit 8 Lesson 14 (title not provided in excerpt)

task_events.excerpt: What is the value of (3.1 × 10⁴) · (2 × 10⁶)?

task_events.answer: 5.63 × 10⁵ (Noah's answer 56.3 × 10⁴ is incorrect because it is not in proper scientific notation form)
task_events.excerpt: Evaluate: 5.4 × 10⁵ + 2.3 × 10⁴. Do you agree with Noah's solution? Explain.
Select all the expressions that are equivalent to 3⁸.

boundary_note: This excerpt shows only practice problems (pages 47–48) without instructional context. Students practice exponent rules and scientific notation fluency through multiplication, addition, and equivalence recognition. Key misconceptions: improper scientific notation format (56.3 × 10⁴ instead of 5.63 × 10⁵ in addition) and distinguishing equivalent exponential forms (especially the quotient rule 3⁶/3²). Formal lesson metadata—title, learning goals, lesson purpose, and CCSS standards—are not provided in the pages shown; consult the full lesson document for instructional context.

title: Cube Roots
teacher_goals: Determine the whole numbers that the cube root of a number lies between, and explain (orally) the reasoning.
Generalize a process for approximating the value of the cube root of a number, and justify (orally and in writing) that if x³ = n, then x = ∛n.
purpose: The purpose of this lesson is to encourage students to transition from the geometric characterization of cube roots as edge lengths to an algebraic characterization of cube roots as solutions to equations of the form x³ = n that can be understood as values on a number line. Students begin by considering whether statements about cube root notation are true, then reason about the value of a cube root by finding the two whole numbers it lies closest to, and finally think about ∛n as the solution to equations of the form x³ = n and plot these points on a number line.
student_facing_goal: Let's compare cube roots.

task_events.excerpt: ∛5 lies between which two consecutive whole numbers? 1³ = 1 and 2³ = 8, so ∛5 is between 1 and 2.
∛999 lies between which two whole numbers? 10³ = 1,000, so ∛999 is a little bit less than 10.
x³ = 5; plot x, y, and z on the number line; x should be between 1 and 2
z³ = 700; z should be between 8 and 9

task_events.answer: ∛49 should be between 3 and 4; Lin confused cube root with square root
task_events.excerpt: Where should ∛49 actually be on the number line? Lin placed the point at ∛49 because she thought it was a square root instead of a cube root.

boundary_note: This lesson bridges the geometric understanding of cube roots (edge lengths of cubes) to the algebraic understanding (solutions to equations x³ = n). The central challenge for students is distinguishing cube roots from square roots—a confusion the lesson surfaces in Activity 15.4—and learning to approximate irrational cube roots using perfect cubes as reference points on a number line. Most students have no automaton for this yet: reasoning about cube roots requires them to treat the inverse relationship (if x³ = n, then x = ∛n) as conceptually present rather than algorithmically computed.

title: Decimal Representations of Rational Numbers
teacher_goals: Represent rational numbers as equivalent decimals and fractions, and explain (orally) the solution method.
Help students understand that every number has a decimal expansion.
Enable students to write fractions as repeating decimals using long division and repeated reasoning.
purpose: This lesson explores different representations of rational numbers by helping students see that the same rational number can be written as a fraction, a decimal, or even as roots. Through zooming number lines and long division, students discover that some rationals have terminating decimal expansions (like 3/8 = 0.375) while others have repeating patterns (like 2/11 = 0.1818...). Both types are called rational numbers because any rational number can eventually be expressed as a positive or negative fraction a/b where a and b are integers with b ≠ 0.

task_events.position: Activity 16.3: Rational Numbers as Decimals; illustrated throughout using zooming number lines
task_events.excerpt: All rational numbers have decimal representations, too. Find the decimal representation of each of these rational numbers. [Including 3/8]
Use long division with repeated reasoning to find that 2/11 = 0.1818... (MP8). Students plot this decimal expansion on a series of zooming number lines.

boundary_note: The core insight is that rational numbers exist in multiple representations—fraction, terminating decimal, or repeating decimal—but students often conflate the place-value structure of decimals with the equal-parts partition structure of fractions. A major pitfall: students may not understand why 3/8 terminates (denominator has only factors of 2 and 5) but 2/11 repeats forever, nor why a repeating decimal like 0.1̄8̄ is still rational. The zooming number lines are essential here—they help students visualize that 2/11 gets closer and closer to precise tick marks but never lands exactly, creating a predictable endless pattern rather than a chaotic or incomplete number.
student_facing_goal: Let's learn more about how rational numbers can be represented.

title: Infinite Decimal Expansions
teacher_goals: Compare and contrast (orally) decimal expansions for rational and irrational numbers.
Coordinate (orally and in writing) repeating decimal expansions and rational numbers that represent the same number.
purpose: In this lesson, students explore finding decimal expansions of rational numbers as well as irrational numbers. Students begin by using long division to find the decimal expansion of 3/7, which starts to repeat after the seventh decimal place. Repeated reasoning allows students to stop the long division process and express the decimal expansion following the same steps (MP8). Next, students learn how to take a repeating decimal expansion and rewrite it in fraction form by ordering a set of cards that show the steps and an explanation of the process. They then practice with different decimal expansions following the same steps (MP7). In the last activity of this lesson, students investigate how to approximate decimal expansions of irrational numbers. Since values like √2 and π cannot be written as a fraction, students use successive approximations to find more and more digits of their decimal expansions.
student_facing_goal: Let's think about infinite decimals.

task_events.excerpt: The first 3 digits after the decimal for the decimal expansion of 3/7 have been calculated. Find the next 4 digits.
The cards show Noah's work calculating the fraction representation of 0.485̄. Arrange these in order to see how he figured out that 0.485̄ = 441/990
Use Noah's method to calculate the fraction representation of: 0.186̄
Use Noah's method to calculate the fraction representation of: 0.788̄
Why is √2 between 1 and 2 on the number line? How can you figure out an approximation for √2 accurate to 3 decimal places? Plot √2 on all three number lines.
Elena notices that a beaker in science class says it has a diameter of 9 cm and measures its circumference to be 28.3 cm. Using C = 2πr, what value do you get for π?

boundary_note: This lesson distinguishes rational numbers (with terminating or repeating decimal expansions) from irrational numbers (with non-repeating, non-terminating expansions). Students often misunderstand why 0.999... equals 1 exactly, and struggle to see why the algebraic method of converting repeating decimals to fractions—multiplying by powers of 10, then subtracting to isolate the repeating block—actually works: the machinery reaches what teachers often frame as a limit or pattern recognition, but the formal justification demands facility with equation solving that the curriculum has not yet isolated as a standalone topic."

title: When Is the Same Size Not the Same Size?
teacher_goals: Apply ratios and the Pythagorean Theorem to solve a problem involving the aspect ratio of screens or photos, and explain (orally) the reasoning.
Describe (in writing and using other representations) characteristics of rectangles with the same aspect ratio or with different aspect ratios.
purpose: In this culminating lesson for the unit, students use the Pythagorean Theorem to compare the diagonal lengths of rectangles with various dimensions. They investigate different aspect ratios of items such as photographs and smartphone screens. There is an element of mathematical modeling (MP4) in the last activity, because in order to quantify the screens' sizes to compare them, students need to refine the question that is asked.

task_events.excerpt: If the shorter side of the rectangle measures 15 inches: a. What is the length of the longer side?
If the shorter side of the rectangle measures 15 inches: b. What is the length of the rectangle's diagonal?
If the diagonal of the 4 : 3 rectangle measures 10 inches, how long are its sides?
If the diagonal of the 4 : 3 rectangle measures 6 inches, how long are its sides?

task_events.answer: Yes; the new screen (18.5:9, 5.8-inch diagonal) is 1.1 square inches smaller than the old screen (16:9, 5.8-inch diagonal). Old area: 14.4 sq in; new area: 13.3 sq in.
task_events.excerpt: Before 2017, a phone had a 5.8-inch diagonal and 16:9 aspect ratio. In 2017, a new model had the same 5.8-inch diagonal but 18.5:9 aspect ratio. Were customers correct that the new phones had a smaller screen? If so, how much smaller?

boundary_note: Rectangles with the same diagonal can have different areas; aspect ratio determines screen size. Students often conflate diagonal length with "size," missing that proportions dramatically affect area. The lesson requires solving multi-step equations where ratio constraints (e.g., sides as 4x and 3x) combine with the Pythagorean theorem to fix a scale factor, then comparing areas—not diagonals—to determine which screen is actually larger.

title: Tessellations of the Plane
student_facing_goal: Let's explore geometric patterns!
teacher_goals: Create and describe patterns with specific polygons that fill the plane.
purpose: This optional lesson focuses on the idea of a tessellation: a repeating pattern that covers a plane with no gaps or overlaps. The lesson begins with a visual analysis of tessellations where students apply their knowledge of geometry and transformations to describe repeating patterns. In the next activity, students create and compare tessellations by making use of structure (MP7). Finally, students work to describe and recreate tessellations from a partner's description. As they become more precise in their descriptions, their recreations become more exact (MP6). This lesson also includes examples of tessellations used in art from a variety of cultures, which can be explored as an extension.
boundary_note: A tessellation tiles a plane with no gaps or overlaps; the key constraint is that certain shapes (like regular hexagons) force a unique pattern because exactly three must meet at each vertex, while other shapes offer multiple tessellation possibilities. Students often conflate "describing a tessellation" with "knowing its geometric properties"—precision in specifying placement and transformations (translations by direction-distance, rotations by center-and-angle, reflections by line) is the cognitive work, not numerical computation.

title: Regular Tessellations
student_facing_goal: Let's make some regular tessellations.
teacher_goals: Justify (orally and in writing) that regular triangles, squares, and hexagons are the only regular polygons that can be used to create a regular tessellation.
purpose: In this second lesson in the sequence of three optional lessons, students look at regular tessellations, which are made of regular polygons. Students construct arguments about which regular polygons can create regular tessellations (MP3) and reason about why others cannot (MP2). This reasoning requires an understanding of the structure of interior angles of regular polygons (MP7). Through the lesson, students show in detail that triangles, squares, and hexagons give the only possible regular tessellations. Students begin by exploring triangles, squares, pentagons, hexagons, and octagons to determine which can create a regular tessellation. Then students focus on equilateral triangles and the angle measurements that guarantee equilateral triangles will tessellate. Finally, students experiment with shapes that have more sides to determine if any of those can create regular tessellations, bringing in their knowledge of angle measures to support their claims.

task_events.excerpt: What is the measure of each angle in an equilateral triangle? → 60 degrees. The sum of the angles in any triangle is 180 degrees, and they are all congruent, so each must be a 60-degree angle.
How many triangles can you fit together at 1 vertex? → 6, because 6 · 60 = 360
Explain why there is no space between the triangles. → 6, because 6 · 60 = 360
The triangles, squares, and hexagons all tessellate the plane... four squares meet at each vertex
The triangles, squares, and hexagons all tessellate the plane... Three hexagons meet at each vertex
The angles in a square are 90 degrees, greater than the 60-degree angles in a triangle.

boundary_note: The lesson hinges on a deceptively simple constraint: a regular tessellation works only when a whole number of polygons fit exactly at each vertex with their interior angles summing to 360°. Students often underestimate how restrictive this angle-sum requirement is and may not understand why a 'perfect' regular pentagon fails despite being a valid regular shape—they confuse regularity with tessellability.

title: Tessellating Polygons
teacher_goals: Generalize (orally) that any triangle or quadrilateral can be used to tessellate the plane.
student_facing_goal: Let's make tessellations with different polygons.
purpose: In this third in the sequence of three optional lessons, students examine tessellations using non-regular polygons. Students first study triangles and show that any triangle can tessellate the plane by composing two copies into a parallelogram (a grade 6 idea revisited). Next, students show that all quadrilaterals can also tessellate the plane by using rigid motions and the fact that the sum of the angles in a quadrilateral is always 360 degrees. Lastly, students investigate a pentagon whose tessellation leads to rotational thinking. Through experimenting with these shapes, students construct arguments about which polygons can tessellate the plane and why (MP3) and apply their knowledge of interior angles of polygons to support their arguments (MP7).
boundary_note: Students often believe only regular or symmetric polygons can tessellate; this lesson proves that any triangle or quadrilateral works because their interior angles sum perfectly to fill vertices (180° and 360° respectively). The key misconception students face is not recognizing how angle arithmetic forces the tessellation pattern, and assuming all pentagons tessellate once they discover some do.

title: What Influences Temperature?
student_facing_goal: Let's see if we can predict the temperature.
teacher_goals: Create a plan to model a relationship involving bivariate data.
purpose: In this optional lesson, students investigate how to predict the temperature of a location in North America. This question is purposefully vague and is meant for students to think critically about the variables needed to model the situation with mathematics.
boundary_note: This lesson introduces the modeling cycle by establishing that temperature depends on multiple variables, so students must strategically fix some (time, geographic scope) while investigating others (latitude). The key misconception: students often expect a clean functional relationship without recognizing that confounders like elevation and ocean proximity create real-world messiness that violates the function definition.

title: Plotting the Temperature
student_facing_goal: Let's construct a model.
teacher_goals: Create a mathematical model of bivariate data using a scatter plot.
purpose: In this second in the sequence of optional lessons, students construct a mathematical model to investigate if there is an association between latitude and temperature (MP4). The lesson begins with an example of a scatter plot and how to talk about associations seen in data. Then students create and answer questions about the data they gathered or the data provided in the lesson. In either case, students create a scatter plot of the data and approximate a line of best fit. They must make decisions about reasonable values when creating their axes and interpreting the data (MP2).

task_events.answer: minimum 12.36 degrees north (Chinandega, NI), maximum 64.84 degrees north (Fairbanks, AK)
task_events.excerpt: For latitude, the minimum value is 12.36 degrees north in Chinandega, NI, and the maximum is 64.84 degrees north in Fairbanks, AK.

task_events.answer: minimum 50 degrees Fahrenheit (Yellowknife, NT), maximum 96 degrees Fahrenheit (Tucson, AZ)
task_events.excerpt: For temperature, the low is 50 degrees Fahrenheit for Yellowknife, NT, in Canada, and the maximum is 96 degrees Fahrenheit for Tucson, AZ.

task_events.answer: 24 ordered pairs (latitude, temperature) plotted with latitude in degrees north on x-axis and temperature in Fahrenheit on y-axis
task_events.excerpt: Make a scatter plot of the data.

task_events.answer: y = -0.87x + 111, where x is latitude and y is temperature
task_events.excerpt: The line's equation is y = -0.87x + 111, where x is latitude and y is temperature.

boundary_note: Students model the negative association between latitude and temperature using scatter plots and lines of best fit. The key challenge is understanding that the line of best fit is an approximation—students need to accept that different groups' eyeball estimates will vary slightly and that choosing appropriate axis scales based on data range often requires explicit scaffolding.

title: Using and Interpreting a Mathematical Model
student_facing_goal: Let's use a model to make some predictions.
teacher_goals: Interpret a scatter plot and line of fit that model temperature and latitude, and explain (orally) limitations of the model.
Use a mathematical model of bivariate data to make predictions (in writing).
purpose: To complete this section, in this optional lesson students analyze their model and use it to make predictions and draw conclusions. Students validate their model by seeing how well it predicts the actual temperature for locations that were not included in the original data. They also look for and investigate outliers in the data. Finally, students interpret mathematical features of their model (slope and intercepts of the line) and discuss limitations of the model.

task_events.excerpt: Use your model to predict the average high temperature in September for the following cities that were not included in the original data set: a. Detroit (Lat: 42.33 degrees north)
b. Albuquerque (Lat: 35.09 degrees north)
c. Nome (Lat: 64.50 degrees north)
Find the vertical and horizontal intercepts and interpret them in the context of the situation.
Find the vertical and horizontal intercepts and interpret them in the context of the situation.

boundary_note: This lesson teaches that a mathematically valid model has real-world limitations tied to the data it was built from. Students often assume an equation that works mathematically will predict accurately at all values of the independent variable; they struggle to recognize that predictions at extreme latitudes are unreliable and that geographic-specific climate patterns constrain the model's domain to North America alone.
